VOLUME XIX.

Friday, 24th January, 1936.
DEMISE OF THE CROWN.
It having pleased Almighty God to
take to His Mercy our late Most
Gracious Sovereign Lord King George
of blessed memory, who departed this
life on Monday, the 20th of this instant
January, between the hours of eleven
and twelve o'clock in the evening, at
Sandringham House; and the Most
Honourable Privy Council of Northern
Ireland, and others, having met this
day at Parliament Buildings, Stormont,
and having signed a Proclamation proclaiming His Majesty King Edward
the Eighth as King, it was directed by
His Grace the Governor that the said
Proclamation should be read to both
Houses of Parliament.
The Senate met at half-past Twelve
o'clock, pursuant to the Proclamation by
his Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland.
Mr.
SPEAKER: His Grace the
Governor commands this honourable
House to attend him immediately in the
Central Hall.
Mr. SPEAKER, with the House, proceeded to the Central Hall, where
His GRACE THE GOVERNOR said:
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons, my Lords, ladies and gentlemen, we are met to-day under the shadow
EDWARD VIII.
of a grievous loss, and as a token of
respect to our late well-beloved King, his
Gracious Majesty George V, I would ask
you now to pause for one brief moment
with me.
The House stood for a short time in
silence.
His GRACE THE GOVERNOR: It is
now my duty to proclaim the Accession.
of his Majesty the King.
His Grace then read the Proclamation
as followeth :
A Proclamation
WHEREAS it hath pleased Almighty
God to call to His Mercy our late
Sovereign Lord King George the Fifth,
of Blessed and Glorious Memory, by whose
Decease the Imperial Crown of Great
Britain, Ireland and all other His late
Majesty's dominions is solely and rightfully come to the High and Mighty Prince
Edward Albert Christian George Andrew
Patrick David;
WE, therefore, the Governor of
Northern Ireland, being here assisted with
these of His late Majesty's Privy Council
with Numbers of other Principal Gentlemen of Quality, with the Lord Mayor
and Citizens of Belfast, do now hereby,
with one Voice and Consent of Tongue
and Heart, publish and proclaim, That
the High and Mighty Prince Edward
Albert Christian George Andrew Patrick
David, is now, by the Death of our late
Sovereign of Happy Memory, become our
only lawful and rightful Liege Lord
Edward the Eighth, by the Grace of God,
of Great Britain, Ireland and the
Members Sworn
24 JANUARY 1936
Adjournment
Colonel Thomas Sinclair.
British Dominions beyond the Seas King,
Defender of the Faith, Emperor of India;
To whom we do acknowledge all Faith
and constant Obedience, with all hearty
and humble Affection; beseeching God,
by whom Kings and Queens do reign,
to bless the Royal Prince Edward the
Eighth with long and happy Years to
reign over us.
Given at the Houses of Parliament,
Belfast, This Twenty-fourth day of
January, in the year of our Lord
One Thousand Nine Hundred and
Thirty-six.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
The House having returned,
Mr. Speaker took and subscribed the
Oath.
Then the following Members took and
subscribed the Oath:-
The Right Hon. the Viscount Charlemont.
John Andrew Long, Esq.
Sir Crawford McCullagh.
Sir Emerson Herdman.
James Hill Dickson, Esq.
Thomas Robert Lavery, Esq.
Captain John Robert Perceval-Maxwell.
Dr. William Gibson.
Sir Joseph Davison.
John Porter Porter, Esq.
The Right Hon. Henry Bruce Armstrong.
John Clements Glendinning, Esq.
The Right Hon. Samuel Cunningham.
James M'Mahon, Esq.
James Graham Leslie, Esq.
Colonel Herculus Arthur Pakenham.
Joseph Cunningham, Esq.
Andrew Quigley, Esq.
William Barclay, Esq.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): This House will meet tomorrow at 11-30 a.m., and I hope that
every Member who can find it convenient
to attend will do so. I beg to move,
"That this House do now adjourn."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
One o'clock until 11-30
a.m. to-morrow.
Death of
King George V.
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Saturday, 25th January, 1936.
The Senate met at Twenty-five minutes
before Twelve of the Clock Noon, Mr.
SPEAKER in the Chair.
DEATH OF KING GEORGE V.
Address to Her Majesty.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move:-
"That an humble Address be presented
to His Majesty as follows:-
:-
"Most gracious Sovereign,
"We, your Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of Northern
Ireland, in Parliament assembled, beg
leave to convey to your Majesty the heartfelt sympathy of this House in the grievous
affliction and loss which your Majesty has
sustained by the death of the late King,
your Majesty's father, of blessed and
glorious memory, whose reign this House
will ever remember with grateful affection;
"To offer to your Majesty our loyal
congratulations upon
upon your Majesty's
auspicious accession;
"To assure your Majesty of our devotion
to your Majesty's person, and of our sure
conviction that your Majesty's reign will,
under the favour of Divine Providence, be
distinguished by unswerving efforts to
promote the virtue, prosperity, and contentment of the realm, and to guard the
rights and liberties of your Majesty's
faithful people.'
By leave of the House and for its convenience, I propose to cover all the
Motions on the Order Paper in one speech.
The Motions themselves will be put
separately.
It is with a deep sense of personal loss
that I address the House to-day upon the
calamity which has overtaken us—a feeling which, I think, is shared by all the
millions of King George's subjects. It is
only a few weeks since we listened to
his broadcast on Christmas Day, rejoicing
that modern science was able to bring into
every home throughout the Empire the
sympathy and affection that the King felt
for all his people, and few of us realised
that we should hear that message of inspiration and courage from him no more.
We have all read the tributes which
have been paid to his memory from every
country in the world, and it would be
possible to take one of the many aspects
of his character and dwell upon it only,
to the exclusion of all else. I think,
however, that the place King George held
in our hearts was due to the fact that his
character was the ennobled character of
the average representative man.
King
The Sovereign has often been a kind of
abstraction-the embodiment of our
Constitution and Empire, but devoid of
the attributes of ordinary humanity, exalted above the difficulties and trials that
beset ordinary people, and therefore, lacking in sympathy towards them.
George's kindliness of heart, exemplified
on so many occasions, and notably in his
two Christmas messages, brought the
occupant of the Throne into real personal
contact with all of us, and we could feel
that could he but know, he would be willing to help the humblest of his subjects
and we could regard him, as has been said,
as the father of his people.
But while the King's personal kindness
and sympathy secured him the love and
affection of his subjects, these qualities
were enhanced by respect for his courage
and wisdom, and the statesmanship which
he displayed on all the occasions in which
it was needed throughout his reign. We in
this House had evidence of those qualities
nearly fourteen years ago; we remember
only too well the state in which Ireland
was at the time, and who is there who did
not admire the courage which was displayed by King George and Queen Mary
when they drove so slowly through the
streets of Belfast, and who did not admire
also the statesmanlike speech with which
King George opened the Northern Parliament?
King George's reign was cast in times
of extraordinary perplexities both at home
and abroad-the controversies round the
Parliament Act and Ireland, the outbreak
of the Great War, and all the post-war
troubles down to the formation of the
National Government. That we have
Death of
King George V.
25 JANUARY 1936
been able to come through these times with
our Constitution intact, our Empire
united, and our Throne secure is due to
the influence of King George, and if
success is to be measured by the difficulties
of achievement, his name will live in
history amongst those of the greatest of
our kings.
To Queen Mary the respectful but
wholehearted sympathy of this House is
extended.
Queen Mary has won her way into our
hearts by her devoted services to the
country which, with King George, has
always been the subject of her thoughts
and care. A singularly happy married
life of nearly forty-three years has come
to an end, and we can only pray that
God will ease the burden of grief she
is called upon to bear.
And now we offer our loyal congratulations to King Edward VIII upon his
auspicious accession. No King has come
to the Throne with a wider knowledge of
his Empire, or with a greater experience
of men and cities in all parts of the
world. He has given proofs of his
sympathy with his people
his people by his
unremitting work
on behalf of the
unemployed, by his practical efforts for
the coming generation in the inauguration of King George's Jubilee Trust, by
his wise and constructive speeches on
many occasions.
While the King has delighted to take
part in the sport which appeals to the
majority of his subjects he has never
neglected public
public duty for private
pleasure, and he has ever followed the
ideal laid down in the motto of the
Princes of Wales-"Ich dien," "I serve.'
He takes up a heavy burden—
,,
"What infinite hearts ease must kings
neglect that private men enjoy."
He can command the allegiance of all
his subjects, but he can be fortified also
with the knowledge that all our affection
and sympathy are with him-God Save
the King."
Mr. LESLIE: It is very difficult to
follow the noble Viscount, for he has, if
I may say so, voiced in beautiful and
considered language the sentiments of us
all. In approaching a subject of such
Imperial interest, and also of such human
interest, one scarcely knows how to choose
one's words or even how to direct one's
thoughts. I think perhaps that, taking
the people of Great Britain and Northern Ireland as a whole, one of the
first thoughts that occurred to them
when the fateful news was communicated through the medium of broadcasting must have been that they were
experiencing another instance of that
significant fact that mortality is common
to us all. They must have felt, though
they may not perhaps have recognised it,
that the passing of his late Majesty was
merely a reminder that, as was said so
very many years ago, "Pale death bursts
with indiscriminating step alike through
the doors of the hovels of the poor, and
through the gateways of the castles of
the Kings." That, perhaps, was the
first thought that occurred to everybody.
Then, following that would come, I
imagine, the more Christian sentiment
in which most of us believe, that
transition is not the end. Knowledge of
that sort was hidden from the Roman
poet on whose writings I was drawing a
moment or two ago, but the fact remains
with us that we believe that passing is
not the end, and we are also able to
rejoice that, through the assistance of
modern science, His Majesty's voice has
been preserved to us, and, indeed, part
of the Christmas message was broadcast
to us again during those anxious days.
The noble Viscount has alluded to the
presence of His Majesty in Belfast at
the inception of our Parliament. Many
of us were present on that occasion, and
many of us have seen His Majesty on
other occasions. I confess that I
myself shall always like to think of him
best as I saw him one day at a race
moving about just like any other ordinary
meeting in England, when he
country gentleman, and he gave one the
impression of a squire amongst the
villagers.
was
Of the great work he did as head of
the State I need not speak. The noble
Viscount has covered that ground, and
one passes to the theme of congratulation
to King Edward VIII. Many will recall
how ardently His Majesty served with his
father's troops in France during the late
war. Others will recall this or that
incident of his thoughtfulness for others,
to which the noble Viscount has alluded.
Death of
King George V.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
As regards Her Majesty the Queen
Mother, there is nothing to be said. It is
a case where words are wholly insufficient
to convey one's meaning. I imagine
most of us heard, again on the wireless,
the words addressed by the Prime
Minister of England a few nights ago.
Mr. Baldwin spoke of the fact that consolation might come to Her Majesty
partly through the thought that she was
left to bear the sorrow, not her husband.
I cannot think of anything so touching
as those words of the Prime Minister of
England.
It remains only to associate oneself, as
the mouthpiece for the moment of the
House, with the words used by the noble
Viscount, and with the Resolutions on
the Paper.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I had
not intended to speak on these Resolutions, but, perhaps as representing the
business classes of Belfast, I should like
to say a few words. I was always taught
to believe that the late Queen Alexander
was one of those exceptional mothers who
brought up her family in the most ideal
way, and in the best way one could wish.
I happened to be in London when King
George, then Duke of York, was married,
and I recall my excitement on that
occasion. It was the first procession I
remember seeing as it came along St.
James's Street.
It strikes me that perhaps we do not
realise, and will not realise for some time
to come, that a very good and a very
great man has passed away. What he
has done for the British Empire has not,
I think, been due to his actions so much
as to his example. In the dark troubled
days of the war, when the people were
despondent, and when everyone was
asking what we were to do and how we
were to come to terms, the King seemed
to give the lead. To-day the nation and
the British Empire and the majority of
the people of the world can look back and
say, "Here was a great leader." The
interest he took in his people at home
appealed to everyone.
There was one particular thing that
made a deep impression on my mind.
When King George was taken ill, eight
or nine years ago, his eldest son, the
present King, was away in Africa. I
never followed any event with such
interest as I did his speedy journey home.
Here was a devoted son who, when he
learned that his father was seriously ill,
hastened home immediately. The way in
which the French people facilitated his
progress through their country and the
respect they showed him earned our gratitude. It was significant that from the
time the then Prince of Wales arrived
home his father began to improve. That
is one of those incidents which went to
show the great attachment the son had
for his father and the father for his son.
""
It has occurred to me that if any title
could be bestowed on his late Majesty
it should be "the good." We read in
history of several men who have been
called the good," and of some who
have been called "the Great," but I do
not think that any man has deserved
those titles more from the world, and
especially from the British Empire, than
the late King George.
I should also like to express my deep
sympathy with the Queen Mother.
We
can all realise what the loss must mean to
her Majesty after more than forty years
of happy married life. She is the first
lady in the land, but that fact should not
prevent us extending to her our respectful
and sincere sympathy in her hour of
sorrow. I am pleased I had this opportunity-I did not mean to take it of
expressing what I think of King George.
Perhaps he is one of the men who stand
out in this century like Lincoln in the
last. He is one of the great men of this
age and of all ages.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
sure most of us in this House feel that
the noble Leader has expressed in very
suitable words our feelings on this particular occasion. I know that this House
is very representative of the people of
Northern Ireland-from those in the best
positions and professions down to those
who represent the working classes. I feel
that a word should be said for the working
class people who are a very important part
of the community, and I may take it on
myself to say a word as their representative. I believe that in the whole British
Commonwealth there is not a spot where
the working-class people are more loyal to
the Crown of England than in Northern
Ireland. I am satisfied that there is not
a spot in the British Commonwealth where
Death of
King George V.
25 JANUARY 1936
the working-class people feel this loss more
than our people at the present time. Even
in the most humble dwelling in Northern
Ireland where there may not be many
valuable pictures, there will certainly
always be found a picture of the King of
England. The people are
very much
attached to the Crown, and I am perfectly
satisfied that they feel very much the loss
they have sustained.
His late Majesty King George endeared
himself more to the people than any previous Monarch. He had the opportunity
of speaking over the wireless, and every
person had the privilege of listening to the
very words spoken by himself. His
Majesty assured us on more than one
occasion that he was the father and friend
of the people. We recognise that he did
all he possibly could for the benefit and
welfare of the working classes. We all
join the noble Lord in expressing our
sincere sympathy with her Majesty the
Queen in the loss she has sustained. The
people recognise what the mother is in the
home and they recognise what a loss it
must be to her Majesty to have to finish
her years in loneliness.
I am sure there is no people who wish
the present King Edward more sincerely
good health, long life and success in the
position to which he has been called than
the people of Northern Ireland. He has
been amongst us as Prince of Wales; we
have
seen
him and shaken him by the
hand, and, therefore, we feel very familiar
towards him, and we wish him long life
and a happy and prosperous reign. I just
wish to associate myself on behalf of the
working-class people of whom I am one,
with what has been said.
Mr. SPEAKER: When I put this
Question I should like hon. Members to
signify their assent by rising in their
places. The Question is, That this Motion
be agreed to.
Question agreed to nemine dissentiente,
the House standing in silence.
Resolved, "That this House do approach
his Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland with the request that he will be
pleased to cause the said Address to be
presented to his Majesty."—(Viscount
Charlemont.)
Resolved, "That the said humble
Address and Resolution be presented to
his Grace the Governor by such Members
of this House as are Members of his
Majesty's Privy Council in Northern
Ireland." (Viscount Charlemont.)
MESSAGE OF CONDOLENCE.
Resolved, nemine dissentiente, "That
a Message of Condolence be sent to her
Majesty on behalf of this House as
follows:
"We, the Senate of Northern Ireland, beg leave to tender to your Majesty
the deep sympathy which this House
feels for your Majesty in this melancholy time of sorrow and irreparable
loss." (Viscount Charlemont.)
Adjournment.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until such day as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Adjourned accordingly at
five minutes past Twelve
o'clock until such date as
Mr. Speaker shall appoint.

The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.

DEATH OF KING GEORGE V.
Message from Queen Mary.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have received the
following messages from his Grace the
Governor:
I have received from the Senate of
Northern Ireland in Parliament assembled
the Address of Sympathy with her
Majesty on the death of the late King.
I shall be pleased to forward the
Address to her Majesty.
Her Majesty Queen Mary, having
received from the Senate of Northern
Ireland the Message of Sympathy on the
occasion of the lamented death of His
Most Gracious Majesty King George the
Fifth, desires me to convey her grateful
appreciation of this manifestation of their
sympathy, and to state that Her Majesty
is deeply touched by the kind thought
which is conveyed by the message.
Message from His Grace the Governor.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House) reported that a Privy Councillor of Northern Ireland had waited on
His Grace the Governor with the Address
from the Senate (pursuant to the Order
of 27th November, 1935), to which His
Grace was pleased to return the following
answer:
I have received with great satisfaction
the expression of your thanks for the
Speech with which I opened the present
Session of Parliament.
Superannuation Act, 1935.
Viscount CHARLEMONT reported that
a Privy Councillor of Northern Ireland
had waited on His Grace the Governor
with the Address from the Senate
(pursuant to the Order of 10th December,
1935), to which His Grace was pleased
to return the following answers:-
I have received your Address praying
that it may please His Majesty to make
an Order in Council under sub-section
Address to His Majesty
Private Business
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
(2) of section eighteen of the Superannuation Act, 1935, directing that
certain provisions of the said Act shall
apply to existing Irish Officers within
the meaning of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, to whom the Superannuation Acts, 1834 to 1914, apply by virtue
of sub-section (2) of section fifty-five of
that Act.
I shall be pleased to forward the
Address to His Majesty's Secretary of
State for the Home Department, who
will bring the matter before His Majesty
in Council.
I have received your Address praying
that it may please His Majesty the King
to make an Order in Council that the
Superannuation Act, 1935, shall apply to
existing Irish Officers within the meaning
of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920,
to whom the Superannuation Acts, 1834
to 1914 apply.
His Majesty has been pleased to
comply with your request.
Address to His Majesty.
Viscount CHARLEMONT reported
that a Privy Councillor of Northern
Ireland had waited on His Grace the
Governor with the Address from the
Senate (pursuant to the Order of 25th
January), to which His Grace was
pleased to return the following answer :-
I have received from the Senate
of Northern Ireland, in Parliament
assembled, the Address expressing
sympathy with His Majesty on the
death of the late King; also offering
congratulations to His Majesty King
Edward VIII upon his Succession.
I shall be pleased to forward the
Address to His Majesty.
Viscount CHARLEMONT reported to
the House that the following Message
had been received from His Grace the
Governor :
I have received the King's Command
to convey to the Senate of Northern Ireland His Majesty's thanks for their
sympathy, expressed in their loyal and
dutiful Address, in the grievous loss
which His Majesty has sustained by the
death of his father, the late King.
His Majesty appreciates the congratulations of the Senate and the assurance
of their loyal devotion to his person.

Housing (Grants) Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The object
of this Bill is to extend the period during
which housing subsidies can be paid. Under
the existing Acts the subsidy cannot be paid
on any houses which are not completed
on or before the 31st March, 1936, and
under the Bill now before the House
it is proposed to enable payment
to be made in respect of all houses completed on or before the 30th September,
1936, with an extension in favour of
houses built by local authorities up to the
31st March, 1937.
Very considerable success has attended
the working of the Housing Acts, and
the time is approaching when it will be
possible to dispense with the need of a
subsidy to stimulate building. Since the
establishment of this Government some
32,000 houses have been built with the
aid of the Government and local authorities, and it is anticipated that this
number will have risen to 37,000 by the
time the period of the subsidy comes to
an end. As, in addition to these, we
expect some thousands of houses to be
erected in rural areas under the Labourers
Act recently passed, it seems likely that
our housing problem will be solved at no
very
distant date.
But at present, as I have said, there is
still a shortage, as is evidenced by the
number of applicants for houses on the
waiting lists of builders, and I am sure
that everyone will agree to the proposal
to continue the subsidy for some time
longer. We think that six months should
be sufficient, and accordingly the general
proposal in the Bill is to extend the period
to the 30th September of this year. This
will apply in the case of all private
builders. In the case of local authorities,
as some of these may have to clear sites
of old houses before they can start building it is proposed in certain circumstances
that they should be allowed up to the
31st March, 1937, to complete their
operations. I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read a Second Time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Question to Minister.
CENSUS OF NORTHERN IRELAND.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether it is proposed to
take a census of the population of Northern Ireland during the present year.
I think that the question explains.
itself. I may perhaps say that in putting
it down I was not unmindful of the fact
that it was ten years since a census
was taken, nor was I unmindful of what
was said in 1925 when the necessary
legislation was promoted in this House.
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
answer is in the negative. I think a
census is likely to be taken in Great
Britain in the year 1941, and I do not
think it would be advisable to take a
census of Northern Ireland alone in the
meantime. The question is not entirely.
decided, but a census will not be taken
this year.
Adjournment.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until Thursday." (Viscount
Charlemont).
-
Adjourned accordingly at
half after Three o'clock
until Thursday next.

Questions to Minister.
HISTORY BOOKS IN SCHOOLS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the
of
name
Mr.
Quigley :-
To ask the Leader of the House,
whether there is or was in use in any
of the schools to which grants are
made by the Government a book
entitled "Bell's Modern School
Histories," Vol. III (1832-1931), by
D. C. Somervell, M.A.; whether this
book contains statements in regard to
the Union between Great Britain and
Ireland and the struggle by
by the
loyalists of Ulster against Home Rule
which are at variance with the facts;
whether these erroneous statements are
calculated to mislead the rising generation; and whether the Ministry will
prohibit the use of this book, and
other books of a similar type in any
school under its control or in receipt
of Government grants.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Perhaps I better
explain why I put this question. Mr.
Somervell's book contains many statements which, in my opinion, are not in
accordance with facts. I will read a
few of the statements and then hon.
Senators can judge for themselves:
:-
"The Irish people hated us (the
British)... because the Union of
Great Britain and Ireland had been a
failure from the start."
"It was a pity that the Conservatives did not combine with Gladstone in giving Ireland Home Rule
in 1886. If they had done so the
tragic events we shall have to describe
in the latter part of this book would
never have taken place."
"(
The Conservatives gave their
encouragement and support to the
Ulster threat of rebellion against the
law. The long, miserable story is not
worth telling now."
"Ireland has since the war obtained
far
more freedom than Asquith's
Home Rule Bill would have given her,
and we can see it was bound to come,
and is quite right."
"We offered them (after the Great
War) only the old Home Rule terms,
which meant that the British Government retained a good deal of control
over Ireland. The result was war."
"The
new Ireland has made an
excellent start, and there is every
reason to think that it will find a
happiness it never enjoyed so long as
it was ruled from London as part of
the United Kingdom."
These are some of the statements in
this book, and I am sure hon. Senators
will be greatly surprised to hear them.
Such a history book might be used in the
Irish Free State, but it is against the
whole attitude and outlook of the people
of Northern Ireland-or at least the
vast majority of them. Some persons
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
5 MARCH 1936
have said that the Sinn Fein rebellion in
Dublin in 1916 had really its origin in
the national schools of Southern Ireland, where anti-British feeling was
engendered and fostered in the pupils by
the teachers who drew their salaries from
the British Treasury. If that be so, it
should be a warning to us to provide for
a little closer supervision over what is
taught in the way of history in our
schools.
It is painful to think that the rising
generation should be taught that the
Union was a failure, when we remember
the strenuous efforts made to maintain
it by such eminent leaders as Arthur
Balfour, Bonar Law, Austin Chamberlain, Walter Long, Lord Londonderry,
Lord Cave, Sir Edward Carson, George
Wyndham, Lord Randolph Churchill,
Earl Percy, Primate D'Arcy, and our
own Prime Minister. Were they all
wrong? Yet we are seriously told in this
book that the opposition to the Home
Rule Bills of Gladstone and Asquith was
a mistake, that the action taken by
Ulster loyalists to retain their place in
the United Kingdom and under the
Union Jack was a blunder, and that the
establishment of the present anti-British
régime in Southern Ireland was right.
What is occurring in the Irish Free
State at the present time demonstrates
beyond all doubt that the attitude taken
up by the Ulster loyalists was right and
proper. If they had submitted to the
Home Rule Bills of Mr. Gladstone and
Mr. Asquith we would all now be within
an independent Irish Republic owing no
allegiance to His Majesty the King, and
none of us would like that. Fortunately,
we fought against being driven out of
the United Kingdom, and as a result we
are still part and parcel of the British
Empire. It is easy enough to face and
fight an open foe, but in history books
there can be undermining of a most
insidious type-the poisoning of the
minds of the children with one-sided and
biassed statements.
I think it is time that we in Northern
Ireland wakened up and insisted that the
children in our area, whether they be
Roman Catholics or Protestants, be
taught the true facts. We ask for
nothing more, and we shall be satisfied
with nothing less.
The MINISTER OF EDUCATION
(Viscount Charlemont): The selection of
text-books for use in the schools is left
in the hands of managers and teachers,
subject to the general rule that no book
may be used to which a reasonable objection might be entertained on religious
or political grounds. In the case of
history readers used in public elementary
schools, the choice of books must be
limited to those on a list prepared and
published in the Ministry.
The book referred to in the question
was included in the Ministry's list, but
so far as I have been able to ascertain,
its use has been confined to a very few
schools. It deals with the last hundred
years of British history, and apart from
the references to Ireland, which fill less
than a dozen in a volume of 270 pages,
it appears to me to be on the whole, an
interesting and well-written work. Its
attitude towards controversial topics is
distinctly liberal in tone, but is usually
judicious and calculated to impress on
the young that there are two ideas to
most questions, indeed to every question.
As an example of this judicial point of
view, the hon. Senator quoted what the
writer or author of this book thought of
the Conservative Party with regard to
Ulster, but he omitted to say that in the
same paragraph the author said the
Liberals accepted the full Nationalist
programme in spite of the injustice to
Ulster, so that the book is not entirely
one-sided.

The allusions to the Irish and Ulster
question may be considered too partial
towards a certain section of Irishmen,
and not as sympathetic to our point of
view in Ulster as we should like, but at
the same time I think there are expressions in the book which show that the
writer recognises the justification for the
attitude of Ulster loyalists in the events
of the past few years.
Although I think it is possible that
an error of judgment was made by the
Ministry in including this work in their
list, I would deprecate the idea that
history readers should give only one
point of view towards contemporary
events. After all, the teacher is there
to guide the child and direct his attention to the importance of using his
critical faculties in dealing with the assertions of writers, but propaganda in the
form of history is a thing to be deplored.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers

THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
I am at present arranging for a reissue of the list of history readers, and in
view of the fact that the work in question
has given rise to controversy and may,
from certain points of view, be considered objectionable, I do not intend to
have it included in the revised list from
which alone history readers may be
chosen for use in public elementary
schools.
IMPRISONMENT IN DEFAULT OF
PAYMENT OF FINES.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel
Cunningham:
-
To ask the Leader of the House,
whether he can give the number of
persons imprisoned for failing to pay
fines and rates, also wife and children
maintenance, and the expense to the
country caused by this course of
imprisonment.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
ask the question standing in my name
believing that if the law of Northern Ireland is the same as that in England up
to the beginning of this year a considerable amount of expense might be saved.
In England more than one-third of those
imprisoned were so treated not because
any court had deliberately decided after
trial that they ought to go to prison. I
feel sure that at least a proportion of the
11,000 people who go to prison every
year for non-payment of fines would not
have been sent to prison if the magistrate
had had before him the question whether
imprisonment was the proper course or
not. These statements were made by the
Home Minister in London, Sir John
Simon, and I think his views are worth
considering. He also says that the
justices should be required in every case
of default in the payment of rates to
consider whether the default is due to
wilful refusal or if the defaulter is not
to blame for his failure to pay.
There is another aspect which I think
should save considerable expense, and
that is the case where a man is sentenced
to pay maintenance for his children. I
know of a case where a man was to pay
for his children and he did not pay. He
was put into jail. He was in a good
situation and he lost his job. In that
case I think the law would be improved
and expense to the country saved if the
court made an order that that man's
employer should pay an amount of his
wages to the court, or in some way or
other if it was decided that a proportion
of the wage should be so paid. The man
would still be working and his children
supported instead of putting him out of
his work and sending him to jail as а.
punishment.
a
I think this principle has been used,
but not as a punishment when sailors go
to sea. The man is going to sea, and the
court assigns to his wife at home
certain proportion of his wages. The
employers pay that wage and get a good
receipt for it. I think something like
that could be done. But as to the justice
of it, what I really want to know is if
money could be saved to the country by
some change. I hope I will get a favourable answer to this inquiry.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am informed by my right hon. Friend the
Minister of Home Affairs that during
the year 1935,
1935, 1,174 persons were
imprisoned in default of payment of fines
and eight persons in default of wife and
child maintenance; there were no cases
of imprisonment for default of payment
of rates. As the terms of imprisonment
in these cases vary materially, it would
be impossible to state definitely what the
cost of the maintenance of these prisoners
amounted to, but at a very rough estimate it may be said that the cost was in
the neighbourhood of £1,400.
Further, I would like to remind the
hon. Senator that the courts in Northern
Ireland follow the principle laid down.
in the Criminal Justice Administration
Act, 1914, which requires time to be
allowed for payment of fines. Full use
is made of these powers, and it may be
said, therefore, that committal in default
of payment is only resorted to as a last
expedient, and it seldom operates except
in cases where defendants are able but
unwilling to pay. I think the hon.
Member has in mind recent legislation in
England in this connection, that is to
say, the Money Payments Justice Procedure Act, 1935. The general principle
underlying the provisions of that Act is
that the sending of the defaulter to
prison can only operate as a result of the
Housing Grants-
Committee
5 MARCH 1936
considered opinion of the justices. The
iaw in England, particularly in regard to
debt, is not the same as in Ireland, and,
as I have already stated, the courts here
are careful to avoid imprisonment where
that is possible. As to debt imprisonment can only operate where the debtors
are recalcitrant, that is to say, are able
to pay but refuse to do so.

Unemployment Assistance (Temporary
Provisions) (Extension) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is a
Financial Measure and contains nothing
that is new in principle to hon. Members. Its purpose is to prolong until not
later than the 31st day of this month
the powers accorded to the Government
by the Unemployment (Temporary Provisions) (No. 2) Act, to recoup to local
authorities the approximate cost to them
of continuing to provide Poor Relief in
consequence of the postponement of the
second appointed day under the Unemployment Assistance Act, 1934.
The amounts of grants made under
the present Bill are calculated on the
same basis as for the first six months of
the Financial Year save where it is provided that an increase shall be made in
the amount payable to the Down County
Council, in view of the
view of the exceptional
expenditure of the Guardians of the
urban and rural districts of Killyleagh
and Castlewellan in providing for the
special distress in these areas.
I beg to move, "That this Bill be now
read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Rent Restrictions Bill
Government Loans Bill

THE SENATE
Government Loans Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In March,
1921, that is, after the passing of the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920, but
before the establishment of this Parliament, the Local Government Board for
Ireland sanctioned the borrowing of a
sum of £3,700 by the Londonderry Port
Sanitary Authority for the building near
Moville in County Donegal of an Intercepting Hospital for infectious diseases.
cases landed from ships in Lough Foyle,
and the Commissioners of Public Works
in Ireland agreed to issue a loan for the
amount. £2,400 of the loan was
advanced by the Commissioners of Public
Works from the Imperial Local Loans
Fund to the Sanitary Authority prior to
the transfer of the Local Government
services to our Government, which, by
the Government of Ireland Act, was
made responsible for the collection of the
loan instalments payable. The balance
of the loan, amounting to £1,000, was
advanced by the Ministry of Finance on
11th September, 1922, and it is to the
outstanding principal and interest of
this advance that this Bill relates.
The hospital, which is situate in the
Free State, has never been used, but
the burden of the payments in respect of
it has fallen upon ratepayers in Northern Ireland, principally upon the ratepayers of the County Borough of Derry.
The Government feel that these payments, for which the ratepayers, for
reasons out of their control, receive no
return, is unjust, and the Bill, therefore,
provides for the writing off of the
amount of the principal still outstanding
of the loan made from the Government
Loans Fund, and for the extinction of
the amount of principal and interest outstanding. I beg to move.
Question proposed, "That the Bill be
now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I did not quite follow.
Who is responsible for the upkeep of the
hospital which is not used? Did the
noble Viscount say the taxpayers or the
ratepayers? Is it a general or particular
liability so to speak?
Mr. GLENDINNING: Just out of
curiosity I would like to know what is.
to become of the fabric of this building
Of
in the future. Is there any possibility
of it being sold, or is it of any use?
course I know it is away down on the
the
shores of Lough Foyle, and, as
Leader of the House says, it has never
been used.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member had better wait until the Leader
of the House answers Senator Leslie's
question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : The
balance of the loan which I have mentioned is levied on the ratepayers-
Mr. LESLIE: On rates.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The rates
of Derry and the surrounding districts.
With regard to the question put by the
hon. Senator who spoke last, I do not
know if there have been any infectious
cases received in this hospital, but in any
case the hospital is outside the jurisdiction of this Parliament and, therefore,
I need not go into details.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Rent and Mortgage Interest
(Restrictions) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
is intended to prolong the period of protection afforded to tenants under the
Rent and Mortgage Interest (Restrictions) Acts.
The present Acts will, unless renewed,
expire on the 1st May of this year, and
it is proposed to continue them for a
further four years, that is, up to the
1st May, 1940.
The Acts apply to all houses of £8
valuation and under, and also to a
number of houses between £8 and £26
valuation. A certain number of houses
in the latter class came out of control
between the years 1925 and 1932, and
it is not proposed to touch these as it
would clearly be unjust, to re-impose
restrictions on houses which may have
been bought in the belief that the purchaser would be free to fix a rent in
accordance with the ordinary
the ordinary laws of
supply and demand.
Rent Restrictions Bill-
Second Reading
5 MARCH 1936
The necessity for the Bill arises from
the fact that the housing shortage has
not yet been completely met. As I
informed the House on Tuesday, when
moving the Second Reading of the Housing (Grants) Bill, the Government
expect, as a result of their housing legislation, that this shortage will be
remedied at not too remote a date, and
the need for control of rents should then
no longer exist, but in the meantime, it
will be necessary to give tenants some
protection against possible exploitation.
That the Bill be now
I beg to move,
read a Second time."
""
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot but think
that we are taking this Bill with somewhat undue haste. It has only been in
our hands for a very few days. I admit
it is a very short Bill, and its principal
object, for all practical purposes, is to
extend the operation of the last of the
Rent Restriction Acts, that is to say the
one which we passed in 1932. That is
four years ago, and I cannot help thinking that it may be possible that some of
us have forgotten a good many of the
provisions contained in that Act, and
have not, perhaps, remembered everything
that was said during the discussions
which took place before that Act was in
fact enacted.
I must say personally that I am very
disappointed that it should be necessary
to bring forward a Bill of this sort again.
I had hoped that the necessity for this
control would have disappeared when the
time came under the last Act for the
expiration of control. I had hoped that
that would have happened, and I am
very sorry to hear that it is not to be
the case.
It seems to me that before
the House gives its consent to this Bill-
I do not necessarily mean this afternoon,
but before we finally pass the Bill-the
House ought to receive something in the
way of, I was going to say an account of
stewardship, but that is scarcely the
correct word to apply, because the Government is not exactly in the position of
steward in this matter, but we ought to
receive something more in the way of a
general report as to what has happened
between 1932 and the present day, what
has been the effect of these various provisions, some of them of a very drastic
character, which were inserted in the
Act of 1932, and also have some means of
forming an opinion as to whether the
fears expressed in 1932, and on previous
occasions, that the effect of these Acts
would be to restrict the building of
houses have been realised or not. We
ought to be told whether there is any
ground for the view that the existence
of these Acts has in fact restricted
private building.
Again, I think we ought to be told
whether there is any evidence to support
the claims, which are made now and
again, that the effect of these restrictions
is to impose
impose distinct hardship in
individual cases.
I do not mean so much
hardship on the landlord, because Parliament apparently has accepted the theory
that a landlord cannot possibly either
have any rights or any sense of right. I
was thinking rather of the hardship on
what I would call the purchaser and
would-be occupier. And again, there is
the question of taking over a tenancy for
the purposes of a relative of the owner
or one of his employees. There are
restrictions in those matters, and one
would like to know whether there have
been any complaints, which have been
proved to have been well-founded, that
those restrictions have caused hardships.
I do not think it is necessary that anybody should undertake the responsibility
of moving the rejection of this particular
Measure. I do not think that is a course
that would appeal to any of us. It has
been passed by a Chamber of first
instance, presumably more directly in
touch with their constituents and, perhaps, more directly liable to feel the dissatisfaction of those constituents at some
later date. But I do think we ought to
consider very carefully whether we
should not limit the period of extension.
The suggestion in the Bill is that these
limitations shall continue until May,
1940, that is four years. I think, speaking from memory, that is the same length
of extension as was given on the former
occasion. That former occasion-1932was, one may say, the first opportunity
Parliament had of expressing its indignation and its general sense of the provocation which was naturally given by the
publication of the Report dealing with
housing conditions The provocation to
deal drastically with the matter in 1932
was, as I say, very great, and one would
111 Rent Restrictions Bill

THE SENATE
Adjournment
[Mr. Leslie]
like to know whether the steps we took
then have had any effect by now.
It was never intended that these
restrictions should continue for ever.
They were primarily intended, I think I
may say, to deal with an immediately
post-war problem, a problem which was,
perhaps, almost entirely confined to Belfast, although the effect of these restrictions naturally radiated through the
country. I think we ought to know, as
I have endeavoured to show, a great deal
more about the matter. Particularly do
I want to know whether there was any
chance that the uncertainty, and so on,
introduced by these Acts has had a detrimental effect on building of the class
which we all desire should continue. I
do not propose, as I say, to oppose the
Second Reading to-day, but I do propose
to bring forward in Committee a suggestion on the lines I have indicated, and
which, I think, will enable us to discuss
the whole question. As a matter of fact
this is practically a one-Clause Bill,
that the debate in Committee will be
practically the Second Reading over
again.
so
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
certainly consider that it is necessary
that houses of £8 valuation should continue to be controlled, but no doubt there
are hardships in the cases of houses of
higher valuations. Only the other day
my attention was drawn to the case of a
working man who purchased a house
some few years ago, and was unable to
obtain possession of that house owing to
the fact that the property was controlled.
I would also like if the noble Lord would
give us some information on another
matter about which questions are asked
very frequently. Does the limit of £26
valuation apply to the new valuation or
to the old valuation? There are quite a
number of people whose houses were
under £26 valuation and were controlled,
but since the revaluation there have been
considerable changes. Not so much for
my own information, but for the information of a good number of people outside, I would very much like if the noble
Lord could possibly tell us if this £26
is to be taken under the new valuation
or under the old valuation, or if he could
give us some assurance that revaluation
would not affect these houses.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think it
would be better if I gave most of the
information asked for on the Committee
stage, as I am probably not at the
moment as conversant with the facts as
I might be. I would like to say, however, that this rent restriction does not
apply to new houses and has no effect on
building. On the whole I think it would
be better if I gave the information asked
for at a later date.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Motion for Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
that this House do now adjourn
until this day week, I would like to find
out from hon. Members whether in their
opinion it would be possible to get
the Unemployment (Agreement) Bill
through in one day, that is to say this
day week. The Bill merely revises the
Agreement with the Imperial Government with regard to Unemployment
Insurance, and it would be advantageous
to get it through as soon as we can. If
anybody has any objections to that
course I would like to hear them.
Mr. LESLIE: If I appreciate the
position correctly, this is obviously a Bill
which raises the contribution from
United Kingdom resources as a whole to
a particular part of the United Kingdom
which has some little difficulty in meeting what I might describe as expenses
common to the whole United Kingdom.
It is very difficult to find the right word.
It will raise the appropriate share to
something more approaching its proper
amount than it has been in the past. I
think it amounts to some £500,000 or
thereabouts.
Viscount
assent.
CHARLEMONT indicated
Mr. LESLIE: I am inclined to think
that we should all be glad to pass that in
one day.

Unemployment Bill
11 MARCH 1936
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 11th March, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Business of the House.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That
Standing Order No. XXI be suspended
for this day's sitting for the purpose of
taking all stages of any Bill received from
the Commons dealing with the Unemployment Agreement."
Mr. LESLIE: I think the Parliamentary Secretary meant to move the
suspension of Standing Order No. XXV.
I think there is a mistake on the Order
Paper. The Order Paper has made it
Standing Order No. XXI, and the
Motion moved was to suspend that
Standing Order, but the Standing Order
we are about to suspend is, I think,
No. XXV.
Mr. LONG: That is so. I beg to
amend my Motion accordingly.
On Question Motion, as amended,
agreed to.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the
Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill
and the Unemployment (Agreement)
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Ordered: That the Bill be read a
Second time on Tuesday next.
Unemployment (Agreement) Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."—(Mr. Long).
Mr. LONG: I think that hon. Members will agree that it would be difficult
to take exception to this Bill, of which
the purpose is to ratify an agreement
made between the British Treasury and
the Ministry of Finance as to the method
whereby the British and Northern
Ireland Exchequer expenditure on unemployment may be equalised. This agreement replaces the Agreement made with
the same object in 1926, but which has
become ineffective mainly because of the
introduction of the transitional benefit
system, which caused an enormous
increase in the expenditure of the British
Exchequer on unemployment.
As hon. Members are aware, under the
1926 Agreement a contribution was payable to Northern Ireland by Great
Britain amounting to three-quarters of
the excess of the Northern Ireland actual
expenditure on unemployment over her
total population proportion, namely 2.8
per cent., of British expenditure. The
increase in British expenditure to which
I have referred, naturally raised the
amount which Northern Ireland had to
bear herself before receiving any contribution from Great Britain,
Britain, and
resulted in Northern Ireland receiving
no Exchequer contribution in 1932, 1933,
and 1934, in spite of the fact that the
percentages of the insured persons unemployed in Northern Ireland in those years
were between 5 and 7 per cent. higher
than in Great Britain. Clearly, parity
of burden was not being maintained.
the
Under tbe new Agreement
Exchequer burden in the two areas will
be equalised on the basis of the ratio of
insured populations instead of that of
total populations, as hitherto, that is,
2.1 per cent. approximately in place of
2.8 per cent., is the proportion of British
expenditure to be borne by Northern
Ireland before qualifying for contribution from Great Britain.
The principle of maintaining parity
between the two Funds is not, as has
been suggested in some quarters, the product of Northern Ireland acumen and
British charity. The Agreement works
both ways; should unemployment in
Northern Ireland fall below the level in
Great Britain then we shall become liable
to contribute towards the cost of unemployment there. I would point out
further, that it is clearly to the advantage of both parties to the Agreement
Unemployment Bill-
First Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
that unemployment benefits should be the
same in the two areas, as the economic
disequilibrium which would otherwise
ensue would be more costly than the
maintenance in respect of this important
service of the principle that there shall
be no difference in the social services
available to citizens residing in different
parts of the United Kingdom.
Before sitting down I should like to
pay tribute to the way in which my
colleagues, the Minister of Finance and
the Minister of Labour, have served in
this matter, as in so many others, the
interests of the Province, and also to the
Ministers in
statemanship of those
London who have worked with them to
make this important Agreement. I beg
to move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time."
Mr. LESLIE : My first words will
be very much the same as the last words
of the Parliamentary Secretary who has
just sat down, for they must consist of
the most hearty congratulations both to
the Minister of Finance and the Minister
of Labour for the happy result which has
crowned their persistent and consistent
labours. I shall be saying something
more about the Minister of Labour in a
few moments' time. To come to the Bill
itself, I would suggest to the House that
the new Agreement carries out the spirit
of the Colwyn award. I think the spirit
of the Colwyn award did not, perhaps,
entirely preside over the proceedings in
connection with the earlier Agreements
of 1926 and 1929, but it has now come
back to its own, and the result is that
we shall have a system which is equitable
as between one part of the United Kingdom and another.
we
The main point is one to which
drawn.
attention has already been
That is,
substitute as
to
are
the
the basis of
calculation
our
number of the insured populations
and not the number of the populations
themselves. Of course, the new arrangement will result in the transfer of larger
from the British Exchequer, or
rather the Exchequer of the United
Kingdom, to the Exchequer of Northern
Ireland. I entirely agree with what has
been said here this afternoon and in
another place to the effect that such a
payment is not in any way comparable
sums
to such a payment as is normally called
a subsidy or subvention. It is nothing
of the sort, for partners and we are
partners with the other parts of the
United Kingdom in this matter-do not
subsidise each other. If you want to
compare the situation with anything, the
only thing I can think of is to remind
the House of the complaints that are
sometimes made across the water that districts surrounding
surrounding Portsmouth and
Aldershot get considerably greater benefits from the votes of the Navy and Army
than do other parts of the country.
Those two places do reap a great deal
more advantage than any other district
which can be found in Dumfries or Rutland or Armagh, but nobody ever
suggests that there is anything unfair,
or that those two particular districts are
subsidised at the expense of the rest of
the United Kingdom.
Besides being a step towards more complete equity, the new Agreement will, I
think, lay to rest, and I hope finally lay
to rest a bogey which has for far too
long overshadowed the preparation of our
Budgets, and it has threatened the due
balancing of those Budgets, and has
really been a menace to the financial
stability of the whole Province. This
bogey was to a great extent responsible
for a passage in the report of a Finance
Committee issued some four years ago.
The Committee was referring to the
immense expenditure cast upon the State
at that time, and the passage to which I
am alluding runs as follows:-
We favour the alternative of drastic
retrenchment, and we think that, so
far as the State is concerned, that path
should be pursued even if it leads to
the adoption in respect of unemployment benefit and other social services
of a level below that which is accepted
as the standard in Great Britain.
If it had ever been necessary to accept
that standard we should have regretted
it most profoundly, and more than once
I have felt bound to say, when the
Minister of Labour was proposing this or
that extension of the system in this
House, that I was very doubtful whether
the financial resources of the Province
enabled us to comply. I do not expect I
shall ever have to say that again so far
as industrial insurance is concerned, and
that alone is before us this afternoon.
117 Unemployment Bill-

11 MARCH 1936
First Reading
In mentioning that I cannot refrain from
saying that the Minister of Labour,
when challenged on those lines, while
admitting the financial difficulty, as he
was bound to do with his knowledge of
the financial circumstances of the Province, invariably expressed his opinion.
that, if it should ever come that continuance on the British lines was out of
the question, the deficit should be made.
good by the United Kingdom as a whole,
and that, I suggest, is what is about to
be done now.
sure
answers
I could wish I had had rather more
time to study the Bill before us.
As a
matter of fact, I did not receive it until
10 o'clock this morning, and I did not
even then get the White Paper to which
reference was made so repeatedly across
the water. I did not get that until a
few moments ago, and hence it will be
that when this Bill reaches the Committee
Stage in a few minutes I shall have to
put one or two questions to which I am
will be forthcoming,
answers which I could have found myself
if I had had more time at my disposal.
I may say at once, in order to give as
much time as possible, that the questions
will relate to the third sub-clause of
Clause 3, to paragraph (a) of sub-clause
(1) of Clause 6, to the proviso to Clause
6, to paragraph (b) of the first paragraph in the Schedule, and to the proviso
to the fourth paragraph in that Schedule.
I do not imagine we shall find that we
are involved in any great difficulty.
There is a more important point, perhaps, which has only just come to my
notice. That is, that as an appendix to
the appendix is a printed letter from our
Minister of Finance to the Chancellor of
the Exchequer. In that letter agreement is expressed on our behalf as
follows:
I understand and agree that if the
liability of the United Kingdom
Exchequer under the Agreement should
in any
one year exceed the sum of
£1,000,000, you will be entitled, if you
so desire, to re-open on its merits the
question...
My recollection is that there was a
similar understanding in relation to the
Agreements of 1926 and 1929, but I have
not been able to check my memory,
am I sure whether such an Agreement
was embodied in the resultant legislation
nor
or not. Further, there is agreement
expressed in the same letter that if new
legislation is introduced materially
modifying the position, the terms of the
Agreement can, if necessary, be reconsidered. There is nothing about either
of those two matters that I can find in
the Bill before us, and I think it is rather
unfortunate that the Agreement as
extended and explained by that letter
should not be attached to the Bill so that
when it becomes an Act the whole of the
matter may be on the Statute Book
available for ready reference. I do not
imagine that anything can be done now,
and it may be explained that those two
points are covered by some passage in the
Bill which has hitherto escaped my
observation.
In spite of all those remarks I most
cordially support this Bill, and I recognise with gratification, and I may add
with gratitude, the imperial spirit which
has been shown by the Parliament of the
United Kingdom and the Treasury
authorities at Whitehall in their method
of dealing with this subject which to us
was, and still is, an all-important matter.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
should like to add my congratulations to
those of the hon. and learned Member
who has just spoken to the Minister of
Finance and the Minister of Labour in
negotiating so favourable a settlement
of our unemployment contribution difficulty. I feel that everyone in this
Province is immensely grateful to the
British Government for the way in which
they have met our
Although I
case.
believe we are entitled to what we have
got in this imperfect world you cannot
always expect to get all you are entitled
to-I cannot see why the two Funds
should not have been consolidated and
made into one Fund, and that would
have prevented any possibility of any
questions arising in the future such as
have been provided for in the Bill. At
the same time it would have enabled
Ulster to go on in the certainty that no
further question would ever arise in the
future. A large part of this Bill would
then have been absolutely unnecessary.
The British Government have treated
this matter from a statesmanlike point of
view, because had Ulster become unable
to provide adequate unemployment
insurance, then there would probably
have been an efflux of workers to places
Unemployment Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
such as Glasgow and Liverpool, where
better unemployment insurance could be
obtained. That might have seriously
increased English unemployment at some
future date, so that in coming to this
agreement I believe the British Government have not only been generous but
they have been far seeing.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said

In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]

3 refers to the limit of the amount of

income for administration
would be greatly swollen.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
Clause ordered to stand part of the

CLAUSES 4 (Current account and

Fund), and 5 (Chargeability of certain

CLAUSE 6 (Extinction of balance outstanding on suspense account of the Unemployment Fund).
Mr. LESLIE: The question I have to
ask here relates to paragraph (a), that
sum of £40,000. If I remember rightly,
the arrangement at the Agreement existing, now about to be determined, was for
an annual payment of £100,000 in order
to reduce the liability, and although I
have no doubt the Minister of Finance
would be very glad to be relieved of
having to find £65,000 year by year, I
should like to be assured that the reduction is not to be held in any way to
debar us, if we find it convenient to do
so, to reduce our capital liability, which
at the moment appears to stand at
£2,400,000, by such larger amount as we
may find it convenient to provide. I
conclude that the intention is not
necessarily to limit us to this £40,000;
it is only to introduce it as the irreducible
minimum that is to be provided year by
year.
Mr. LONG: The view of the hon. and
learned Member is correct to the
£40,000.
as
Mr. LESLIE: And we can, if we like,
increase it?
Mr. LONG: Yes.
CLAUSE 7 (Provisions as to advances
to current account of the Unemployment
Fund).
Mr. LESLIE: I was going to ask
what Section 60 of the United Kingdom
Act is about. From the Interpretation
Clause I see that the United Kingdom
Act is a Consolidation Act of the present
year, but I do not think I have seen a
copy of it up to the present. It would
seem to have some bearing on the
financial position, and we might perhaps
be told something about it. Perhaps this
had better stand over, because I am
afraid I misled the Parliamentary Secretary when I was speaking on the Second
Reading. I referred to the proviso to
Clause 6, whereas it is the proviso to

Mr. LONG: I am informed that
£40,000 is the annual repayment of
suspense debt in cash.

Mr. LESLIE: I do not think we need

SCHEDULE.
Mr. LESLIE: On the Schedule I have
two questions, and I think they hang
pretty much together. The short point
I was going to put was, what has the
total number of persons insured under
the Acts relating to Widows', Orphans',
and Old Age Contributory Pensions to do
with the total number of persons insured
under the
Unemployment Insurance
Acts. The second question relates to the
proviso on page 8, about line 26. It
refers to a certificate to be given by the
Government actuary, and on that certificate apparently is to be based the contribution payable. I should like to be
told a little more about that, or, if the
answer is to be found in the White
Paper, as may very well be the case, I
should like the Parliamentary Secretary
to tell us. It is extremely difficult to
follow these things properly when the
time at one's disposal is so very short.
Of course, I do not blame anybody for
that particularly, because, after all, the
Post Office is a reserved service, and the
time taken in getting from one place to
another, whether on foot or by means of
the post, is a very variable quantity. It
is not always easy, I know, to get papers.
from Stormont to remote parts of the
Province as quickly as desired, but if we
can be referred to a paragraph in the
White Paper explaining this matter I
think we shall be quite satisfied.
Mr. LONG: I am informed that the
Widows' and Orphans' Contributory
Pensions population is used for unemployment assistance.
Mr. LESLIE: Why?

Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 12th March, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented.
Ministry of Finance:
Estimates for Services under the Government of Northern Ireland, 1936-37.
Presented (by Command) and ordered.
to lie on the Table.
Question to Minister.
MAGGOTS IN SHEEP.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether he could inform the House of the
probable loss to sheep-owners caused by
maggots; whether on some sheep walks in
Scotland the trapping of flies has been
found useful, and, if so, whether the
Government will consider the advisability
of trying this method of dealing with the
trouble.
I put this question because I understand that maggots cause sheepowners a
great deal of expense. I have been talking to sheepowners in Scotland and learned
that they think maggots can be eradicated
pretty well. I know there are traps
made in Scotland and sold there, but
they are too expensive, and I do not know
that they would be a success.
I understand if you hang some dead
carcase up these bluebottles attach themselves to it and lay on it.
Their eggs
drop to the ground and hatch and grow
into more bluebottles. What was suggested to me was that there should be a
box of poison or perhaps hot lime, and
when the bluebottle maggot is hatched it
crawls down and instead of getting to the
ground it lands into this. They say in
some of the places in Scotland that they
have reduced the danger a great deal. I
hope that the Minister of Agriculture will
consider this, and if he finds there is anything in the statement which I have heard
and which I believe, he would be doing
something to help the sheepowners.
I
really ask the question to draw attention to
the matter and to see if the Ministry of
Agriculture can recommend anything to
reduce this trouble.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In answer
to the first part of the question I may say
it is not practicable to indicate in figures
the loss caused to sheepowners by maggots.
Such losses depend upon seasonal conditions and would be much greater in a
particularly dry season. I am informed
that there has been talk in certain parts
of Scotland regarding the trapping of
flies which cause the maggot. Developments in this connection have, however,
been of a purely private nature and not
on any extensive scale such as would
warrant the drawing of definite conclusions as to the efficacy of this method. On
such evidence as is available, my right
hon. Friend the Minister of Agriculture
is satisfied that it would be inadvisable
to suggest a general adoption of trapping
methods in this country although here as
in Scotland it is, of course, quite open for
private individuals to do so.
The experience of the Ministry of Agriculture is that the maggot can best be
prevented by the well-tried method of
dipping sheep in a standard dip. Where
the maggot has gained a foothold on an
animal, dressing with a suitable preparation is recommended. These are old and
recognised methods which every good
flock master practises
practises and in the
Ministry's opinion Northern Ireland
sheepowners would be well advised to fight
the maggot along these lines.
Pier and Harbour Order (Larne
Harbour) Confirmation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."-(Viscount Charlemont).
Ordered, That the Bill be committed
to a Joint Unopposed Committee.
Housing (Grants) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
12 MARCH 1936
Housing Bill

Unemployment Bill

Resolved, "That this Bill do now
pass."(Viscount Charlemont.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.
Mr. McMAHON: In connection with
this Bill, which is now to receive its
Third Reading, I think the Government
might at this late stage have done something to get more houses. At one time
we were told in another place that when
we got a certain number of houses built
the rents of those houses would be regulated according to the number of houses
built, and that would solve the problem.
But now we find that the Government
is about to discontinue the subsidy in six
months' time-the clearing of old houses
is to my mind farcical, because no one
will pull down old houses to build new
ones. They are about, as I say, to discontinue the subsidy.
I do not know whether this House is
aware of it, but for the past twelve
months there has been a rent strike
going on in the Falls district. That rent
strike is assuming very serious proportions
at the present time. I understand that
the tenants in the houses there have
been mulcted to a great extent by having
to pay civil bills, and I understand now
that the latest is that there will be
evictions about Friday or some time early
next week. Now, it is inborn in every
Irishman to hate evictions. I know perfectly well if any attempt at eviction is
made on the Falls Road a breach of the
peace will probably take place. Nine or
ten big landlords have settled with their
tenants, and settled in a satisfactory
manner. I think there are about three
people outstanding. One happens to be
a constable of the Royal Ulster Constabulary who owns some houses there. He is
adamant and will not in any circumstances make any reduction.
You would naturally think that a man
who is a peace officer would have some
respect for law and order, and would not
intimidate or enrage the people of that
particular district. We moved in the
matter this morning with a Member of
this House, and I hope something will
be done, but I think the attention of this
House should be drawn to the fact that
a member of the Ulster Constabulary, a
peace officer, should have some respect for
law and order. I know perfectly well if
any attempt is made at eviction either at
the end of this week or at the beginning
of next week there will be trouble in that
district, and that is a thing we would
all deplore.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have to
report to the House, That his Grace the
Governor of Northern Ireland, in the
name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his Assent
to the following Bill agreed upon by both
Houses:
Unemployment (Agreement).
Unemployment Assistance (Temporary
Provisions) (Extension) Bill.

and 26 Geo. 5, c. 19) and 2 (Short title
