Local Government (Finance) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Hon Members will recollect that in 1929 the first
Derating Bill was introduced into this
House. That Bill set up a completely
new principle in local government administration and finance, a principle the
carrying into effect of which has been of
immense benefit to farmers and industrialists in this Province, and through
them to the whole community.
The administrative procedure laid down.
by the Act of 1929 was of necessity experimental, and I am glad to say that
on the whole it has worked very satisfactorily for the past six years. Naturally,
however, certain difficulties have arisen,
and in some respects the machinery has
proved unnecessarily cumbersome. The
amending Bill now before the House is
intended to remedy defects which have
been found in the existing system and
to remove certain anomalies which have
arisen. As my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Home Affairs, has said in
another place, it is not the object of
the present Bill to make any saving at
the expense of the ratepayers. In 1929
the Government promised to make grants
towards meeting the losses incurred by
local authorities by reason of the provisions of that Act, and the Government
is reiterating that promise in the present
Bill.
The first of the main provisions of the
Bill meets a situation which has arisen
As hon.
as a result of revaluation.
Members are aware, the rateable value
of underated hereditaments, that is to
say, most buildings, has been very substantially increased, whereas the valuation of derated hereditaments, mainly
agricultural land, has not increased to
anything like the same extent. The consequence of this is, of course, that the
proportion of rates which the Government
would have to pay when the new valuations become operative, would be very
much less than at present. The Government, believing that this would not be
equitable, sought in collaboration with
the local authorities a formula which
would be fair to both ratepayers and
taxpayers.
It can be readily understood that it
was a difficult matter to arrive at a fair
adjustment, but as hon. Members will
see from Clause 5 of the Bill, it is the
intention of the Government to hand back
to local authorities eighty per cent. of
the savings which they would otherwise.
make by reason of revaluation. This
proportion was arrived at after a full
discussion of the position with the local
authorities, and was agreed to by them.
to be a very fair settlement of the
problem. This 80 per cent. will be
handed back to the rural authorities who,
in their turn, will hand on an agreed
proportion to the urban authorities.
The second main provision of the Bill
is concerned with the payment of the
derating deficiency contributions. These
contributions are paid in two separate
and distinct ways. One method is used
with rural authorities and the other with
urban authorities. It might be well at
this stage to point out what should be
an obvious fact, but is apparently frequently overlooked, namely, that derating is primarily a matter for rural
authorities, and that it concerns urban
authorities only in a very minor degree.
I am informed in this connection that
fully nine-tenths of the derating grants
are payable to rural authorities.
In the case of the rural authorities
the method which was adopted in 1929,
and which is being continued in the
present Bill, is to pay the deficiency contribution by means of standard grants and
supplemental grants. The standard
grant is based on what I may term the
normal expenditure of a local authority,
and the supplemental grant covers ex-
129 Local Government Bill-

12 MARCH 1936
Second Reading
penditure of a more abnormal character.
Under the 1929 Act the standard grant
was subject to revision every five years.
The only change made in the present
Measure is that the standard grant is to
be fixed at a permanent figure, but there
is this very important proviso, that the
grant will not be subject to any reduction.
if it is not fully spent by the local
authority concerned. The effect of this
is that if a local authority does not spend
up to its maximum standard grant in
one year the amount thus saved will be
available to meet expenditure in a
subsequent year.
The supplemental grant continues on
exactly the same lines as before, that is
to say, it is limited to the amount of a
6d rate on the full net annual value of
the county. As the net annual valuation has very materially increased as a
result of revaluation it will be realised
that this is a very generous limit of
expenditure.
our
I should like to say a word with
regard to the grants to education
authorities in which I am particularly
interested. Under the 1929 Act no limit
was made for the payment of supplemental grants, as at that time
educational services were in a state of
transition. The expenditure of education
committees has now become somewhat
more settled, and the Government has
decided that it is possible and desirable
to set a limit to the supplemental grants
which may be paid, and accordingly the
limit has been fixed at the amount produced by a rate of 3d in the £ on the full
net annual valuation of the area. I am
sure that every education committee will
be satisfied with this very generous limit.
The payment of grants to urban
authorities under the 1929 Act was made
on a different basis. In each case the
local authority was recouped the actual
loss of revenue sustained by it. This
method is retained in the present Bill,
the only change being the institution of
a maximum limit for such payments.
Certain criticisms have been advanced
against this alleged curtailment of Government contributions, but hon. Senators
will observe that the limit fixed is the
greatest amount which has been paid to
any local authority in respect of any of
the years during which derating has been
in operation, and I think they will agree
that the Government has not in any way
tried to save at the expense of the local
authorities.
Before sitting down I feel that I
should direct the attention of hon.
Members to Clause 14 of the Bill, which
provides that the expenses of lighting
in a rural area shall be charged on the
whole rural district instead of on the
particular area affected. Although the
Government will lose money by this revision, the resulting saving of time and
labour to local authorities and to Government officials will more than compensate for any financial loss in the
matter. I beg to move, "That this Bill
be now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE : I think that the Bill
which the noble Viscount has introduced
this afternoon might fairly be described
as an admirable Bill. It is, as it seems
to me, a Bill designed on broad lines and
based throughout on very generous treatment at the expense of the State of the
local ratepayer. The expense of the
State means, of course in this connection,
the expense of the taxpayer, and the
resultant beneficiaries, of course, are the
ratepayers. Indeed, when I first read
the Bill and began to consider it I was
driven to wondering whether the Bill permits whether the old Parliamentary
convention that it is the duty of
Parliament to protect the taxes
the expense of the rates had been
fully borne in mind-I was doubtful
whether the Bill permitted that old
maxim to retain its pristine dominance. Candidly, I do not think that the
Bill does altogether regard that ancient
and honourable maxim, but I think it has
been right to disregard it in this case because, to put it shortly, the proportion
of revenue raised in Northern Ireland by
indirect taxation as compared with direct
taxation is abnormally high. Wealth, as
such, in Northern Ireland as compared
with Great Britain gives a far lower proportionate yield than does consumption.
at
I do not know how the ultimate financial result of the new proposals will compare with the £50,000 suggested by the
Financial Relations Committee as the possible reduction in the cost of derating to
the State. I do not know how the ultimate figure will work out. Revaluation,
of course, rather exceeded the bounds of
our estimates, but after the lavish manner
in which the Government is proposing to
с
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
distribute the profits, so to speak, of revaluation, it seems to me to leave very
little of that £50,000 available as a saving
towards the Budget. I do not know the
figures, and possibly the Government does
not know them either, because there
may not have been time to ascertain them, and, therefore, I
cannot say what the effect may
be, but I do know that there is no indication whatever in the Bill of any attempt to transfer from the shoulders of
the State to the shoulders of the local
authority any portion of the State burden.
For reasons which some Members of the
House may appreciate, I personally
welcome that decision, and, indeed, I
think a great many other people will
welcome it also for the reason which I gave
before, and that is, indirect taxation as
compared with direct taxation.
To my mind, the Bill gives every
promise of founding a lasting settlement,
and if that be so it must be due to the
various qualities which the Bill possesses
and exhibits. The Bill demonstrates, as
it seems to me, the fruits of the conciliatory policy adopted by the Minister
of Home Affairs in particular, and by
the Government in general as regards local
authorities, and the Bill also exhibits
special virtues of its own. The effect of
the policy of conciliation is shown in the
almost universal acceptance of the proposals in the Bill. The City of Londonderry and all the urban district councils
and county councils are acquiescent as
regards these proposals. There has been
but one objection. I will deal with that
objection before I sit down, and, if the
House will bear with me, I propose to deal
with that objection at some little length,
possibly at greater length than the objection itself deserves. But the objection has
been couched in such terms that I think
it desirable that an answer should be
given and that comment should be made
upon it in this House. I am almost inclined to think-again on account of the
terms of the objection-that an answer
should be given from the back benches
rather than by a Member of the Government.
Passing from that
that to
to the special
virtues I observe in the Bill, first of all
I should like to mention equity. There
again the noble Viscount drew attention
to the fact, which will be seen, that there
is no violent disturbance-one might say
really no disturbance at all-of the
situation which has developed during the
experimental period. And still speaking of equity, from the consideration which
is shown to the urban district councils
in the schedule, hon. Members will be
aware that of the revaluation contribution to be made to the counties certain
fixed proportions are to be paid over to
the urban district councils. The amounts
involved are not large, but it does show
a very distinct sense of equity, because
there are charges on which the urban
district councils have, of course, to contribute to the counties.
And again there is discernible in the
Bill a provision for elasticity, and without elasticity any Bill, I think, dealing
with local government has no chance of
success. Let me refer the House for a
moment to the provisions as regards the
supplemental grants adumbrated in subclause (5), paragraph (a), of Clause 4 of
the Bill, and let me digress to express
appreciation of the alteration made in
the Bill in another place, that is, allowing sixpence to stand instead of fivepence
as at first proposed. The reference to
that sixpence will be found in the first
sub-division of that paragraph of Clause
4, which is rather a long Clause. It is
Clause 4, sub-clause (5), paragraph (a).
There again there seems to me to be considerable liberality allowed both as
regards interpretation and administration.
ment
The House can refer to Clause 10,
which extends some earlier definitions, I
fancy, to the very great advantage of
the local authorities. I rather gathered
that the noble Viscount was thinking
of that when he said that his Departwas completely satisfied. Then
there is the possibility of allowing unexpended balances to accumulate. That
is, if my information is correct, an entirely new proposal. Then there is noninterference. The responsibilities of local
authorities are in no way diminished.
They must continue to manage their own
affairs economically. There are,
course, certain checks still maintained,
and these checks are necessary if liberty
is not to degenerate into licence, and if
the authorities are to continue to prefer
economy to extravagance. I may remind the House that some fears were
expressed, lest in the years immediately
preceding the expiration of the five-years'
period, local authorities might break out
of
133 Local Government Bill-

12 MARCH 1936
Second Reading

into unjustifiable extravagance in the
hope that they might get sufficiently
generous treatment to cover their misdeeds. Those fears were expressed, but
I am glad to say not realised-very much
to the contrary-as I shall say again in
a minute.
Then there is the question of convenience indicated in Clause 7 and subclause (7) of Clause 5 in the methods
of payments to local authorities. The
local authorities have complained again
and again that the arrangements at first
adopted introduced great confusion in
their budgets, and put them to a world
of unnecessary trouble. That will be
swept away. I said just now there is
no reason to accuse local authorities of
extravagance in the particular matter
I have alluded to. I think the Bill shows
quite plainly that the Government is
satisfied with the general success of its
experiment, that is to say, the experiment launched in 1929. That experiment has been a very expensive one
indeed. It has cost some £7,000,000.
Let me read what was said about it:-
This expenditure amounts to about
10 per cent. of the State's Budget-an
appreciable figure, indeed, but no
more remarkable than other items. It
is only one of a cumulative series of
inroads into the national purse, or
rather the taxpayers' pocket, and the
prominence that it has secured is
perhaps due to the fact that it is the
latest example of State spending on an
extensive scale in a new direction.
That was written in 1931, and I think
it is still quite true. But when the
Committee that prepared that report was
set up, there was not only the bogey I
referred to yesterday afternoon standing
in the background of the proceedings,
there was also the rather shadowy figure
of the revaluation which the committee
recommended. That shadowy figure of revaluation has proved to be a good fairy,
and it follows from that, that the expenditure incurred and still to be incurred is well within our means. I do
not want to keep dragging in the
Financial Relations Report, though it is
almost inevitable when dealing with
Measure of this kind. It is
an example of the character doubly
associated with Charles Dickens, and
King Charles's head is before us.
But if revaluation was the good fairy at
a
the back of the proceedings there was a
troublesome little gollywog that kept
interposing itself, and that was, of course,
the cost of derating. One tried to treat
him as a jack-in-the-box, but you could
not disregard him altogether. You
could not, as it was statute born, and
had to be tolerated. Indeed now under
the influence of the good fairy it does
not seriously matter. Whatever danger
derating threatened to State finance is
now safely at an end.
""
Also I am inclined to say, rather
courageously, that the Bill shows to my
mind a very remarkable trait of simplicity. I say that, notwithstanding the
use of such terms as "deficiency contribution,' "standard and supplemental
grant," "revaluation contribution," and
"unreduced standard." The reason why
I do not think these make against simplicity is that some of the expressions at
all events are not new, and whether new
or old they are all explained in their
proper places in the Bill.
But, however, admirable the Bill
may be, I think it is very necessary
that in one House or the other
the proposals of the Bill should be
examined. Moreover, certain inquiries
are inevitable, and it is not impossible
that there may be certain amendments.
I would deal with the question of amendment first. They are, of course, for the
Committee stage, and I anticipate that,
unless the objection alluded to is put
forward very violently during the Committee stage nothing violently controversial is likely to be introduced, and the
whole Committee stage, to my mind, is
more likely to be an interchange of
opinions and discussion than anything
else.
As regards amendments, I expect myself to lay two before the Committee.
One relates to the proviso to Clause 3,
and though it may be a scintilla of principle is involved it may resolve itself into
a matter of drafting. The other is a
more thorny subject. I shall ask the
Committee to consider the question of
criminal injuries and exemption from
rates. I was very doubtful whether you
would permit that to be discussed, having
regard to the title of the Bill. I think
Clause 12, freight transport rebates fund,
and Clause 14 are sufficient for allowing
discussion. I shall not develop the point
to-day, but I should like hon. Members
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
to consider it, so that they will be prepared to discuss it in Committee.
Now as to inquiries-they will not take
long. The first question I ask is, briefly,
why town commissioners are not given
It will be
anything in the schedule.
remembered that town commissioners are
mentioned in Clause 4, sub-clause (8),
and also appear in Section 12 of the Act
of 1929. I think I know the reason they
have been left out, that is, because they
are no longer an effective rating authority.
I think
They do appear in the old Act.
the Government might perhaps tell us
either when the noble Viscount replies
this afternoon or later why it is considered that there is no necessity to provide
specially for town commissioners.
Then again it is required under Clause
4-one keeps referring to Clause 4-Subclause (1), paragraph (a), that the
Minister of Home Affairs is to certify "an
annual standard grant being a fixed
amount to be certified by the Minister of
Home Affairs." I would be inclined to
wonder whether the Government would
think it worth while to develop that a little
further in order to prevent the possibility
of a question arising on the part of those
not fully conversant with the principles.
Could the Government make it clear that
that standard grant is going to be fixed,
and that is that, and is going to remain
until Parliament sees fit to alter it? Could
it also be explained more plainly that the
certificate is to be given as soon as may
be after this Bill has become an Act? It
is a matter on which I do not propose to
put an Amendment on the Paper myself.
I think if anything is to be done it
might be done from the Government
benches.
There remains Clause 12. That Clause
was introduced in another place. It is a
Clause relating to an alteration as regards
freight transport rebates. It did not
appear in the Bill when the Bill
itself first appeared elsewhere. That
Clause was added to the Bill there. It
was introduced without a word of explanation, and accepted without a word of
discussion. Now I am prepared to
believe indeed I do believe that the
freight transport arrangements which prevailed during the experimental stage were
not very satisfactory. I am prepared to
believe that they did not produce any
good result. I am also prepared to believe that the new arrangements made by
the Road Transport Board between the
railway and motor interest are such as
to put this freight rebate fund entirely off
the map.
But I do think at some stage
the House should be given some explanation of the reasons for introducing the
Clause and the probable effect of the new
Clause.
Now I said some few moments ago that
I proposed to deal before I sat down with
the one objection taken to Clause 3 of the
Bill. I am now about to fulfil my undertaking. I spoke of the general approval
which the Bill had received. But one
discordant note has been struck. It was
struck by the Finance Committee of the
Belfast Corporation, and that note does
not ring true. Not only does the note
jar, but it offends those finer susceptibilities which all of us desire to see cultivated
and developed in all who move in public
life, whether as legislators, administrators
or as critics. Those susceptibilities revolt,
as they should revolt, against anything
savouring of arrogance, misrepresentation
or injustice. To my mind, the complaint
made by the Finance Committee and, I
cannot but add, to some extent reiterated
in a leading article which appeared in one
of the Belfast newspapers on the second
of this month, offends in these respects. I
exclude in relation to the newspaper the
charge of arrogance.
Mr. McALLISTER: I wish to raise a
point of order. I understand the
question the hon. and learned Member
is referring to is at the present moment
being dealt with by a Court in England,
and that the issue has not yet been determined. Pending the determination of
that issue I respectfully submit that it
is not proper or in order for this House
to discuss the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: May I say a word before you give a ruling, Mr. Speaker?
I know to what the hon. Member refers,
but I am not going within forty miles
of that. I am not going to discuss that
matter at all. I am talking of the
general memorandum of the Belfast Corporation which does include some reference to that question, but that I am
leaving entirely outside. It has nothing
whatever to do with my argument.
'Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
and learned Member (Mr. Leslie) is
quite in order.
12 MARCH 1936
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
The deficiency contribution to be
paid under this Act to the Council of
a county borough, borough, or urban
district, the commissioners of a
town not being an urban district,
and the water commissioners, shall
be a sum equal to the deficiency
on account of rates leviable by such
council or commissioners in respect of
each local financial year:

Mr. LESLIE: I was referring to the of the Act of 1929. I cannot read it
article in the newspaper. I was going
all:
on to say that the Committee voiced
their complaint against Clause 3 in a
circular sent, I imagine, to all of us, and
of which I have a copy in my hand. The
arguments, somewhat specious arguments,
in the circular itself, are not very easy
to follow, for the figures necessary to
understanding are not given, and certain
irrelevancies are introduced. Those
irrelevancies do not assist us; they serve
merely to obscure the issue and perhaps
provoke ill-feeling. Those points, perhaps,
are somewhat unimportant, for it is the
Bill, not the circular, we are concerned
primarily to discuss, but I must read
some of the circular to the House in
order that what are perhaps its most
objectionable features may
may be made
manifest. I am going to read from
page 2 of the circular:
Under the present Bill it would
appear that urban authorities, other
than county boroughs, are interested
in certain other matters arising in the
Bill in connection with the effect of
revaluation on their relations with
rural authorities, which do not affect
Belfast, and a bargain would appear
to have been arrived at between such
urban authorities and the Government
satisfactory to such authorities under
the particular circumstances in which
they were placed. The consent, however, of the Belfast Corporation, who
have no interest in the other arrangements referred to, has not been
obtained to a variation of the agreed
enactment contained in the Act of
1929, and, apart altogether from the
question of the Minister's promise, no
argument has been put forward to
justify such a proposition on the merits.
The words I have quoted appear to me
to indicate that the Corporation expects
to share with this Parliament, the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, such
sovereignty as that Parliament possesses.
It also suggests that Section 12 of the
Act of 1929 was based on some agreement. Parliament is ignorant of any
such agreement. So far as we know
there was no agreement whatever by the
Government with anybody. Section 12
stood on its Own merits. I think
perhaps it would be convenient if I
refreshed the memory of hon. Members
a little as to what is said in Section 12
Provided that,
and so on. That is what that Clause was
about. But whatever may be the intention of the circular as regards those
matters to which I have particularly
alluded the matter of dual sovereignty
and alleged agreement, there is no
possibility of mistaking the meaning of
the penultimate paragraph of the
circular, and that penultimate paragraph says:-
For the foregoing reasons, the Committee hereby place on record their
considered opinion that the maximum
limit for derating grants fixed by the
Bill as introduced, as far as it affects
Belfast, is a departure from the
promise made by the Minister of
Finance when introducing the Bill of
1928, is not founded on any logical
reason, and is unfair to the underated
ratepayers of the city.
The paragraph definitely and deliberately charges our right hon. Friend
-I think I might add our revered right
hon. Friend-the Minister of Finance,
with a departure from a promise made
in 1928. There is no mistaking the
words: they are quite plain. In the
year 1928, it will be remembered, the
right hon. Gentleman introduced the
Rating and Valuation (Apportionment)
Bill as
a necessary preliminary to the
subsequent enacting Measure. Now, the
charge of breach of faith, if brought at
all, might just as well, or just as ill, have
been laid against the Minister of Home
Affairs, for that Minister has used
almost the same words as those to which
so much prominence has been given in
the circular, and on which the charge
directed against the Minister of Finance
is based. Any Minister, if attacked in
his individual capacity, can defend himself on the score of joint Cabinet responsibility. Here there is no need of that.
139 Local Government Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
The Ministers concerned, jointly and
severally, can point to the tacit acceptance
in both Houses of Clause 12 of the Act
of 1929, and to similar acceptance in
another place of Clause 3 of the Bill
before us.
There was also, as those who have
really looked into the matter will remember the plain warning given in Section 13
of the Act of 1929 that the payments to
the local authorities were to be dealt with
as respects any local financial year, within
any such period of five years as aforesaid the annual standard grant
to be certified by the Ministry of
Home Affairs, and being of an amount
fixed from time to time for periods of
five years, the first such period beginning
on the first day of April, nineteen
hundred and thirty. Those passages
occur in Section 13 of the Act of 1929,
and I say that they convey the plainest
possible warning that the first arrangement was entirely experimental, and
revision might be expected at the end of
the first period of five years. No doubt
that Section 13 was directed, and in fact
relates, to the rural councils. That is
undoubtedly so, but Belfast really must
not delude itself into thinking that
either the city of Belfast or any other
urban district is of particular importance
in connection with derating. Derating
was intended, as indeed the noble
Viscount reminded us again this afternoon, in the main to assist rural ratepayers, and urban authorities bulk anything but largely in the problem.
What the effect of the whole thing on
Belfast city is I do not know, but it is
a very small proportion. The derated
portion of Belfast forms a very small
proportion of the total rateable capacity,
and it was on that account more than
any other, and for general convenience,
that the urban districts received somewhat different treatment from the rural
areas during the years of experiment,
and that they will continue to do.
They will still be treated somewhat
differently. Apart from all that, Mr.
Pollock, if he chose, could unmask
another battery, and that battery consists of the context of the words reported
in column 3852 of Hansard of 26th
November, 1928, and reproduced in the
circular of the Finance Committee. The
Minister was speaking, not in introducing
the Bill, but in reply to the debate, and
he was referring to some observations of
one of two Members representing County
Antrim, which was undivided at that
time. What he said was:
One assurance he asked me to give
was that the valuations in County
Antrim-or in any of the counties for
that matter-should not necessarily be
raised by the operation of this
Measure. He suggested that valuations on general property over the
whole country are to remain stabilised
in regard to any particular Measure.
That is a thing no Parliament can
agree to, because another Parliament
can change it.
I do not think the Finance Committee
has succeeded in silencing that particular
battery, and even if it had I am afraid
the only result would be to bring the
Minister of Home Affairs' private battery
into operation, because he said the same
thing in introducing the Second Reading
of the 1929 Bill. As a matter of fact,
the Corporation has enjoyed for fully five
years that which the Finance Committee
of the Corporation now claims in perpetuity. The only deduction, I think,
that any reasonable person can draw
from the evidence before him is that the
Committee has failed to recognise not
only the comparative insignificance of
Belfast in this matter of derating, thinking, of course, of the finance of derating
as a whole, and also that the effect of
the first general revaluation has been to
introduce a new and over-riding factor.
That does not seem to me to have been
appreciated.
That
Again, the Committee does not appear
to have given full value to the future
position. That is to say that the only
alteration, so far as affects Belfast, which
is to be made by the Bill is to impose a
limit on the maximum to be paid.
maximum, in the face of the heavy increase due to revaluation, may be the
equivalent of the highest amount paid
during any year in the expiring period.
That is to say, in future, in spite of
revaluation, as much can be paid in the
form of that grant or contribution as has
been paid during the most productive of
the past five years.
of
a
I am sorry that such acute controversy should have arisen to disturb the calm consideration
matter of the greatest public interest and
importance. I resent the use which has
141 Local Government Bill-

12 MARCH 1936
Second Reading
been made of the debates on the introductory Bill of 1928; I regret the attack
made on the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Finance, in the article to
which I have referred, and particularly
do I regret that the word "disingenuous”
was allowed to creep into that article. I
speak as one not wholly without
experience in this Parliament and one
not completely ignorant of the financial
problems with which it deals, and I can
find no foundation for a most unprovoked attack, a most unfair attack, and
a most unsupportable attack. So far as
this Bill is concerned at all with the
promises of the past, I desire to say, and
to say emphatically, that so far from
affording any example of breach it
exemplifies most completely a redemption,
and the promise which is now about to be
redeemed is one made by Parliament
itself. I will read it to the House. It
will be my last quotation. It is Section
24 of the Valuation Acts Amendment
Act, Northern Ireland, 1932.
It is hereby declared that it is the
intention of this Act that as soon as
the effect of the first general revaluation upon the portion of rateable
property is ascertained provision shall
be made for a review of the capacity
of the local rates based upon such
valuation to bear the expenditure.
required by the law then in force to
be defrayed out of those rates.
That is the promise given by Parliament, and I say that that intention is
now redeemed-most fully and honestly
redeemed-in the Bill before us. And,
therefore, I very cordially support the
Bill. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I do not know
that I, a humble ratepayer, can express my
the Bill,
concurrence with
because I find that the average layman
and the average member of the rural
councils and county councils understands
very little of the figures on which this
agreement was come to, and so the matter
was entirely in the hands of the officials
of the various councils and the officials
of the Government. There is one thing
I do know, that under this agreement
the County Armagh grant is reduced.
£5,500 compared with last year, and that
as a consequence the ratepayers of
County Armagh are obliged to pay an
increase of 1s 8d in the pound on the
old valuation. I do not see where the
saving to the ratepayer comes in there.
The Government in these matters are
curtailing their grants to the county
councils, the rural councils and the urban
councils, which means that the ratepayer
has got to pay the increase all the time.
It is all very well to talk about these
things, but when one comes to consider
that under the Revaluation Bill a good
many ratepayers are obliged to pay next
year practically treble the rates that they
have been paying up to now it shows the
compliment and favour this Government
is granting the ratepayers as a whole.
Then the taxpayer's taxes under the new
Valuation Bill are also trebled, so that
it comes to this that the ratepayer has
got to pay through the nose in all the
agreements and he is getting no favours
and certainly no increased benefits. And
talking about derating, it is giving with
one hand and taking away with the
other. It is an extraordinary fact that
under the new Valuation Act a great
many farmers' dwellings have been increased in valuation, which means that
the farmer will still have to pay more
rates. Therefore I venture to suggest
that as far as the ratepayer and the taxpayer are concerned under this agreement
and under this Bill they will not get the
advantages to which Senator Leslie has
referred. I, as a ratepayer, protest
against the way the county councils and
rural councils have been treated.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before I
reply to the questions which have been
raised on the Second Reading I would
like to say a few words which I might
have added to the Second Reading speech
about Clause 12, but, unfortunately, I
had not got them in time. I think,
under the circumstances, the House
would like to have the explanation which
I might have given earlier. It is not
very long.
The effect of the Clause is, it will be
seen, simply to repeal all the enactments
which set up the Freight Rebates Fund.
This Fund was established in connection
with the derating of railways and harbours and the principle applying to the
derating of these undertakings has been
that they pay only one-fourth of their
rates to the local authority and the remaining three-fourths to the Freight
Rebates Fund. The Ministry of Finance
143 Local Government Bill

THE SENATE
Adjournment
[Viscount Charlemont]
then fixes certain rebates which are to
be allowed for the charges made in
respect of certain traffics, and the railways or docks are then recouped the cost
to them of these rebates out of the
Freight Rebates Fund. The underlying
motive of the provision was partly to
assist the productive industry, and partly
to assist the railways by diverting to
them certain traffic which would otherwise go by road.
Apart altogether from the fact that
any such artificial diversion of traffic
from road to rail is contrary to the spirit
of the new transport policy, the administrative expense of these rebates was SO
heavy that experience has proved that
they were of little benefit to the general
public. The benefit that the farmer
appeared to gain from the rebates on
agricultural traffic were, in fact, very
largely illusory because a a rebate has
obviously very little meaning unless it is
related to some definitely fixed charge.
In the case of agricultural traffics on the
railways there is, in effect, no such
fixed charge because the rates actually
charged by the railways are and have
always been very far below the maximum
charges fixed by the statute.
It is to be borne in mind further that
a provision in the Transport Act, which
preserves to the farmer the right to use
his own lorry for the transport of his own
and his neighbour's merchandise, will
mean that the Transport Board will
always be obliged to keep their rates for
agricultural freights down to the level
at which a farmer could transport his
own produce in his own machine, and
there is therefore no need for any
further safeguard for agriculture in this
respect.
I hope that hon. Members will forgive
me for omitting on the Second Reading a
statement which, as it is, is now coming
at the end of the debate instead of at
the beginning.
As to the actual points raised by the
hon. and learned Senator, he has so
admirably stated the Government's
position in regard to the issues raised by
the Finance Committee of the Belfast
Corporation that I think it would be
absolutely unnecessary for me to say anything further. I could not state the
position of the Government any better.
With regard to what he said about
Clause 3, the Government do not think
there is very much in his contention, but I
think that I had better say that Clause
3 will receive the careful attention of the
Government. As to Clause 5, he asked a
question as to why town commissioners
are not included. The answer is this.
These contributions are being made to
urban district councils to meet the
increased proportion of common charges
which they will have to pay as a result of
revaluation. The town commissioners do
not have to meet such common charges
at all. There is another point about
Clause 4. Clause 4, sub-clause (3) (b) 1
makes it perfectly clear that only the
standard grant is fixed on the basis of
the three financial years. I think that
these questions are better raised on the
Committee stage when I shall be able to
give a rather more definite reply to them.
With regard to the question raised by
Senator McLaughlin I would rather
reply to it at a later stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.

(Restrictions) Bill.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: By leave
of the House I will postpone the Committee stage of this Bill until a later
date.

was
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I
going to move that this House do adjourn
during pleasure. It is very important

Bill to-day, but it will not come from

will do exactly what they like.

12 MARCH 1936
Mr. LESLIE: The only point that
occurs to one about taking the First and
Second Readings of the Revaluation
Consequential Provisions Bill together is
when we may expect to have a copy of
the Bill, as it leaves another place, in
our hands. That is the point. I had
occasion yesterday to point out that a
Bill which we were perfectly prepared to
pass through all its stages was not in our
hands until the very morning when we
had to discuss it. If the noble Viscount
assures us we can get the Bill say on
Monday there would be no objection.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Saturday
morning perhaps, but I hope Monday
morning at the latest. I give such
assurance as I can for Monday morning.
I do not think it will be later. Under
these circumstances perhaps there will be
no objection.
The House indicated assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Then I
move, "That this House do now

THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 19th March, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Regulations made by the Council of
the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern
Ireland, 1936.
Ministry of Labour:
The Unemployment Insurance (Insurance Industry Special Scheme) (Variation
and Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland), 1936.
The National Health Insurance and
Contributory Pensions (Voluntary Contributors) Amendment Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1936.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Revaluation (Consequential Provisions) Bill,
to which they desire the concurrence of
this House.
Business of the House.
Resolved: "That Standing Order No.
XXV. be suspended for this day's

Long).
-
(Mr.
Revaluation (Consequential Provisions)
Bill.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long).
Mr. LONG: This Bill is intended to
prevent undesired consequences arising
from the carrying into effect of the first
general revaluation of rateable property
in Northern Ireland.
For example, in order that approximately the same type of person shall be
eligible for jury service in the cities when
the new valuations come into force as is
at present eligible the rating qualification is raised by sub-clause (1) of Clause
1 of the Bill from £20 to £29, the increase representing approximately the
average increase in the valuations of
rateable property in the cities as a result
of revaluation. Other Clauses deal in a
similar way with the rating qualification
of Harbour Commissioners and of citizens of Belfast or Derry who may vote
for their election, with the amount of
excise duty chargeable on a valuation
basis to premises licensed to sell intoxicating liquor and with other arrangements which it is not desired that the
revaluation should alter.
I do not think that hon. Members will
desire me to take them Clause by Clause
through the Bill in the cases where the
underlying intention is simply to preserve
the status quo, but some explanation is
necessary in respect of Clause 12, which
adjusts the basis of valuation upon which
the Belfast Harbour Commissioners contribute towards the rates of the Belfast
Corporation.
Hon. Members are aware that the
Belfast Harbour estate provides for
itself most of the services provided for
the rest of Belfast by the Corporation.
Because it is to such a large extent selfcontained a special arrangement
made by Statute in 1896 for the assessment of the Commissioners' contribution
to the rate of the city, and this arrangement was confirmed in 1925 by Section
13 of the Belfast Corporation Act.
was
The arrangement is that the Commissioners' valuation is the amount represented by the application of 2d per ton
to coastwise vessels, and 4d per ton to
foreign vessels entering the port. On
that amount the Harbour Commissioners
pay 58 per cent. of the rate struck by
the city. If this arrangement were left
untouched, the Harbour Board would
pay substantially less to the Corporation
when the new valuations took effect.
This in the view of the Corporation would
be unjust to other ratepayers.
The Board, on the other hand, maintained that their property had already
been revalued inasmuch as, with increased tonnage using the port, the basis
of its valuation had increased year by
year while the increase in the real value
Revaluation Bill-
Second Reading
19 MARCH 1936
of other property had not been reflected
in its rateable value. Increases in the
rates struck had also affected them,
although they had not been provided
with additional services.
The Government took the view that
justice would be best served by compromise and the basis of the Board's valuation has been raised to 2d per ton on
coastwise vessels entering the port and
5d per ton on vessels entering it from
foreign ports. The rate payable upon
this valuation will continue to be 58
per cent. of the ordinary rate. This
increase in valuation is appreciable, but
not so considerable as the average increase
for the city of Belfast as a whole.
beg to move, "That the Bill be now read
a Second time.'
I
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill which has just
been introduced to our notice is one which
I am bound to describe as a very difficult
Bill, a very disquieting, and, I am
afraid I must add, a very dangerous Bill.
Indeed, I am in some doubt as to whether
the House ought to give it a Second
Reading, or whether they ought to
require a somewhat different Measure to
be submitted to them. As to that I shall
hope to hear the opinions of hon. Members generally. I do not myself propose
to make any Motion of rejection or, in
the more accustomed manner, of delay.
We cannot, as I think, properly take a
decision on the Second Reading until we
have examined the Bill and have satisfied ourselves that we understand it and
that we agree that what it purports to
do should be done, and further agree that
the Bill does in fact carry out its own
intentions. After that examination, as
it seems to me, we ought to consider
whether the purpose of the Bill ought to
be effected and is effected.
The fact is that the Bill seems to me
to be divisible into two parts, although
in a disjointed manner perhaps. One
part is that dealing with a rearrangement of purely money matters which
might be disturbed by the unrestricted
operation of revaluation, and the other
is an alteration in the law dealing with
various subjects which is introduced, to
use the wording of the Bill, in order to
prevent the effect of the Measures being
upset by the new standard of values
which the Revaluation Bill has introduced. I propose to endeavour to review
the subject from both aspects, the general
as well as the particular.
First I want to say a word about the
very Title of the Bill. The Title runs,
as of course hon. Members are aware,
they have the Bill in their hands:
as
A Bill to make provision for securing that the first general revaluation
of rateable property in Northern Ireland shall not disturb the effect of
such enactments and instruments as
operate by reference to specified
amounts of rateable value or specified
rates in the pound.
So to speak, a general Title which
makes claim to be exhaustive. After the
events which have happened in another
place it is obvious that the claim to be
exhaustive can be no longer maintained,
and therefore a trivial verbal alteration
will need to be introduced at the appropriate stage. But the Title is, I think,
a very astutely drawn Title, and it indicates the purport of the Bill with singular clearness. I crave leave to direct
special attention to the word "effect"
which is, I presume, introduced to warn
us that it is the results flowing from the
enactments that we should watch, not the
enactments themselves. Scrutinising
the effect of some of those enactments is
likely to be a task of some little difficulty
and trouble.
Taking general considerations as giving
rise to the inquiries that I think one
ought to put, I want the House to consider first of all whether it is right, and
whether it is the wish of the House that
the revaluation of certain hereditaments
should result in the devaluation of the
pound. I think that that is, in brief,
one of the points which we have to consider. Again, is it in the contemplation
of the Government that a somewhat
similar Measure to this should be introduced after each succeeding general revaluation? That is a point which seems to
me to be of very great importance. We
had one scale until the first general
revaluation, and it is suggested to tamper
with that scale in various ways, but are
we going to do it again when there is
another general revaluation? Is there
any essence of permanency about the proposals to-day, or is there not? If it is
not intended that a Measure like this
should recur and recur again, and if it is
proposed that the standard to be intro-
Revaluation Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
duced under the Bill, or rather the set
of standards, is to be stabilised on that
scale, is there not a very grave risk that
that scale may prove entirely out of tune
with the conditions of the future? If it
should happen that the next revaluation.
results in a general reduction of what I
have called the scale of the present day,
what is going to happen then? Are we
going to be faced with the same conditions as those which the Bill seeks to
remedy? We must consider the possibility of a general reduction, because I
think it is agreed that the scale of the
first general revaluation was, on the
whole, something high. That would seem
to appear from the results of the
appeals, firstly to the chief commissioner
himself, and later on to the
And if there should be any very general
reduction will not some of the new figures
suggested in the Bill be thrown somewhat out of date?
courts.
I would ask the House to look particularly at Clause 8, sub-clause (1), paragraph (a). They will find it on page 7
of the Bill. The wording of that paragraph is:
In the said section seventy-two as
applying in rural districts, for ten
pounds there shall be substituted eleven
pounds.
That,
That is a change of 10 per cent.
perhaps, is the most remarkable of the
minor changes which the Bill suggests.
And again, going back, can we really
estimate the effect of the enactments?
Can we do that fairly and invariably by
connecting it with the money rates to
which the operation of the particular enactment is necessarily connected? I do not
think we can.
I do not think it is at all
possible. And, therefore, taking one
consideration with another, I have to suggest, or rather, perhaps, I should say
inquire, whether the adoption of this Bill
will not expose us to some danger of
sacrificing principle to an apparent and
perhaps only temporary expediency, and
also are we not exposed to some risk of
being charged with giving undue preference to one interest as opposed to
another.
One is inclined to doubt very seriously
whether it would not be more prudent
and safer generally for us to let the effect
of the revaluation take its normal course,
and in considering that I think we must
remember that the enactments concerned,
which the Bill seeks to modify, are of very
varying dates. Some of these enactments
are pre-1852, that is the date of the first
valuation scheme which received the
approval of Parliament. Some of them
are before that, and some of them are
after 1924, the date when this Parliament passed a temporary statute for
effecting a temporary rise in certain
valuations. I say that was a temporary
statute, but it was not repealed until
1932. We did not take any special steps
such as those foreshadowed in the Bill
after the enactment of 1924.
ex-
Hitherto I have spoken almost
clusively against the Bill, and, therefore,
in fairness-because after all, in this
House, it is our business to set out both
sides of any question without giving undue prominence to one point of view more
than another-I think I should remind
the House that although we accepted, and
indeed admitted the justice and equity
of revaluation—a revaluation which I
may again remind the House, perhaps,
could have been forced upon us at any
time by the Imperial Governmentalthough we did accept revaluation we
did not ask for it, and we recognised that
certain difficulty and perhaps certain
hardship would inevitably accompany any
general revaluation. That being so, it
well be thought that it
may
was
part
a statesmanlike act on the
of the Government to ask Parliament to join with them in doing
something or, at all events, in endeavouring to do something, which will soften
some of the immediate asperities which
are attendant upon that recent revaluation. Perhaps that line of thought may
help some of us to appreciate the Bill.
Apart from that I must admit that
there are Clauses in the Bill which I,
myself, should be very sorry to see disappear. But if we give a Second Reading to the Bill I presume I may take it
that we
shall not be debarred from
moving in Committee to omit any Clause
that displeases us. Neither shall we be
fettered in any way as to a general
amendment of the Bill. I may say,
parenthetically, that as for general
amendment I think there will be very
little. But it will naturally be understood that no alteration must be suggested at a a later stage which would
Revaluation Bill-
Second Reading
19 MARCH 1936
originate a disturbance of what the
Parliamentary Secretary referred to as
the status quo.
We ought not to do that.
Indeed I am afraid I have to say that
the Bill itself creates or seeks to create
sufficient disturbance without the Committee of this House endeavouring to add
to it. We ought not to do that, and,
subject to that we ought to be entirely
unfettered. We ought to be told in
reply that that meets with general concurrence, that is, if the House gives a
Second Reading it will be at complete
liberty to leave out any Clause it desires,
and also to deal with the Bill in the
ordinary way, subject, of course, to that
one restraint which I have mentioned.
As
And now I want to run very briefly
through some Clauses of the Bill. First
of all, naturally enough we begin with
Clause 1, the Clause which suggests
alterations as respects our juries. I cannot regard the operation of the jury laws
as related solely and to be considered
solely with reference to rates and valuations, and as at present advised, I feel
that this Clause ought not to stand.
regards the second Clause, I confess that
I do not like interference under a public
general statute with a local Act. There
are several instances of that in the Bill,
and this is the first that comes before our
notice. Of course there are ample precedents for such a course when necessary,
and I gather that this particular Clause
has been agreed upon by the interests
particularly concerned. If that is so, I
do not see any object in making objection
to it myself. But I think one must put
in what is called a caveat as regards the
paragraph immediately preceding subsection (3) of Clause 2, page 3. I want,
myself, to put in a slight caveat until I
have an opportunity of finding out how
Section 24 of the Commissioners Clauses
Act, 1847, is applied by the local Act of
1854, and on what that says would seem
to depend whether there is or is not any
objection to be taken to that Clause.
I do not agree that the Clause is of an
undemocratic character. A Harbour
Board, after all, is not a municipal body
depending upon a suffrage, which is
practically universal. And furthermore,
there are not, I imagine, any two harbour
boards in the United Kingdom which are
working under actually identical conditions. I do not know, for instance,
how the support or the creation of supervision by electors of, let us say, Belfast
Harbour Board or the Derry Board-how
that compares with the situation that
exists at Bristol, Cardiff, or the Mersey,
or the Clyde, I do not think it matters,
because I am quite satisfied their conditions of operation are not identical. It
may be, perhaps, that some amendment
ought to be introduced further to protect
existing rights, that is, existing rights of
persons. That is a matter for a later
stage and is, perhaps, rather doubtful.
I do not think I need say more about that
to-day. What I said before about the
immediate asperities resulting from the
first revaluation may apply to Clause 3.
Perhaps the spirit of the 1920 Act may
occupy the foreground rather to the exclusion of the other point of view. That
particular Clause is rather an example of
the danger that I think we are in of
giving undue preference to one interest
at the expense of another.
Clauses 4 and 5, dealing with matters
of excise are, I submit, scarcely for this
House at all. I presume the Government will be able to assure us that the
course proposed in the Bill is within the
powers of this Parliament. I think we
are entitled to ask that. I have not made
independent investigation on the subject
myself.
I like Clause 6. I think it will
prevent disturbance and it will ensure
sufficient money for the service and it
is a service which the House desires
should be adequately supported.
It is
not very long since we dealt with the
Bill on the subject of public librariesit is 12 years ago. I had forgotten it
was so long. I think the House will be
glad means are taken to secure the supply
of sufficient money, and that yet there is
sufficient check to prevent extravagance.
I do not like either Clause 7 or Clause
8. I fear they are Clauses which would
introduce rather than allay disturbance.
Clauses 9 and 10, for reasons I have
already given, seem to me to be in order.
As to Clause 11, I must say I shall be
very interested to hear the Government
defence. It seems to be quite harmless.
But I would like to know how the Government suggests that Clause 11 will not disturb the existing fabric-in short, how
does the Government bring Clause 11
within the Title of the Bill. Clause 12
has already been explained by the Parliamentary Secretary. Clause 13, subject to
Revaluation Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
the proposed insertion of a time limit is
likely to be convenient. Clause 14 calls for
consideration. I do not think, supposing
the Clause stands, the Minister of Home
Affairs is the proper arbiter in a dispute
such as suggested. As regards the whole
Clause, I propose to move its omission
at a later stage. I think that is the best
method of ensuring a thorough discussion.
The remaining Clauses are formal. The
Schedule is dependent on Clauses 3 and
4, dealing with matters of excise, of
which I have already spoken.
Now let me conclude what is, perhaps,
an inconclusive speech by saying that
whatever differences of opinion may
develop during our discussions, and whatever feeling of disquiet may be engendered by the revelation that for all these
years we have been living under the
shadow of horrors of which we knew
nothing, owing to the fact that our scale
or standard of valuation was not in accordance with that now prescribed for ushowever all that may be, there can be
no doubt of the very artistic skill with
which this Bill is produced and brought
in here. I do not think any Member
will find any insuperable difficulty in
following its proposals, but I think we
shall all find great difficulty in making
up our minds as to how far the Bill is
really in accordance with the object as
disclosed in the Title.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I wish to state
that I am definitely opposed to this Bill,
and I also wish to express the hope that
the Government are not going to rush
the Measure too quickly through the
House. It has been my experience since
I became a Senator that the Government
are rushing Bills wholesale through the
House, and they are doing it more SO
every day. This is the second Bill in
this very Session that is being practically
rushed through in the one day's sitting.
I say that is not good for the country,
and it is being done with a view to trying to dose the public while the Bill is
being passed. I go further and say that
now we are getting the snag of the
original Revaluation Bill. I suggest to
the Government that they could well
have introduced a great many of the
Clauses in this Measure into the original
Revaluation Bill. If they had done so
there would have been no cause to bring
in this consequential Provisions Bill. I
go further and say that if these Clauses
had been introduced in the original
Revaluation Bill it would never have
passed through either House of Parliament, because the public would have been
alive to the fact that the Bill was going
to increase their rates unduly.
Here we have a Bill now introduced
which, I venture to say, allows the
ordinary man in the street, whom we call
ratepayer, to pay the piper, and on the
other hand, it refuses to give him the
advantages to which he should be entitled under this very Bill. If a ratepayer's valuation is increased from £13
to £30, and if he is entitled to certain
advantages under the £30 rateable valuation, why in all justice is he not allowed
to get them? Does it not show that the
Government really did not think that
when the Revaluation Bill was passing
through Parliament it would have such
an enormous effect on the ratepayers? As
I pointed out a few days ago, a number
of young Englishmen were brought over
for the purpose of revaluing our property
all over Northern Ireland. These young
men, I venture to say, were all going by
the rule of thumb. As a matter of fact,
it came out on appeals against a number
of the valuations that some of them really
admitted that they had valued the
premises in dispute under rule of thumb.
In some cases their valuations were reduced by half, and in the coming sessions.
they will be reduced a great deal more.
I think that one of the principal classes
to suffer under this Bill are the people
who were thrifty and who, a few years
ago, were building their own houses.
They were anxious to get out of thickly
populated towns and into the country to
get the fresh air. They used whatever
little savings they had for the purpose of
building homes for themselves. Then,
under our valuation system these houses
were valued. Some of them were valued
at about £13, others at £20, and so forth.
They had not such a large amount of
rates to pay, but now the bombshell has
come. A house valued at £13 five years
ago has increased to £30. I would like
the Government to give an explanation
as to why a house valued at £13 five years
ago by their own valuer should, under the
Revaluation Bill, now be revalued at
£30. I will give another case in point.
A house built five years was valued
by your own valuer at £14, and under
19 MARCH 1936
Revaluation Bill-

Second Reading
that tenants of houses under the Small
Dwellings (Acquisition) Act and also the
tenants of urban and rural district
council houses will be obliged to pay
increased rates. In a very short time the
ordinary man in the street will see the
effect of the original Bill and also the
effect of the Bill you propose to pass
through this House to-day with such
haste. When they see that the effect will
be to touch their pockets I venture to
say they will then express their sentiments in no uncertain manner.
the Revaluation Bill it is now valued at
£46. I would like an answer as to why
there should be such a difference. Is that
fair to those individuals who put their
life savings into these houses? In the
case where the valuation has been
increased from £13 to £30 the occupier
is obliged to pay practically double rates,
and in the case where the valuation has
been increased from £14 to £46, instead
of having to pay £8 10s in rates the
occupier has, under the Revaluation Bill,
to pay £17 4s Od.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry to interrupt the hon. Member, but I think he is
talking about the original Revaluation
Bill and not about the Revaluation (Consequential Provisions) Bill.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I thought I was
entitled to raise this matter under the
present Bill. As I stated at the start,
the ratepayers, while paying through the
nose for everything, are being deprived
of any advantages they should be entitled to. However, I bow to your
ruling. The position is that the Government are prepared, as I have pointed out,
to allow the ratepayer to pay through
the nose but refuse to allow him any of
the advantages of the increased valuation
qualification to which he is rightly entitled. I object to that. Clause 14,
which is another snag in the Bill, shows
The Lord Mayor of Belfast

Mr. LONG: In reply to the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) I wish to
say that it would be impossible for the
Government to delete any of the Clauses
in the Bill. I should point out that
individual amendments will be considered
at the proper stage. I should like
further to point out that it would have
been impossible to bring these Clauses
into the original Revaluation Bill as no
one could have foretold in 1929 what
the new scale of valuations would be.
That this Bill would be necessary was
made known to the House when the
1929 Act was going through.

from £50,000 to £100,000, for the proper
auditing of the accounts of the Fund and
for their submission to Parliament.
purpose of this Fund is of course to provide means of meeting emergency expenditure and the increase is necessary

Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like

Trust to thank the Government for increasing their gift to that Trust by

[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (General provision as
to payment of deficiency contribution
out of the Exchequer) and 2 (Amendment as to mode of payment of deficiency
contribution) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Amendment as to deficiency contribution for urban authorities).
*
(1) The deficiency contribution to be
paid to any local authority (other than
the water commissioners) under section
twelve of the Act of 1929 shall not, in
respect of the local financial year ending on the thirty-first day of March,
nineteen hundred and thirty-seven, or
any subsequent year, be in excess of
the greatest sum paid to such local
authority under the said section in
respect of any local financial year
within the period beginning on the
first day of April, nineteen hundred
and thirty, and ending on the thirtyfirst day of March, nineteen hundred
and thirty-six :
Provided that, in any case where a
local authority is first constituted after
the thirty-first day of March, nineteen
hundred and thirty-four, or the
boundary of the area of a local
authority is extended after the said
day, the sum which the deficiency
contribution to be paid as aforesaid is
not to exceed, in respect of the firstmentioned or any subseqeunt local
financial year, shall be such sum as the
Ministry of Home Affairs may determine.
*
*
The following Amendment stood on
the Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
Clause 3, page 3, line 1, leave out from
"that" to the end of line 8, and insert—
"the Ministry of Home Affairs shall
have power, under and subject to the
provisions of section twenty-one of the
Act of 1929, to make regulations for
enabling, in any case where a local
161 Financial Provisions Bill-

19 MARCH 1936
Committee
authority is first constituted after the
thirty-first day of March, nineteen
hundred and thirty-four, or the boundary
of the area of a local authority is extended after the said day, the sum which
the deficiency contribution to be paid as
aforesaid is not to exceed, in respect of
the first mentioned or any subsequent
financial year to be determined."
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: In
connection with Clause 3 I want, if I
may, to take this opportunity of saying
a few words, particularly in reply to a
statement-
The CHAIRMAN: The Amendment
I will have to be taken first.
Mr.
LESLIE: The Amendment on
the Paper is the minor Amendment to
which I referred on the Second Reading.
It seems to me that the contingencies to
which the proviso to the Clause relates
-it will be found at the top of page 3
-are so analagous to those dealt with
in Section 21 of the principal Act, that
is the Act of 1929, that we are practically
driven to apply the same system of dealing with them. I had better read to the
Committee so much of Section 21 of the
Act of 1929 as is material, but before I
do that I want to direct attention to
Clause 9 of the Bill before us. That is
the Clause which deals with what is to
happen if a local authority ceases to
exist, and provides that in such a case
regulations are to be made. That is
exactly the course I am proposing under
the Clause now before us. The passage
I have to read to the Committee is from
Sub-section (1) of Section 21 of the Act
of 1929, and it says in effect:
The Ministry of Home Affairs with
the approval of the Ministry of
Finance as respects regulations for the
purposes of Part II. of this Act, and
after consultation, so far as relates to
education authorities, with the Ministry of Education, shall have power to
make regulations generally for carrying
this Act .. into effect, and in particular-
•
(b) for enabling adjustments to be
made in the deficiency contribution
or any items thereof, if and so far
as such adjustment is required to
give effect to the provisions of this
Act, or in consequence of an alteration in the boundary of the area of
any local authority, or the constitution of any new local authority. . .
Such regulations are to be laid before
both Houses of Parliament. I submit
that the conditions are as nearly as possible identical and that although we do
not know when, or how often, such contingencies may arise, nor do we know
what amount of money may be involved
when they arise, we ought to provide for
consistent administration, and therefore
the suggestion on the Paper has been
put down in order that it shall be made
plain that we may, under regulations,
necessarily of a wide character, avoid the
disadvantage of any wholly ad hoc
decision which certainly would give rise
to trouble, and also we shall escape the
possibility of a treatment of any case
which is discordant with one or other
There is the
provisions of the code.
scintilla of principle of which I spoke,
that we shall retain such protection as is
given to the taxpayers by consultation
with the Ministry of Finance. The
interests in rates and taxes, as we
have heard this afternoon more than
once, are sometimes opposed, and
both parties ought to have a voice in
the regulations. Also, the opinion of the
Ministry of Education might be of value.
in this connection.
Accordingly I move, in effect, the
Amendment on the Paper, but I must
tell the Committee that since the Amendment was put down it has been pointed
out that under the existing regulations
under Section 21 the determination is
effected by the Ministry of Home Affairs,
and if that be so, and if the Committee
accepts this Amendment, the determination under this proviso will also be made
by the Ministry of Home Affairs. That
being so, it is thought desirable that we
should say so in the Act of Parliament.
Accordingly there will be a trivial
alteration in the wording of the Amend.
ment as in print. It will run:
"The Ministry of Home Affairs
shall have power, under and subject
to the provisions of section twenty-one
of the Act of 1929, to make regulations
for enabling it in any case where a
local authority is first constituted after
the thirty-first day of March, nineteen
hundred and thirty-four, or the
boundary of the area of a local
authority is extended after the said day.
to determine the sum which the

deficiency contribution to be paid as
