Mr. LONG: The proviso to this Clause
in effect is an extension of certain provisions of Section 21 of the 1929 Act so
as to make similar arrangements suitable
to the conditions arising as a consequence
of the fixing of a maximum derating
contribution in respect of urban districts.
The Clause as it stands simply provides
that where a new local authority is constituted after the 31st March, 1934, or
the boundary of an existing local
authority is extended after that date,
the Ministry of Home Affairs may adjust
the maximum deficiency contribution.
As the hon. and learned Senator who
moved the Amendment has already
pointed out, a somewhat similar provision is made in Clause 9 of the Bill
to cover the case of the abolition of any
local authority. In both Clause 9 of
this Bill and in Section 21 of the 1929
Act it is provided that regulations may
be made by the Ministry of Home
Affairs, subject to the approval of the
Ministry of Finance, to deal with such
adjustments, and the
the effect of the
Amendment is to provide a similar power
for the making of regulations. It is
therefore an improvement to the Clause
from the point of view of consistency
with the other Clauses in the Bill.
Amendment may, therefore, be accepted.
Amendment agreed to.
The
Question proposed, "That the Clause,
as amended, stand part of the Bill."
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
claim at this stage a little indulgence
from the Members of the Committee to
deal with a matter referring to this
Clause, principally in connection with
the speech made by my hon. and learned
Friend (Mr. Leslie) on the Second Reading. On that occasion I had
had just
returned from a holiday and as a matter
of fact had not seen the document
referred to, which deals with this Clause,
and consequently I was unable to enter
into the debate. Since then I have gone
very carefully into the matter, and I find
things are rather different from what the
hon. and learned Senator stated; indeed,
I am surprised that he went out of his
way to use language in connection with
the Corporation which I think would
have been better left unsaid. Now, in
the speech of the hon. and learned Senator
the Finance Committee are accused of
arrogance, misrepresentation and injustice.
The arguments contained in the circular
are described as specious, and the circular is described as containing a most
unprovoked attack, a most unfair attack,
a most insupportable attack upon the
right hon. the Minister of Finance. The
hon. and learned Senator could not use
much stronger language than that.
Before dealing with the subject in detail,
I wish to say that I regret very much
the differences which frequently crop up
between the Government and the Corporation of Belfast. Nobody regrets
them more than I do. I will not disguise matters and will say openly that
the relationship between the two bodies.
is far from what it ought to be.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH:
Both are supposed to serve the best interests of the community which they
represent, and it is very regrettable that
they cannot serve those interests without
the frequent misunderstandings which
have arisen. Practically all the disputes
which have arisen between the Government and the Corporation have had some
connection with the financing of public.
services for which both the Government
and the Corporation are, or are supposed
to be, responsible. The financing of these
semi-national services is at present on a
most unsatisfactory footing and is not
founded on any consistent principle, each
case being settled according to some expedient, generally a block grant, adopted
by the Government to carry them over
the difficulties of the moment, without
sufficient regard to the relative responsibilities of the parties or the future development of the services concerned.
The Corporation have on more than
one occasion offered the olive branch to
the Government by suggesting that the
financing of all these semi-national services should be placed on a proportionate
basis, to be arrived at according to the
nature of the service and its relative
national and local character. So used
have the Government become to the system of stabilising and limiting their
responsibility in connection with semi-
165 Local Government Bill-

19 MARCH 1936
Committee
national services by the expedient of
block grants that in the present case,
although derating is a purely national
service in regard to which the local
authority merely act as the medium for
passing on relief from rates to the
occupier and the Government recoup to
the local authority the loss sustained by
the local authority on the Government's
behalf-the Government now seek to
obtain a discount off their debt to the
local urban authority equal to the excess
of the derating loss above a certain fixed
arbitrary figure. I will deal with that
in a minute because I want to make the
matter perfectly plain.
Coming now to the document of the
Finance Committee which has been
objected to, I may say that I can find
in it nothing which is either arrogant,
misrepresentative or unjust. I have
looked into it very carefully since the
Second Reading, and I state deliberately
that I can find nothing of that description
in it. I have read it myself. I had not
seen it on the last day the Bill was before
the House, but I have read it since. It
appears to me to contain a simple
history of the transaction and a reasoned
theory as to why this Clause in the
Bill should not receive Parliamentary
sanction. That is all it means.
Before dealing with the arguments of
the hon. and learned Senator, I wish to
correct a false impression which may arise
from his remarks, which suggest that
Belfast has failed to agree with the Bill
where other urban authorities have done
SC. He made a very strong point of that.
Those who have studied the Bill will
realise that the smaller urban authorities
gain an advantage from other Clauses in
the Bill, which adjust the relationship
between urban and rural districts in connection with services financed in common
between them, the cost of which services,
as the result of revaluation, would have
borne heavily on the urban districts in the
absence of the new Bill. These considerations do not apply to Belfast which,
as far as derating is concerned, was not
affected by revaluation in any way whatsoever.
There is another misconception I wish
to remove, and that is as to any personal
responsibility alleged to attach to the
Minister of Finance. I wish to assure
the House that no personal attack on the
Minister was intended-not the slightest.
Coming now to the circular in detail,
I find that the matter of the circular falls
under two headings. The first of these
is that a definite promise was made by
the Minister of Finance that the derating
scheme would inflict no additional burden
on underated ratepayers. My hon. and
learned Friend denied that, but I want
to make it perfectly clear that a definite
promise was made by the Minister of
Finance that the derating scheme would
inflict no additional burden on the
underated ratepayers. The second heading is that apart from the promise, the
proposal to inflict portion of the derating burden on Belfast could not be supported on the merits.
As far as the promise made by the
Minister of Finance is concerned, I can
only find in my hon. and learned Friend's
speech two arguments for the defence,
which are mutually destructive, namely,
first, that the context of the promise
indicated that it was intended to provide
against the possibility of the valuations.
of a county being raised by the operation
of the derating measure; secondly, that
the promise was qualified, inasmuch as
provision was contained in the Bill for
revision of the liability of rural authorities, which might indicate that a similar
revision might be made in the case of
urban authorities. As far as the context
of the promise is concerned, it does not
appear to me to matter how the promise
arose, because the wording of it is explicit, and as the hon. and learned Member did not read it to the House I will
now do so. It is contained in Hansard,
Vol. 9, No. 58, page 3,852, as follows:-
"I would like to repeat the assurance which I have in manuscript. I
very carefully put it down in manuscript, so that there could be no misapprehension in regard to it. I stated:
"The relief will entail a deficiency
in the budgets of the local authorities. This will be made good by the
Exchequer, so that the ratepayers
who do not get reliefthat is those about whom my hon.
Friend behind me is concernedwill incur no additional burden by
the relief given to farmers and industrialists in their own districts'-
I ask you could anything be plainer than
that.
Local Government Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Sir C. McCullagh]
"Could I say anything more
emphatic than that? Between the
present date and the time this Measure
will be introduced, a small Departmental Committee will be sitting, and
the duty that will be imposed upon
that committee will be to get into touch
with the local authorities and to
arrange with them-I hope mutuallyas to the methods and the machinery
that will have to be brought into
operation in order that the details of
this derating scheme shall be carried
through." (Official Report, House of
Commons, 26th November, 1928, Vol.
IX, col. 3,852.)
That is plain. I do not think it is
necessary for me to impress upon the
Committee the importance of a clear expression of that
that kind being strictly
honoured.
The second argument, that provision.
was inserted for the revision of the
liability of rural authorities, is to my
mind entirely irrelevant.
As far as the arguments of my hon. and
learned Friend on the merits are concerned, after reading his speech-I have
read it carefully again, although I heard
it in this House-I find that the only
considerations in support of his attack
which he has put forward are the comparative insignificance of Belfast in the
matter of derating and the effect of the
revaluation as a new and overriding
factor; and that the only change in the
case of Belfast is to fix a maximum for
the sum to be paid by the Government;
and secondly, that Belfast has enjoyed
the present arrangement-under which it
receives full recoupment for the past
five years.
It is particularly unfortunate that the
hon. and learned Member should have
adopted as one of his principal arguments
the comparative insignificance of Belfast
in connection with derating. It is true.
that derated property in Belfast is
relatively small, but it is also true that
Belfast contributes more than one-half
of the taxes paid in Northern Ireland
which form the foundation of the
Government's revenue, out of which the
derating scheme is financed. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) That is also
true.
Moreover, not only does Belfast bear
the brunt of the derating scheme for the
whole of Northern Ireland, but its
burden is enhanced by the fact that
agricultural property, on which the
Government pay the rates, was exempted
from the Revaluation Act. I do not
want, at this stage, to enter into the
derating scheme at all. Personally, I do
not agree with it, but it does not arise
here. I think the whole system is
wrong. I know a lot of my friends do
not agree with me, because many of them
who own land are paying no rates and
many firms in Northern Ireland that are
making huge profits are paying no rates.
But that does not arise here, and so I
pass from it after saying that I do not
at all agree with the scheme.
As far as the other argument is concerned as to the time during which
Belfast has enjoyed the fulfilment of the
promise made by the Minister of Finance,
I can only say that the complaint of
Belfast only arose when the promise
ceased to be adhered to.
As the speech of the hon. and learned
Member consists principally of invective
rather than of argument, I am afraid I
cannot follow him further. I only wish
to say in conclusion, that the question
is really one of principle and of serious
principle. The derating scheme of the
Government was set up expressly as a
State service, and no suggestion was ever
made that it was anything else.
In order to indicate the seriousness of
the present proposed Clause, I should
explain, as indeed was explained in the
circular and this is really where it
touches Belfast-that if the average rate
of increase in the derating figures for the
city of Belfast, which has taken place
since derating began-due, of course, to
the development of the city and its consequent increased expenditure-is continued in the years to come, the operation of the proposed Clause will be felt
in two years' time, and any increase
thereafter in the valuation of derated
premises will bring in no rates to the
Corporation.
I
My hon. and learned Friend said it
was insignificant, but I have got the local
figures to prove what it really means.
took the trouble to see the City Treasurer.
As a matter of fact I have spent a good
deal of time with him during the last
day or two, because the matter is very
difficult to follow and understand. But
169 Local Government Bill-

19 MARCH 1936
Committee
this is what was brought out, and this
is really what I want to put on record.
From the official figures supplied to me I
find that during the last five years, during
which derating has been in operation, the
annual derating grants payable to the
Corporation have increased from £93,566
to £131,847, making an increase in the
five years of £38,281, or an average
increase of, say, £7,650 a year.
That is
the annual increase in the derating
grants brought about by revaluation or
the valuation of new premises and all that
sort of thing.
It is stated in the Finance Committee's
circular:-
"Owing to the fact that the average
increase of the valuation of industrial
hereditaments in the city under the
revaluation is much less than the
average increase of the valuation of
other hereditaments, the amount of the
derating deficiency grant payable to
the Corporation when the revaluation
comes into force will be less than it
would have been under the present
valuation. As matter of pure
arithmetical calculation the derating
grant next year, payable on the new
valuations and estimated on the basis
of a rate expenditure identical with
the present year, would drop below the
present year's grant to the extent of
£14,750."
a
That is the Corporation's case, that under
this Bill it is going to cost the Corporation that amount. And the circular
goes on to say:-
"If the average annual increase of
£7,650 in the derating grant payable
to the Corporation over the past five
years is maintained, it will necessarily
follow that the maximum now proposed
will be felt by the Corporation the year
after next, and any subsequent increase
as the city, and particularly as industrial buildings develop, will become a
cumulative burden upon the ratepayers
who do not enjoy the benefit of derating."
In other words, under normal conditions, in two years' time the Corporation, under the new Bill, will make
allowances to derated occupiers amounting in the first year to £7,650 less than
they will receive in recoupment from the
Government. In the following year the
shortage will be twice that sum, in the
following year three times that sum, and
so on until an intolerable burden is cast
upon the occupiers of underated property
in the city. And that is what my hon.
and learned Friend says is a small thing
and hardly to be considered.
I think it is better that the House
should clearly understand this and should
realise that in putting forward their
memorandum the Finance Committee
were acting from a pure sense of duty to
the underated occupiers of the city. If
they have failed to convince the Government that an injustice is being done the
responsibility will rest with the Government, and the Committee will, at least,
have the satisfaction of having done their
best in the interest of what the hon. and
learned Member describes as "equity."
The hon. and learned Senator talked
about the Government having treated
the Corporation generously. The Corporation do not want generosity; the
Corporation are prepared to pay their
way; they do not want special treatment.
What the Corporation say is this, derating is a responsibility of the Government's. The Corporation want no profit
or commission out of it. They say to
the Government, "You have caused
derating; you should foot the bill. We
will collect the money for you, but for
goodness sake do not ask us to lose a
huge sum of money every year in carrying out derating." There has been a
certain amount of ill-feeling between the
two bodies, and I do think even at this
stage this Clause should not go through
Committee. I am sure that if the
officials of the House and the officials of
the Government together were to consider these figures they would arrive at
the conclusion that an injustice is being
done to the Corporation. I do not
believe that the Government desire to
impose anything unfair upon the Corporation, and I suggest that, even at this late
hour, something should be done to get
the two bodies together, with the object
of having the Clause amended in such a
way that the Government should pay
whatever derating costs the Corporation.
That is all I have got to say. As I said,
I am not moving an Amendment. I say
that the responsibility belongs to the
Government. I appeal to the Government to go into the whole matter in order
to see if they cannot arrive at a satisfactory conclusion as between the two
bodies.
171 Local Government Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
Mr. LESLIE: The Question before us
is, "That Clause 3 stand part of the
Bill." I understand from what has
fallen from the Lord Mayor that he does
not propose to move an Amendment. I
rather gather that he proposes to divide
the Committee against that Question,
"That the Clause stand part of the Bill."
I gather that principally from his general
remarks on the subject, which can only
point in that direction. However, it
will be for him, of course, to decide that
for himself when the time comes. As to
the Lord Mayor's remarks regarding the
speech which I delivered on the Second
Reading of the Bill, extracts from my
speech were quoted-
The CHAIRMAN: I do not think I
should allow this discussion to go on any
longer. The hon. and learned Member
was given a good deal of latitude on the
Second Reading, and the right hon. the
Lord Mayor of Belfast has enjoyed a
good deal of latitude to-day. I cannot
allow any further discussion on this
matter.
Mr. LESLIE: Then all I can say is
that in my considered judgment the
Clause before us is absolutely fair to all
the urban authorities to whom Clause 12
I shall supof the principal Act relates.
port the Clause.
matter.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: May
I ask the indulgence of the Committee
for a minute on this particular Clause?
As has been said by the Lord Mayor of
Belfast, there has been a good deal of
unpleasantness between the Corporation
and the Government on this
Only a few weeks ago I complained of
that in the Corporation, and I did say
I thought there would be a better understanding arrived at between the two
bodies if the local finance people and the
Government would come together and
discuss the matter. We heard this
matter discussed on the Second Reading
of this Bill, and if ever it was true that
there are always two sides to a question
it is true in this case, as has been made
clear to us to-day. I listened attentively
to the hon. and learned Senator dealing
with the complaint of the Corporation.
We have now heard the Lord Mayor put
forward the City Council's case, which
undoubtedly is fair and deserves some
attention from the Government.
If this Clause is submitted to a vote
I am afraid there are some Members of
this Committee who will be compelled
to vote against it, knowing perfectly well
that it is not strictly fair to the ratepayers of Belfast. The Lord Mayor has
told us that a burden, increasing by
about £7,000 a year, is going to fall on
the ratepayers of Belfast. If that is so,
and if the Government are only going to
pay a certain amount, what will the Corporation have to meet in the forties? It
will be an exorbitant amount. While
I am not an expert in financial matters
I, as a ratepayer, know that it requires
a penny in the £ to raise £7,000. That
means that the ratepayers will have to
shoulder a very big burden for derating.
As the Lord Mayor has pointed out, there
are very prosperous concerns in Belfast,
making huge profits, which are derated,
and the smaller concerns and ratepayers
find it very difficult to pay their rates.
Yet this charge is imposed for the purpose of paying the rates of industrial
concerns that are very prosperous and are
making huge profits. This is an anomaly
that should be rectified. I would ask the
Deputy-Leader of the House to give us a
promise that the Government will consider this matter, so that the Clause will
not be put to a vote of the Committee.
I do feel that the Lord Mayor has made
a strong case in favour of amendment or
the deletion of this Clause.
Mr. LONG: With regard to the figures
quoted by the Lord Mayor, according to
the Government's calculations the Corporation will continue to receive increases
in derating grants until their new expenditure is £500,000 in excess of their
present expenditure. However, approaching the matter from an entirely different
angle and working on the basis of the
Corporation's own figures, it is perfectly
clear that their figure of £14,750, which
is in fact low, does not represent, as the
Corporation states, a figure of under
£150,000, but it represents over £200,000.
To this may be added at least £100,000
in respect of unemployment expenditure,
making a total of over £300,000, exclusive of any education rate. There is
always in practice a limit beyond which
the expenditure of a local authority cannot go, because there is a point beyond
which rates cannot, in practice, be raised
or the ratepayers will be driven to revolt.
173 Local Government Bill-

19 MARCH 1936
Committee
It is perhaps relevant in this connection
to remind the Committee that a very
great part of the Corporation's expenditure is financed by way of loan charges,
which are spread over long periods of
years, and it will be seen, therefore, that
a margin of £200,000 or £300,000 may
represent a capital expenditure running
into millions of pounds.
It was explained in the Second Reading speech that the provisions of this Bill
were not introduced with the object of
limiting the activities of local authorities. I desire to repeat that such was not
the intention of the Government at all.
a
It would appear that the Corporation
regard with complete equanimity
steadily increasing expenditure represented by an average annual increase of
£7,650 in the derating grants. That is
to say, a steady increase of over £100,000
each year in the Corporation's expenditure. I cannot think that they can
really intend this, but if they do, then
I can only say the provisions of the
relevant Clause in, incidentally, putting
an obstacle in the way of unbridled expenditure on the part of the Corporation,
more than justify the entire Bill, for I
think a Government would be entirely
insensitive to its responsibilities if it
would allow any local authority to proceed
unchecked in a financial policy that could
only lead to an intolerable burden being
imposed upon its citizens, and that those
Barclay, Mr. William
Cunningham, The Right Honourable Samuel
Davison, Sir Joseph
citizens would be entitled in all fairness
to say that the Government had failed
in its duties in neglecting to protect them
from such disaster.
It is the view of the Government that
the limitation proposed to be imposed
on derating grants payable to boroughs
and urban districts in future has been
placed at so high a figure that the limit
is a generous one and should not be
reached by local authorities for a long
time if their expenditure is kept within
reasonable bounds. It should be borne in
mind that in this respect urban authorities in Northern Ireland have been in a
specially favoured position since 1929 as
compared with the same authorities in
Great Britain, and will continue to be
better off even under this Bill.
On the question of the relative merits
of percentage grants and block grants, my
friend, Senator Leslie, is more competent
to deal with this difficult subject than I
am, as he was chairman of the committee
that inquired into and reported on this
question some years ago. We obviously
could not discuss this thorny subject on
this Bill.

Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I wish to point
out to the Committee that for a house
in County Armagh valued at £4 10s
under the old valuation the amount paid
in rates was £1 15s 7d. Under the new
valuation of £7, the rates amount to
£2 12s 41d. For a house valued at £12
under the old valuation the amount paid
in rates was £6 18s 3d, for the same house
under the new valuation of £25 the
amount to be paid in rates is £9 6s 6d.
For a house valued at £13 under the old
valuation the amount paid in rates was
Local Government Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
£7 11s 8d; under the new valuation of
£30 the rates will be £11 3s 9d. For a
house valued at £14 under the old valuation the amount paid in rates was £8 5s;
under the new valuation of £46 the
amount of rates will be £17 4s. In the
case of
the last mentioned house the
property tax is £7 4s, so that the total
increased amount for which the ratepayer is responsible in consequence of the
revaluation is £15 18s. The standard
grant from the Government to the
County of Armagh for the year 1935-36
was £100,886; the standard grant for
the year 1936-37 is £95,140, a reduction
of £5,746.
I also find that the total grant to
Armagh County Council for the year
1935-36 was £115,378, and for the year
1936-37 it is only £110,144, a drop of
£5,234. The supplemental grants for
the same years were £14,492 and £4,583
respectively, representing a reduction of
£9,909. This means that as compared
with the old valuation the ratepayers of
County Armagh are obliged to pay an increased rate of 1s 8d in the £. We have
been told that the splendid agreement
made between the Government and the
local authorities is a good thing for the
ratepayers, but it is beyond dispute that
it has resulted in reduced grants to the
Armagh County Council to such an extent that the rates have had to be increased by 1s 8d in the £. For these
reasons I would like to have an assurance from the Government that under
this proposed Clause the grants to the
various county councils will be maintained and will not be reduced.
so
Mr. LESLIE: Is not the hon. Member
really asking the Government and Parliament to consent to what throughout
the discussions attendant on the enactment of the derating code they have
steadily refused to do; that is, to give an
assurance that the valuations and
forth will remain constant. It is in the
nature of things that they cannot do so.
The only thing the Bill provides for as
regards absolute constancy appears to me
to be payments to urban district councils
mentioned in the Schedule, and standard
grants when determined will be fixed also
until they are altered by Parliament. I
do not think the Government can possibly be asked for anything that could be
construed into a definite, permanent,
everlasting promise, irrespective of
future Parliaments which might tear it
up, that there will be no variation in
valuation on any account whatever.
Mr. LONG: That is so. No assurance
can be given that standard grants will
not be reduced.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Then I would
like an assurance that they will not be
reduced in successive years. Not only
under this Bill, but under other Bills it
has been the consistent policy of the Government to pay less money than hitherto
in grants and to place a bigger burden
on the ratepayers, and it is time that
policy was discontinued. I suggest to
the Government that in view of the
criticism of the proposals in this Bill they
should not force it through the House
at this stage but that they should withdraw it for further consideration.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Rent and Mortgage Interest
(Restrictions) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Long).
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Prolongation of period
fixed by 22 and 23 Geo. 5, c. 14, s. 1).
1. The Rent and Mortgage Interest
(Restrictions) Acts (Northern Ireland),
1920 to 1932, shall continue in force,
with respect to dwelling houses and
mortgages to which the said Acts
apply, until the first day of May, nineteen hundred and forty.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg
to move, in
Clause 1, page 1, line 9, to leave out
the word "forty" (nineteen hundred and
forty), and to insert instead thereof the
word "thirty-nine." It may be remembered that on the Second Reading I
said that Parliament ought to have a
great deal more information before consenting to the extension to which this
Clause and practically the whole Bill
relates; that is, extending the operation
of the Rent and Mortgage Interest
(Restrictions) Act for four years instead
of allowing it to expire on 1st May next.
I said I thought the time had come when
Parliament should do something definite
to give effect to its intention that these
restrictions should not prevail for ever.
They have been in operation, as the Title
of the code itself suggests, since 1920,
and we know they were not quite born in
1920. It is to obtain the first, and to try
to attain the second, that I have put
the Amendment on the Paper, and I am
anxious to hear what the Government
has to say to us about what has been
done.
The particular question I asked was,
how far is this a Belfast question? It
will be remembered that Newry bulked
rather largely in the evidence given before
Mr. Crawford's Committee in 1931, and
I have heard from my own locality that
the continued existence of these restrictions, although they are not considered
necessary in that area, is found to lead to
confusion. I have a letter in my hand
from a correspondent in a country district complaining of the continuance of
the Acts. In effect, I am sorry to say,
he regards them as a muddle of control
of houses. He also complains of unfairness, and puts forward arguments with
which the House is familiar. That is the
first inquiry.
Then I asked whether any effect of the
special provisions of the Act of 1932
could be reported to us. The particular
provisions I was thinking of were, of
course, the steps taken to prevent the
continuance and repetition of certain
scandals referred to before the Committee
to which I have alluded. I want to
digress for a moment to read to the
Committee an extract from that report.
The extract is from the main report,
not from the appendix:-
The Committee are of opinion that
these Acts, amended in the manner
suggested, should be continued until
the shortage of houses of a modern
type of the classes to which the Acts
apply has ceased. It will, in the
opinion of the Committee, be some
time before this state of affairs exists
and the Acts might, therefore, be extended to the 1st May, 1936, before
which date the whole situation can be
reconsidered in relation to the circumstances then obtaining.
One would rather like to know whether
there has been any reconsideration of the
circumstances now obtaining. It is plain,
Rent Restrictions Bill-

Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
of
I think, that the Committee anticipated
that the 1st May, 1936, was a sufficient
extension. I would like to know something about the complaint such as my
country correspondent suggests,
owners who want to occupy, and owners
who want to give houses to others such
as a relation or employee. Particularly
as regards the evils reported by the Committee do I want to know what the result
of the Act of 1932 has been as regards
sub-letting. I also want to know, as
bearing on the question of whether
further extension is desirable, how many
houses were rendered uninhabitable in
Belfast owing to the disturbances in the
city in July last, and whether those
houses are houses to which the Acts
apply, because if any material number
of houses were destroyed or rendered uninhabitable it seems very questionable
whether a special code ought to be kept
in existence for the benefit of people
living in such localities.
It was suggested in reply on the
Second Reading that the Acts had not
had a restricting effect as regards new
building, because it was pointed out that
the Acts did not relate to new buildings.
But again I want to refer to the report
of Mr. Crawford's Committee. I want
to read, briefly of course, what Mr. Armstrong, an estate agent in Belfast, had to
say as reported on pages 79 and 80 of the
report.
My own opinion is that the housing
problem is fast becoming solved.
That was on 22nd October, 1931.
If building goes on as it has latterly,
history is going to repeat itselfthousands of houses in the city will be
vacant. That is my conscientious
opinion about the state of Belfastthat the housing problem is nearly
solved, and if great care is not exercised history is going to repeat itself by
an almost multitudinous quantity of
vacant houses.
That was Mr. Armstrong's opinion on
22nd October, 1931. And, again, to
return to the country, Mr. McCartney of
Ballymena, who, I understand, is an
estate agent in the neighbourhood-the
hon. Senator (Mr. McAllister) knows
more about him than I do. First of all
he thinks private enterprise would be
best, because he does not approve very
much of some of the houses that were
being built. Then the Chairman put it
to him directly:-
If control is taken away entirely
would it, in your opinion, interfere
with building?
That seems to be the point.
Mr. McCartney: I think it would
induce people to build. If control is
reimposed the private owner will get
out of it altogether.
And again:-
If you get plenty of houses rents
will come down.
He spoke of the continued scarcity of
houses in the Ballymena district. As
regards scarcity of houses, I hope we
are going to be told something about the
number of houses built in Belfast and
other places since the last extension of
these Acts, the number of houses now
vacant in Belfast. Mr. Alexander, rate
superintendent, who was also a witness
before that same Committee put in a
paper showing that there were
houses vacant in Belfast, of which-
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
what valuation?
2,123
Of
Mr. LESLIE: I am coming to that.
I rather extend the statement in order
to meet the point very properly raised
by the hon. Senator. The total was
2,123 of very varying valuations. There
were vacant, or in course of erection,
rent, 10s to 12s 6d, 342; rent 13s to 15s,
495; rent, 16s to 18s, 48; rent 19s to
20s, 12; for sale, price under £500, 292
-total 1,189. Add plans of new houses,
137; also vacant over £8 valuation, 146.
Of course 146 is not a large number for
Belfast. These were the numbers then,
and one naturally wants to know how
these numbers compare with numbers
now. Finally, if the Government really
does mean, as I think they do mean, to
get rid of these Acts as soon as possible,
I do suggest something of a gesture such
as a reduction of the proposed period of
extension of four years to three. Accordingly, I beg to move. The Motion means,
"That the period of extension be three
years instead of four."
Mr. LONG: Hon. Members will
no doubt recollect that when this Bill
was last before them on the occasion of
181 Rent Restrictions Bill-

19 MARCH 1936
Committee
its Second Reading, several questions
were put, to which the right hon. and
noble Leader of the House promised to
reply to-day. I propose, therefore, if
the hon. and learned Member has no
objection, to fulfil this promise while
speaking to his Amendment.
The first question was: "Why was it
necessary to continue rent restriction?'
In this connection may I refer to the discussion on the Second Reading of the
Bill in the Chamber of first instance?
In the course of this discussion instances
were quoted of £1 a week being charged
for an uncontrolled house exactly similar
in all respects to controlled houses in the
same street let at 11s 6d a week. In
this connection also, the position with
regard to houses which are being built
in Belfast with the aid of the Government subsidy is of interest. Local authorities were empowered by the Housing
Act of 1933 to attach conditions to the
making of their grant, one of which
might be the limitation of rent. The
Belfast Corporation have fixed rents of
7s 6d and 7s 9d per week, according to
the density of houses in the area.
Thousands of houses are being built to
let at these rents, which, therefore, may
be presumed to be profitable, but other
similar houses built before the limitation
of rents was imposed are being let at
at from 12s 6d to 15s a week.
""
The second question was: "If it was
necessary to continue rent restriction,
why was it necessary to continue it for
four years?
so long a period as
Although the Government hope that the
housing shortage will be at an end in a
much shorter time, they consider it wise
to allow a reasonable period for rents to
settle down and reach a stable and proper level before removing all control. If
rents come down sooner the Act will do
no harm.
The third question was: "Have the
Rent Restriction Acts had any effect in
preventing the building of houses?"
The reply to this is "No," as these Acts
have never applied to new buildings.
The instance of limitations of rent in
Belfast, which I mentioned earlier, is
not a case of restriction under the Rent
Restriction Acts, but of restriction
imposed under the Housing Acts as a
condition for the grant of the subsidy
and, in any case, it is not preventing the
building of subsidy houses.
The fourth question was: "Do these
Acts impose hardship upon persons buying houses for their own occupation, and
who are prevented under them from securing possession? Admittedly cases of hardship do occur in these circumstances, but
it is impossible to provide for such cases.
without opening the door to evasions of
the Act generally. Moreover, purchasers
should surely be aware of the provisions
of the Acts, and know if the houses they
are buying are subject to them.
To return to the Amendment before
the Committee, I would add to the
remarks I have already made upon the
question at issue, that in the view of the
Government it is not possible to estimate
with any degree of accuracy how soon
rents will stabilise themselves when the
housing shortage has been overcome and,
in order that they may have a full opportunity to do so, and in order that the
effect of the fulfilment of the Government's housing policy may be quietly
considered, they believe that it would be
unwise to remove rent restriction before
the day mentioned in the Act. I am,
therefore, unable to accept this Amendment.
a
I might also point out with regard to
the question how far it is a matter affecting Belfast alone, it is, of course,
Belfast matter, but not entirely so, and
the Government cannot properly confine
legislation to one area. I am further
informed that no statistics exist showing
the number of houses rendered uninhabitable by the disturbances.
me
Mr. LESLIE: I confess the point that
appeals to most in the Parliamentary Secretary's speech is the suggestion of allowing a margin after the
cessation of the necessity. I do not think
it would serve any useful object to divide
the House. I therefore ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short title, construction
and citation) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.

without amendment to the House.

Mr. LONG:
I beg to move,
"That
this House do now adjourn until Wednesday next."
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: What will be
the order of business when we resume?
Mr. LONG: The remaining stages of
the Bills we have dealt with.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Will you sit on
Wednesday and Thursday?
Mr. LONG: Wednesday for certain,
and probably Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
minutes after Five o'clock,
until Wednesday next.

Local Government (Finance) Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendment read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): In moving, "That this
Report be now received," I should like
to take this opportunity to reply on behalf of the Government to the case made
by the Corporation and voiced so ably
by the Right Hon. the Lord Mayor of
Belfast during the Committee stage of
this Bill.
187 Local Government Bill-

THE SENATE
Report Stage
[Viscount Charlemont]
be
the
In the first place I should like to endorse whole-heartedly all that he said on
the Committee stage of this Bill on the
desirability that there should
harmonious relationships between.
Government and the Belfast Corporation.
It has always been a matter of deepest
regret to the Government that there
should be any misunderstandings between the two bodies, for I am convinced
that both are equally anxious to promote
the welfare of the citizens of Northern
Ireland.
The Government has been somewhat
severely attacked by the Corporation for
introducing this new Derating Bill, and
I should just like to take this opportunity of clearing up one or two misapprehensions which appear to exist in
the minds not alone of the Corporation,
but, if I may judge from articles appearing from time to time in the Press, in
the minds of the ratepayers of Northern.
Ireland generally. May I repeat the
statement which I made, and which was
made by my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Home Affairs, in another
place, that the Government had
thought of making a saving at the expense of the ratepayers by means of this
amending Measure.
no
The Lord Mayor in his speech stated
that the Government were now seeking
to obtain a discount off their debt to the
local authority equal to the excess of the
derating loss upon a certain fixed
arbitrary figure. As was pointed out on
the Second Reading of this Bill, this is
not the case. The Government merely
wish to determine, within reasonable
limits, what their expenditure under
derating may be. The Government do
not wish in any way to limit the reasonable expenditure of local authorities,
and, as I hope to point out clearly, the
limit which has been fixed by the present
Bill is a very generous one indeed.
Before I proceed with the part of the
statement issued by the Corporation on
this point, I should just like to deal
with a statement made by the Lord
Mayor to the effect that the smaller
urban authorities are given advantages
under the Bill which are denied to the
Belfast Corporation. This is not so.
Belfast does not appear on the schedules
to the Bill, simply because these payments to the small urban authorities are
by way of compensating them for an
increased share of common. charges which
would fall upon them as a result of revaluation.
no
Belfast suffered
such
increased burden in respect of such
common charges and naturally, therefore,
it could not share in this arrangement.
I now come to the actual position as
regards Belfast. The Lord Mayor stated
that the annual derating grants payable
to the Corporation had increased from
£93,566 to £131,837, making an increase
in the five years of £38,281, or an
average increase of approximately £7,650
a year. This increase, which he
attributed to the development of the city,
was due in fact almost entirely to the
increase in rates from 10s 5d in 1930 to
13s 2d in 1935. During this period the
valuations of the derated hereditaments
only increased by approximately £12,000,
and had the rates remained at the figure
of 10s 5d, as in 1930, the derating grant
would have been increased from £93,566
to approximately £98,255, that is, an
increase of £4,689. The increase in the
grant is, however, £38,271, and the
difference of £34,032 is due entirely to the
increase of 2s 9d in the rates which
occurred during the period, an increase
which amounted to 26.4 per cent.
The Lord Mayor, quoting from the
Finance Committee circular, went on
to say that next year's derating
grant payable to the Corporation
would be £14,750 less than this year's
grant. It is not disputed that the grant
will be reduced. As a matter of actual
fact it is estimated that the grant will
be nearer £16,000 less than last year.
The Lord Mayor, however, suggests that
this reduction of the grant means a loss
to the Corporation, and this, of course,
is entirely incorrect. The derating grants
paid to urban authorities represent the
loss of rates owing to derating. If, therefore, the derating grant payable to the
Corporation next year is £14,750 less
than the grant paid this year, it means
that the Corporation's loss of rates due
to derating will be less by that amount.
But, leaving aside all technical considerations, I feel bound to refer to a most
disquieting aspect of the Corporation's
statement. This is the reference to the
average annual increase of £7,650 in the
derating grant payable to the Corporation
during the past five years, and which it
is suggested will be maintained. The
189 Local Government Bill

25 MARCH 1936
Adjournment
Lord Mayor pointed out that this increase
would mean that in two years' time the
Corporation would reach the maximum
fixed by the present Bill. As an arithmetical statement, and assuming his premises, this is perfectly correct, but do
the ratepayers realise what an increased
derating grant of this magnitude involves? The valuation of the derated
hereditaments in Belfast is less than oneeleventh of the total valuation of the
city, so that for every £7,650 by which
the derating grant is increased the
expenditure of the city must increase by
over eleven times that amount, that is,
over £84,000.
This
This is taking the Corporation's
figures, and, as I have said, they are
somewhat of an under-statement, so that
I am not overstating the case when I say
that an annual increase in the derating
grant by an amount such as the Lord
Mayor contemplates represents an increased expenditure of £100,000.
will mean that in the year 1938-39,
when, according to the Lord Mayor's
statement, the maximum fixed by the
Bill will be reached, the expenditure of
the Corporation will have increased by
over £200,000. Nor must it be forgotten that a very substantial part of
the Corporation expenditure is met by
means of loans repaid at a small rate of
interest over a long period.
It will be realised that if this £200,000
were expended on loan charges it would
represent a capital expenditure of over
three million pounds.
It must also be borne in mind that the
Corporation will also have to be responsible for expenditure in respect of
education and unemployment, and as
this expenditure is not related to the
deficiency contribution paid by the
Government it must form part of the
total up to which the Corporation may go
before their derating limit is reached.
Putting the position in the terms of rates
on the old valuations, the Corporation
will have to strike a rate equivalent to
almost 18s in the pound before the
maximum fixed by the present Bill is
reached, and I submit that expenditure
that calls for a rate greater than this is
more than the ratepayers can afford.
I cannot but think that when the ratepayers of Belfast realise the facts I have
set out to-day they will agree that the
present Measure is by no means opposed
to their interests, and is in every way
justified. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendment was accordingly
reported and agreed to by the House.

Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have to
report to the House that His Grace the
Governor of Northern Ireland, in the
name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his Assent
to the following Bills agreed upon by both
Houses:
Consolidated Fund.
Rent and Mortgage Interest (Restrictions).
Exchequer and Financial Provisions.
Housing (Grants).
Unemployment Assistance (Temporary Provisions) (Extension).
Government Loans.
Agnew's Divorce.
Local Government (Finance) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before
moving the Third Reading of
of this
Measure I should like to reply to the
statement made by Senator McLaughlin
regarding the derating grants for County
Armagh. The hon. Senator pointed out
that the total grants to Armagh County
Council for the year 1935-36 amounted
to £115,378, while for the year 1936-37
they amount only to £110,144, a drop of
£5,234. His suggestion was that this
indicates a loss to the county arising as a
result of the operation of this Bill or of
the Valuation Bill. This, however, is
not correct. The difference in supplemental grant in the two years concerned
alone fully accounts for the difference in
the total, and this diminution in the
amount of the supplemental grant is
accounted for simply and solely by the
fact that the county's estimated expenditure over the standard expenditure was less by £18,000 in
1936-37 than it was in 1935-36. The
hon. Senator will understand that the
supplemental grant is fixed every year
and is based on estimates put forward
by the county councils themselves.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I quite understand the answer which the noble Leader
of the House has given just now, but I
think it is naturally to be concluded that
owing to this Revaluation Bill the grants
to the County Council of Armagh have
been reduced by £5,234. The supplemental grant has been reduced
£14,492 to £4,583, a reduction of £9,909,
together with the £5,000 just mentioned.
It means that County Armagh loses
£5,234 as the result of the Revaluation
Bill.
from
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved, "That this Bill do now
pass." (Viscount Charlemont.)
Revaluation (Consequential Provisions)
Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont.)
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
26 MARCH 1936
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Rating qualifications for
jurors).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: The Motion that I have
on the Paper amounts to a suggestion
that the Committee should excise the
whole of Clause 1 dealing with the alteration in the law in relation to jury
service. The question, I venture to submit is of very great importance, and not
altogether free from difficulty. Before
we begin to consider whether we ought to
leave the Clause out or subsequently
to amend it, we must go back for a
minute to the title of the Bill, and remember what it is we are trying to do.
Undoubtedly, this Clause comes within
that title in so far as the Jury Acts
operate to some extent in relation to
rateable values. But the question arises
as to the effect. The object of the Bill
is not to disturb the effect of some enactments which operate by relation to rateable value. I am going to suggest the
fact that the jury laws go a great deal
further than a mere matter of pounds,
shillings and pence, which represent the
qualification of a juror. I suggest we
should have regard to other considerations.
Now, our present system depends
primarily on the Act we passed ourselves
in 1926, only ten years ago. It is one
of the Acts I spoke of the other day as
of very recent date. The main object
of the Jury Laws Amendment Act, 1926
-which I may pause to remind the
Committee applies to quarter sessions just
as much as to assizes and the City Commission-the objects of the Act were in
effect threefold. They were the simplification of the jury laws in so far as they
relate to jury service, better arrangements for securing equality of burden
of jury service, and finally for providing
that jury service should not impose an
intolerable burden. The idea underlying
the Act was that no man should be
called upon to serve on a jury more than
once in three years.
These were the main purposes of the
Amending Act, and it is now proposed to
alter apparently very materially the effect
of that Act as shown by the proposals
now before us. At that time, that is,
when the Act I have referred to was
passed, there were very great difficulties
in the working. The difficulties had been
so great that a Departmental Committee
was set up to consider the subject. That
Committee was presided over by Lord
Justice Andrews. I am afraid I shall
have to trouble the Committee with
sundry extracts from the report of that
Committee. First of all, I want to remind the Committee of the numbers of
jurors in the general jurors books at that
time. In County Antrim there were
8,662; in Down, 9,680; in Londonderry
City, 664, and in Belfast, 6,170. I do
not propose to read the details of the
other counties. These are quite sufficient
for my purpose.
It was found as appears
particularly from another paragraph—
For many years past that the general
jurors books for all the counties of Northern Ireland contained a much larger
number of names of common jurors than
was reasonably necessary, whilst the
number available for Londonderry City
was much below the city's requirements.
Prior to the establishment of the
Supreme Court of Judicature for Northern Ireland the number of common
jurors in the City of Belfast was reasonably adequate for the city's requirements, but the additional burden of
service now imposed upon them has
rendered the number of common jurors
wholly insufficient.
The further details of the paragraph do
not matter because as the Committee will
remember the Act of 1926 was directed
very earnestly to solving the problem of
providing a sufficiency of jurors in
Belfast. But the shortage in Belfast was
very marked at that time, and it is one
of the things we have to consider, I think,
whether in the new proposals there will
be any alteration.
Dealing particularly with that question
of Belfast and the common jurors thereof,
the report proceeded, and this is a rather
important matter:-
The most obvious and simplest method
of increasing the number of city jurors
would be the reduction of the existing
£20 valuation. This course, however,
though tempting, we are unable to recommend for several reasons. In the
first place, there is evidence before us
that the day may not be far distant
when a general revaluation of the City
D
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
of Belfast may take place, under which
premises of many citizens who could not
reasonably be expected to serve would
be brought over such reduced valuation.
Again we feel that the burden of service would fall very heavily upon those
who, even if there were no general revaluations would immediately be made
liable to serve on such reduced valuation, as they would be largely wage
earners dependent on their daily work
for their means of subsistence.
these and other reasons we advise that
the rating qualification for common
jurors remain as heretofore at £20.
For
The Bill now proposes to alter that £20,
the revaluation adumbrated at that time
having taken place. I think I must just
read one further short extract:
As already pointed out, the number
of jurors in the cities is considerably
below their present requirements whilst
the books of the counties contain a
superflous number of names. At the
same time, we fully appreciate the
difficulty, if not the impossibility, of
prescribing qualifications in an Act of
Parliament which will ensure that the
burden of jury service shall fall equally
upon all the citizens of the State who
might reasonably be expected to serve,
and who would also make satisfactory
jurors.
The point here is that the question of
the competence of jurors was considered
both by that Committee and in the Act
of 1926 which we propose to alter. Then
they go on to say that various Amendments might be introduced, and they
have been introduced. As regards
special jurors the position was, perhaps, a little different, but that was
the report.
not neglected in
The
number of jurors in the Special Jurors
Books were:-Antrim, 277; Down, 202:
Londonderry County, 190; Londonderry
City, 183; and Belfast, 1,684. Those
figures, of course, relate, as I said before, to the year 1923. The point then
that really arises is, can it now be said
that the new men would not be competent
to act as jurors? I mean by new men,
the men who would come in under the
increased valuation if Parliament did not
interfere. We have to remember also
that a man liable for jury service ought
to serve on juries. There is no reason
a
why he should escape, because for one
reason he is not doing his duty as
citizen, and, for another, he is increasing
the burden which lies on other people.
The CHAIRMAN: I should like to
ask the hon. and learned Member
whether it is his intention in moving this
Amendment to alter the basis of jury
service? If so, it would be outside the
scope of the Bill to move the deletion of
the Clause in order to alter the basis of
jury service.
Mr. LESLIE: No, I was not proposing to alter the basis. I move to leave
the Clause completely out and leave
matters as they are. On that point, I
think it was agreed that we should be at
liberty in Committee to move the
omission of any particular Clause, a
practice which was followed in another
place. There is also another small point
I desire to make, although I do not wish
to press it very far. That is that the
public ought not to be divorced from their
fair share of the administration of
justice, and the reduction of the number
of jurors perhaps works to some extent
in that direction, unless it can be shown
that the jurors who will come in under
the new revaluation are such as would
not be competent to discharge their
duties, and as to that we have no
evidence. There is also another point
which I do not want to press, and that
is the right of a man to be judged by
his peers. I cannot press that too far,
although there is a difference between an
ordinary criminal and the man of the
valuation suggested in the Bill. If we
carry it too far we might find ourselves
in a difficulty when a man was on trial
for his second murder, because you might
not be able to find a sufficient number of
homicides to complete the jury.
But the questions before us now are
whether a man who has had his valuation
raised ought not, as some compensation
for that, to get his liability for jury
service spread over a larger number.
Does every
man under the proposed
Clause, or will every man bear his fair
share and no more of the service, and
will there be a sufficinecy of jurors from
Northern Ireland if the proposals in the
Clause are accepted? The Committee
will remember that as regards the
counties the valuation of land has not
been affected by the last revaluation, but
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
26 MARCH 1936
there is a proposal in the Bill to raise
the qualification from £10 to £16. Before
we accept the Clause I submit that we
ought to have definite figures, and if,
as regards those figures, we cannot be
satisfied on the point I have mentioned,
then I must move, as I do, that the
Clause be left out of the Bill. Whether
you will allow, if that course is adopted,
an amendment of the detail at a later
stage would be a question to be settled
when the time comes, but, in the meantime, I am moving the deletion of the
entire Clause in the hope that we may
be able to have figures which will help
us to decide on the subject.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Clause
has been carefully drafted after consultation with the clerks of the crown and
peace in order to ensure that as far as
practicable the same number of jurors will
be available in the future as in the past.
In the existing jury enactments it must
be presumed that the Government in
fixing a valuation minimum had in mind
the desirability of having as jurors persons of some substance; on the other
hand, the rateable limit could not be
placed too high as otherwise an intolerable burden would be placed on the jurors
on the list, who would have to serve an
unreasonable number of times over a
period of time. The present Clause
maintains these principles exactly. For
this reason I am unable to accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: If I may say so, if we
had had before us at an earlier date the
information which the noble Viscount
has now given us the Committee would
not have been troubled with this Amendment. The noble Viscsount tells us
definitely what we wanted to know, and
that is that the numbers will be sufficient.
I ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Rating qualification of
Harbour Commissioners and electors).
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
:-
At the end of Clause 2, on page 3, to
add:-
:-
(c) A person qualified or registered
as in this sub-section aforesaid, who
ceases to be the occupier of the premises
he occupied immediately before the
passing of this Act and becomes the
occupier of other premises in respect of
which he would, if this Act had not
been passed, have been qualified to hold
office, or to be registered and to vote,
shall be deemed to continue to have the
same qualifications as, by virtue and
subject to the provisions of the foregoing paragraphs of this sub-section,
he would have had if he had remained
in occupation of the premises first
aforesaid.
Mr. LESLIE: This is a very different
question and not one approaching the same
importance as the last. The object of the
Amendment is, in rather lengthy language, to preserve if necessary the rights
of a
man moving, as it may be, from
house to house within his area. I discovered that there is a proviso in the
Londonderry Act, which protects that
man, for some time at all events.
That
As
is the man occupying a house of a valuation which would be now too low, but
who moves into another house of the
same valuation within the area. Whether
that proviso in the Londonderry Act
operates indefinitely I am not sure.
regards Belfast I can discover nothing
similar, but it is extremely difficult to find
my way about in a local Act. It is
difficult to get hold of local Acts, and
they are much harder to follow than
public general Statutes. I do not think
this question is of great importance, but
if there is any chance of injustice to anybody then the Committee ought to interfere.
I am, of course, as the Committee will
see from the words as they appear on the
Paper, relying on the printer to see that
the conjunction "and" is removed and
put into its proper place. Before sitting
down I might, perhaps, say that when
speaking on the Second Reading I said.
I must enter a caveat against some words
about the same place in the Clause until
I had had time to ascertain whether
another point arose. The point in question was, what was to happen to a man's
second vote, but that question does not
arise. It is provided for in the local Act.
Meantime, I have only to move the words
on the Paper.
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Clause
makes provision in sub-section (3), which
ensures that no person who is entitled
to be a Harbour Commissioner in Londonderry or Belfast at the present time
because of the valuation of the house
in which he lives, or is entitled to be
placed on the roll of electors for the same
reason, is deprived of that right because.
of the increases in the valuation qualification proposed in the Bill. It is quite
clear that the Bill can only operate on
broad lines, and that it may well be that
an individual, the valuation of whose
house has not risen in the same proportion as the general average, might find
himself deprived of a right which at
present he possesses. The Bill proposes
to continue that right to him so long as
he remains in the particular house whose
valuation attracted that right in the past.
But to go further, as the hon. and
learned Senator proposes, and allow that
occupier of a house when he removes to
another house to carry his right with him
if after examination it is found that he
has moved to a house which, so to speak,
carried this particular rating qualification
in the year of grace 1935, but lost it in
1936, would introduce complications into
the Harbour electorate and other lists
which I think would present serious
difficulties. The Government is, therefore, unable to accept this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount
suggests that the proposed change would
be more trouble than it is worth. Nobody
else seems to mind, and, therefore, I ask
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Effect of revaluation upon
Rent and Mortgage Interest (Restrictions)
Acts).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out Clause 3. This is, I am afraid, a
more serious question than the last, and
I must really begin again by asking a
question. The object of the Clause is,
of course, to prevent the recent rise in
valuation from affecting the Rent and
Mortgage Interest (Restrictions) Acts.
That is to say, a house is not to become
decontrolled merely because its valuation
has gone up, within limits, of course. The
first question is, are we bound to pass a
Clause of that nature? What I mean by
asking "are we bound" is, did we, so to
speak, get the Revaluation Bill through
a presumably reluctant Parliament in any
way on the understanding that a Measure
of this class would be subsequently introduced, because if we did there would be
no good in continuing the argument. If
we did not, if we are not, so to speak,
bound to pass this Clause for that reason
in redemption of a Parliamentary undertaking, then we should not allow the
Clause to stand.
If we do allow the Clause to stand, what
we are doing is, I think, undoubtedly
robbing Peter to pay Paul, and the fact
that there are few Peters and a
great many Pauls does not in the
least, to my mind, reduce the moral
turpitude of the transaction. Peter,
I am using the analogy, is distinctly injured, because when he got the £26 limit
his house might have escaped from control,
for the action which Parliament took, I
think, in the year 1932 readjusted these
matters. The Committee will remember
that what we did was to prevent certain
houses escaping from the control which.
the previous statute made. They were
treated like the frog climbing out of the
well. They were getting to the top and
were pushed back again. It seems to me
we are distinctly going against the spirit
of Section 5 of the Act of 1920 because
we are taking property of a kind and we
are not paying compensation.
Needless to say, the Clause if accepted
by the Committee will disappear when
the Rent Restrictions Acts disappear,
which we have some reason to hope will
be in 1940. The Act which has just become an Act, having received the Royal
Assent a few moments ago, is limited to
1940, and this Clause will go with it.
Then that question arises again, are we
not doing an injustice if we allow this
Clause to stand. I may say it might be
worth while to consider if the Clause does
stand whether it would not be convenient
to alter the dates mentioned from 1932
to 1936 which is a more correct description now at ten minutes to four. I am
not satisfied myself whether we can
accept this Clause without an explanation, if at all. Therefore, I move to
leave out the Clause, and I hope to hear
from others what they think about it.
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
26 MARCH 1936
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I may say
at once that the Government is unable to
accept the proposal to delete this Clause.
The Clause is, as a matter of fact, merely
declaratory, and it is quite possible that
it might be held that even without this
Clause the effect of the Rent Restriction
Acts is to keep such houses within the
scope of rent restriction even after revaluation. As a precaution, however, the
Government decided to include the Clause,
and it is, I think, obvious that it is a
protection which must be afforded to
tenants.
The hon. and learned Senator asked me
whether this House was bound to accept
the Clause. I do not think it would be
right or proper for me to say that this
House was bound to do anything. The
Government naturally think the Clauses
must be passed. Whether they are
passed or not rests with this House.
Mr. LESLIE: Apparently the Committee is prepared to accept that explanation. I do not press the point.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Alteration of certain
duties on liquor and refreshment house
licences) and 5 (Amendment of certain
enactments as to minimum valuation of
licensed premises).
Question proposed, "That the Clauses
stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: On these Clauses I
asked a question on the Second Reading.
Perhaps the noble Viscount is prepared
to give an answer? The question was
whether these two Clauses which deal with
the matter of liquor licences were considered definitely to be within the powers
of this Parliament. Perhaps if the noble
Viscount has not the information available it might stand over.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : I will
answer at a later date.
Clauses ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Limit on rate for purposes
of Public Libraries Acts) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Enactments as to valuation of property on which rates are paid
by owner).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: The effect of this Clause,
as it seems to me, is to extend rather unfairly the burden cast upon the owners
of certain property. Now that burden
was cast as recently as the year 1934,
speaking in a Parliamentary sense, we
may say the day before yesterday, and
the 1934 revaluation was actually in progress though I do not think the effect of
it could have been in any way gauged. I
cannot but think that the provisions of
this Clause mean an unfair extension of
the liability of the owner. I quite see
that to extend the provisions as regards
the collection of rates SO as to
put the burden on the owner up to £11
instead of £8 may suit the local authorities very well indeed. No doubt it does.
I am sure it will suit the rate collectors
even better, because they get the money
without a struggle. It may be that the
owner who complies with the Clause as
to existing rates gets a discount on the
rates, and that is continued, but at the
same time the man may not get the money
at all. It is a very material addition
to the burden on a man owning property
formerly value £8 and now £11 if he
has to pay the rates on the property and
take the chance of getting the rates
through the rent. I am afraid it is the
experience of many owners of property
that it is not so easy to collect rents as
it is for Parliament to pass a Rent
Restriction Act.
The Clause seems to be for the further
protection of local authorities, and to be
a Clause which has not received the assent
of the owners as a body-at least we have
not heard that the owners as a body have
consented. It is indeed desirable to ease
the position of the local authorities, but
we have to remember that the income of
the local authorities is going to be very
much increased by reason of the Revaluation Act and the results flowing from it.
Therefore, it seems to me they cannot
expect to have it both ways. On the
other hand, the owner has to pay more
in taxes probably quite rightly. I am
not arguing that but I do not think
there is any justification for casting any
further burden upon him. I, therefore,
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
submit to the Committee that this particular Clause ought not to stand. It seems
to me to be unfair to the owner, and,
as the Committee knows, I do not like
unfairness. I beg to move to leave out
the Clause.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to support
the hon. and learned Senator. I think
it is rather much to ask the owners,
particularly the owners of small property,
to have this collection of rates imposed
upon them and raised from £8 to £11
valuation. I think it is a very great
hardship on a great many owners of small
property. In fact it amounts to this,
that it is making the landlord the collector
of rates, and it certainly will get in all
the rates for the local council from the
landlord. Purely and simply it relieves
the rate collector of a great many duties.
What are the effects so far as the landlord
is concerned? The landlord is obliged to
pay the rates whether he gets them in
the rent or not, for the simple reason
that so long as the tenant occupies the
house under £8 but increased in this Bill
to £11, the landlord must pay the rates.
Now I have a case in point. Recently a
landlord was obliged to take proceedings
against a tenant for his rent. Then he
had to go for an ejectment, and, whilst
getting the ejectment, almost six months.
elapsed before he was able to get possession
of the house. The result was that the
landlord was obliged to pay the rates on
that house although the tenant of the
house was under notice to quit and there
was an ejectment against him. He re-
Dickson, Mr. James H.
Leslie, Mr. James G.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Cunningham, The Right Honourable
Samuel
ceived no rent for that house, but had
to pay the rates. I hold it is unfair to the
landlord, particularly the landlords of
small property. This Clause is fair
neither to the landlord nor to the tenant.
If the tenant is in such a way as to be
receiving local charity or outdoor relief,
or if he is in such a way as to be unable
to pay the rent, there is no reason why
the landlord should be made responsible.
Under these circumstances I certainly
agree that this £8 should not be raised
to £11.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government is unable to appreciate why the
hon. and learned Senator proposes that a
provision which the Government thought
proper to make as recently as the year
1934 in relation to the payment of rates
by owners in the case of certain small
house property should now be allowed to
be repealed in part by reason of the effects
which revaluation has brought about. The
Clause has the full support of the local
authorities concerned. I am afraid the
hon. and learned Senator will not
appreciate that point, but I am unable
to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I am unable
to agree that the Clause would effectually
repeal the Clause of the 1934 Act. The
Committee must settle the matter in the
usual way.
Question put, "That the Clause be left
out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 5;
Not Contents, 12.
CONTENT
McLaughlin, Mr. T.
McMahon, Mr. J.
NOT CONTENT
Davison, Sir Joseph
Gibson, Dr. Wm.
Glendinning, Mr. John C.
Lavery, Mr. T. R.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Enactments as to payment of water rates by owners instead of
occupiers).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill.”
Mr. LESLIE: Again I move to leave
out this Clause. I rather regret to have
Perceval-Maxwell, Capt. John
Robert
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
to trouble the Committee again with it,
because, of course, the Clause is to some
extent a corollary of the Clause the
Committee has just accepted, but still
there is this difference between them.
The proposal in the Clause is to continue
the operation of Section 72 of the Waterworks Act of so long ago as 1847, and to
adjust that to the new valuation. I
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
26 MARCH 1936
said just now that the Act of 1934 was
too recent to be disturbed, and I am
afraid I have got to say now that the
Act of 1847 is too old to be disturbed,
because since 1847 we have had not only
Griffith's Valuation, but one or two
intermediate Valuation Acts, the Act of
1924 and others. Moreover, on this
Clause again arises rather pertinently a
question I asked on the Second Reading.
Is it proposed that we should have a
Consequential Provisions Bill after every
revaluation? We are going to have a revaluation every five years. Are we going
to have a Bill like this after every one
of them? Are we going to deal with the
ups and downs that may come upon us
after every revaluation? The Bill says
that in an urban district for £10 shall
be substituted £13, but in the rural
districts for £10 is to be substituted £11.
We are legislating for the alteration of
£1. I do not think that such a minor
alteration as that can possibly stand the
blast of repeated revaluations.
Moreover, there is this difference again.
between this Clause and the last one. In
the last Clause, it will be remembered,
an owner, having paid the rates, is entitled to recover them, if he can, from
the tenant, but in this case, so far as I
can make out, the owner has no right of
recoupment at all, and has simply got
to pay the rate on a higher valuation
than he paid before. He is made liable
for a rate on a higher valuation than
before. I cannot see that any great case
has been made out for an alteration.
I think the whole thing is rather too
trivial for Parliament to touch. There is
again a distinct unfairness to the owner,
and again the rights of property appear
to be invaded for no particular reason.
Accordingly, I move that the Clause be
left out. There is no possibility of
would
if you
suggesting amendment if
allow it, and I think the Clause ought
to go. It is a trivial matter relatively.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Having
declined to accept the omission of Clause
The Lord Mayor of Belfast
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Cunningham, The Right Honourable
Samuel
The
7, I cannot agree to the Amendment to
leave out Clause 8. I should like to
give some details in reply to the question
asked by the hon. and learned Senator
as to whether, every time a general revaluation is made, a Consequential
Provisions Bill will be introduced.
first general revaluation, coming as it
did, except in the case of Belfast, after
a lapse of more than half a century, was
bound to upset statutes very seriously.
It is very unlikely that future revaluations will have any such drastic effect,
and, although I cannot give an undertaking on the subject, it seems unlikely
that a Consequential Provisions Bill will
be required.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Might I suggest
to the noble Leader of the House that
the Government really did not know what
was before them when they passed the
Revaluation Bill, and that if they had
thought for a moment that it would have
resulted in such a large increase in
valuations they would have thought more
than twice or even three times before
they introduced such a
In
a Measure.
other words, it has cast a far greater
burden on
the
the ratepayers than
Government ever anticipated.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government possibly was bound to pass a
Revaluation Bill owing to our relations
with the Imperial Government.
Mr. LESLIE: I agree that we were
bound to pass such a Measure, but having
done so I do not see why we should pass
away from the consequences, and I do not
think we can justify this alteration.
Owing to the difference between this and
the last Clause I must again divide the
Committee.
Question put, "That the Clause be left
out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 3;
Not Contents, 13.

THE SENATE
Clause accordingly ordered to stand
