CLAUSES 9 (Poundage for payments
into the General Cattle Diseases Fund),
and 10 (Contributions from rates for purposes of certain harbours) ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 11 (Net annual value of
certain industrial hereditaments).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: It may be possibly
remembered that when I spoke on the
Second Reading I alluded to this Clause
and made some remarks as to how it came
within the Title. I want to explain that
what was in my mind was that we were
jumping from £30 to £50, and it seemed
to me that such a jump distinctly dis-
I said I
turbed existing enactments.
would like information as to the object
and effect of the proposed Amendment.
I did not object to the Clause then and I
do not propose to object to it now, but I
should like more information.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will give
the information at a later stage.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 12 (Provision as to rating of
property of Belfast Harbour Commissioners) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 13 (Provisions with respect
to covenants or restrictions in leases).
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie-
At the end of Clause 13, to insert as a
new sub-Clause:-
(6) Sub-section (2) of this section
shall cease to have effect on and after
the first day of April, nineteen hundred
and thirty-eight.
Mr. LESLIE: For a change I have the
pleasure of introducing a constructive
Amendment to the Committee. The fact
is, of course, that this Clause 13, enabling
alterations to be made in instruments of
somewhat ancient date, is a Clause that
is likely to be quite useful, but I cannot
believe that the number of cases in which
it is provided that houses of such a
valuation should be erected can be very
many. The more modern practice is to
provide that houses costing such and such
a sum shall be erected, but I imagine
there must have been cases brought to the
notice of the Government. I do not
object to the Clause itself, but I
think we ought to put in a little
time limit. We provide that when
there is agreement an alteration may
be made without further trouble,
but where the lessor or lessee fail to
agree the matter is to be referred to the
Commissioner of Valuation to settle the
dispute. It seems to me that you cannot
leave that sort of possible difficulty
hanging over people's heads for ever,
and therefore, I think, if the question
is to be considered at all it should be
done within a reasonable time. I therefore suggest, as on the Paper, that the
provisions of that particular sub-clause
should cease to operate after 1st April,
1938. That gives them two years to
settle their differences or to ask somebody else to settle them, if any arise. I
move the new sub-section as it appears
on the Paper.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Although
I have no doubt that most lessors and
lessees will desire to clear up the position
in regard to leases of land where construction of houses of a certain valuation
is stipulated at as early a date as possible
after the first revaluation comes into
force, it may well be that by reason of
the non-development of such ground for
building purposes at an early date the
question of an amendment in the lease
may not arise for quite a number of
years. Although, therefore, the Govern
ment has some sympathy with the proposal of the hon. and learned Senator to
limit the operation of this section to the
1st April, 1938, it is considered wiser
not to introduce any such limitation at
the present time. Moreover, the Incorporated Law Society has pointed out that
the valuation question may arise at any
time in the case of a house destroyed by
fire, where the lease contains a rebuilding
clause and the minimum valuation provision. I therefore regret that I am
unable to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Obviously it is not an
Amendment that should be pressed. If
anybody does complain they can refer to
the Debates and get their answer.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
26 MARCH 1936
CLAUSE 14 (Rates payable by tenants
under housing schemes).
Mr. LESLIE: My Amendment here
is to leave out the Clause which, I believe,
has been in the Bill all the time. The
object of the Clause is to alter, presumably in favour of the local authority,
the terms of the agreements which the
local authorities have entered into with
their tenants. What the effect of the
Clause will be if it is passed I confess is
a little difficult to follow, but the
suggestion is to substitute the amount of
rates produced in the year for a percentage of the poundage rate prevailing
at that time. I have to ask the Government to tell us what will be the effect
of the proposed alteration, and whether
in effect they are giving to the local
authority a power which they have denied
to the private owner. That is the power
perhaps I do not say more than perhaps
-of doing an injustice to their own
tenants. There must be some object.
behind the proposal, and I should like
to be told what it is.
a
In any case, in the event of a dispute
arising as to the amount referred to in
sub-clause (3) I cannot agree that the
proper arbitrator is the Minister of
Home Affairs. If the only point at issue.
was a mere matter of arithmetic I should
not object, but I cannot imagine
that on such a simple matter, if
there were
dispute, it could
be only on a matter of figures.
It is much more likely to have a question
of law or fancied law about it. In that
case the proper court to decide the matter
would, I suggest, as the matter would be
obviously small, be the court of summary
jurisdiction and ought not to be the
Ministry. That can be dealt with at a
later date if the Committee decides that
the Clause ought to stand. Unless the
noble Viscount can give us some definite
information about the purpose of the
Clause and its probable effect on the
tenants I do not think the Committee
ought to allow the Clause to stand.
Accordingly I move to leave out the
Clause.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Clause
has been inserted at the request of the
Belfast Corporation as otherwise it would
have been necessary for them to cancel a
very large number of letting agreements
and issue fresh ones in order to make the
conditions under which the tenants hold
these houses conform to the new state of
affairs which arises on revaluation. This
would have been an inconvenience not
only to the Corporation, but might have
caused alarm amongst the tenants themselves, and the Government thought it
reasonable to meet the suggestion in the
way proposed to be effected in the Bill.
It was made of general application as it
may well be that houses owned by local
authorities in other areas are in the same
position. I cannot, therefore, accept the
Amendment.
I am
The LORD MAYOR OF BELFAST:
I rise to oppose this Amendment.
rather surprised that my hon. and learned
Friend with the knowledge he possesses
of the law should make a statement such
as he has made here.
Mr. LESLIE: What statement?
The LORD MAYOR OF BELFAST:
He turns the whole argument on the law.
I want to point out that it is to save us
from a difficulty that this is proposed. I
can best explain the position by illustrating the facts as they exist. The Belfast
Corporation under the 1923-4 Act built
something like 2,000 houses and let those
houses to tenants. They made an agreement with the tenants that they were to
pay a certain rent and that in case of any
increase in the rates over what existed at
that time-which was roughly, I think,
12/2d-the tenant should pay four-ninths
of that increase. That was the arrangement and it was a just and generous one.
The rates have gone up from 12/2 to 13/2
and of that shilling the tenants each year
have been notified in the month of April
how much they had to pay. They paid
and were perfectly satisfied. If this
Clause is left out you would really have
a state of affairs that I do not know how
you could get over. You would create a
state of uncertainty, and there, again, the
element of law creeps in. The only alternative that I can see is that the Corporation would have to notify those 2,000
tenants-serve notice on them to quit, and
then make new agreements with them including this increase, because it is quite
obvious that if you take that rate of 13/2
-I hope the new rate will be 8/- and
something
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Mr. McMAHON: Eight shillings and
tenpence.
The LORD MAYOR OF BELFAST:
Do you not see what trouble will arise?
Some legal gentleman might say you contracted to let these houses when the rate
was 12/2-and now with the rate at 8/9
we are paying too much. They might
claim a rebate of the difference between
8/9 or whatever it is and 12/2. That
would give rise to no end of trouble. It
would not be to the advantage of the
tenants because to get rid of the legal
difficulty they would have to get notice.
to quit and have to make new agreements
with no end of trouble. In my opinion,
that would be to the disadvantage of the
tenants and they do not want that.
My hon. and learned Friend says it is
not a matter to be left to the Minister of
Home Affairs. If this Clause is left in I
for one can see no difficulty. It removes
all the legal difficulty, because it can be
easily determined on the 1st of April in
each year what is the increase, and fourninths would be the actual amount of increase the tenants would have to pay, the
same as is done at the present time. There
would be nothing to add to it, and if a
dispute did arise-I do not see
how it
referred
could-it would be
to the Minister of Home Affairs, and he
in turn would give his decision in the
matter which, of course, would be final.
Therefore, in the interests of all concerned, and particularly in the interests
of those 2,000 tenants, I say leave the
matter as it is in the Bill, otherwise you
will create trouble and inconvenience. I,
for one, strongly oppose the Amendment,
and I am glad to see that the noble
Leader of the House is not going to agree
to it. I hope the House will support
him.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I certainly agree
with the proposal that this should be
left out, but I would like an assurance
first from the noble Leader of the House
that this Clause would not affect the poor
people whose agreements with the local
authorities are that rent includes rates.
The LORD MAYOR OF BELFAST:
It is to help them.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I am speaking
now of houses let from 1914 to 1920,
where the rent includes rates. It is

suggested now that when the tenants'
new agreement is prepared they are
going to bear the increase in rates.
If so,
I am going to oppose it. The reason is
this. The people living in the small
council houses in the small towns, and
in the Province, are not earning as much
now as they earned in pre-war days, and
therefore they are not in a position to
pay increased rates, which would mean
increased rent. Their earnings at the
present time are not anything like what
they were, and they are certainly not in
a position to have their rents increased
let alone the rates. I suggest that from
the point of view of the people in the
country, particularly the mill workers
who are affected by lack of employment
and are practically unemployed at the
present time. This Clause should not
affect the tenants whose rates are included
in the rent. There is an agreement between the tenant and the local authority
whereby the tenant pays so much rent
and the local authority pays the rates.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
Gentleman's fear is unfounded. The
Clause only applies where there is a
specific agreement that the tenant is to
pay a certain increase.
Mr. LESLIE: The Lord Mayor, I must
say, has rather added to the confusion
which arises from this Clause than allayed
it. He said if the Clause was not inserted
in the Bill it would be necessary for the
Corporation to give notice to some 2,000
householders-notice of eviction, if I
understood him correctly. Do I understand that the terms on which the
Corporation lets a house are such as to
allow of eviction if the rent is paid? Are
the terms such that because Parliament
alters the law the Corporation can come
in and tear up an agreement with its
own tenant?
The LORD MAYOR OF BELFAST:
Perhaps I should make it plain. The
Corporation have entered into an agreement with the tenants. The agreement
provides that in addition to the rent then
fixed-they do not come under the Rent
Restrictions Acts-they are subject to
four-ninths of any rate that may be increased. What this Clause provides for
is only dealing with these houses, that
any increases that may take place in the
rates will also be counted as four-ninths.
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
26 MARCH 1936
It is doing no hardship to the tenant. It
is only carrying out the bargain made.
with the tenants.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry to say I do
not see that. If that were so there would
be no necessity for the Clause. Until we
hear the case for the tenants I do not
think the Committee ought to pass this
Clause.
The LORD MAYOR OF BELFAST:
When the agreement was entered into
the rate stood at 12s 2d, and any increase
beyond that 12s 2d was to be paid as to
four-ninths by the tenant. Now I come
to the point that under this revaluation
the rate instead of being 12s 2d will be
eight and something. Do you not see,
if we are going to carry out the original
agreement, it can be argued-I think the
hon. and learned Senator would be the
first to argue the point-that the difference between 8s and 12s 2d should be in
the nature of a rebate, and, instead of
carrying out the agreement which ought
to be carried out, it might be argued they
should get a lesser rent. It is not in
The Lord Mayor of Belfast
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham,The Right Honourable
Samuel
Davison, Sir Joseph
the
the slightest degree imposing on
tenant. It is only carrying out the
agreement entered into between the
Corporation and the people who rented
the houses.
Mr. LESLIE: I am still unsatisfied,
and I have not had an answer to my
question whether the Corporation claims
the power to evict tenants, and, I may
add, whether all the other local authorities claim the power. If the tenant can
sit there this Clause has the effect of
depriving him of an advantage he otherwise would get. Until we have heard
the case for the tenant we ought not to
consent.
The LORD MAYOR OF BELFAST:
I am afraid I can make it no plainer.
Mr. LESLIE: I must press the amendment.
Question put, "That the Clause be
left out."
The Committee divided: Content, 2;
Not Content, 14.
CONTENT
Leslie, Mr. James G.
McLaughlin, Mr. T.
NOT CONTENT
Dickson, Mr. James H.
Gibson, Dr. William
Glendinning, John C.
Lavery, Mr. T. R.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 15 (Interpretation), 16
(Commencement of Act), and 17 (Short
Title) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Preamble agreed to.
TITLE.
A Bill to make provision for securing
that the first general revaluation of
rateable property in Northern Ireland
shall not disturb the effect of such enactments and instruments as operate by
reference to specified amounts of rateable
value or specified rates in the pound.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the
Title, on page 1, to leave out the words,
"such enactments and instruments as,'
""
McMahon, Mr. J.
Perceval-Maxwell, Capt. John
Robert
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
and to insert instead thereof the words,
"certain enactments and instruments
which". It will be remembered that I
drew attention on the Second Reading to
the fact that a trivial alteration would
apparently be necessary to the Title, and
the alteration I suggest is on the Paper.
The point is that when the Bill entered
another place bearing the same title as
it bears to-day three Clauses, 7, 8, and 9,
appeared in the Bill, and those Clauses
have disappeared. Two of them, I believe,
were taken out as being merely of a
declaratory character. Clause 7 dealt
with the rate of poundage permissible in
the case of parks. There was also the
somewhat debateable question of the levies
in respect of education and unemployment
assistance. An explanation of those
matters was given in another place by
Revaluation Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
the Minister of Finance, and no question
about those arises.
Still, if one has to base the case entirely
on Clause 7 the case is still good, and
I am afraid that the Title, which runs,
"shall not disturb the effect of such enactments and instruments as operate by reference to" and so on, cannot stand. We
are no longer doing anything to prevent
the disturbance of certain enactments and
instruments, and, therefore, I suggest we
should make our Title conform to the
facts and alter it as shown on the Paper.
I beg to move accordingly.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I accept
the Amendment. May I take this opportunity of thanking the hon. and learned
Senator on behalf of the Government and
of this House for the painstaking way
in which he examines proposed legislation
passing through Parliament. He has on
many occasions been able to clarify
obscurities which would have given rise to
difficulties of interpretation. Although
the Government do not always see eye
to eye with the hon. and learned Senator
-they have turned rather a blind eye
to him to-day-they do sincerely appreciate the value of his criticisms.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
Amendment agreed to.
Title, as amended, agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill, as amended,
be reported to the House.
Ordered, That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendment.
Ordered, That the Report of the said
Amendment be received on Monday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Monday." I regret that on Monday
I shall have to move the suspension of
Standing Order No. XXV for the purpose
of taking the remaining stages of the
Revaluation (Consequential Provisions)
Bill. The only alternative to that would
be to meet for five minutes to-morrow,
but I think I am interpreting the
wishes of the House by taking the Bill
on Monday.
On Question, motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twentyfive minutes before Five o'clock
until Monday next.
Increase in Foxes
Increase in Foxes
30 MARCH 1936
THE SENATE.

Monday, 30th March, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Suspension of Standing Order.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That Standing Order No. XXV.
be suspended for this day's sitting for
the purpose of taking the remaining
stages of the Revaluation (Consequential
Provisions) Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: We cannot on the 30th
March object to the proposal, but I think
it is right to say, in order to guard ourselves against accusations in the future,
that it is a little unfortunate that we
should have to take this particular Bill
with which we may have some difficulty
with undue rapidity. I do not object.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Divorce Bills.
Resolved: "That Dr. Gibson be named
of the Joint Standing Committee on
Divorce Bills in place of Mr. Leslie."-
(Mr. Long.)
Question to Minister.
INCREASE IN FOXES,
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether the Government is aware of the
increase in foxes in County Antrim, and
that one trapper killed 129 between May
and July last year in the mountain
districts of the county. I ask this
question, because at the beginning of the
19th century foxes were exterminated in
County Antrim, and for a very long
time there were no foxes there. I understand that in the manor of Glenarm in
47 years there were 1,462 foxes killed,
and they disappeared out of the district
until there was one trapped by Lord
Antrim in his park in 1870, but that fox
proved to be a liberated fox which had
got off. Then about 1910 there were six
foxes liberated, and I believe they were
all accounted for, but foxes have now
commenced to be very numerous. As I
said, there were 129 killed last year by
one man.
In County Down there have been
foxes. Last spring a friend of mine was
walking over the hills and came upon a
shepherd who dug out a fox, and he
found two dead newly-dropped lambs in
the den. I think we may take it that
these foxes do a lot of damage in the
district, to mountain sheep especially.
In favour of the fox it may be stated
that they kill rats and mice. I believe
that is so, but as far as I can learn they
have not affected the rats and mice in
County Antrim. I raise the point to
see if the Ministry of Agriculture can
suggest or do anything. A hundred
years ago the leet court at Glenarm gave
a small reward for the head, and foxes
were wiped out. It is just a question
that something could be done now to get
rid of this pest, which I think is an
expensive item in the country.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to support
the hon. Senator on this very important
question. It is a matter that affects the
farming community very much in some
districts. Only last summer I was informed by a couple or three farmers of
the destruction caused by these foxes
amongst poultry, geese, lambs, ducks
and anything like that. These foxes
prowl about at night, assisted by badgers,
and I think that something should be
done, or the Government might encourage some means of doing away with
this pest that is causing so much destruction amongst farmers to-day. I
saw in the paper yesterday that the
Farmers Union in Coleraine has offered
to subscribe to any organisation brought
into existence for the purpose of
exterminating foxes and badgers throughout the country. I have pleasure in
supporting the hon. Senator.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I think this
is a very useful question the right hon.
Senator has asked. There is no question
about the enormous depredations, not
only amongst poultry, but the little bird.
called the grouse, a very pleasant bird
both to shoot and to eat. They have decimated them in the Sperrin Mountains.
It is quite true in County Antrim one
man alone killed 85 foxes last year. I
Increase in Foxes
Revaluation Bill
THE SENATE
[Colonel Fakenham J
think some notice ought to be taken by
the Ministry of Agriculture. I think
they are the right people to do it. They
can offer a small reward for the masks,
which they could nail up on the walls
as ornaments if they liked. I think it
is a very important question and some
notice ought to be taken of it.
Mr. LESLIE: I also would rather
like to support the right hon. Senator
in asking the question, because the evil
is undoubtedly a spreading one in Co.
Antrim. The 129 foxes referred to in
the question were mostly killed in the
neighbourhood of Ballycastle Mountains
-Knocklayd, and so on. But in my
own potato patch, which lies between
Ballycastle and the Bann, and out
through the Sperrin Mountains we met
three, but two of them will not trouble
the hen roosts again. The third one had
an interview, which I hope he found
unsatisfactory, with somebody whose business it is to watch them, but the most we
can say is it is missing, and believed
killed. At the outskirts of Glary Bog,
which used to be the largest bog in
Ireland, you have foxes, and throughout
the area between the Sperrin Mountains
and Ballycastle Mountains, and unless
something is done to check the pest it
is an evil likely to develop. Lambs have
disappeared in my district. I do not
know so much about hens. If something
could be done to assist the efforts of the
farmer and the game protector I think
it would be a great deal of benefit.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I would like
to remind the House that these foxesvermin we call them in this country-are
not confined to Antrim alone. The question has come from the Antrim side, but
it is within my own knowledge that they
are doing a tremendous lot of damage to
poultry in County Down, and up the
Mourne Mountain district, where it is
impossible to hunt them, the only way
to get rid of them is by shooting.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
like to exclude badgers from this question.
(Hon. Senators: Hear, hear.) They
were
introduced by my hon. Friend
opposite. I keep badgers myself, and I
keep poultry as well, and the damage
done to the poultry by the badgers is
il. I do not say that occasionally badgers
do not attack them, just as a tiger may
become man eating, but I do not think
it is the general practice. The hon.
Gentleman who asked the question stated
that foxes would on occasion eat mice,
rats, beetles, and that sort of thing, but
they certainly prefer poultry. I think
it is the other way with the badger. The
badger will take what is nearest, and if
he cannot get that he will go somewhere
else. I think as a general rule the badger
is comparatively harmless.
was
With regard to the general answer to
both parts of the question, it is in the
affirmative. It is not considered that this
is a matter in which the Government can
advisedly assume responsibility, particularly as such intervention would have the
effect of discouraging voluntary enterprise and
for
initiative,
it
initiative that
voluntary effort and initiative
exterminated foxes in Antrim 100
years ago, and I believe we are not
less clever now than then. From information received by the Ministry of Agriculture, it is clear that much effective
work towards combating the fox menace
is already being done by individual and
combined action on the part of farmers
and others threatened with losses, and of
organisations, such as the Wildfowl, Game
and Fish Protection Society. At the
same time, if any concrete proposal is put
before the Ministry of Agriculture they
are perfectly prepared to consider it.
Revaluation (Consequential Provisions)
Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendment read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) made
inquiries on the Second Reading and
Committee Stage of the Bill whether
Clauses 4 and 5 are within the competence
of the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
There is a prohibition in the Government
of Ireland Act against imposing excise
duties on articles manufactured and produced, such, for example, as a tax on
whisky in bulk; a tax of that nature is
reserved to the Imperial Parliament.
Liquor licence duties are not of this type.
It is true that they fall under the general
denomination of duties of excise, but they
are, in fact, as their name shows, payments not on a commodity, but in respect
Revaluation Bill-
Report Stage
30 MARCH 1936
of a licence to sell a commodity.
Clause
4 amends those duties, but is clearly not
within the ambit of the excepted provisions of Section 21 of the Government of
Ireland Act. In order further to allay
the hon. and learned Senator's fears, I
should like to add that the administration
of this service is carried out by the
Customs and Excise authorities under an
agency agreement arranged between them
and the Ministry of Finance. If, therefore, there had been the slightest suggestion that Clause 4 is ultra vires the
Customs and Excise department, who
were consulted as to its effect, would at
once have raised the point with the
Ministry of Finance.
As regards Clause 5, this refers to
minimum valuations requisite for the
issue of new licences and the same observations apply, but with even greater
force. If it were thought to be ultra
vires then the Section of the Intoxicating
Liquor Act of 1923, which it amends was
also outside the competence of this Parliament.
He also asked for some explanation in
regard to Clause 11. The Rating and
Valuation (Apportionment) Act (Northern Ireland), 1928, provides that where
premises of not more than £30 valuation
are used partly for industrial and partly
for other purposes, no attempt will be
made to apportion for derating purposes
the valuation between the respective uses,
but that the whole premises will be classed
as "industrial."
In Great Britain the figure introduced
into their corresponding Act of 1928 was
£50. The lower Northern Ireland figure
was fixed because it was known that
Northern Ireland valuations were subnormal.
Now that revaluation has come about,
this sub-normality disappears, and on the
best information obtainable the figures
here may be brought into line with those
in Great Britain. If any complaint can
be made, it is perhaps that they are a
shade in favour of the small industrialist.
The Clause appears to be essentially
germane to the title of the Bill.
I beg
to move, "That this Report be now
received."
""
Mr. LESLIE: It is hardly necessary
to thank the noble Viscount for the in-

formation he has given to the House,
but I may perhaps express the opinion
that it is very desirable that the information should be placed on record, which
will now be done.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendment was accordingly
reported and agreed to by the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."
Mr. LESLIE: I proposed, after the
Report stage, to direct the attention of
the House to a small matter which might
be held to require technical amendment.
I think that as the Report stage has been
passed and the Third Reading entered
upon, the point might be taken after the
Third Reading. It was, as your Lordship
will remember, an extremely small point.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 3 Effect of revaluation upon
Rent and Mortgage Interest (Restrictions Acts)-
It is hereby declared that, notwithstanding any alteration in the rateable
value of dwelling-houses effected by the
coming into force of the first revaluation, the Rent and Mortgage Interest
(Restrictions) Acts (Northern Ireland),
1920 to 1932, shall thereafter continue
to apply, subject to the provisions of
those Acts, to dwelling-houses and
mortgages to which the said Acts
applied immediately before the said
revaluation came into force.
Mr. LESLIE: The small amendment
I referred to was, it will be remembered,
in Clause 3 of the Bill, in the first line
on page 4. Owing to the fact that since
the Bill entered this House the Rent and
Mortgage Interest (Restrictions) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1936, has become
law, the question arose whether it was
not desirable to alter "1932" in that
line to" 1936." It was rather doubtful,
I think, whether that required a formal
amendment or whether it might be left
to the operation of the printer, or
whether it made any difference whatever
whether we altered it or did not.
As a
matter of convenience the date "1932"
ought to be "1936." In order that
there may be no doubt that this Bill,
Adjournment--
Sittings of the House
THE SENATE
[Mr. Lesliej
which will be passed later than the Rent
and Mortgage Interest (Restrictions) Act,
1936, applies to that Act. I think it
is just possible that the question might
arise, or at all events that some inconvenience might be caused, and, therefore,
I suggest that "1932" should be altered
to "1936." Whether you consider that
can be done without a formal amendment
is another question. In case it could not
be properly done I will move formally
in Clause 3, line 1 on page 4, to leave
out "1932," and to insert instead thereof
"1936."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I accept
that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

I
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
ing, "That this Bill do now pass,

On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House

Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving, "That this House do now adjourn
until Wednesday next," I have got
rather an unpleasant communication to
make. We have a very large mass of
material coming which has to be digested
with an extraordinary rapidity, only to
be attained by those who have as good a
constitution as have hon. Senators.
are
I should like to know the wishes of
hon. Members with regard to the
passage of the Unemployment Insurance
(Agriculture) Bill through this House.
First, may I explain that, under the
terms of the Bill, contributions
payable in respect of agricultural workers
as from Monday, 4th May. This means
that arrangements must be made for
more than 30,000 persons to receive their
unemployment books before Saturday,
2nd May. The books will be issued as
from Monday, 27th April, at Employment Exchanges, and at a number of
other centres throughout Northern Ireland, and it will be necessary to advertise
fairly widely in the local Press in the
preceding week, SO that intending
applicants for books may know what they
should do, and may be informed as to the
nearest centre at which they may make
application.
The Ministry of Labour will not, of
course, be able to make any Press
announcements until the Bill becomes
law, and for this reason it is desired
that Royal Assent be obtained by not
later than Tuesday, 21st April. The
Bill will leave another place on Wednesday next, and, if Sessions of both Houses
are to be avoided between Easter and
20th April, it must have its Third
Reading in this House on Tuesday, 7th
April. Copies of the Bill will reach hon.
Members on Thursday, 2nd April, and
I would like to know if they are prepared
to take First and Second Readings on
Friday, 3rd April, Committee stage on
Monday, 6th April, should there be any
amendments to the Bill, and report and
Third Reading on Tuesday, 7th April.
I would like to say that, quite apart
from anything this House may do, there
may be amendments made to the Imperial
Bill, as the Measure is passing through the
House of Lords, and any such amendments would require to be introduced in
the Bill here. I am afraid our Bill will
be substantially the same as the Imperial
Measure, except, perhaps, for a few local
variations. The rates of benefit are the
same, so that it does not appear that the
Senate, without throwing out the whole
Bill, will be able to do much in the way
of amendment, as I think hon. Members
will realise. The alternative to my proposal would be to sit on Easter Tuesday,
which would be the latest date of sitting.
It is not for me to express an opinion
either way, but I should imagine that
sitting in Easter week would be rather
less popular than my other proposal.
Mr. LESLIE: I suppose it is necessary
to try to elicit an expression of opinion.
I am bound to say that what the noble
Viscount has just said has failed to convince me that there is, so to speak, such
a desperate hurry about this Bill.
Bill he speaks of he admits himself has
not yet passed through the Imperial
The
Adjournment-
Sittings of the House
30 MARCH 1936
Parliament, nor has it passed another
place here, and I do not think that this
House ought to be asked to rush it in
any way whatever. For all we know to
the contrary, the Bill may be a very excellent Bill. The principle has, of course,
been generally accepted, but the detail,
and the question of the application of the
principle to Northern Ireland opens a
very wide question, indeed. I do not
think we ought in any way to hurry the
Bill, and we ought not to expedite any of
its stages. We ought to have ample time
to consider it, and I do not see how we
are going to have ample time in three
weeks.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Taking into consideration the fact that this
Bill will be almost exactly the same as the
Bill going through the Imperial Parliament, and that, therefore, it is not likely
to be amended, I think we ought to fall
in with the Government's suggestion. The
Ministry of Labour naturally wish to have
as much time as possible in which to
notify agricultural labourers of the provisions of the Bill and to issue the necessary books, and I think we ought to
facilitate them in whatever way we can.
I would
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
like to say that I personally, as Leader
of the House, do not like the idea of
rushing ordinary Bills through the House.
There is sometimes pressure brought to
bear on any Leader of the House-the
same thing happens in the House of
Lords to rush Government legislation.
through, and, as far as I have been able,
I have always resisted that sort of pressure. However, I do not think this
applies so much to this Bill, once its principle is accepted. It is really the same
Measure as that passed by the Imperial
House of Commons, and I do not think
that under the circumstances the Senate
will be ill-advised to pass it in the time I
have indicated. However, I thought it
would be as well to ascertain the wishes
of hon. Members, and, if necessary, I
will divide the House on the proposal.
Mr. LESLIE: The Question before us
at the moment is "That this House do
now adjourn."
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I would
like to know how we can divide the House
on the question of whether the Bill in
question shall be taken in a week, or
postponed until later.
Mr. SPEAKER: There would have to
be a separate Motion.
Mr. LESLIE: If the noble Viscount
would move that the First and Second
Readings of this Bill be taken together
on such and such a date, that would be a
convenient method of ascertaining the
opinion of the House.
Mr. SPEAKER: It can be done that
way, or it can be done by the noble
Leader moving an amendment to the
Motion, "That this House do now
adjourn."
Mr. LESLIE: I must move the direct
contrary, "That this House do not now
adjourn. Then, of course, it will be
open for a new amendment, "That the
House do adjourn," to be moved in about
two minutes.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I endorse all
that has been said by the noble Leader
of the House. I think this Bill should
be facilitated in every way. As the noble
Leader has pointed out, it is not likely
that this House will amend a Bill that is
the same as the Imperial Measure.
Mr. SPEAKER: I shall now put the
Question, "That the House do now
adjourn until Wednesday next." We can
divide on that.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
minutes before Four o'clock
until Wednesday next.

Wednesday, 1st April, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
Statement of Guarantees given by the

Act (Northern

Transport Act (Northern Ireland).
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons agree to the Amendments made by the Joint Committee to
Bradshaw's Divorce Bill without Amendment; they agree to the Pier and Harbour Order (Larne Harbour) Confirmation Bill as reported from the Joint
Committee without Amendment; they
agree to the Amendment made by the
Senate to the Local Government (Finance)
Bill, and to the Amendments made by
the Senate to the Revaluation (Consequential Provisions) Bill without Amendment; they have passed the Canals and
Inland Navigation Bill and the Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture) Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
Canals and Inland Navigation Bill.

a First time."-(Mr. Long).
Ordered, That the Bill be read a
Second time on Friday next.
Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture)
Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long).

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn

Mr. LESLIE: With regard to
Friday's sitting, and referring back for
a moment to the conversation on the
Motion for the Adjournment on Monday, we have now received the Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture) Bill,
to which reference was then made, and
I understand we shall be taking the

the Bill is in our hands to-morrow morning. I lay some emphasis on that matter
because my Order Paper for to-day had
not reached me when I left home at 11-30
this morning, so that I am a little afraid
as to what might happen to the Bill.
may say that if my Order Paper had

not have been here this afternoon. I
hope we may take it that the Bill will
be sent off in such time as will enable
it to be in the hands of country Members by the first post to-morrow morning.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I hope

PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Bradshaw's Divorce Bill.
The Amendment made by the Joint
Committee to the said Bill was considered
and agreed to.
Pier and Harbour Order (Larne Harbour) Confirmation Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.

Unemployment Insurance (Increase of
Benefit in Respect of Dependent
Children) Order (Northern
Ireland), 1935.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move :
That the Order in Council made by
the Governor of Northern Ireland on
the 29th October, 1935, under Section
18 of the Unemployment Act (Northern Ireland), 1934, for increasing the
amount of the unemployment benefit
payable in respect of dependent
children, be hereby approved.
Under the terms of Section 18 of the
Unemployment Insurance Act (Northern
Ireland), 1934, His Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland
may apply to
Northern Ireland with any necessary
adaptations, Orders made by the Minister of Labour in Great Britain amending
the Unemployment Insurance Code there
on the recommendation of the Unemployment Insurance Statutory Committee
with the object of securing the solvency
of the Unemployment Fund or of disposing of a surplus.
On 25th October, 1935, the Minister
of Labour in Great Britain made an
Order increasing the weekly rate of unemployment benefit in respect of a
dependent child from 2s to 3s, with
effect from 31st October, 1935.
Order for which approval is now sought
made the increase payable in Northern
Ireland as from the same date.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The
Canals and Inland Navigation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
propose to occupy the time of the House
at any length with this Measure, which
is of a simple nature and of which the
effect will be to relieve canal undertakings of certain burdens.
The Bill will enable these undertakings to procure, by means of Provisional
Orders, instead of by the cumbrous and
expensive method of Private Bill legislation, the powers necessary for the
execution of works of improvement that
must be carried out from time to time.
This procedure will not impair the control of Parliament, of which the approval
must be obtained in a Confirmation Bill.
Unemployment Insurance Bill 232
Canals Bill
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Other interests will be safeguarded by
the obligation placed upon applicants for
a Provisional Order to give public notice
of their application and the works proposed to be effected. An even simpler
procedure is provided for an application
for the fixing of tolls and charges, and
this opportunity has been taken to repeal
the Canals (Continuance of Charging
Powers) Act (Northern Ireland), 1925,
which has been kept operative in the past
by the Expiring Laws Continuance Act.
Two other matters are dealt with in the
Bill. Clause 4 proposes to treat as
county-at-large charge the special rate
now levied on specified areas to meet the
loss in working of the Upper Bann navigation. The three counties concerned-
Armagh, Down and Tyrone-have, I
understand, approved of this course.
a
Under Clause 5 the Lagan Navigation
will be relieved of the annual rent charge
of £300 payable originally to the Government, and, since the passing of the
Derating Act, 1929, into the Freight
Rebates Fund. This Fund will be wound
up on 1st January next and, accordingly,
the Clause provides that the rent charge
be abolished simultaneously. This
charge has pressed hard upon the undertaking, which has had a long and
honourable career of service to the community. It may be of interest to hon.
Members that the Lagan Canal was constructed as long ago as 1753, when its
sponsors could certainly have had no
foreboding of the variety and intensity
of the rivalry with which such undertakings would be faced in time to come.
I beg to move, "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Monday next.
Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The purpose of this Bill is to extend to some
33,000 farm workers insurance cover
against unemployment on similar lines
to that which already exists in respect
of industrial workers. I scarcely need
to elaborate upon the desirability of
mitigating the burden of unemployment
in the case of the farm labourer.
Unemployment among farm labourers
is considerable at certain times of the
year and the more secure position of
the industrial worker has been a powerful agency in stimulating the drift of
our people to the towns-a movement
which is not only detrimental to agriculture, the most important of our
industries, but clearly also to the townbred working-class population.
The Bill provides that workers in agriculture, horticulture and forestry, whose
employment is under a contract of service to their employers shall be insured
against unemployment. To this, however, there are two important exceptions
named in the First Schedule to the Bill,
namely, (1) persons who stand in certain
degrees of relationship to the employer,
and (2) private gardeners. As regards
the latter class, it will be observed that
one of the provisions of the Fourth
Schedule to the Bill empowers any class
of employment as a private gardener to
be brought within the scope of the Agricultural Insurance Scheme by Order in
Council, if a Statutory Order for this
purpose is made in Great Britain. The
question of including such classes in the
scheme is to be further considered by
the Unemployment Insurance Statutory
Committee, on which Northern Ireland
is represented.
Contributions under the scheme are
payable as from 4th May next. Employers and employed will pay equal
contributions at the rate of 44d for a
man 21 years of age or over; 4d for a
woman in the same age group; 4d for
a young man between 18 and 21; 3d
for a young woman between those ages;
2d for boys between 16 and 18; 1d for
girls between those ages; 1d for boys
under 16, and 1d for girls under 16. As
is the case under the existing scheme the
Government will contribute at a rate
equal to half the total contributions of
employers and employed.
Benefits under the scheme commence
from 5th November, 1936.
The proposed weekly rates of benefit are as
follows:-14s for men over 21; 12s 6d
for women over 21; 10s 6d for young
men between 18 and 21; 9s 6d for young
women between 18 and 21; 6s for boys
between 17 and 18; 5s for girls between
233 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

3 APRIL 1936
Second Reading
17 and 18; 4s for boys between 16 and
17, and 3s 6d for girls between those
ages. The weekly allowance for an
adult dependant is 7s and for a
dependant child 3s, but no claimant
can receive more than 30s a week in
all.
The total income from the scheme will,
it is estimated, amount to £69,000 in
a full year, and the expenditure is estimated at £108,500 made up of £100,000
benefits and £8,500 administrative costs.
It has been arranged that the scheme.
shall be brought within the scope of the
Reinsurance Agreement between Northern Ireland and Great Britain which was
recently before the House, and the
difference between income and expenditure will accordingly be made good in
the first instance by our Exchequer to
which a payment will subsequently be
made from the British Exchequer based
upon the principle of equality of burden
and assessed in the same way as the
comparable payment in respect of the
existing scheme.
There are four Clauses in the Bill
which perhaps call for special mention.
Clause 5 of the Bill provides that the
duration of benefit under the scheme
shall bear a fixed relation to the number of contributions standing to the
applicant's credit at the beginning of his
benefit year.
Benefit will be at the rate
af 12 days' benefit for the first 10 contributions so standing to the applicant's
credit, and 3 days' benefit for every
contribution in addition to ten.
Clause 9 of the Bill applies to the
special conditions affecting the halfyearly and yearly hiring system. It is
reasonable to assume that under this
system the risk of unemployment is considerably smaller than where employment is on a weekly basis. The Clause,
therefore, provides that regulations may
be made whereby a rebate of contributions shall be payable at the same rates
as in Great Britain, that is 25 per cent.
in the case of yearly hirings, and 12
per cent. in the case of half-yearly
hirings.
The payment of such rebates
will be conditional upon notice of the
hiring being given within 28 days of its
commencement, upon its continuance for
the full period agreed, and upon application being made within a prescribed
period after the termination of the employment.
Clause 10 empowers the Ministry of
Labour to make regulations governing
the position of persons employed at times
in agriculture, and at other times in
occupations insured under the general
scheme. Any such regulations, however,
must allow an applicant to draw to the
extent of his full rights under each
scheme, provided that he does not benefit
simultaneously under both. Here I
should perhaps make it clear that under
the terms of the Bill the Agricultural
Insurance Scheme will be separate from
the existing general insurance scheme,
inasmuch as contributions paid in respect
of agricultural employment will not be
valid for the purposes of a claim to
benefit under the general scheme and the
other way about.
The effect of Clause 13 is to exclude
from the scheme labourers who ordinarily visit the United Kingdom merely
to engage in harvest work and afterwards return to their homes. Such
persons will not be required to pay contributions but their employers will have
to pay the employer's contribution. My
right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Labour, has been kind enough to come
into this House to answer any questions
that
may arise during the debate. I
beg to move, "That the Bill be now read
a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: This is a most important and a most far-reaching Bill, which,
if this House accepts, may work, I am
much afraid, something in the nature of
a revolution amongst our quiet country
farms, particularly those in the remoter
districts. I cannot help thinking that
the Bill and the possible effects of it have
been taken somewhat light-heartedly,
perhaps, by the farmers, but certainly
I shall enthroughout the Province.
deavour to examine the Bill this afternoon, and in doing so I must make some
claim
the forbearance of the House.
upon
I must ask that forbearance partly
because my observations, I am afraid,
will occupy some little time, and partly
because, owing to the rather great
rapidity with which we have had to
approach this Bill, I have not had time.
to reduce my notes to normal form, and
I must, therefore, read a little more than
usual.
I am going to move that the word
"now" be left out of the Motion of the
235 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
noble Viscount, that is, I am asking the
House not to pass this Bill at this time.
I am taking that course, not because I
am in any way opposed to what one
might describe as the general principle,
that general principle being that the
agricultural worker has the same right
to protection against hardship resulting from unemployment as has his fellow.
citizen who works in a factory. My view
is that as far as that goes there should be
equality of treatment, and I would,
therefore welcome any scheme which
appeared to me reasonably likely to provide for equality in the way of affording necessary protection to a deserving
class, and a scheme which is
calculated to increase the amenities and
the general prosperity of the countryside.
also
The problem has to be examined under
various aspects. We ought to be satisfied before we pass the Bill on four main
grounds. The first of those grounds is
equity, the equity of admitting agricultural labourers to membership, we will
say, of a scheme for assisted and at the
same time contributory insurance.
Secondly, I think we must inquire as to
whether such a scheme is, in fact,
necessary, by which I mean whether
there is sufficient unemployment amongst
the labourers whose lifework is agriculture-whether there is sufficient unemployment in that
that class to call for
Parliamentary action. Then, if we have
decided that action ought to be taken by
Parliament, we must consider whether
the Bill before us is on the right lines
and whether it seems to us reasonably
likely to effect its object, and to do so
without undue difficulty and, above all,
without friction.
Then again, we come
to the last of my four points, and
that is whether there is any overpowering necessity to bring such a Bill into
operation on the 4th of May. The 4th of
May is just under five weeks from the
present time. In other words, we must,
I think, inquire into the equity, necessity,
propriety and urgency.
to
As to equity, I think we are all agreed.
My own article of belief in that matter
is that the farm worker has just as much
right perhaps rather more right
State assistance, perhaps rather less right
to assistance from his employer-as has
the factory worker. The question of
contribution is a matter common to both.
In any case, the governing consideration,
obviously, must be that any scheme of
contributory insurance is infinitely better
than any scheme of poor-law relief.
That disposes of the question of equity.
But the other parts of the problem seem
to be more difficult and more
to me
perplexing.
The next one I suggest to take is the
question of necessity. On the question.
of necessity it is obvious that there is
need of authoritative figures, not vague
generalities. The noble Viscount spoke
this afternoon of a number somewhere in
the neighbourhood of 33,000. I do not
know whence that information was
obtained, but I do know that our last
census was 1926, that is ten years old.
In Great Britain the last census was in
1931, and apparently from the note prefixed to the Bill now before the House
of Lords in England, some difficulty
seems to have been experienced in obtaining information as to the figures applicable there. Figures were given, and I
will not quote them to the House. These
figures were given as assumed figures.
They made no pretence to definite
accuracy. The figures in Great Britain
were persons to be brought under the Act,
750,000, of which one-fifteenth, or 5,000,
were females. The noble Viscount has
not given the information of the proportion of men and women.
to
The rate of unemployment in Great
Britain is assumed to be 7 per cent. We
have not yet heard the rate of unemployment the Government suggests as unfortunately likely exist in this
country. No doubt that will be one of
the many points on which our Friend,
the right hon. the Minister of Labour,
will enlighten us later on. The English
Bill also assumes that the employees concerned under long hirings, that is a year
or six months system, will not exceed 15
per cent.
I do not know whether we
have any figures here, but as we all know,
the practice of hiring for the half-year or
year is
common in the North of
very
Ireland and it is not at all unusual in
Scotland. Whether Scotland or the
North of Ireland has the greater percentage we have no information nor,
perhaps, is it particularly important.
The income for Great Britain for the six
months is estimated at £900,000, and the
cost of administration £100,000. As I
237 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 3 APRIL 1936

small. And we know that on the small
farm little or no extraneous labour is
likely to be employed.
say, I should like the Government to give
us at all events some approximation to
the figures here, and particularly I should
like information as to a certain proportion I am about to mention.
I cannot myself offer any figures of
any great value to the House. The
latest I have are from the Ulster Year
Book of 1932, page 45. I believe these
figures are of some authority. I think
they were definitely based on the census
of 1926. I must give the House some
of these figures, because they have some
bearing on that proportion of which I
spoke and shall speak again, and also
on the first Schedule of the Bill, the
Schedule relating to persons excepted
from unemployment insurance. The
figures given in 1932 were:-Persons in
charge of cattle 1,353, including 166
females; persons in charge of horses
1,468, including 1 female; not otherwise.
distinguished 31,975, including 612
females; gardeners and their labourersthe House might be interested to see that
gardeners were lumped with the agricultural labourers for the purpose of that
return-1,028, including 31 females;
others-I do not know what that means
-767, including 104 females, giving a
grand total of 36,591 including 914 women.
That figure is rather above the 33,000
mentioned by the noble Viscount, and,
therefore, that confirms my view that
since 1932 the numbers of the agricultural workers have distinctly declined.
If that be so, that gives rise to another
line of thought. Indeed the noble
Viscount referred to it.
From the same book I get other
interesting information. The number of
farmers is given as 68,156, including
8,646 women. The number of relatives
of farmers assisting them on the farm is
given as 42,767. This number of
relatives assisting them on the farm is
very considerably greater than the
number of agricultural labourers.
Whether that figure has been reduced or
increased during the last few years
I
have no information. Again, finds
that there are said to be just over
100,000 holdings, and just over 3,000 are
in excess of 100 acres, just over 11,000
are between 50 and 100 acres.
compare that figure with the 68,000
farmers, we get confirmation of the fact
which we all know, that the farm in
Northern Ireland is on the average very
one
If we
I shall submit that it is on the 100acres-and-upwards farm that the bulk of
the agricultural labourers likely to be
affected by this scheme are to be found.
Of that class of holding, the holding
above 100 acres, I am going to suggest
that a large proportion consists of what
are called demesnes, and I must ask the
House what proportion of workers in
demesnes stand in any risk of unemployment. I think most people who have
knowledge of demesnes would tell the
House that there are people employed in
these demesnes who have been there 20,
30 or even 40 years and have never been
out of work, except through sickness,
during that period.
Those long service men, of course, go
to swell that proportion of which I spoke
just now, and that is the proportion of
what, if I may borrow a word rather
from the factory vocabulary than the
agricultural vocabulary, I shall describe
as the key men of those labourers, the list
of which I have just read-nearly 3,000
-the horsemen and cattlemen are distinctly key men, but they do not exhaust
the list.
There was a very sympathetic article
in the London "Times" of 24th February,
dealing with this general question of
agricultural insurance, and I will quote
from it, if I may:
Taking the country as a whole, it is
probable that at present 75 per cent.
of the men employed in agriculture
are regular workers, indispensable to
the running of the farms. These are
the cowmen, carters, pig men and
poultry men, whose services are wanted
as much in winter as in summer. They
are not subject to the casualisation of
which a good deal was heard in the
House of Commons during the debate
on the Bill. Assuming that this
estimate is correct, 75 per cent. of the
agricultural workers in the country,
the men who are never likely to lack
employment, will be required to contribute weekly to the cost of benefits for
the other 25 per cent. who are, in the
main, less skilled and, therefore, more
subject to unemployment.
The article goes on to say, relating to the
rate of contribution, that 4d a week out
of 40s or thereabouts, is not likely to
239 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
cause a serious drain on the resources of
the employees. The article deals alsobut I need not read it—with that attrition
among the country workers, of which
something has been said, both this afternoon and, I believe, in another place.
I am afraid I have to say that I do
not believe that unemployment insurance will have any appreciable effect as
regards the drain of men from the country
to the town. That particular subject
does not belong to my present argument,
and, therefore, I will leave it for a few
moments until later, but I must ask the
House to ponder very seriously whether
any plea of necessity can be made out for
the taking of this scheme. Is there really
enough unemployment in agriculture.
amongst the people whose lifework agriculture is, to justify a scheme which
necessarily imposes a burden on those
who are not likely to be affected?
I ought, I think, before I sit down,
to remind the House that the Parliament
of the United
United Kingdom, to which
Northern Ireland sends eleven representatives of the same way of thinking as
the majority of us here, and eleven representatives to support Mr. Baldwin's
Government that Parliament can fairly
claim to have something in the nature of
a mandate from the electors of the
United Kingdom for a Bill dealing with
unemployment insurance for agricultural
workers, and it can also claim that mandate as of some recent date.
I will now leave the question of
necessity and go on to the propriety of
the Bill. I have to say that to my mind
this Bill is not a Bill appropriate to the
conditions which exist in Northern
Ireland where we have not got those large
farms which exist in Lincolnshire and
other parts of Great Britain. There are
scarcely any large farms. The average
size is very small indeed.
As regards the Bill itself, one
is inclined to criticise first of all
the general character and form and
structure of the Measure before us. I.
am going to describe the Bill as a graft—
a graft not as in the case of the English
Bill, which is now in their Parliament,
not a graft on a consolidated Act of
Parliament, but a graft on an unconsolidated code. That is the position, I
think, fairly described. I am not going
to suggest for a moment that the grafting has not been performed in the most
husbandlike manner possible. I have
not had sufficient time to satisfy myself
on that.
The fact remains it is a graft, and the
result of this grafting on an unconsolidated code is to my mind a tessellated
pavement of legislation by reference
which the plain man- and the people
for whom the Bill is intended, the
farmer and his employee, are plain men
-unskilled in tracing a path through
the mazes of the cumulative legislation of
fifteen years past-cannot hope to find
his way and understand the provisions
and enactments referred to in the Bill.
Even Members of this House, with the
assistance of that admirable White Paper
circulated with the Bill and who have had
opportunities of studying and following
unemployment insurance
insurance during the
fifteen years I have spoken of, may well
be pardoned if they lose their way in
trying to follow the subject.
We ought, I suggest, to have had a
Bill not of this sort, but a Bill complete
in itself and distinct from the main industrial code-a Bill so drawn that if it
were necessary to refer to any detail or
to any Sections of Acts relating to the
industrial code those Sections should
have been set out in the Schedule to the
Bill. I know it would be a Bill of great
length, but it would have resulted in a
Bill which the ordinary person could
understand. The present Bill is not one
that the ordinary person can possibly
understand, and I do not think that
is a right thing.
Passing from that. To my mind the
Bill reproduces and extends certain
vicious provisions of that unemployment
code which I myself would wish to see
either abated or abolished. They are provisions which I do not like, and I do not
see any particular necessity for extending the scope of these provisions from the
industrial code to a new Bill relating to
agricultural workers. The provisions to
which I have myself always had an objection
are first the excessive power of legislating
by regulation given to the bureaucracy,
in this particular case the Ministry of
Labour. A very great deal of this Bill,
if this House helps to enact it, will be
worked out by means of regulation and
not by Act of Parliament. I do not think
that is right. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) This Parliament at all events is
241 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

3 APRIL 1936
Second Reading
not so over-worked that we could not do
a little more of that work ourselves.
non-
Then there is what I always thought
was the comparative savagery of the
method by which the defaulting employer
is attacked in the matter of
stamping of cards. The feeling is-and
it applies to health as well as industrial
assurance that the general provisions of
unfair to employers
these Acts were
when it came to the matter of penalties.
Particularly do I dread the extension of
that system to the agriculturist, because
to some extent the amount of the stamp
to be affixed to the card under the Bill
will depend on the age of the employee.
I do not know how many people concerned with the preparation of this Bill
have also been concerned, let us say, at
census time, in endeavouring to get
from the employees a true statement of
their age.
The ordinary countryman.
does not know when or where he was
born, and if anything goes wrong
between employer and employee in nine
cases out of ten both parties are equally
guilty and it is not fair to put all the
burden on the employer. I rather object
to extending that system.
I object to another thing, that is the
settlement of disputes that may arise by
the Ministry and not by open court. I
lay a great deal of stress on that. I
am quite satisfied that every care is taken
by the Ministry to do justice in these
matters. I do not say that justice is
not done. I would go further and say
that if the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Labour, has anything to do
with it justice is done, but all the same
public confidence can never be the same
in a tribunal presided over by some
selected judge sitting behind closed doors
as in a trial by a court unbiassed in
carrying out matters of that kind.
Passing from that to other imperfections that I think I observed in the
Bill. Some of them are original, and
others acquired by the method adopted.
I refer to the question of contributions
and benefits. As to contributions, I am
not going to suggest that 4d a week
will break the employer. It will make
very little difference to him. If he employs ten men the whole year round it
will not cost him £10. That would not
hurt him much. As a matter of fact
the Ulster farmer is fairly wily where
pounds, shillings and pence are concerned. He can, I think, so apply this
Bill as to make money. He will be
able to get rather cheaper labour in order
that stamps may be acquired and be able
to play the long day against the short
day and keep his labour bill under co
trol in that manner. I do not think
that the farmer being generally a good
fellow and willing enough to help his
labourer will object to 44d. He will,
conhowever, object very much to the proportion of that 4d which will go to
the cost of administration.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): May I ask Senator Leslie to
repeat that? I did not catch where the
objection would be from.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry I dropped
my voice. The objection is likely to be
to the cost of administration, that is the
proportion of the 4d or cost of stamp
which shall go to the cost of administration. The figures which I gave to the
House just now in England were
£900,000 and £100,000 for administration, or one-ninth. The noble Viscount
did mention, I think, that the expenses
here would be about £8,500. I suppose
that will be practically the same proportion. At all events, if it is one-ninth
it is a very high rate. He will object to
that, I think. Then, again, he is not
going to object to the 4d, but is the
contribution going to stay at 4d, or
rise? As far as I could ascertain rather
hurriedly the contributions under the
1911 Act-the first of the Insurance
Acts-began at 5d for health insurance
and 2d for unemployment. How do
these rates stand to-day? It will save
the right hon. Gentleman telling me,
if I say that I know the benefits have
been entirely transformed.
The original health tax of 51d as
applied to Ireland did not include medical benefit. I cannot charge my memory
as to what happened in the case of unemployment. I admit, of course, that
benefits have increased enormously, but
so have the insurance premiums. Does
anybody really think that on the English
figures and on the fact that seems to be
admitted that Northern Ireland is going
to be £40,000 to the bad every year,
that the amount will remain at 4d?
In considering that I hope the House
will not forget there is a reminder by
means of an addition which was mad.

in another place to the Fourth Schedule

understand it correctly, and I know my
right hon. Friend will correct me if I

that if the Statutory Committee which

agriculturist-if

recommend, owing

or an anticipated deficiency in the
