an
I hope I am not misleading the House.
I would ask a correction again if I am
wrong. As I understand it the position
is that in England the same condition
prevails. The Statutory
Statutory Committee.
recommend an increase and an Order in
Council is made, but it has to be laid
before the Parliament of the United
Kingdom, and until it is confirmed by
that Parliament the Order does not take
effect. Here the position is rather
different. The Order is made and is then
laid before Parliament and it comes into
operation unless this Parliament objects.
There is rather a strong difference, and,
therefore, I venture to doubt very much
whether this 44d will remain a constant
factor.
Again, and once more I have to ask for
correction if I am mistaken, so far as I
understand the Bill, it implies unjustly,
even spitefully, that in the case of an
agricultural labourer who has attained
the age of 65 the employer's contribution is to continue although the man
who has attained the age of 65 is no
longer eligible for benefit. That is not
a provision which I feel ought to be
enacted. It is in favour of the Bill, as
has been said, that the contribution of
the employer and the employee are on a
fifty-fifty basis. We have dealt with the
4d as regards the employer. What
about the employee? He has already
to pay 10d a week for health and
pensions. I think I am correct in saying that an additional penny was added
quite recently, whether it was 1d from
the employer and the employee or 2d
from one I do not remember. At all
events the man has to pay 10d, and the
cash wages generally ruling throughout
Northern Ireland are nearer 20s on the
average than they are to the 40s which
was referred to in the article in the
Times." Fourpence
Fourpence halfpenny may
not be much out of 40s but it is a good
deal out of 20s, especially if a man has
a wife and three children for which, if
he is unemployed, he is supposed to get
30s, whereas if he is in full work he only
gets 20s.
""
Mr. ANDREWS: May I ask my hon.
and learned Friend if he is assuming
that the 4d is too much, because, of
course, if the contribution was reduced
the benefit would also be reduced?
Mr. LESLIE: For the moment I am
not dealing with the benefit. There are
two fourpence halfpennies. I said I did
not think the employer minds his 44d,
but I am suggesting the employee will
feel the 41d rather a burden on him. if,
as the right hon. Gentleman suggests,
that 4d were reduced it would seem to
follow that the benefits must be reduced
also, that is, that the payment during
idleness would be brought into closer
relation to the payment received in
return for services rendered. That
would undoubtedly be the case.
But as regards both employer and
employee I must say that I should have
preferred an Act on a voluntary basis.
I have never heard any satisfactory
answer to the suggestion that in a democratic country a man ought to be allowed
some choice as to whether he will come
into an insurance scheme or stay outside,
the same sort of choice an ordinary man
has when he decides whether he will insure his own life, his house against fire,
and so on. However, I do not think
there is much use putting that forward
this afternoon, but I may mention there
is some suggestion in this Bill in favour
of a voluntary decision. I am referring
for the moment to Clause 11, sub-clause
245 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
3 APRIL 1936

Second Reading
(1) which will be found at the bottom of
page 10. That sub-clause points
definitely to the decision of certain questions being left to choice. If the employer
agrees this may happen and if the
employee agrees that may happen, a clear
reference to the possibility of choice.
I am trying to avoid points of detail,
but as
we are on Clause 11 there is a
point I should like to mention. I cannot
refrain from asking what is to be done
under sub-clause (1), of which I have
just spoken, and sub-clause (3) on the
if the two parties do not
next page,
agree, and if the man was in fact
employed Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday in industrial work and Thursday,
Friday, and Saturday in agricultural
work. It seems to me there is something
in the nature of a deadlock there. I
believe the point was taken in another
place, but if so, the answer escaped me.
I do not know if there is any juggling
to be done on Sunday on an occasion of
that sort, but if it is it seems to me
some little alteration had better be made
later on in the Committee Stage, if there
is a Committee Stage. I do not detect
any similar provision in the United
Kingdom Bill.
Now I want to say a word or two
about the scale of benefits. I think,
perhaps, the House will agree with me if
I suggest the scale of benefits has already
been discussed almost ad nauseam. I do not
propose to say much about it myself, nor
do I wish to associate myself with any
particular arguments that were used
either pro or con in that regard, but I
must say I do think, having regard to
the scale of wages generally prevailing in
Northern Ireland, it would have been
better to adopt a lower standard. I want
to guard myself against the possible suggestion that I am imagining that a man
is going to get a pension for life, or for
300 days in a year, merely because once
upon a time he did a week's work.
Suggestions almost as fantastic as that
have been made, but with those I have
nothing to do.
I do think, however, the scale of
benefit is unduly high. I do not find anything in the Reinsurance Agreement
which would prevent our adopting a
lower scale, both as regards benefits and
contributions, if we decide to do so. In
fact, paragraph 7, if I remember rightly,
of the Reinsurance Agreement seems
an
rather to point in the direction that we
may see fit to do something of that sort.
Moreover, I am afraid, conscientiously
afraid, and in grave doubt and difficulty,
as to whether the introduction of these
benefits will not prejudically affect the
morale of the countryside. It cannot, I
think, be denied that even industrial
benefit has been anything but
unmixed blessing. I am very much inclined to think it has led to a multiplication of the corner boy, and that is a
consummation not to be desired. If I
remember rightly my hon. Friend on my
left voiced somewhat similar opinions in
this House not so very long ago, and I
am also afraid as regards these benefits
that the bulk of the money will get into
the wrong hands.
By that I mean that it is not the
decent bona-fide, whole-time worker in
agriculture who is going to reap the
advantage of this Bill; it is much more
likely to be the corner boy or the wastrel,
or the now-and-again labourer, the fellow
who works here and there now and again.
He will get the benefit. That is the man
who will very soon discover that the
qualification for benefit is not a week's
work, or so many weeks' work, but the
acquisition of SO many stamps, and
apparently a stamp can be acquired by
putting through a Monday somehow and
receiving payment for the same, and then
idling for the rest of the week, under
this regulation unless attendance at
exchanges may prevent it. Still the test
is not the honest performance of so many
weeks' work, but the possession of so
many stamps, no matter what may be
given in exchange for them.
In speaking of exchanges I must
digress for a moment to say I welcome the
decision arrived at yesterday in the
House of Lords to the effect that benefits shall be paid through the Post Office
instead of at Labour Exchanges. I
welcome that very much for I think the
less the country labourer has to do with
Labour Exchanges the better, especially
if he wants to get employment with a
country farmer. I think the further
the agricultural labourer keeps from the
Labour Exchange the better it will be for
that man and for his chances of employment. Therefore, I think it very desirable that he should get his money
through the Post Office if possible.
Indeed, I am afraid I do not see how
247 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
the Labour Exchanges are going to fit in
the new picture as regards pensions.
I also dislike the idea of a man having
to walk as much as six miles to get his
benefit. This was mentioned by Lord
Hutchinson in the debate in London.
That seems to be the idea underlying
the English proposal. Whether the right
hon. Gentleman proposes the same sort
of thing here I do not know, that a man
should walk six miles to get his benefit.
I conclude the Government will introduce an Amendment in the Committee
Stage, if there is one, in order to bring
in force in Northern Ireland the same
system of payments through the Post
Office as that which is apparently to be
provided in Great Britain. I imagine
for that the Government will first have
to obtain the consent of the Postmaster-
General, because the Post Office, as we
know, is a reserved service.
There was another point taken yesterday afternoon, but I will not deal with
it. I only saw it in a Belfast paper, and
it was very abbreviated. Apparently
some question was raised of paying benefit not to the man himself but to some
society with which he was connected. I
do not know whether that system prevails, to any extent in industrial
insurance, but if it does I presume there
will be no great objection to adopting it
here. That would have to be a matter
for a later stage. I have no information
I can give the House on that matter myself.
I am afraid I have to say as regards
the general question that I am not satisfied as to what I called the propriety
of the Bill. There is too much of that
in-and-out labour on farms where extra
labour is entirely of a casual nature.
That means the casual labourer. It may
be good or it may be bad, but I think
that is one of the great obiections to the
scheme. Dealing with that I rather
regret the disappearance of what was
paragraph 2 in the original Schedule
attached to the English Bill, the paragraph exempting emplovment in harvesting, peeling onions, picking flowers, and
things of that sort. I regret the disappearance of that exemption because, as
I think. it rather gave an opportunity of
preventing some. I cannot call it fraud,
but something in the nature of sharp
practice, which will result from the compulsory insurance of a man who just
works a couple of days binding up corn
and so on. It was rather a move in the
direction which I indicated a few
moments ago that I thought was not to
be desired. Therefore, I rather regret
the disappearance of that paragraph from
the Schedule-that is the English
Schedule, not our own.
Now I pass to the question of urgency.
What is the desperate hurry for this
Bill? What is the magic of the 4th of
May? Is it simply and solely because
the English Bill, if it survives an apparently not wholly unchequered Parliamentary course, is to come into operation
in England on the 4th of May? What
is the necessity for our adopting the same
date? I cannot accept as an answer
that we are bound to do the same and to
bring our Bill into operation on the same
day as Great Britain, nor can I admit
that the Agreement about insurance is
in any way concerned. To my mind
step by step-an expression with which we
are familiar-is not the same as rush for
rush, and, although general principles
are no doubt very admirable things, we
must not, I think, press them to the
extent of trimming facts to theory, as I
am very much afraid we shall be doing
if we adopt hastily the English scale on
the day appointed.
We have not had time to examine this
Bill properly or to appreciate its possible
results, those results of which I spoke
as possibly being almost revolutionary in
the countryside. And when I say we
have not had time I am not thinking only
of this Senate or only of this Parliament.
I am taking the Northern Ireland agricultural interests as a whole. To my
mind, before the Bill was prepared the
whole question ought to have been referred to a committee, a committee sitting in Northern Ireland, composed of
gentlemen of experience, with, perhaps,
a woman or two to help them. That
committee, after investigation throughout the rural districts, could, I think,
have prepared a report which would have
given us valuable information and would
have enabled us to decide rather better
than we can do to-day as to whether the
Bill before us is the Bill we really want
or whether a better one could be produced. Indeed I think this is a
case in which Lord Palmerston's cele-
249 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

3 APRIL 1936
Second Reading
brated advice might very well have been
followed. I am sure the House remembers it: "Fiat experimentum in corpore
vili." I am going to translate it, but
in doing so I shall paraphrase the last
word in case there may be a Scotsman
present: "Let us try it first elsewhere.'
I think we should have done better to
have let them try it in Great Britain
and to have seen how it got on there
before adopting it word for word, or
practically word for word, over here.
a
If
Delay, or time for thought, is really
what I am getting at in the Amendment
which I have put before the House.
we cannot get a suitable breathing space
it would be better that we should reject
this Bill and allow it to come up in
another form at a later date. But if the
House is satisfied, or if the right hon.
Gentleman is able to satisfy us, that the
4th of May is, so to speak, imperative,
and that there are really sound material
reasons, not merely that somewhat
vainglorious desire to imitate England
and to keep in step, and if the House
agrees to the 4th of May, then I think we
can scarcely blame the Government so
much for clutching hold of a system
already in being and ready to their hand,
rather than embarking on the course of
creating a new system, as opposed to what
I would call grafting. If that happens,
and if the Bill is accepted, then we must
do what we can in Committee with one
or two of its provisions, without, of
course, injuring in any way what I might
describe as the main structure of the Bill.
But we cannot, I am afraid, do much in
Committee. This is Friday evening and
I understand that it is desired to take the
Committee stage next Monday. The
possibility of getting amendments drafted
with reasonable care, put into print, and
circulated to the House-it would
possibly be Saturday before it could be
done for three o'clock on Monday is
not attractive. However, something,
perhaps, could be achieved.
And now I want to say a word about
that attrition of which I have already
spoken, that trend from the country
to the town. I feel that what
will help you to keep your men
in the country-I mean, of course,
the countryside as distinct from the town
-is partly housing, and particularly
housing in groups, that is making small
villages, a method which has not yet been
tried here. That might help and might
also reduce your expenditure on unemployment. That is one thing, and the
other is, of course, the prospect of constant employment, and also local attractions. If you could put a cinema in the
village the boy or girl would not have to
go to the town to see it. But I must
not develop that to-day.
On the whole, I feel bound to say that
the action of the Government as regards
this Bill suggests to my mind the action
of a young man in a hurry, a young mar
who is taking a wrong turning, who is
hastening to burn down a house to roast
a pig. I am not satisfied that the pig,
when he gets it, will be fit for service
at the table, and I am very much afraid
that the flames of the house will spread
and cause a conflagration which will get
beyond control. As I say, it is a farreaching Bill. We cannot see where it
is going to take us, but, taking into consideration the comparative immobility of
our labour, I do not think the Bill ought
to pass at this time. Therefore, while
thanking the House for their forbearance,
I would urge once more that the word
"now" do not stand part.
Mr. SPEAKER: Before I put the
Amendment of the hon. and learned
Senator, I would like to ask him whether
he wishes to add at the end the words
upon this day six months."

Mr. LESLIE: Yes. If the first goes
through.
Mr. SPEAKER: Otherwise
Question would not mean very much.
the
Mr. LESLIE: Yes. Leave out "now"
in order to insert at the end the words
upon this day six months."
""
Question proposed, "That the word
'now' be left out."
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
feel very much tempted to agree with a
great deal of what the hon. and learned
Gentleman (Mr. Leslie) has just said.
Considering the short notice he had, I
think the House will agree with me when
I say that he has produced one of the most
helpful and critical speeches of his very
brilliant career. There were many points
which, taken individually, would have
taken most of us several weeks of hard
work, with all the latest text books on the
251 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
THE SENATE

Second Reading
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
subject, before we could even have
arrived at the question involved, much
less at the answer. Personally I must
confess that I have not had time to go
into the matter in anything like the detail
of the hon. and learned Gentleman
opposite, and I shall have to be content
to deal with the Bill from a rather more
general standpoint.
I
First of all, I would like to say that,
in common, I am sure, with every other
Member of this House, I agree with the
principle of extending unemployment
insurance to agricultural workers.
cannot see the slightest argument in
equity for withholding it. If it is fair
and right, having regard to public
interests, that workers in other industries
should be relieved of some of the terrible
anxieties which affect manual workers,
and should have some compensation for
the possibility of long periods of unemployment, then I cannot see any argument against those benefits being
extended to the agricultural worker as
well.
And one must remember that there is
another point of view. Not only has one
to regard the feeling of the employed
person himself, but one has to regard his
efficiency. I believe that anything we
can do within reason to remove the fear
of unemployment is going to add to his
efficiency when he is in work. It has
been found in the past that social welfare, as carried on by big industrial employers, has definitely added to the
efficiency of the employees, and I believe
that one of the strongest arguments in
favour of unemployment insurance is
that the continual nervous strain and
doubt as to what may happen to him if
he loses his employment, is to some
extent removed from a man's mind.
Anyone who had the opportunity, as
I had, of observing the Germans after the
war, must have been struck by the effect
that continuous privation had had upon
the youth of the country. Indeed, I
believe that a great deal of the unrest
and excitement that we see in Europe
to-day is due to some extent to the
nervous exhaustion of the people from
privation. Anything we can do in this
country to remove the fear and the effect
of privation is going to be of immense
benefit to future generations.
But when I have said that I have said
very nearly all I can in support of this
Bill. Although I believe the principles
of the Bill are sound as far as they go,
I think the Bill is largely vitiated by the
fact that it does not go far enough. I
believe it to be actuarially unsound, and I
think it is going to lead to a great deal
of hardship amongst a certain class of
employers as well as amongst a certain
class of employees. If the House will
forgive me for prolonging this particular
line of argument for a minute I would
ask, if the House agree that in logic there
should be an extension of unemployment
insurance to agricultural workers, is
there the slightest logical reason why
private gardeners should be excluded.
Take the case of two brothers; one is a
cowman and the other is a gardener; one
will get the benefit of unemployment
insurance and the other will not. What
is the difference between these two
brothers? They are both doing good
work. They are working just the same
length of time in the day, and I cannot
see that there is any reason why a private
gardener should be more sure of his employment than a cowman.
Then again, although I admit that in
this case there is considerable reason for
exception-and I am strengthened in my
objection to this exception by some of
the remarks made by the hon. and
learned Gentleman-I think that the
exclusion of so many of the relations of
the employer from the Act is going to
make this
this Act actuarially unsound,
because I believe the bulk of the
employees of farmers are related to them,
and if you exclude all those employees,
who, after all, are more certain of their
employment than those who are not
related to their employers, then where
are you going to get the actuarial basis
for the insurance fund? If we heard of
an insurance company which said that
they would only insure those owners of
houses whose houses are likely to be
burned, would there be a reasonable basis
for insurance? The whole principle of
insurance is that you cover the risk by
taking in a wide class of individuals,
and hope that at least a large part of
that class will be to some extent immune
from accident, in fact, that the risk will
be very small.
In this case I cannot see that the risk
of a man's son-in-law being put out of
253 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
3 APRIL 1936

Second Reading
employment when he is working for his
father-in-law is negligible. The fatherin-law may die, or he may go to another
part of the country, or one of the many
accidents of life may happen and the
son-in-law be put out of employment.
Therefore, there is some risk of a man
being out of employment, even though
he is employed by his father-in-law, but
the risk obviously is not so large as if
that man were employed by a stranger.
That to my mind is a class which you
ought to include in the Bill, because you
have a very large proportion of the agricultural workers in that class. The risk
of unemployment is comparatively small,
and there is just the scintilla of risk to
justify that man being brought into the
insurance scheme.
May I put another case to the House?
This Bill includes workers in afforestation;
it does not include private
gardeners, as I have already mentioned.
Suppose, again; that a man employs two
brothers, both as gardeners, and he has
a piece of rough land which he cannot
plough and he employs one of his
gardeners to plant some trees there.
Who is to say what forestry work is? If
that work is forestry work that man is
entitled to benefit by the Act. The other
brother who is employed only on gardening work is excluded from the benefits
of the Act.
I have developed the objections I have
to the Bill from the point of view of
the employee, and I should now like to
consider the matter from the point of
view of the employer. The Government
are by this Bill, it seems, penalising the
farmer who farms as a trade or business.
In the Bill there is a definition of agriculture. I apologise to the House for
being unable to find it at the moment.
I really have not had long enough to
study the Bill in detail, but in some
place, I know, it is mentioned that the
employer must be engaged in agriculture
for the purposes of trade or business. I
agree that that is only a reasonable view
to take, but why should the Government
impose this levy-and of course there
must be a levy-on the working farmer
who is making a living out of his farm,
and exempt the prosperous business man
who resides in the town and keeps cows
to produce milk for his own consumption; he appears to be excluded from the
Act.
Mr. ANDREWS: Such a man will be
treated as if he were a farmer.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
withdraw that point. I am told by the
right hon. Gentleman that such a man is
reckoned to be a farmer. I apologise
for bringing that in, but it is due to the
fact that I have had very little time to
read the Bill. I am not sure that I agree
with the hon. and learned Senator when
he states that he endorses the view which
has apparently been taken by the House
of Lords that unemployment benefit
should be paid through the Post Office.
He knows far more, no doubt, about the
machinery for the payment of unemployment insurance than I do, but in the
case of agriculture a man may be an
agricultural worker for part of the year,
and an industrial worker in another dis-
It
trict for another part of the year.
does seem to me that the machinery that
already exists in the Labour Exchanges is
probably better machinery for deciding
the many vexed questions that arise in
the case of unemployment insurance than
a Post Office official, although I sympathise with the view that the less the agricultural labourer has to do with the
Labour Exchange the better it is for
agriculture as a whole. On the other
hand, I think it will require considerable
organisation of the Post Office to make
it an efficient medium for the payment of
unemployment benefit.
I think this Bill contains a great many
ambiguities which will eventually have
to be decided by a court of law. There
is a practice very common in this country
among farmers of not employing their
labour in the ordinary sense, but of
giving their labourer, who is perhaps a
distant cousin or something like that, the
money to buy a few sheep or the grazing
for a few sheep. Whether that makes a
labourer an employee or whether there is
some form of partnership can only, I
think, be decided by the courts. It may
be a sort of profit-sharing agreement, but
I do not think anyone can give an answer
except a judge. Other ambiguities will
arise. What is to happen to a hedger and
ditcher? Is he an agricultural labourer?
He may never do anything except take
the mud out of a ditch. What is to
happen to the many estate employees who
are employed on big estates in clearing
or cutting hedges, doing ordinary estate
255 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
THE SENATE

Second Reading
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
work which it would be difficult to say
was either private gardening within the
definition of the Act or farming work?
All these points I think ought to be
We
defined more closely than they are.
do not want more legislation giving work
to the courts, although no doubt by that
many may benefit too. That sort of thing
ought to be reduced as much as possible.
man
to
Personally I think the Government are
rushing this Bill through at too great
a speed. All these points ought to be
thrashed out, and if necessary
the
Government ought to welcome the opportunity that the hon. and learned Gentlethem
has offered
consider
amendments. I feel that they are unduly
restricting the opportunities of the hon.
and learned Senator who has offered to
help to improve this legislation. I think
this House is entitled to more consideration. The Bill has been available for
only a very short time, and I think more
time should be given to the consideration
of a Measure of such outstanding
importance, which affects not only the
present but the future to a very considerable degree.
I must say that I should welcome a
statement from the Government that
they would postpone the Committee
Stage until the Members of this House,
who are not out to damage the Government or to damage the Bill, in any way,
but who thoroughly approve of the principle contained in it, have had a longer
time for consideration of the Measure, so
that those who wish to help the Government can produce a case which the
Government can consider. I believe that
if the Government postponed the Bill a
little longer, say until after Easter, they
would avoid a great deal of difficulty in
future. This Bill is bound to lead to
criticism. It cannot help doing so. I
can hardly imagine that the Government
want to add to that criticism by the
criticism that they rushed the Bill
through.
As
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I thoroughly
endorse the sentiments expressed by the
last two speakers in regard to the rushing of the Bill through this House.
has been pointed out, it is one of the
most important Bills of recent times. It
is now being rushed unduly, and, to say
the least of it, very little consideration
or respect has been shown to this House
I am afraid that the
by rushing it.
Government may possibly be contemplating following the example of the Free
State and doing away with the Senate,
or, if they are not going to do that, they
show as little toleration for it as possible.
We are treated merely as figureheads.
However, be that as it may, I certainly
approve of the principle of the Bill. I
think it is long overdue, and I think the
sooner it is in operation the better. I
have always thought that in this country
our working men should, without any
qualifications, be liable to compulsory insurance. I speak not only from the point
of view of the present conditions of the
labourer, but from the point of view of
his wife and children in the event of his
death. It is a deplorable state of affairs
that owing to existing conditions the
widow and children of a labourer are
faced with the prospect of being thrown
on outdoor relief. I think that the right
hon. Gentleman the Minister of Labour
ought to give the public an assurance in
regard to the position of the business
man or civil servant who has a residence
to which is attached a large or a small
garden. To keep that garden going he
is of necessity bound to employ a casual
labourer. Is it suggested that such
casual labourer should be excluded from
the provisions of this Bill? If so, I
think it is a very great hardship. Surely
if the employee of a farmer is entitled to
the benefits of this Measure its provisions
should be equally applicable to the civil
servant or the business man who is
anxious to give employment to the casual
labourer, because he must employ such a
man if he is to make a success of his
grounds. If such a person is anxious to
provide employment for a casual labourer
surely he should be in a position to stamp
that man's card and make him eligible
for unemployment benefit. If any of us
were engaging labour in that way we
ought to be able to place the labourer
in such a position that in the event of
his death his widow and orphans would
become a charge upon the State. I think
that is a very important point, and one
that should not be lost sight of by the
Government.
a
There is also the case of the labouring
man who possibly does not get very much
work in the country. He has attained
fifty years of age and is not in very good
257 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

3 APRIL 1936
Second Reading
health, and he is obliged to accept the
class of employment to which I have
already referred, being able to work for
perhaps only three or four days in the
week.
Mr. ANDREWS: May I inform my
hon. Friend that no matter who employs
a farm labourer to do farm work he will
be obliged to stamp his card, and that
stamp will be the equivalent to a stamp
put on by a farmer. Therefore the hon.
Member may be quite satisfied on that
point.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I would like a
definite assurance on that point, because
it is very easy to stamp cards, and it is
very easy for the Minister of Labour
to allow them to be stamped and to
accept them as stamped, but I would not
leave too much to the Ministry of
Labour, with all due respect to the
Minister, or to any of the Ministries. I
have here a typical case in point. In
this particular case a great hardship was
done to the individual concerned, and if
I were dealing with it I would use a
much harsher word to describe it. This
person had been insured for a great
number of years when he reached sixtyfive years of age. He then applied to the
Ministry of Labour for his contributory
pension. His application was investigated and it was turned down by the
Ministry, who wrote:-
You were not continuously insured.
for the five years immediately before
your 65th birthday (Section 8 of the
1925 Act).
The contributions paid by and in
respect of you during the period 28th
April, 1930, and 3rd April, 1932,
were not paid in respect of insurable
employment and do not serve
satisfy the Statutory conditions imposed by the Contributory Pensions
Act.
to
I contend that when
a person has
stamped his cards for so many years in
the expectation that when he attains the
age of 65 he will be entitled to and
will receive a pension it is not fair that
he should be presented with a letter of
that nature, especially after the Ministry
have accepted the payment of his stamps
for so many years. If the Ministry
adopts that attitude I say they should
return to that individual whatever contributions he had paid within the period
of his insurance. I also suggest that
there should be some provision in this
Bill whereby persons of that type should
receive benefit. He is now over the age
of 65, and is thus deprived of the
benefits of this Bill and he has got to
wait until he is 70 years of age before
he can qualify for the old age pension.
This is a matter that should receive the
careful consideration of the Government.
I note that in the Bill it is proposed
that a farmer must give twenty-eight
days' notice when he engages a servant.
We have all heard of hiring fairs, and
if a farmer goes into the May or November hiring fair and hires a servant he
will not be in a position to give twentyeight days' notice of that hire to the
Government. As regards the position of
the relations of a farmer and their exclusion from the provisions of this Bill,
I entirely agree with the views of the
last speaker. I think a great deal more
consideration should be given to the
farmer, and he should at least be trusted.
There are a great many districts in this
country where the farmers inter-marry,
and in certain townlands there is a
relationship between practically all the
farmers. I think the farmer should be
encouraged to keep the home life going
and that, if he is not in exceptionally
good circumstances, he should be encouraged to keep any members of his
family at home and give them a little
wage for whatever work they do instead
of employing outside labour. Such a
farmer should be allowed to employ
members of his own family and to stamp
their cards and thus qualify them for the
dole and, if they are married, qualify
their widows and orphans for the pension
they should receive if anything happened
to the breadwinner.
I think the suggestion made by Senator
Leslie that payment of benefit under this
Bill should be made through the Post
Office is a very good one. Our farmers
and farm labourers should be kept out
of the towns as much as possible, except
when they have business there, and we
should not encourage them to visit the
towns more than they can help.
possible we should keep them in the
country and not compel them to go to
Labour Exchanges where they will see a
crowd waiting for their dole or to sign
on. It would be a great deal better if
these men were at home engaged in some
E
If
259 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. McLaughlin]
sort of employment, because we all know
that when idle men congregate at a
street corner or wait in a crowd at a
Labour Exchange they are apt to discuss
amongst themselves the best way they can
get money by doing the least for it. To
some extent they become discontented,
and look at their fellow men going to
their work and say to themselves, "Why
cannot I have work and wages the same
as those men?"

"3
Again, these men will not leave the
towns without spending some of this unemployment money on something that
perhaps they would be better without,
and it might be possible that they would
not go straight home, because I generally
find at the present time on coming into
our towns and villages we have these
gee-gee shops-betting houses-that
you have in nearly every street, and
when you see a farmer in the town in
the morning you can conclude that he
is neglecting his farm and backing horses.
The same thing will apply to the
labourer. The labourer when he gets his
dole rather than go to work will do
the same thing, and I say it is teaching
him a bad habit.
In conclusion, I certainly do think too
much control should not be left in the
hands of any Ministry. I agree that if
any dispute should arise with regard to
what the nature of the employment is
or as to whether the person is entitled
to benefits under this Act or not that
should be left to an impartial tribunal.
I agree that the Ministry are not the
proper persons to decide that.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
should like to say a word or two in
favour of this Bill. With the other
speakers I am strongly in favour of this
insurance being extended to farm
labourers. They seem to be a class that
is completely neglected. No one seems
to have any interest in them, and yet
I suppose they were lower paid and lived
under worse conditions than any class
of worker. Much has been said here
to-day about impostors or about the
abuse of this benefit. But I remember
very well when the original Insurance
was introduced in 1912. I was at that
time attending a delegate meeting of the
Engineers' Society in Manchester and
there was no one criticised the intro-
In
duction of that scheme more than the
trade unions themselves. No one was
more opposed to it. But it turned out
well for the working-class people.
fact had it not been for that and the
accumulation of money after the war it
is quite possible that there would have
been great trouble in England with the
number of unemployed. I feel that the
scheme will right itself.
There is no possibility of introducing
a scheme like this amongst a remarkclass
ably poor
without causing a
great deal of trouble. We are bound to
have a number of people likely to impose
or to take advantage of something that
is new. Then if the benefits are in some
cases greater than the wages they earn
at the present time I have no doubt that
the honest workers will prefer work to
benefit. Even in the industrial districts
after the war I think you will remember
that the benefits at that time paid to
the industrial workers were even larger
in the case of a man with a family than
a tradesman's wages, and yet I remember very well the majority of men preferred work even to drawing benefit
greater than the wages they would
receive. I believe this will occur in the
country just the same-if the work is
there for them, and if work is not there
for them it is all the more reason benefit
should be provided, because the farm
labourer has a wife and family to keep.
Very often the children turn out some
of the best citizens, because they are
healthy physically and healthy mentally.
I notice that these people coming into
the city do very often much better than
town-reared people such as myself.
There is a shrewdness, about them that
enables them to get on better than towns
people.
I think it is a very wise thing
to extend the benefit to these people. I
believe that the farmer himself will feel
more satisfied because he will not have
the worry of providing for the farm
labourer and keeping him when he does
not want him. If he is keeping two or
three men and has to dispose of one, he
will be satisfied that the poor fellow is
not going to starve. There will be something provided for him out of the State
Insurance Scheme. I would point out to
the House while this is new to the farm
labourer, the farm labourer is an insured
person under the Health Insurance
261 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
3 APRIL 1936

Second Reading
Scheme and we have heard nothing of
the imposition of the farming class in
that particular benefit. Indeed, I think
we have heard that the industrial districts, where women are concerned, are
the most troublesome claimants of health
insurance benefit. I do not think we
have too much reason to fear the introduction of this Bill. The troubles that
exist will be rectified in the course of
time. Under the principal scheme you
have had amendment after amendment
to rectify difficulties. I expect the same
thing will occur in this new scheme.
There are bound to be a considerable
number of amendments after it has
worked for a time. Therefore I feel
justified in giving the Bill my earnest
support for the benefit of the poor people
who have been terribly neglected for the
past number of years.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to support
the Second Reading or the principle of
the Bill. I am not going to say very
much because there has been a great deal
said this afternoon. While I support
the Second Reading, at the same time
I have certain fears. I have a fear that
amongst the smaller farmers they will
dispense with the men or man they have
employed, and instead of the Bill being
a benefit to that man it might be the
other way about. Had there been no
Bill like this the man would be constantly employed the year round, and
now the Bill comes into force he will
find himself unemployed.
I
The reason I make that statement is
this. The standard rate of wage was
fixed for farm labourers and after that
came the National Health Insurance.
have five farmers before my mind at the
present time who used to employ a man
the year round, a servant boy, as they
called him. When these two things
came into existence these five farmers
dispensed with the servant boys and
have not employed one since. They work
among themselves-what is known
morrowing. One, two or three farmers
go to this farmer to-day and reap his
corn or raise his potatoes or help him in
some other way, and then these farmers
go back and help the others. That is
the way they are working among each
other, and the result is that five servant
boys are put on the employment market.
I have a fear that perhaps this will
extend, and if it does I am sorry for the
as
poor fellow who is going to be put on
the road.
Another case came to my mind the
other day. I met a farmer and I asked
him, "What do the Farmers' Union
think of this Bill?" This farmer, who
was chairman of the Farmers' Union,
said "I have heard nothing about the
Bill and know nothing about it; I employ a man the year round, but there
are two or three months when I can do
without him, and when this Bill comes
into force I will put this man on the
dole." I know this man that he employs,
and I said to the farmer, "What will the
poor fellow do when you put him on the
road?" He said, "Well, I will charge
him 12s a week for his board and lodging." This poor fellow has no home to
go to.
What I want to know is if a
farmer employs a boy and is going to put
him on the dole for three months, can
that farmer keep this boy and charge
him for board and lodging?
As far as the Post Office is concerned,
it would be very convenient for the farm
labourer to draw his money there. I
would like to know where the farm
labourer is going to register for employment? Will it be at the Post Office or
the Labour Exchange? Of course, it
would mean a great deal of work for
the Post Office. I do not know whether
the Ministry are thinking of transferring
the registration from the Labour
Exchange to the Post Office. I know the
matter must be worked on the cheapest
lines. I have much pleasure in supporting the Second Reading of the Bill. I
hope some of the suggestions made will
be taken notice of and amendments
brought in to make the Bill more
workable.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I have, indeed, very
great pleasure in supporting this Bill.
I think it will prove a great boon to the
agricultural worker. Up to the present
time these workers have been neglected,
and why they have not had the same
opportunities in connection with insurance I do not understand. I have
listened to-day to the very excellent
speech of the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie). I have no hesitation in
saying it was an excellent speech. He is,
I think, improving every day. He said
this was a most important Bill. I agree
with him in that, and I also say that
263 Unemployment Insurance Bill—

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Quigley]
there is great necessity for it. Senator
Leslie dealt with four points-equity,
necessity, propriety and urgency, and he
dealt with them very well indeed.
It has been put forward here that we
have not had sufficient time to deal with
this Bill. I spent one and three-quarter
hours on this Bill last evening. I think
we agreed at our last sitting to take the
Second Reading to-day. Senator Leslie.
seems to have a peculiar idea of
insurance. He says it affects the morale
of the worker. I cannot agree with him
on that. He also said it is the means of
making corner boys. I have had great
experience in dealing with the unemployed and those seeking employment,
and I know how little I can do for them.
I do not hold the opinion that Senator
Leslie seems to hold in regard to that
matter. I hope the Government will
carry this Bill through as quickly as
possible.
I am very pleased to see that the
Government have selected the Post Office
in connection with this work. There is
no better department that I know of
than the Post Office to deal with work.
They have a good obliging staff and will
deal with it in a very satisfactory
manner. I have very great pleasure in
supporting the Second Reading of the
Bill.
Mr. ANDREWS: I am quite sure
that we all listened with pleasure to the
very able and interesting speech of
Senator Leslie to-day. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) Whether we agree with it
or not is a different matter. I would like
to assure him that this Bill has not been
light-heartedly entered upon. It has, in
the abstract at any rate, received very
great consideration in the last fifteen
years. Innumerable experts have investigated this problem believing it was
an anomaly that industrial workers
should be insured against the effects
of unemployment while agricultural
workers were left out of that secure
position. Again it was thought it was an
anomaly that farm workers should be
insured for health under our Health
Insurance Acts and Pension Acts with
exactly the same contributions and benefits as industrial workers while as regards
unemployment they were left out of the
picture.
The hon. and learned Member seemed
to approach the matter from a very
reasonable point of view at the beginning
of his remarks. He approached it from
the standpoint of equity. I feel that
would be very easily dealt with, because
I believe it would be a great injustice if
this matter was left undealt with any
longer. I feel that for the farm workers
across the Channel to obtain this advantage while the farm workers in Northern
Ireland are deprived of it would be an
injustice which no Government could
justify. To postpone the Measure would
mean that for a time that injustice would
exist. Whether that time was short or
long would be another matter, but I
believe that it would be also quite contrary to the Government's considered
policy which it has adopted for fifteen
years, namely, that our people here as
regards these social services which my
Department administers, and which
mean so much to our workers during
times of adversity, should be in the same
position as those across the Channel.
Therefore, I may say that there is a
necessity for the Measure, and a necessity for its being brought into existence
at the same time as it is brought in in
Great Britain.
I am sorry that hon. Senators should
feel that it has been rushed as regards
this honourable House. The difficulty
has not been of our Government's
creation. The position is that the Bill
was only recently considered in the
Parliament at Westminster, and it is
still under consideration in the House of
Lords. Yet the British Measure is to
come into operation on 4th May. What
has complicated the position for us here
is that if we had proceeded at a quicker
rate than that taken at Westminster we
would have had, obviously, a large
number of amendments to introduce into
this Senate. Then the Bill would have
to go back to the Commons, which procedure, undoubtedly, would have caused
a great deal of delay. As far as the
Ministry of Labour are concerned, if we
obtain the Royal Assent on the twentyfirst of this month that would do us, but
the difficulty is that Easter comes in
between, and I do not think that hon.
Members in either House would care to
have their Easter holiday unduly interfered with.
265 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

3 APRIL 1936
Second Reading
The hon. Senator made a point as
regards the cost of administration probably being high. It is laid down in the
Bill that it must not exceed one-eighth of
the total income of the Fund. There has
been a considerable amount of discussion
in this House to-day as to whether
gardeners working for private indivduals
should be included in the Bill. It is
quite open to some people to feel that the
scope of the Measure should be extended,
but the difficulty in connection with all
these matters is to know how far to go
and just when to stop.
For instance, it
could be argued that domestic servants
should be brought in, and it could be
argued that chauffeurs should be brought
in. It may be argued some day with
considerable force, but when this Bill
was first introduced it was thought that
private gardeners, being very largely
domestic servants, should not be
included.
That was discussed at Westminster to
very considerable extent, and the
Government there have inserted a Clause
in their Bill to give power to the Government to bring in private gardeners if
they think it desirable so to do, and
we have taken power in our Bill to do the
same thing by Order in Council. There
is a little difference in the procedure.
They have to lay the Order before Parliament before it is agreed to, but we have
to make the Order first and lay it before
Parliament afterwards. I can
quite
understand that being criticised, but
there is a reason for it. The reason is
that if and when it is decided to do it at
Westminster these Houses possibly may not
be sitting, and then we would have the
expense, and some of us would have the
inconvenience of having to be brought
back specially to consider this matter.
In my opinion, apart from the constitutional aspect, I do not really think it
makes much difference what procedure is
adopted. The important thing, in my
opinion, is that the thing should be done.
It was suggested that workers might
not desire to work if they could get
benefit without working, and it was even
suggested by one hon. Senator that they
went to the Exchanges and there discussed how they could get benefit instead
of working. I have had some experience
ranging over a period of fifteen years of
these matters, and I do not say that is so.
My very definite opinion is that our
workers in Northern Ireland desire work,
and that it is only with reluctance they
take help of any kind either in the form
of unemployment benefit, transitional
payments, unemployment assistance, or
what is known as outdoor relief. While
I do feel strongly that those who are
anxious to work and cannot get it,
whether they be farm labourers or industrial workers, ought to receive at any
rate help to such an extent as to give
them the necessaries of life, I know no
better way by which that can be done
than through an insurance scheme.
It has also been suggested that farmers
will be inclined to stand off their workers
to a greater extent than in the past
during what is known as the dead season.
It will not be so simple for the farmers
to do that as is suggested, and for the
reason that farmers, I believe, are, in
the first place, always anxious to play
fair, and I am sure it is their desire that
their workers should feel secure, that
they should be interested in their work,
and that they should realise they are
treated fairly, as fairly at any rate as can
be afforded. But when a farmer does
stand off his man he will no longer be
able to claim that man as his servant,
and it will be the duty of that man who
becomes unemployed to seek work elsewhere if he can get it. Further, it will
be the duty of the Exchange if there is a
vacancy, the duties of which it can
reasonably be felt the man is capable of
performing, to offer him that work, and
if he refuses-
Mr. LESLIE: May I interrupt for
one moment? I am sure the fault is
mine, but I did not quite follow what
the right hon. Gentleman said. Did I
understand him to say that it would be
open to the Exchange to offer work to
the employee? The Exchange does not
employ a man. I did not quite follow.
Mr. ANDREWS: The man will have
to come to the Exchange to draw his
benefit. He will also have to come to
the Exchange to register as an unemployed man, and it will be the duty of
the Exchange in connection with.
Measure, just as it is in connection with
industrial insurance, to endeavour to
place as many unemployed men as possible in work. What I want to make quite
267 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Andrews]
an
an
clear is that the minute a farmer standsoff his employee, just in the same way as
under our existing legislation
employer may dismiss or suspend
employee, that man is free to accept work
elsewhere, and is obliged to accept it if
it is available. In addition it is the duty
of the Exchange to endeavour to assist
that man to obtain work, and if it can
offer the unemployed person work and if
the unemployed person refuses it then
the unemployed person very properly
forfeits his benefit. If he does not desire
to work then obviously he forfeits hist
right to benefit.
Mr. HILL-DICKSON: I am sorry to
interrupt. Might I ask what would
become of the labourer who gets a house
as part payment of his wages and who
goes away on the dole? If he remains in
that house he becomes a tenant and has
no claim to the house.
Mr. ANDREWS: I think I can
answer that point. It would be quite
open to the farmer to charge him a rent
for the house if he remains in the house.
On the other hand, if the farmer wants
the house for another employee he would
be quite entitled to ask him to give way.
However, there are other matters which
have been mentioned in connection with
this Measure and to which I wish
refer.
to
Of course I do not think for a
moment that difficulties will not arise in
connection with this matter, difficulties
such as the hon. Senator has suggested,
but, after all, we had difficulties in
various forms in connection with the industrial scheme-the general scheme-and
they were gradually overcome. On the
whole I think it is admitted that the general
scheme, first of all, was absolutely necessary, secondly, that it has largely saved
the situation for the nation, and that it
is just, reasonable, fair, and working
wonderfully smoothly. I am very hopeful that this scheme will follow the same
course.
The hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) is not very fond of legislation by
reference, and he is not very fond,
naturally, of regulations. But the reason
why, in some instances, regulations are
preferred to actual Clauses in a Bill is
that regulations are somewhat more
flexible and can be altered from time to
time more easily to meet whatever situation may arise. After all this Measure is
a pioneer one. We are ploughing new
fields. The operation has to be tackled
sometime, and in my opinion, and for
the reasons I have mentioned, the sooner
the job is tackled the better. Again it
was mentioned that relations of farmers
are excluded, and it was asked why they
should not be brought in. I can quite
understand a difference of opinion in connection with a problem of that kind, but
there is a practical difficulty in bringing
them in. One is as to who is to certify
that a son, say, is unemployed. It is
rather difficult. It is a pretty large
responsibility to place upon the father.
If a
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: In
places where there is a great deal of
inter-relationship does it not always arise
in the case of unemployed men?
man is a second or third cousin of the
employer who is to certify that that man
is unemployed?
we can
Mr. ANDREWS: Take, for instance,
the case of health insurance where the
person is sick. He may be the farmer's
son, or he may be anyone else, but the
certification there is done by the local
doctor, and further there is the Government doctor who comes into the picture
pretty frequently. In connection with
industrial insurance if the Exchange is
in doubt about the matter
always write to the employer whose
relationship is very different from
that of father and son. But, rightly
or wrongly, such people have not
been brought into the British Measure
and, therefore, it follows that they cannot be brought into this Measure. You
see we cannot have it both ways. We
either must
agree to follow Great
Britain in a matter of this kind or we
must go off on our own. There are only
the
two choices, and my considered
opinion, and the considered opinion of
my colleagues, is that we are doing the
best that can be done for our industrial
workers, and also now for our farm
workers, by following Great Britain.
The fact that our Reinsurance Agreement exists is proof of the advantage it
is. If we were to go off on our own
we really could not carry the burden,
and I am glad to be able to tell the
House that it has been arranged that
this scheme is to be brought within the
269 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

3 APRIL 1936
Second Reading
scope of the Reinsurance Agreement.
Therefore, the financial position of the
scheme will be ensured. The contributions from employers and workers will,
it is estimated, amount to £46,000 a
year, and the statutory contribution will
be half of the combined sum of £23,000
a year. Those two sums added together
amount to £69,000 a year. On the other
side of the account, it is estimated that
the benefits will cost £100,000, and the
administration costs £8,500, giving a
total of £108,500, which shows an actual
deficiency of about £40,000 a year.
How is that gap to be made good? It
will be made good by our Exchequer in
the first place, and then our Exchequer
will be placed in a position to bear that
excess burden by a grant from the
British Exchequer. We need not try to
disguise from ourselves that that is an
enormous advantage. I think it is an
unanswerable argument as to why we
should keep in step with Great Britain
in connection with these matters. The
other unanswerable argument, in my
opinion, is that this is necessary in
justice to our unemployed people. I do
not for one moment want to say that
this Measure is the last word. It
obviously is not. All our insurance
schemes have been a growth, and this
scheme will probably follow the same
course. It is only in that way that
progress is made. But what I do claim
a
is that this Measure will relieve to a
considerable extent great deal of
poverty which unfortunately exists in
many homes in our country districts.
It will give a confidence and a security
to those homes that is not there at the
present time. I should just like to
repeat that although it is not the last
word in connection with this matter,
what I do feel is this, it is the foundation well and truly laid upon which we
or others, sometime, somehow, will build
something greater still. The Government cannot accept the proposal for the
rejection of the Bill.
Mr. HILL-DICKSON: The fact of
the matter is that the Government have
passed this Bill, and it does not matter
very much what we say inside or outside this House.
Mr. LESLIE: Being the Mover of the
Motion, I should like to say a few words
in reply. Really the course of the debate
has been that everybody, except the last
speaker, has spoken in support of the
principle of the Bill, and everybody,
with the exception of two hon. Members
on my left, has criticised—and I might
use a stronger word—the details. I must
deal very briefly with some of the
remarks that were made. I notice that
the hon. and gallant Senator opposite
(Captain Perceval-Maxwell) wanted to
bring in the sons-in-law and so forth.
That was exactly the reason why I read
to the House the figures from the Year
Book showing how greatly the number
of farmers' relations exceeded the number
of ordinary farm labourers. As regards
the
question of partnership being a
matter for the courts, naturally the hon.
and gallant Gentleman and I agree
about that, but I am afraid he will find
that the Ministry claim the right to
settle questions of that sort, and they
have the right.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
am afraid they have.
Mr. LESLE: I notice that he agrees
there is too much control left to the
Ministry, but my hon. Friend on my
immediate left (Mr. Quigley) suggested
that the agricultural labourer had been
neglected for years. I should like to
point out that the agricultural labourer
has not been neglected by his employer
for years.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I did not say so.
was referring to insurance.
I
Mr. LESLIE: You were referring to
neglect by the public? His view was
that we should pass the Bill and trust
to luck, and the right hon. Gentleman
seemed to agree with him. The right
hon. Gentleman, I noticed, did not give
me any answer about the man of 65.
Mr. HILL-DICKSON: The employer
has to continue paying.
Mr. ANDREWS: Would the hon. and
learned Senator repeat the question?
Mr. LESLIE: What I said was that
the Bill contained what seemed to me an
unjust, even spiteful, implication that
when the agricultural labourer became
ineligible for benefit his employer has to
continue to pay the employer's contribution in respect of him. I asked whether
that was so or not.
271 Unemployment Insurance Bill.

THE SENATE
Business of the House
Mr. ANDREWS: Yes, it is so, and he
is going to get a pension of 10s.
Mr.
LESLIE: Who is? The employer? The employer is the man I am
asking about.
Mr. ANDREWS: The employee will
get a pension of 10s.
Mr. LESLIE: Does the employer have
to go on paying the employer's contribution under the Insurance Act? That is
the question I asked.
Mr. ANDREWS: Yes, he will.
Mr. LESLIE: Oh, the employer will
have to continue. Thank you. I do
not withdraw either unjust or spiteful.
Of course I do not apply those words to
the right hon. Gentleman, but to the
Bill. The only other matter I wish to
refer to is that the right hon. Gentleman
rather supported the practice of doing
so much by regulation. I hope he will
not think I am unkind in saying that
this has come to such a pitch that one
can only say that it is used to destroy the
rule of law by virtue of the sovereignty
of Parliament. That is really what it
comes to, and I do not think this House
ought to agree to that.
Mr. ANDREWS: It is very difficult
to know just what is best. I can understand the hon. Senator thinking that it
is rather unfair to ask the farmer to continue to pay contributions, but, on the
other hand, if you do not ask him to do
that there would be a direct inducement
given to the employer to keep the older
men, the pensioned men, to the disadvantage of the younger men who are not
getting pensions.
Mr. LESLIE: But there are other
considerations. However, we cannot
continue it.
And now the question arises, having
regard to what I said about the whole
House being in favour of the principle of
the Bill, whether the Motion should be
withdrawn or the decision of the House
left to a division. I am disposed to take
advantage of that admirable provision of
our practice, that withdrawal must be
unanimous, and, therefore, unless anybody objects to the withdrawal, I think
you would agree that it would be better
to ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question, "That the Bill be now read a
Second time," put, and agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be committed
to a Committee of the Whole House for
Monday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
"That this House do now adjourn," I
should like to remind hon. Senators that
when I last moved the Adjournment I
said that the Committee stage would
follow rather quickly on the Second
Reading. The alternative was to sit in
Easter week, and I understood there was
a universal shout of "No." Arrangements were made accordingly. I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn.'
Mr. LESLIE: Until?

Viscount CHARLEMONT: Monday.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Will the noble
Lord tell us how many days we will
sit next week?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: On Monday and Tuesday, and on Thursday at
twelve o'clock.
Captain
PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
When will the Report stage be taken?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid that all the remaining stages will
be taken quickly.
Mr. LESLIE: We are to understand
that it is intended to take the Committee
stage on Monday next?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter before Six
o'clock until Monday
next.
Canals Bill-
