6 APRIL 1936
THE SENATE.

Monday, 6th April, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Canals and Inland Navigation Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." Viscount Charlemont).
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Amendment of local or
private Acts relating to canal and inland navigation undertakings), 2 (Provisions with respect to provisional
orders), 3 (Classification of merchandise
traffic and fixing of tolls and charges),
4 (Expenses of county councils in connection with Upper Bann Navigation),
5 (Abolition of rent charge payable by
the Lagan Navigation Company), 6
(Power of Ministry to make regulations),
7 (Interpretation), and 8 (Short title)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
SCHEDULE.
*
2. The inquiry shall be held in
public and any objector and any other
person who, in the opinion of the
inspector, is affected by the application
may appear at the inquiry either in
person or by counsel, solicitor or agent.
*
*
*
*
in
Mr. MCALLISTER: I beg to move,
the Schedule, on page 7, to leave out
the words "solicitor or agent" in line 9,
and to insert instead the words "or
solicitor." This paragraph in the
Schedule as it stands provides that anyone affected by the application may
appear at the inquiry either in person
or by counsel, solicitor, or agent.
effect of my Amendment, if adopted,
would be that the person affected might
appear in person if he so desired, but
if he seeks representation that representation must be by counsel or solicitor. I
The
submit to the Committee that the
description "agent" is too indefinite, too
vague, and too loose. Anyone can be
an agent, and the position might be
abused. You might have a case of a man
without proper qualifications who might
ingratiate himself into the confidence of
a person affected by one of these inquiries, and he might represent to that
person that he had the necessary qualifications and knowledge to conduct what
was really a legal case when he had no
such knowledge or qualifications at all.
The inspector holding the inquiry has
power to summon witnesses, and if those
witnesses do not appear they are liable
on conviction to a fine of £5. We must
assume that these proceedings will be
conducted in a legal manner; that the
case must be presented according to the
ordinary rules of law; and that the
evidence given must be in accordance with
the rules of evidence. We might have a
witness who is summoned to give evidence
against his will, and, if so, he is entitled
to the protection of a legally qualified
practitioner who will, by custom and
duty to his profession, confine himself
to the ordinary legitimate rules of crossexamination. On the other hand, if
the witness is to be cross-examined by an
agent, that agent may be anybody. He
may be vindictive, and a person who has
none of the legal knowledge a qualified
representative would and should possess,
I submit that this is a vital matter.
Any tribunal such as this naturally
seeks the help of an advocate, because it
must largely depend upon the legal
knowledge of that advocate, and must
found its judgment upon the law,
whether it be case law, Statute law, or
common law. The inspector is placed in
a position to come to a legal decision
only when he has the law put before him
by a properly qualified person. In conclusion, I would like to impress upon the
Committee that my Amendment does not
prevent a person appearing before the
tribunal in person if he so desires. It
merely means that the position of agent
should not be abused, and that if that
person elects to employ someone to represent him that representative should be a
properly qualified practitioner with a
legal knowledge of the subject.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to support the
Amendment, and for a reason which the
275 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
hon. Senator who moved it did not give,
although he gave numerous reasons why
it should be accepted. The reason why I
particularly wish to see the Amendment
carried is because, from the point of
view of the man holding the inquiry, I
think he is entitled to the assistance of
properly qualified advocates. A case is
rarely as well presented before any form
of tribunal by an outside mind as it is
by a professionally trained mind.
think that as these inquiries are likely
to affect the public interest to some
extent the Bill as a whole does not deal
with very serious matters-we ought to
require that the case for or against
should be made at the inquiry by
properly qualified people. Therefore I
support the Amendment.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): For the purposes of this Bill
I am prepared to admit that solicitors
are braver and more honest agents, and
probably most of them are able to read
and write. Therefore I have pleasure in
accepting the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Schedule, as amended, agreed to.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be reported
with Amendment to the House.
Ordered, That the House do
resume.
House resumed accordingly,
now
Bill reported with Amendment.
Ordered, That the Report of the said.
Amendment be received to-morrow.
Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture)
Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Employment in agriculture to be insurable employment).
(1) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, employment in agriculture shall
be insurable employment for the purposes of the Unemployment Insurance
Acts, so, however, that Part II. of
the First Schedule to the Act of 1920
(which contains the general list of
excepted employments) shall have
effect as if there were included therein
the employments specified in the First.
Schedule to this Act.
(2) The power of the Ministry of
Labour (hereinafter referred to as "the
Ministry") to make regulations under
the Unemployment Insurance Acts
shall include power by such regulations
made with the consent of the Ministry
of Finance to provide, either generally
or in such circumstances or in such
areas as may be specified in the regulations, and either unconditionally or
subject to such conditions as may be
SO specified, that where manual
labour in agriculture is performed in
Northern Ireland under a contract for
the performance of such labour for the
purposes of any trade or business, the
performance thereof shall be deemed to
be insurable employment in agriculture, notwithstanding that it is not
under any contract of service or
apprenticeship, and such regulations
may, in relation to the performance of
labour which under the regulations is
to be deemed to be insurable employment in agriculture, and in relation to
persons performing such labour, make
provision as to the manner in which
and the periods for which contributions
are to be paid, as to the manner in
which claims for benefit are to be made,
and as to the circumstances in which
such persons are to be deemed to be
unemployed; and where, in accordance
with such regulations, the performance
of such labour as aforesaid is deemed
to be insurable employment in agriculture, the person for the purposes of
whose trade or business the labour is
performed shall, in relation to the person performing the labour, be deemed
to be the employer of that person for
the purposes of the Unemployment
Insurance Acts.
*
*
*
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 1, page 1, to leave out
from the beginning of the second line
277 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

6 APRIL 1936
Committee
of sub-clause 1 to "as" in line 10, and
insert instead:
"every employment in agriculture that
is an employment specified in Part I
of the First Schedule to the Act of
1920 shall be an insurable employment
for the purposes of the Unemployment
Insurance Acts unless it is an excepted
employment and Part II of the said
Schedule (which contains the general
list of excepted employments) shall
have effect as if Paragraph (a) were
omitted therefrom."
The object of this Amendment is to
make it clear that not all employments
in agriculture are included in the scheme
by virtue of Clause 1 (1). The Clause
places it beyond doubt that only agricultural employments within the categories specified in Part I of the First
Schedule to the Unemployment Act of
1920, are insurable. Sub-section (2), of
Clause 1, however, gives power by regulations to bring into insurance the
"manual contractor" class, which is not
included under Clause 1 (1).
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 1, sub-clause (2), on
page 2, line 10, to leave out "under
any contract of service or apprenticeship"
and insert "an employment specified in
Part I of the First Schedule to the Act
of 1920."
This Amendment is consequential upon
the previous Amendment. The words
"under any contract of service
or
apprenticeship" were used to refer to the
kind of employment covered by Part I
of the First Schedule to the Act of 1920.
Since Part I of the First Schedule to the
Act of 1920 is now referred to specifically under the amended Clause 1 (1),
it is more convenient that Clause 1 (2)
should refer to it rather than contain the
explanatory words previously inserted
in it.
Mr. HILL-DICKSON: I would like
to ask the Minister if under this Clause
father can take his son
apprentice.
a
as an
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): It does not prevent a father
taking his son as an apprentice, but it
does prevent him making his son an
insured person.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Reduced rates of contribution in respect of persons employed
in agriculture).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of Clause 2 to add: :-
"Provided that, notwithstanding
anything contained in any enactment,
no contribution under this Act shall
be payable by an employer in respect
of any person in his employment after
such person has attained the age of
sixty-five years."
I mentioned, on the Second Reading,
what seemed to me the sad case of the
man over 65 years of age and his employer. I think, perhaps, I was
accused of making out that the sadness
of the case affected the employer rather
than the employee. That is not the case
at all, and if anybody deduced that from
what I said last Friday I can only suggest that he has entirely misunderstood
the situation and also failed to appreciate the situation, particularly as it
affects the rural districts. What are the
facts as regards the man of 65 working
on a farm in a rural district? He has
probably been working for the same employer for very many years, and has
attained the age of 65 during his period
of service. When he arrives at that particular time of life he probably gets the
pension of 10s a week to which he has
contributed. But as he has probably
been drawing 25s a week he stands to
go down 15s a week. Fifteen shillings
out of 25s is rather a big drop for a
man of 65, whereas if he remained in his
employment he can have his 25s a week
or what not and his 10s as well. That
seems to be his point of view, or part of
his point of view.
The employer may not wish to part
with the man of 65. That man may
have used some of his 65 years in learning the job and he may do that job
rather well. The suggestion in the Bill,
and I am afraid the suggestion was made
more definitely by the right hon. Gentleman himself, is that the employer ought
not to be encouraged to employ a man
of 65. He should employ a younger
man, and if he does not employ a younger
279 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
man then he should pay the 4d a week
as a sort of tax. But when the point
was put to us the right hon. Gentleman
did not distinguish between a man employing a man of 65 and a man employing his own son-in-law. He thereby
escapes employing somebody else, and
should pay the 4d just as much as the
other man. If the House allows the text
to stand there will be a distinct encouragement to the farmer to get rid of the poor
old man of 65, because the farmer is
already paying about 10d for health and
pensions in respect of that man. There
will be a distinct inducement to get rid
of that man rather than pay another 41d
on top of the 10d.
Now consider the man himself. What
are his feelings? He does not like to
think his employer is paying money for
him for benefits he is not likely to get.
He has been with the farmer for years
and probably likes that farmer, or he
would not have stayed so long. But
there is something more important even
than that and this is a point that seems
to be neglected or not thought of throughout the Bill before us. You have got a
man of 65 and he has got to live somewhere. Where does he, in fact, live?
He probably lives in a house belonging
to or convenient to the farm where he
works. If he lives in a house belonging
to the farmer, and the farmer has to put
him away, where is that man going to
live? You are exposing that man of 65
to the distinct chance of having to find
a house somewhere if he can. What are
you going to get out of him? You are
going to get as many times 41d a week
as there are men in agriculture over 65
in Northern Ireland. The advantage to
the State is microscopical; the injury to
the man is really great.
I do hope the Committee will insist
that words will be inserted in the Bill
which will protect the man of 65 from
the injustice that is likely to be done. I
think the words put on the Paper are
sufficient for my purpose, but if the Committee thinks they can be tightened up
I shall be most happy to consider it on
the Report stage. In the meantime I
ask the Committee to accept the principle
for which I have contended.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am unable to accept this Amendment, but the
Minister of Labour will explain the
reasons better in every way than I can.
Mr. ANDREWS: I can quite understand my hon. and learned Friend's point
of view. This is just one of those
matters that can be approached from two
points of view. But there is no reason
why an injustice should be done to the
man who is over 65 years of age, because
it is quite open to his employer to keep
him on, and I cannot think that the
balance is SO delicate as to whether
he will keep him on or whether he will
not, that 44d would just make the difference with the employer. The reason
for this Clause is that there shall
not be actual financial advantage
to the farmer by keeping him on in
preference to taking a younger man.
That is really the point which influences
the Government to a very appreciable
extent.
And again, this is no new Clause. We
have had a similar provision in the
general unemployment insurance scheme
and it has worked smoothly. And, notwithstanding the fact that I have had a
good many years experience now, I have
not heard of any industrial employer
objecting to it. The choice as to whether
he keeps the man on or not remains with
the employer. If, however, he does keep
him on he is obliged to pay a contribution
for him, it is true, notwithstanding the
fact that the man is no longer entitled to
benefit, but he is probably receiving
instead 10s a week pension. This provision is partly an actuarial one, because
if the fund loses contributions in this
or other ways, of course the contributions would have to be raised to
the general contributor, else the
benefits would be affected. But I can quite
appreciate another taking a different
view in connection with this matter.
Still, as I said, having had actual experience of it, I have no hesitation in
recommending that it should remain.
or
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
feel a good deal of sympathy with the
hon. and learned Gentleman's Amendment, but I have already expressed considerable doubts as to the actuarial basis
of the scheme, and I am sorry to say that
my symapthy will not go to the extent
of allowing me to vote in such a way as
to upset the balance which has been carefully thought out. Although I have
281 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 6 APRIL 1936

Committee
absolutely no information as to the
number of men over 65 employed in
agriculture in Ulster, I should think the
proportion is fairly heavy, because the
agricultural labourer lives a very healthy
life and lives to a great age.
At least
that is my experience.
But I hope the Government will bear
the hon. and learned Gentleman's point
of view in mind and, if necessary, introduce an Amendment at some future stage
to the scheme, so that the employer does
not have to pay insurance on a man who
can receive no benefit under the scheme.
For that reason, and because I am
already in some doubt as to the actuarial
basis of the scheme, I shall be unable to
support the Amendment.
Mr. HILL-DICKSON: Is there anything in the Bill to prevent the employer
reducing by 4d a week the wages of a
man who draws the old age pension of
10s a week?
Mr. ANDREWS: No. There is no
power in the Bill to do that. Of course
if the employer wishes to do that, and the
man is agreeable to it, there is no harm
in their entering into a contract of the
kind. I cannot think it would be
frequently done.
He
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
Senators who have spoken have represented the farmers as being very unsympathetic people, and suggest that they
will reduce the wages because the men are
drawing 10s a week pension.
I may
point out that it is understood in all
insurance schemes that a man may pay
money and never draw any benefit.
pays for someone else in more need of
it and is very thankful he is not called
upon to ask for benefit. It is the same
with the State pension to a man who
attains the age of 65. Really his employer has never paid sufficient to justify
or qualify him for the amount of the
pension, and there is no harm in continuing to pay the 4d afterwards, because
it is all going to make up for the 10s a
week the man is receiving. I can see
no hardship in the employer being asked
to continue to pay that contribution. I
am sure he will be satisfied that he is paying something towards an old servant
and entitling him to get 10s a week.
As has been pointed out by the Minister of Labour, this has been done in the
The
Very
insurance scheme for years and years
and there have been no complaints.
There are some firms I know turn a man
off at 65, but they are very few.
great majority keep the men on.
often the man of 65 years of age has
greater experience and is probably able
to do the work better than a younger
man, and, therefore, his employer is
quite entitled to keep him. I do not
think there should be any support for
the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I seem to have more to
reply to than I expected when I rose
somewhat prematurely a few moments
ago. I must take the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister. I am not a bit convinced by what he said. It seems to me
that he is anxious to exploit for the
advantage of the State the charity of the
employer of a man of 65.
Mr. ANDREWS: I rather take exception to that word exploit. (Laughter.)
Mr. LESLIE: I shall be happy to
substitute any other word the right hon.
Gentleman can suggest. Shall we say,
take advantage of the man's generosity?
It comes to much the same thing. However, it is not only that, but the right
hon. Gentleman did not disavow the
attempt to dictate to the farmer in the
direction of employing younger men.
The right hon. Gentleman spoke also of
the fact that there is in the industrial
code a similar provision to that to which
I am objecting. That, undoubtedly, is
the case. But I cannot accept at this
time of the day the suggestion that two
wrongs make one right. I really cannot, and again, we cannot deal with the
industrial code at the moment. It is not
before us.
Those of us who think the
proposal wrong can, at all events, protect
ourselves by refusing to accept it. As
regards the actuarial basis of which the
hon. and gallant Member (Captain
Perceval-Maxwell) spoke, he certainly
did attack the actuarial basis on Friday
last, and showed there was not any basis
left. I think that was admitted by the
noble Viscount when he said we were
going to be £40,000 down in the first
year. The right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Labour, again mentioned the
actuarial basis. I suggest that if he is
going to use an argument like that he
ought to give us some figures as to the
283 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
number of men employed. Presumably
the Minister has those figures, and I do
not think anybody else has.
The last attack on the Amendment
was by way of a suggestion that I had
implied that the farmer was unsympathetic to the man of sixty-five. I
cannot accept that, because if the farmer
were unsympathetic he would not keep
the man on until he was sixty-five years
of age. That seems to settle itself.
Then something was said about the
general tendency of insurance, which
takes us back to the actuarial question
again. I rather thought the suggestion
was going to be made that if a man
insures his life and dies his heirs and
executors were to continue to pay the
premium. I do not know whether it was
intended to go quite so far as that, but,
although there is undoubtedly a necessity.
-and a very much misunderstood necessity to pay insurance without getting
anything directly for it, there is
absolutely no precedent, except the industrial one, that I know of for continuing to pay the premium after the
event insured against has come to pass.
I always hate disturbing the even tenor
of this Committee, but I am afraid on
this occasion I cannot withdraw the
Amendment. If it is defeated then, at
all events, the workman of sixty-five
years of age will know who is responsible
if what I think possible does actually
happen.
Mr. ANDREWS: With the permission
of the Committee might I say just a word
or two. In the first place I would be
equally sorry with the hon. and learned
Gentleman to disturb the even tenor of
The Mayor of Londonderry
Cunningham, The Right Honourable
Samuel
Dickson, Mr. James H.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount.
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Davison, Sir Joseph
this Committee, but I have a duty to perform just as he has. I quite agree with
him that two wrongs do not make a
right, but my point of view is that this is
a wrong that is not done in either case.
May I further point out to the hon. and
learned Member that there is no inducement given to the employer to pay off
the older man at sixty-five, because when
he takes on the younger man he will have
to pay the contributions for him.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN : I think this
Amendment is a very reasonable one. I
do not see why when a man comes to
sixty-five years of age he should be
victimised under this scheme. With
reference to the suggestion that he might
even have to stamp his card, I might ask
the Minister how many public boards
have in their employment men of sixtyfive, and when those men attain the age
of sixty-five and secure the sixty-five
pension either deduct that from their
wages, threaten to reduce their wages, or
actually reduce their wages in such a way
that really the older men do not benefit
under the scheme. I certainly think that
a man who comes to the age of sixty-five
-and particularly the man with a large
family, and possibly with an invalid wife
or child in that family-is entitled to the
consideration not only of this House but
of the public, and there should be no
pinching, as we might call it, as far as
he is concerned. Under those circumstances I have great pleasure in supporting the Amendment.
Question put, "That those words be
inserted."
The Committee divided: Contents, 9;
Not Contents, 11.

CLAUSE 6 (Reckoning of agricultural
contributions).
285 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

6 APRIL 1936
Committee
*
(1) In reckoning the number of agricultural contributions standing to the
credit of an agricultural contributor at
the beginning of any benefit year,
benefit shall be deemed to have been
paid in respect of a contribution at the
rate of five-sixths of a contribution for
each of the first twelve days' benefit
paid to him in any benefit year and at
the rate of one-third of a contribution
for each further day's benefit paid to
him in that benefit year, and the
number of contributions in respect of
which; as so reckoned, no benefit has
been paid (including any fraction of a
contribution) shall be taken to be the
number of contributions standing to
his credit.
*
*
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in Clause 6, page 5, line 38, after
"year" ("the beginning of any benefit
year') to insert "agricultural."
In moving the next four Amendments
which stand in my name I would like to
say that they are of a purely drafting
nature and are designed to make it clear
that for the purposes of ascertaining
what agricultural contributions have been.
exhausted by receipt of benefit under the
ratio rule only agricultural benefit shall
be taken into account.
Amendment agreed to.
Further Amendments made:
Page 6, line 2, after "days" ("first
twelve days' benefit") insert "agricultural'.
Page 6, line 4, after "day's" ("further
day's benefit") insert "agricultural”.
Page 6, line 6, after "no" ("no benefit
has been paid") insert "agricultural".-
Viscount Charlemont.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

(2) For the purposes of this section
the expression:-


"3
Period of yearly hiring means a

Period of half-yearly hiring
means a period of less than fifty
but not less than twenty-one consecutive weeks;
Appropriate proportion " means in

hiring twenty-five per cent., and

per cent.
(3) The amount of the repayments
made in any year in accordance with
regulations made under this section.
shall be deducted-

under sub-section (3) of section

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in Clause 9, page 8, line 40, at
the end of sub-clause (2) to insert (3)
In calculating the amount of any repayment to be made in accordance with the
regulations made for the purposes of this
section fractions of a penny shall be disregarded ".
The object of this Amendment is to
give effect to what is a customary
practice in such matters, that is, to disregard an odd halfpenny which may
appear in the sum due by way of refund
to the employer or employee in respect of
half-yearly or yearly hiring,
calculated under the provisions of the
section.
a
as
Mr. LESLIE: I think I understood
from an all too brief conversation with
the Minister that the net effect of this
Amendment may possibly be 3d twice a
year, that is to say that that fraction
only arises as regards the total and does

287 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

to

The CHAIRMAN: I am
sorry to
interrupt the hon. and learned Member,
but this must be mentioned when the
question is proposed "That the Clause.
stand part of the Bill".
Mr. LESLIE: That will be after the

The CHAIRMAN: Yes. There is no

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in Clause 11, page 12, line 20, to
leave out from "deemed" ("deemed to
be insurable employment in agriculture")
to the end of line 21, and to insert "not
to be an excepted employment".
This is a drafting Amendment which is
consequential on the Amendment to
Clause 1 and the Amendment proposed
to Clause 16 (1). Under the Amendment to Clause 1, sub-clause (1),
employment in agriculture in an excepted
employment will not be insurable. The
class of person who is dealt with in
Clause 11, sub-clause (4), is a person
who, although employed in an excepted
employment, is in the circumstances
"deemed to be insurable". The proper
expression is that his employment shall
be deemed "not to be an excepted
employment".
Amendment agreed to.
Question proposed, "That the Clause,
as amended, stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: Now I can resume the
argument which, I am sorry
to say,
I brought up in the wrong place a

Committee

suggest taking out, and I want them to
think back a little to last Friday to what
was said on the Second Reading as
regards what I might describe as the
border-line man, the man engaged, for
instance, in cutting hedges. I think the
man engaged in mowing a lawn was also
mentioned. He is the border-line man
of whom it would be very difficult to say
whether or not he was engaged in agriculture. There will be cases like that,
more perhaps on the larger farms. The
words in this sub-clause, which are merely
a reproduction from early enactments-I
think they occur in the Act of 1920 and
perhaps in other cases-may be very well
suited to industrial occupations, but I
do not think they are suitable for
inclusion in a purely agricultural matter.
moment or two ago. The question which
I asked before, and which was not

equally in the week between one class
of employment and the other. There is
provision apparently for dealing with the

agreement not being reached-and it is
possible that the farmer and his worker
may not agree, just as the industrial
worker and his employer may not agree
-I think we ought to be told what is to
happen. I mention the matter now so
that any trivial alteration which is necessary can be made on Report.
Mr. ANDREWS: Where the employment is really half and half it will have
to be determined by the Ministry with
an appeal to the High Court if agreement cannot be arrived at between the
parties concerned.
Mr. LESLIE : And is there power
given to the Ministry in this Bill, or in
any Act construed with it, to enable

power to intervene in that case?
Mr. ANDREWS: Oh yes.

Clause, as amended, accordingly

of
(2) In determining any question as
to whether any employment in which
a person is or has been employed is or
was employment in agriculture, regard
shall be had to the nature of the work
on which he is or was employed, rather
than to the business of the person by
whom he is or was employed.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
12, page 12, lines 28 to 33, to leave out
sub-section (2).
I want the Committee to think rather
carefully about this sub-clause that I
any
I think we should be better without
them, because if the idea of Parliament
in accepting this Measure is, as I believe
it to be, that people employed on the land
are to have what is called the benefit of
unemployment insurance, if Parliament
wants that, I think Parliament wants the
matter handled on broad lines. They do
not want any niggling here or
niggling there. They do not want it
said, "Oh, that fellow does not come
within it, because he cuts hedges.' We
do not want that at all. I think it
would help us if these words were not in
the Bill, because the suggestion in these
words is that regard is to be had to the
nature of the work the man is employed
on, and not to the business of the person
who employs him. As I appreciate this
Bill, it is just the other way round. I
think the test as to whether a man is
employed or not for the purposes of this
class of insurance is whether he would
be employed or not if the farm was not
there. I think that is the test. It will
help you to apply that test if these words
are taken out. Therefore, I beg to move
that the Committee do take them out.
Mr. ANDREWS: I think this subclause really ought to be retained, just
for the sake of preventing any niggling
such as the hon. and learned Member
suggested might occur. The sub-clause
is to make it clear that it is the nature of
the work performed rather than the
business of the person by whom the
worker is employed that shall determine
under which scheme the man shall be
insured.
291 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Andrews]
You can
Let me take a case in point.
quite well have a manufacturer who also,
in connection with his manufactory, has
a farm. His employees who work in the
factory would be industrial workers and
insured under the industrial scheme; but,
on the other hand, if they were working
on his farm, notwithstanding that his
main work was industrial, his employees
on the farm would be insured as agricultural workers. I think that the subclause is necessary to clarify the position.
Mr. LESLIE: There seem to be two
diametrically opposed points of view, but
it is entirely for the right hon. Gentleman. If he thinks he can work the Bill
better with the words in, then I ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 12, after line 33, to insert:
"Provided that if any person concerned as a party in such question so
desires, and gives notice to the Ministry, the question shall be determined
summarily by the court having jurisdiction in the district wherein such
person concerned was resident at the
time the question arose."
This is a distinctly more difficult
question, and one which will, perhaps,
give rise to more pronounced differences
of opinion. As the Committee know, I
am not in favour of the decision of
questions by a Ministry-I say "a"
Ministry; it is just the same for all the
other Ministries as for the Ministry of
Labour. I am not in favour of the
decision of questions by a Ministry when
they are questions that, in my judgment,
and in the judgment of many other
people, should be decided by the established courts of the country. That is, I
am opposed to any growth of bureaucratic
power in this manner. Although we cannot attack to-day the provisions of the
law as regards industrial insurance, we
can prevent what I regard as a vicious
system from extending.
Here we are dealing with questions
which may arise as to whether employment or a class of employment is employment in agriculture; that is to say,
whether the employment in question, if
there is employment at all, is such as
ought to come within the ambit of this
not.
particular Bill or
That is the
question before us. Questions relating
to agriculture are likely to arise in the
rural districts. I do not think any
questions will arise, let us say, in or
about the city of Belfast. The questions
are purely rural questions; they ought
to be decided in the rural districts, and
they ought, as I suggest, to be decided
by the court. If the Committee agree
with the proposal on the paper the effect
will be that the questions will be decided
at petty sessions. They will be heard by
magistrates having jurisdiction over, and
sitting in, the petty sessions district
where the dispute arises. That court will
have to decide these questions according
to law, and there will be the usual possibilities of appeal.
I do not think the Ministry should
settle these matters. There is the usual
possibility of appeal to the High Courtthat is so, I presume, in this case as in
others but that is not of very much use
for the small farmer or agricultural
labourer. He wants his justice short and
quick, and he wants it at home, and I
cannot see any reason why he should not
have it. These are things not for
decision by a Ministry, because everyone
knows the Ministry are interested parties.
If the point at issue is whether a contribution is to be paid or not, that is a
matter in which the Ministry are
interested party. I think I am right in
saying I have heard the right hon.
Gentleman himself admit that, though
I am not sure whether in this House or
elsewhere, but I think the right hon.
Gentleman has said on one occasion, when
some question arose as to whether a claim
should be insisted on, that he was
trustee for the Fund.
an
a
I think we all regard him as a trustee
for the Fund. If a man is a trustee for
a fund, he is an interested party to any
dispute arising in relation to that fund.
I do not think you can get away from
that. The less the Ministry have to do
with deciding questions as between man
and man the better. I am quite sure
that they take steps to do it as well as
they can. They probably appoint some
outside lawyer, but they appoint him ad
hoc more or less, and nobody is present
at the inquiry. The inquiry is held in
private, not with open doors, and that
does not appeal to my sentiments at all.
293 Unemployment Insurance Bili- 6 APRIL 1936

Committee
I think that in this new undertaking on
which we are embarking to-day we should
see that the court of inquiry is a court
above suspicion, whether the suspicion be
well founded or ill founded. I think we
should let the decision rest with the
courts of the country, and not with the
Ministry. Accordingly, I beg to move
the words on the paper.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to support
this Amendment, and lest the opinion be
held that my opinion is tainted in any
way, I should like to ask the House to
accept the arguments of the Lord Chief
Justice of England with regard to officialdom in these matters. I think it is most
unfair that a man should go on paying
contributions to the Ministry, and that
the Ministry should accept those payments week by week until he becomes 65
years of age, and then raise a question
for the first time as to whether that man
was legitimately insured or not. I know
myself of cases where respectable and responsible people in a district were making
contributions for a period of five years.
They made sworn affidavits that they
were quite satisfied that the employment
was quite legitimate as between
ordinary employer and employee, but
the Ministry held that there was no contract of employment, and that the insurance was not bona fide or genuine.
an
Mr. ANDREWS: May I ask the hon.
Gentleman if he is referring to pensions?
Mr. MCALLISTER: Yes. It is most
unsatisfactory to have these inquiries
held behind closed doors.
What you
want in any scheme of insurance or anything else is the confidence of the public.
If you are going to accept insurance contributions for a period of five years from
an ordinary working man, and then set up
a tribunal which is not a public court, but
which sits behind closed doors, and
declares to that man that he is not a bona
fide insured person, you are not securing
the confidence of the public.
What is the advantage of refusing the
Amendment of Senator Leslie ? It is not
on the ground of expediency; it is not on
the ground of cost, because there is no
less expensive tribunal than the ordinary
summary jurisdiction court. It is a
court held in public, a court where the
public are entitled to be present to hear
the pros and cons and to hear the
evidence. The judgment is not the judgment of a nominee of the Ministry, the
judgment is the judgment, according to
the Summary Jurisdiction Act, of an
independent man. I support in the
strongest possible manner the Amendment which has been so ably proposed by
Senator Leslie.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
must say that I am not for the Government tribunal. I believe that it shakes
the confidence of the public. I do not
believe that any Government Department or its nominee-can get away
from its mind the fact that it has a
certain duty to do as a Government
Department. I have seen something of
tribunals from Government Departments,
and I must say that I feel very anxious
at the extension which is being made of
these tribunals in nearly every Bill that
is passed either in this Parliament or at
Westminster. There might have been
some objection in the past to bringing
these cases before local courts of summary jurisdiction, because they were
composed of lay magistrates who had no
knowledge of the law. But now the cases
at petty sessions are heard by a trained
lawyer, or should be heard by a trained
lawyer if the appointments are properly
made; a man who should be in a position
to adjudicate on these matters very much
better than the ordinary tribunal which
the Ministry might choose to give a
decision. Many points, I can see, may
crop up. There may be legal points
which nobody but a man trained in one
of the most complicated branches of the
law could possibly decide. Points of
partnership and points affecting the law
of master and servant may crop up. It
is almost impossible to say what law may
have to be considered. I have the
strongest objection to this question being
considered by lay tribunals of any sort,
and for that reason I support the Amendment.
Mr. HILL-DICKSON: I wish also to
support the Amendment. The general
public are of opinion that Government
Departments should not be in a position
to decide these matters.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The decision on questions of scope and insurability is vested in the Department
subject to a right of appeal to a judge
of the High Court. In the early days of
295 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
Viscount Charlemont]
the Act of 1911, certain appeals could
be taken to the county court, with a
further right of appeal on any question
of law to the High Court. In the interests of simplification and the avoiding
of conflicting rulings, the right of appeal
to the county courts was later discontinued. To bring questions of insurability within the jurisdiction of courts
of petty sessions would, I think, not be
a step fraught with any great advantage.
They are pre-eminently matters which
should be co-ordinated and dealt with by
one authority.
Mr. ANDREWS: I think I can
satisfy my hon, and learned Friend (Mr.
Leslie) on this occasion. I do not like to
see him shaking his head, because that
would look as if he were not going to
give me quite a fair trial. However that
may be, these matters of insurability are
difficult, but they have to be settled by
somebody. In the first instance, they are
settled by the Ministry, but a contending party can be professionally represented at the inquiry. The second course
is that there is a right of appeal. That
appeal must be to some court, and the
only difference, as far as I can see,
between the Government and the hon.
and learned Senator is that he holds the
appeal should be to the county court,
while I hold that it should be to the
High Court. I feel I am right in
advocating that it should be to the High
Court for the sake of getting uniformity
of decisions.
My hon. and gallant Friend (Captain
Perceval-Maxwell) behind me has said
with some force that he does not like
the decision of an interested party. He
prefers it should be given by the court of
petty sessions now that any objections
there may have been to that court in the
past have been removed. But that is not
so in my view, because you have at any
rate a dozen resident magistrates over
the country and each one of those may
take a different view. Therefore you
would have inequality of administration,
and there is nothing more likely to give
dissatisfaction or create actual injustice
than that, while the High Court decision
is given for all claims within Northern
Ireland. The hon. and learned Senator
has said that the
that the Ministry are an
interested party.
Mr. LESLIE: They are.
Mr. ANDREWS: They are interested
but only in doing justice and seeing that
justice is done. The learned Senator
referred to some remarks of mine made
I do not know how many years ago, when
I described myself as a trustee of the
Fund. I never knew that trustees
wanted to act unfairly. I have never
found that, and I have a good deal of
connection with a great many trustees.
For these reasons I think there is really
not the danger that the hon. and learned
Member anticipates either of the
Ministry or the High Court doing a
wrong thing. It is unthinkable.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: It
seems to me the real point at issue has
not been answered. Who is going to pay
the expenses of the High Court? (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) The ordinary
lower courts of the land are comparatively cheap, just as cheap as the
Ministry. The other point that has not
been dealt with is this: when we passed
the Summary Jurisdiction Bill it was
said that the effect of the Measure would
be so to reduce the number of conflicting
decisions that the law would be restored
to its former place of honour in the
minds of the public.

suggest the very minimum, and I think

Clause, as amended, ordered to stand

CLAUSE 13 (Provisions as to persons
not ordinarily resident in the United
Kingdom).
Any person employed in agriculture
who fails to prove that he is ordinarily
resident in the United Kingdom for
at least nine months in every year
shall, for the purposes of the provisions
of the Unemployment Insurance Acts
relating to the payment of contributions, be treated in respect of that
employment as though he held a certificate of exemption under the Act of
1920.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move to leave out Clause 13 and insert
the following new Clause:
Provisions as to certain persons not
insurable in respect of employment in
agriculture.
13.
(1) The following persons
shall not be insured under the Unemployment Insurance Acts in respect of
employment in agriculture, that is to
say-
(a) persons
not domiciled in the
United Kingdom who are
ordinarily resident outside the
United Kingdom;
(b) persons who are, to such extent
and in such circumstances as may
be prescribed by regulations made
by the Ministry under the
Unemployment Insurance Acts,
ordinarily dependent for
their
Long, Mr. John A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
livelihood on occupations in agriculture that are not insurable
employment;
and if any question arises as to
whether any person is by virtue of this
section not insured in respect of
employment in agriculture, the question shall be decided by the Ministry
subject to the provisions of section ten
of the Act of 1920.
(2) Where by virtue of this section
a person is not insured in respect of
employment in agriculture, that person shall, for the purposes of the
provisions of the Unemployment Insurance Acts relating to the payment
of contributions, be treated in respect
of that employment as though he held
a certificate of exemption under the
Act of 1920."
It
The object of the Amendment made
by paragraph (a) is to bring the definition of migratory labourers into line
with the amended definition inserted in
the United Kingdom Bill in the Committee Stage in the House of Lords.
is obviously desirable in view of our
Great
reciprocity arrangements with
Britain that the same definition should
operate in both schemes for the purpose
of defining a class of persons who are to
be left outside the scope of the scheme.
If the definitions were different in the
two schemes, it might happen that a
person coming to Northern Ireland for
pccasional agricultural work would be
held to be insurable in Northern
Ireland, and would, therefore, have contributions paid in respect
respect of him,
although if the same person had gone to

[Viscount Charlemont]
work in similar circumstances in Great

to Great Britain he would not be insurable there, and his Northern Ireland
contributions would not be valid for
benefit purposes there.
be the next Amendment, the insertion
of the words on the Paper. It is also
difficult to keep out the Amendment
standing in my name on the top of page
3 of the Paper. Therefore, with your
permission, if the Committee will agree,
I will discuss the whole points more or
less together. It is obvious as regards my
Amendment on page 3 that if the noble
Viscount's second Amendment is carried
my Amendment would become nonsense
and could not be put. At the same time
it has some little bearing on the situation. What I was going to say when I
came here this afternoon was that I did
not agree with the noble Viscount's
first Amendment leaving out the Clause
as it stands in the Bill at present. Since
then, if I understood correctly, he has
told us he wishes to make the Amendment which was introduced in the House
of Lords which I have not myself seen.
I do not really know how far the House
of Lords have got with the English Bill.
I have not seen a final copy of it.
One result would be that the claims
made in Great Britain by all agricultural
workers who previously obtained contributions in Northern Ireland would have
the
to be specially examined at
Exchanges in Great Britain to ensure
that the contributions paid in Northern
Ireland could validly have been paid in
Great Britain in similar circumstances.
This would obviously create inconvenience
both to the administration in Great
Britain and to Northern Ireland agricultural workers claiming benefit.
Paragraph (b) deals with a new class
of case. The Statutory Committee drew
attention to the risk of unduly large
claims for benefit from small holders. It
is also possible that small holders and
others in the absence of special provisions
might be required to pay contributions
without any prospect of ever drawing
benefit against them. The types of cases
which may arise may be very varied and
it is not possible to lay down exactly in
advance of experience how they should
be dealt with. Power is, therefore,
given to exclude by regulations persons
who are dependent for their livelihood
on agricultural occupations which are.
not insurable. The employers of persons
SO excluded will have to pay
the
employer's share of the contributions.
The sub-section as amended provides
that any question of the scope of the
scheme arising under this provision shall
be dealt with under section 10 of the
Act of 1920.
Question proposed, "That those words
be left out."
Mr. LESLIE : I think it would be
better for me to address the Committee
on that Motion rather than on the one
which might be expected to succeed it.
It is obviously very difficult-and indeed
the noble Viscount did not succeed in
doing it himself to keep out what would
At all events, we may take it that the
proposal now is to substitute a new
Clause for Clause 13 in the Bill. We
can discuss it with your permission on
these lines. I think we ought to do so
before we decide to leave out the existing Clause. The difficulty, of course, to
my mind is-and it must have been
apparent to the Government-I do not
know whether the Government have seen
my Amendment on page 3 before they
put their Amendment down. I am
inclined to think they had not. In any
case it would seem to suggest that the
Government and I must both have been
experiencing some difficulty with the
Clause as it stood. My difficulties were
indicated in the Amendment I put on
the Paper. Whether the noble
Viscount's Amendment is his own or
whether it is the House of Lords new
sub-clause I am not certain.
I will try to deal with the new
Clause suggested. First of all we have
to look at paragraph (a) which introduces, I think for the first time, the
question of domicile. So far as I know
the question of domicile has not been
raised in any other enactment in what
is called the social insurance code. I
think this is an entirely new proposal.
There is no particular reason that I
know of why we should not proceed on
these lines if we see fit, but if we do it
301 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 6 APRIL 1936
Committee

the Ministry. We cannot allow the question involved by domicile to be decided
by the Ministry. That is a matter for
the court. I have to take the unusual
course of opposing the suggestion that
this Clause be left out.
seems perfectly obvious-and I imagine
the Committee and also the Government
will agree that any questions arising
out of the matter of domicile are not
questions for decision by the Ministry.
Domicile gives rise to most difficult
questions on points of law. I do not
know how the Ministry would propose
to start to decide whether a person was
or was not domiciled in the United
Kingdom.
think
I would imagine if the
Ministers concerned gave their close
and complete attention to such questions
they may arrive at some decision sooner
or later, but they would have to consult
their legal advisers or legal text books.
Domicile, I need scarcely remind the
Committee, is a very difficult problem.
Everybody knows or should know that
you cannot have two domiciles at once.
At the same time you are always liable
to have the domicile of origin suspended
by the domicile of choice, and it is difficult to sort out any entanglement that
may arise from that. To pass from that
to the expression towards the last line
but one of sub-clause (1) of the proposed
new Clause-the reference to a person
who "by virtue of this section is not
insured." I find some difficulty in
following what is meant by the expression "not insured," whether it is meant
to be the same thing as not insurable,
I do not quite follow that. I imagine
the "not insured" there means the same
thing as not insurable. I do not know
if it is worth clarifying or not, but the
main point that I object to is that I do
not think the Ministry can possibly be
allowed to decide this question of
domicile, and I am not at all satisfied in
my mind that the question of domicile
ought to be introduced.
If the House of Lords has done it I
should like to know the reason. Therefore, I am rather in a difficulty at the
moment as to whether the Committee
ought to accept the noble Viscount's
first Amendment, or having regard to
the decision taken by a majority of the
Committee a few minutes ago as regards
the question of insurability and so on,
whether it would not be better for the
noble Viscount to withdraw this Motion
and bring it up on Report, when we can
see what alteration can be made as
regards the decision of the Ministry. I
do not know whether that will appeal to
Mr. ANDREWS: The whole question
appears to me to be one of deciding
I do not
where a person is domiciled.
any of
us desire that persons
should come to Northern Ireland from
outside the United Kingdom for the
purpose of obtaining temporary employment and not intending to live here,
especially when we know that we have a
surplus population in the United Kingdom and in Northern Ireland who are
not able to find employment.
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman will pardon me. Evidently I did
not make myself clear. I ought to have
explained more clearly to the Committee that in introducing domicile we
were substituting domicile for ordinary
residence which appeared in the original
Clause. I ought to have laid more stress
on that.
Mr. ANDREWS: The point you want
to make is deciding whether a man is
domiciled in Northern Ireland or not.
That is the point you want?
Mr. LESLIE: That is the point.
Mr. ANDREWS: Your intention
would be that a person who is the son
of a parent born in Northern Ireland
should be looked upon as a person
domiciled here. Is that your point?
Mr. LESLIE: Not necessarily, probably that would be the legal effect. I am
sorry to make such a long interruption.
That was why I had to trot out either
parent. I could not exclude the possibility of a person concerned being
posthumous or filius nullius.
Mr. ANDREWS: The matter, I
admit, is a difficult one. Take for
example a soldier who returned from
India after seven years' service there.
he is not ordinarily resident in the United
Kingdom. He is domiciled there, and
the Amendment, therefore, allows such a
man to be insured. If residence only
303 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE

[Mr. Andrews]
were the test he could not be insured.
The object of the Amendment is to bring
this provision into line with the corresponding provision in the British Bill,
which was recently amended during the
Committee stage in the House of Lords as
regards these particular
classes of
persons, namely, migratory labourers and
the small farmer who may obtain employment very occasionally from a neighbour. That is, a man may have eight or
nine acres of land and work for a time
with neighbouring
a
farmer. The
question arises, should he or should he
not be insured if employed?
Captain
PERCEVAL-MAXWELL :
Judges and anybody trained in the law
know what the word domicile means. It
is obvious, from the statement of the
right hon. Gentleman, that the Ministry of Labour is very vague about it. I
quite see there is a point in this Amendment, but what puzzles me and what I
object to is why the Ministry propose
deciding technical questions on domicile
when they can get the ordinary law
courts to decide them. In this case it
may be said that it will probably save
expense to leave it to the Ministry. As
to the case of soldiers who have been
resident out of the country, it is wellknown that anyone conversant with the
text books ought to be able to decide a
prima facie case whether a man is
domiciled in this country or not. It is
a highly technical subject, and I do not
propose to go into it. I agree entirely
with what the hon. and learned Senator
has said.
There is a great deal of confusion in
the public mind as to what the word
domicile means. If I lived in the County
of Antrim a great many would say I am
domiciled there. That is not the fact at
all. I am domiciled in the country of
my father, the country I was born in.
I may change that domicile from time to
time, but the question of whether a man
changes it or not is a question of evidence.
I cannot understand why the Ministry
arrogate to themselves the right, without making any safeguard, to say who is
going to try the case, regardless of
whether he knows anything about the law
or does not. I cannot understand why
they throw away the advantage they have
of taking the case to the ordinary courts
of law where the judges are competent to
try the case and know the law of evidence.
The question of whether a man has
changed his domicile is a difficult one.
It is a subject on which many people
specialise all their lives, and it arises a
great deal in will cases. It is not a
question that ought to be decided by a
layman.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to consider this matter before the
Report stage. If the hon. and learned
Senator will withdraw his opposition we
will consider the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: I suggest to the Government that the Clause stands with the
omission of the three lines between the
end of paragraph (b) and commencement
of sub-clause 2. Of course my Amendment cannot possibly be moved now. The
noble Viscount has withdrawn his
Motion.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: I have
temporarily withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: And bring it up on
Report.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
After line 40 insert: ("Provided that
any person who proves that he, or one of
his parents, was born in Northern
Ireland shall, for the purposes of this
section, be deemed to be ordinarily
resident as aforesaid").
Amendment not moved.
and

FIRST SCHEDULE.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
At the end of paragraph (1) of the
First Schedule insert:
accordance
Employment in the harvesting of
corn, the pulling of flax, the gathering
of potatoes or in making hay except
where the person employed proves, in
with regulations made
under the Unemployment Insurance
Acts (Northern Ireland), 1920 to 1936,
that he is normally employed in insurable employment.
Mr. LESLIE: The Committee is, of
course, in possession of the words I have
put on the Paper, but I want to read to
the Committee what was paragraph (2)
of the English Bill when it was brought
into the House of Commons at Westminster. What happened to it after
that I do not know. We have just heard
that the noble Viscount's information is
more up-to-date than mine as to what
happened in the House of Lords, but this
particular paragraph of the Schedule was
excised in the House of Commons. It
ran as follows:-
Employment in the harvesting or
gathering of flowers, fruit, peas, or
potatoes, or in the tying, training, or
picking of hops, or in the peeling of
onions, except where the person employed proves in accordance with
regulations made by the Minister
under the principal Act that he is
normally employed in insurable employment.
That paragraph disappeared at Westminster, and in any case, I do not think
it would have been particularly applicable, certainly not in its wording, to our
conditions in Northern Ireland. We had
a good deal of discussion on the Second
Reading as to the desirability of keeping
out of this scheme what was described as
the "in and out worker," the man who
does an odd two or three days' work in
agriculture and never does any more for
twelve months. It occurred to me that
we might put on the Paper something
on the lines of the paragraph which
originally occurred in the English Bill in
order to consider whether we ought to do
anything to leave out those purely
seasonal employments which may be of
such very short duration, and employments which are not taken up as part
of the man's regular work.
They are not his principal source of
livelihood; they are just chance jobs, and
those that occurred to me are harvesting
of corn, pulling of flax, gathering of
potatoes, and making hay. Making hay
is very easy to discuss, because you will
see the local rector turning out with his
curate and his wife and man servant and
maid servant to make hay. To some
extent something of the same nature prevails in connection with the other operations. I therefore thought it was worth
while to put an Amendment on the
Paper, although I do not expect the Committee will accept it in its present form,
if at all, but I thought we ought to put
it down and discuss it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT The reason
for the deletion of a somewhat similar
provision which originally appeared in
the British Bill, was that there is already
power under the existing Unemployment
Insurance Acts to except from insurance
any employment that is ordinarily
adopted as subsidiary employment only,
and not as the worker's principal means
of livelihood. This is to be found in
paragraph (j), Part 2 of the First
Schedule of the Act of 1920. On the
other hand, Clause 11, sub-section (4)
makes provision whereby any person who
is engaged in an employment which is
ordinarily carried on as a subsidiary employment and proves that it is his
principal means of livelihood, may, by
regulation, be brought back into the
scope
of the agricultural insurance
307 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Viscount Charlemont]
scheme. This, again, is an obviously
necessary provision. For these reasons I
am unable to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the noble
Viscount has given us the information
which, if I had had a little more time,
I should have found out myself. I ask
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave withdrawn.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I wish to say
a few words on the First Schedule as
regards the second paragraph referring
to the exclusion of private gardeners. I
would like an assurance from the Government that if in the British Bill provision
is made for the insurance of these persons
the Northern Government will amend
this Bill on the same lines. I will give
the Committee
concrete
a
case to
illustrate the injustice of the position as
it exists under the Bill in its present
form. I have two brothers in my
service. One is engaged planting
potatoes in a field separated from my
garden by a hedge, and the other is engaged planting potatoes in the garden
itself. The first man will come within
the scope of insurance and his brother
will not. I do not think there is any
justice in such a case as that. They are
practically both engaged in the same.
occupation, but while one is insured the
other is not, and it is not just to that
man. I would like to be assured that if
such a gardener should be included in
the British Bill he will be included in the
scope of our Measure.
Mr. ANDREWS: The position at
the moment as regards gardeners is that
the British Government has referred the
matter back to the Statutory Committee
upon which we are represented, and that
Committee will report on the subject.
There is power given to the British
Government to bring gardeners into the
scheme if they so desire, and there is a
Clause in the Bill now before this House
giving us similar power. In the meantime gardeners are out of the Bill.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: In the cities
and towns we have a type of man known
as a casual gardener who goes round
working a day at this house and a day
at another house helping people in their
gardens. I hope they will receive consideration, because they are very industrious and are anxious to make a
living for themselves.
Mr. ANDREWS: If they are insurable, the first person to employ them in
a particular week would be the person
to stamp their card.
Mr.
McLAUGHLIN: The Minister
says, "if they are insurable." I want
an assurance that they will be insurable
under this Bill.
Mr. ANDREWS: I thought I made
it clear that the matter was being considered at the present time by the
Statutory Committee, who will report as
to whether gardeners in the employment
of a private individual will be brought in
or not.
Now

question arise from the difficulty this

back to old Acts of Parliament ?

of
CLAUSES 12 (Determination
questions as to insurability), and 13
(Provisions as to persons not ordinarily
resident in the United Kingdom).
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
Page 12, line 28, leave out from the
beginning of the line to end of line 4
on page 13 (the second paragraph of
Clause 12, and the whole of Clause 13),
and insert instead:-
(2) In determining any question as
to whether any employment in which
a person is or has been employed is or
was employment in agriculture, regard
shall be had to the nature of the work
on which he is or was employed rather
than to the business of the person by
whom he is or was employed.
Provisions as to certain persons not
insurable in respect of employment in
agriculture :-
13. (1) The following persons shall
not be insured under the Unemployment Insurance Acts in respect of employment in agriculture, that is to
say-
(a) persons not domiciled in the United
Kingdom who are ordinarily
resident outside the United Kingdom;
(b) persons who are, to such extent and
in such circumstances as may be
prescribed by regulations made by
the Ministry under the Unemployment Insurance Acts, ordinarily
dependent for their livelihood on
occupations in agriculture that are
not insurable employment;
and if any question arises as to whether
any person is by virtue of this section
not insured in respect of employment
in agriculture, the question shall be
decided by the Ministry subject to the
provisions of section ten of the Act of
1920.
313 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 7 APRIL 1936

Report Stage
(2) Where by virtue of this section
a person is not insured in respect of
employment in agriculture, that person
shall, for the purposes of the provisions of the Unemployment Insurance
Acts relating to the payment of contributions, be treated in respect of
that employment as though he held a
certificate of exemption under the Act
of 1920.
Determination of certain questions
by court of summary jurisdiction:-
14. Notwithstanding anything to the
contrary contained in sections twelve
or thirteen of this Act as to the
manner in which questions arising
under those sections are to be determined, a person concerned as a party
in any such question may, if he so
desires, and upon giving such notice as
may be prescribed by regulations made
by the Ministry, apply to the court for
the determination of that question.
In this section the expression "the
court" means the court of summary
jurisdiction for the district in which
the person making the application was
resident at the time the question arose.
Mr. LESLIE: The House will perhaps
have heard from the Committee that sat
yesterday afternoon that that Committee
was in great difficulty over the new
Clause 13 which was suggested by the
noble Viscount in substitution for Clause
13 as it originally appeared in the text
of the Bill. The Clause was withdrawn
for further consideration during the
night, and since the Committee sat yesterday there has been a conference behind
your Chair, as the saying goes, with the
result that it appeared to those forming
the conference party that the new Clause
13 might very well be inserted in the
Bill provided that it was treated in
exactly the same way as Clause 12 is to
be treated by the Amendment which was
passed yesterday afternoon. Accordingly
the Amendment I am now moving reproduces the Clause that was on the Paper
yesterday, but in order that the Clause
may be treated in the same way as Clause
12 is to be treated it is necessary to
postpone the particular proviso which
related to Clause 12, and which was
adopted yesterday. Owing to that postponement transposition-for it is
nothing more-the noble Viscount, with
his usual courtesy, has suggested that as
I was responsible for that proviso it was
for me to move the Amendment.
I do not think the new Amendment,
so far as it concerns the new Clause 13,
can now cause any serious difficulty. If
a question as regards domicile does arise.
it can only be in individual cases, and if
it goes to a court of summary jurisdiction
the way of appeal lies open. The first
step to be taken is to leave out all the
words in Clause 12, from line 28 on page
12 to the bottom of line 4 on page 13,
in order that we may insert first of all
a new Clause 13, which is on the Paper,
beginning "13 (1)." That having been
done, we insert as a separate Clause the
proviso we passed yesterday. The point
is that it is more convenient to use a
separate clause for a proviso when you
want to cover two Sections, as they will be
when the Bill becomes law. Accordingly
the transposition is made in my Amendment, and some necessary alteration has
been made in the words. No alteration
in principle has of course been made, and
indeed it would not be possible to make
any alteration in principle having regard
to the decision of yesterday, but opportunity has been taken to add a few words
of clarification.
So far all would have been well but
for one of those occurrences which are
beyond the control of man. In this case
the printer took a turn. It may be
remembered that yesterday afternoon I
suggested that sub-clause (2) of Clause 12
should be omitted. The matter was discussed and the suggestion was withdrawn,
and sub-clause (2) of Clause 12 remained.
The printer, I presume, was not satisfied. At all events, he paid me the compliment of leaving it out, and that subclause has disappeared from the Bill
before us and must, of course, be put
in again, but as it was really never taken
out by the Committee I do not make any
Motion on the subject. I beg to move.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
As one who spoke in favour of
the Amendment submitted by the hon.
and learned Senator opposite I am perfectly satisfied with the agreement that
has been come to. The only doubt that
remains in my mind is whether the
labourer will realise that he has this
power to go to the court, or whether he
will feel obliged to submit to what the
Ministry may ordain. However, this is
a point that can be dealt with in other

ways,
and
not
it is
of sufficient
importance for me to ask the House to

Therefore I agree to the Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In accepting the Amendment I should like to say
that I think the hon. and gallant Member who has just spoken underrates considerably the intelligence of the Ulster
labourer. Outside infirmaries and asylums
I have never met one who was not able

The following Amendment stood on

McLaughlin :-
New Clause.
All agricultural
workers who attain the age of sixtyfive and who on or before the thirtyfirst day of December, nineteen
hundred and thirty five, had cards

Mr. SPEAKER: With reference to
the Amendment standing in the name of
Senator McLaughlin, I regret to say that
that Amendment is out of order, because
it simply deals with old age pensions,
which are outside the scope of the Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture) Bill.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I am sorry the

Resolved, "That the Bill be now read

Resolved, "That the Bill do now

And a Message was sent to the House

Motion for Adjournment.
AGRICULTURAL WORKERS AND
PENSIONS.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday."
Mr. MCLAUGHLIN : I desire to
bring to the notice of the Government
the particular case of the agricultural
worker I referred to yesterday on the
Committee stage of the Bill we have just
passed. Although this man had been
insured and had had his cards stamped
since 1926, when he attained the age of
65 years the Ministry of Labour declined
to grant him a pension on the usual statutory ground, namely, that he was not an
insurable person. The Ministry came to
that decision despite the fact that during
all those years they had accepted his
contributions. I venture to say that it
would be illegal for any commercial firm
to do such a thing. I know of no insurance company that would be allowed
to accept premiums for a similar number
of years and then refuse to pay the
insured person the endowment when it
fell due. If the Government had had
any consideration at all for this class of
people they would have consented to an
Amendment being inserted in the Bill we
have passed to-day enabling them to
receive their pension when they attained
the age of 65.
This poor man is now 65 years of age,
and for years he has been paying contributions in the hope and expectation
that he would receive a pension when he
reached 65 years of age. Then he is told
by the Government that he is not an
insurable person. The result is that he
has got to wait until he is 70 before he
can get his old age pension. He is even
deprived of the benefits of the Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture) Bill
which we have just passed, because he
is now over 65. The Government should
have sufficient consideration for a man
of this type to allow him a pension at
65. Surely a case such as this deserves
consideration, and I bring it to the personal notice of the noble Leader of the
House in the hope that he will see that
justice is done to this man. It is not
fair or just that any individual, much
less a poor labouring man, should be
treated in this manner. If an insurance
company treated a person in this way
we would take another method of dealing
with them. For these reasons I hope this
case will be favourably reconsidered by
the Ministry, and this man given the
pension for which he contributed for so
many years.
Adjournment-
Old Age Pensions
7 APRIL 1936
Mr. McMAHON: I rise to support the
plea that has been made by Senator
McLaughlin. There are a great many
anomalies in connection with the Insurance Acts. I could cite cases where
persons have been compelled to pay
insurance contributions, and then when
death took place the widow was informed
she was not entitled to a pension, and
no one benefited in any way from the
contributions that had been paid. I
should like to refer specially to the case
of bookmakers' clerks. I understand the
law compels a bookmaker to stamp the
card of his clerk. I happened on one
occasion to have a case of a man who
had worked for many years in a bookmaker's office. He was suffering from
tuberculosis and eventually died, and
when his widow made application for at
pension it was refused on the ground that
he was employed in an illegal trade.
Just imagine the Government compelling
this man's employer to stamp his cards
and then when he died to refuse his
widow a pension on the ground that he
was engaged in an illegal trade. The
result was that his wife died brokenhearted within three months. I think
this is a terrible hardship, and I hope
legislation will very soon be introduced
to remedy such a state of affairs.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would like to ask
Senator McLaughlin if the case he has
mentioned is the same as the one referred
to in his Amendment to the Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture) Bill,
because in that Amendment he asked that
100 stamps should qualify for a pension
at 65, whereas 104 stamps are necessary. .
Mr. McLAUGHLIN : If the hon.
Senator looks at my documents he will
see that this man has more than 120
stamps on his cards-more than sufficient
to qualify him for the pension at 65.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: If an
application for repayment of health and
pensions contributions is made in such a
case the Minister of Labour will be prepared to consider it, subject to deduction
in respect of any medical or other benefits
received or provided. In general, I am
not really competent to answer questions
about old age pensions and insurance at
such short notice. The question which
Senator McLaughlin has raised was embodied in an Amendment which was ruled
out of order, but if he is not satisfied
with the answer I have given and will
raise the subject again in a short form on
Thursday I will furnish him with an
answer.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Lest
it might seem from the speech of
Senator McLaughlin that the Government
are careless in dealing with these claims,
I should like to say that I have taken
up many of them on behalf of poor
people, claims for pensions, unemployment benefit, and health insurance, and
I must admit that in all cases the
officials concerned endeavour to give the
claimants the benefit of any doubt there
may be about the validity of the claim.
In one
or two cases in which I was
interested I was agreeably surprised at
the way the officials assisted the claimants
to get over difficulties and to establish
their claim.
It would not be fair if it went out
to the public that the Government
deprived those who were legitimately
entitled to benefit or pensions from receiving them without just cause.
All my
experience is to the contrary. Of course,
there are bound to be cases where there
is ample ground for turning down the
claim, and if we knew the full facts
of the case mentioned by Senator
McLaughlin we would be in a better
position to judge whether the Ministry
were or were not justified in refusing the
pension. My whole experience of these
claims is that they are dealt with very
sympathetically and fairly by
by the
Ministry.
Mr. McALLISTER: I should not have
taken part in this discussion had it not
been for the speech just delivered by
Senator Joseph Cunningham. An
Amendment which this House passed
yesterday to the Canals and Inland Navigation Bill will in future protect people
from being treated by a Government,
tribunal in the way an applicant for an
