which I was concerned. It was a
question of whether the woman was entitled to the old age pension. She had
answered the questions on the official
form, and the question arose as to
whether there was bona fide employment,
or whether the stamps were paid merely
for the purpose of getting unemployment
benefit. This impartial tribunal set up
Adjournment-
Old Age Pensions
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
by the Government to decide the point
adopted a procedure which I have never
before encountered in all my experience
of courts of law.
The applicant and all her witnesses were
brought before the tribunal one by one.
While one witness was being heard every
other witness for the applicant was excluded from the room and made to wait
in another room, while all the time the
witnesses for the Ministry of Labour were
present. If that is the way a so-called
impartial tribunal interprets the rules of
evidence it is well we passed the Amendment we did yesterday to protect some,
at any rate, of these people when they
appear before a tribunal.
.
Sir JOSEPH DAVISON: From my
experience I can bear out what Senator
Joseph Cunningham has said as to the
sympathetic manner in which these
claims are dealt with by the Ministry
of Labour. I have taken up hundreds
of cases on behalf of persons applying
for the old age pension and at all times
I have found that the officials of the
Ministry deal with them in a most
generous spirit. I would like to point
out to Senator McLaughlin that if there
is any misrepresentation in a proposal
made to an insurance company the company will not pay when the insurance
matures. I do not know whether or not
there was any misrepresentation in connection with the case he has mentioned
here, but my experience has been that
the officials of the Ministry are always
most helpful, and take the most generous
view possible of any application for a
pension or benefit.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: In reply to the
observations that have been made I
should like to say that I am not finding
fault with any official. At any time
that I have approached any official-
Mr. SPEAKER: I am afraid the hon.
Member is out of order, because on the
Motion for the Adjournment he is
entitled to speak only once.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I only want to
reply to the speeches of other hon. Members. I want to say that two or three
months ago I wrote a letter to the Ministry about a widow who has a family to
support, and I am still waiting for a
reply. That letter was written two or
three months ago. Of course, I am a
Member of the Opposition and we do not
count. But we are here, and while we
are here we are just as much entitled to
consideration as any other hon. Senator.
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon.
Member will respect my ruling.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I had not intended
to take part in this discussion, but as
one who has had a great experience of

no Government could possess more considerate officials than those of our Government who deal with these claims and
appeals.
Mr. McMAHON: Are you always
satisfied with the results?
Mr. QUIGLEY: Are you always
satisfied?
Mr. McMAHON: Very nearly always.
Mr. QUIGLEY: The claimants receive
every consideration. There are cases in connection with both unemployment benefit
and pensions where the claimants were not

every consideration to claimants. Senator
McAllister has referred to the officials
of

Grey Squirrels
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 9th April, 1936.
The Senate met at Five minutes after
Twelve of the Clock noon, Mr.
SPEAKER in the Chair.
Message from the Commons.
The House of Commons have agreed to
the Amendments made by the Senate to
the Canals and Inland Navigation Bill
and the Unemployment Insurance
(Agriculture) Bill without any Amendment.
Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have to
report to the House that His Grace the
Governor of Northern Ireland, in the
name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his
Assent to the following Bills agreed upon
by both Houses:
Canals and Inland Navigation,
Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture),
Pier and Harbour Order (Larne
Harbour) Confirmation),
Bradshaw's Divorce.
Question to Minister.
GREY SQUIRRELS.
Mr. LESLIE: The question on the
Paper calls for a word or two of explanation which, by permission of the
House, I will give. I have not myself
seen any grey squirrels in Northern
Ireland, nor have I heard definitely that
anybody else has seen them. But complaints have reached me of an increase in
red squirrels, and fears have been expressed lest the grey squirrel might
arrive and prove to be the nuisance that
he invariably is. Although I have no
knowledge of grey squirrels being in
Northern Ireland, I have heard rumours
of their having been seen in County
Meath, and County Meath is no great
distance from Northern Ireland. If they
are in County Meath they can very easily,
I imagine, get up to Northern Ireland,
whether they come by land or water. I
do not mean to suggest that the grey
squirrel is a good swimmer, nor even
is the red squirrel very active in that
way. The only water journey of the red
squirrel is that recounted by Miss Beatrix
Potter in "Squirrel Nutkin," but it will
be remembered that he and his companions made rafts of twigs and sailed
across to Owl Island.
However that may be, it is unlikely
the grey squirrel may come to us from
England in that way, but there are complaints of an increase in numbers, and
I observed in "The Times" a day or
two ago that the extension appears to
be more to the west. If there is any
danger I would suggest we ought to take
steps. No doubt we have power to take
them under Section 8 of the Act, which
is conveniently called the Musk Rat Act.
The House will remember that the noble
Viscount when introducing that Measure
referred to the possible invasion of the
grey squirrel. The point is that if action
is to be taken it ought to be taken at
once. An Order can be made at short
notice, but an Order under the Musk
Rat Act has no effect until it has been
confirmed by Parliament, and, therefore,
even if I do not get any answer beyond a
broad negative I think it will serve to
draw public attention to the question. I
beg to ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government has any reason to
suppose that any grey squirrels are at
large in Northern Ireland, and whether
any Order, under Section 8 of the
Destructive Imported Animals Act
(Northern Ireland), 1932, or otherwise,
to prevent the introduction of such
squirrels is, or will be put, in force.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: So far as
the Government is aware there are at
present no grey squirrels at large in
Northern Ireland. No Order to prevent
the introduction of such squirrels has yet
been made, but the question of the issue
of such an Order is at present under
consideration.
I would, however, like to add a
little supplementary information. on
that from my knowledge of natural
history. The danger really is
is that
there may be grey squirrels in
the Free State, but opinion on that
matter is rather conflicting. Some people
say that there are, and others incline
to the negative. The real danger would
be not that grey squirrels might be imported from England but that they might
come up North. On the other hand the
nature of the country might have a good
9 APRIL 1936
Grey Squirrels

Adjournmentquestion of its destruction will also be
considered.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: An Order
preventing its importation has not yet
been made, but the question of its issue
is under consideration.
deal to do with the matter, because I think
squirrels do not travel very much except
in the woods in which they are, and unless
there is a continuous chain of woods it
is unlikely that they will come up. There
is also the point of view of the preservation of the natural fauna of the
country. The grey squirrel is far more
carnivorous than the ordinary squirrel,
which is only carnivorous at times.
does a good deal of damage to trees at
times, and is an extremely undesirable
alien. I can only say that my right hon.
Friend the Minister of Agriculture has
the matter in hands, and will do what
he can.
He
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that,
may I ask that when the Order preventing
its importation is under consideration the
LESLIE:
Mr.
struction?
Including its de-
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.

Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That Standing Order No. XXV
be suspended for this day's sitting for the
purpose of taking the Second Reading of
the Milk and Milk Products Bill and the
Motor Vehicles (International Race)
Bill."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have read a Second time
Trew's Divorce Bill; they have passed
the Milk and Milk Products Bill and the
Motor Vehicles (International Race) Bill,
to which they desire the concurrence of
this House.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Trew's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time.”
Milk and Milk Products Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of the
House): This Measure is in the main designed to achieve two objects. The first
is the removal of an anomaly which has
arisen in connection with the distribution
of equalisation payments and bonus payments to creameries for the benefit of
their milk suppliers. As matters stand,
these payments can only be made in respect of milk supplied to creameries
which are registered under the Marketing
of Dairy Produce Acts. A condition of
registration under these Acts is that the
creamery should manufacture butter by
a process of separation of the cream and
skim milk. Two creameries, namely, the
B.B. Farm and the Gleno Dairy, do not
adopt this process of manufacture, and
consequently are. on merely technical
grounds precluded from participation in
equalisation and bonus payments. It is,
therefore, proposed in Clause 1 to place
farmers who supply milk to these two
undertakings in exactly the same position,
so far as bonus and equalisation payments are concerned as those supplying
milk to other creameries. The Clause is
retrospective in effect, so that a fair and
proper adjustment may be made.
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
12 MAY 1936
Secondly, this Measure is designed to
amend the Milk and Milk Products Act,
1934, so as to secure uniformity with a
provision of the Imperial Parliament,
whereby the period of payment of the
Milk Industry Assistance Grant, provided
by the United Kingdom Exchequer, has
been extended for a further period of
eighteen months. As a corollary to this
extension of period it will be necessary to
make a similar extension in the period
during which, in certain circumstances,
repayments have to be made by creameries
of moneys advanced to them out of the
Milk Fund in respect of equalisation payments. These repayments must be made
when the average price paid by the
creameries exceeds 6d. per gallon for a
summer month, or 7d. per gallon for a
winter month. The repayments, of
course, only extend to the amount of any
such excess. Provisions for repayment
will apply to the B.B. Farm and Gleno
Dairy in similar manner as to the other
creameries.
Advantage is also being taken of this
Measure to make a minor adjustment in
regard to the defrayal of expenses incurred in connection with the operation of
the Milk and Milk Products Acts. At
present only expenses incurred by the
Ministry of Agriculture and the Joint
Milk Council are recoverable from the
Milk Fund, and it is proposed that expenses incurred by Ministries other than
the Ministry of Agriculture in connection
with the Acts should be similarly recoverable. I beg to move, "That this Bill be
now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not imagine that
the Senate as a whole will find any particular difficulty in accepting the Bill which
the noble Viscount has introduced to our
notice this afternoon. Certainly there is
nothing contained in the Bill to which I
myself should be disposed to object. One
hopes, of course, that no difficulty will
arise between the two departments in the
allocation of the expenses referred to, but
if that should unfortunately prove to be
the case it would seem that the Ministry
of Agriculture will have the last word,
and that, perhaps, is at it should be.
To my mind the interest in the Bill lies
more in its Title, and what that Title may
lead to. The Title of the Bill is:
To amend and extend certain provisions of the Milk and Milk Products
Act (Northern Ireland), 1934.
Therefore, it occurred to me that the
field is thrown open for almost any
Amendment of the provisions of the principal Act which any of us may desire to
move. There is one point to which the
House may remember I directed attention
when the original Bill was before us, and
which I want to go into at the appropriate
date again. I want to take the opportunity afforded by this Bill to ask this
House to try to do something for those
very small suppliers to whom I alluded
when the Bill of 1934 was before us. I
am rather emboldened to take this course
by something that occurred a few days
ago in another place, not in relation to
this particular Bill but on the general
question of the Vote for the Ministry of
Agriculture. The point was put up from
North Derry, from South Derry, and from
North Armagh to the effect that there will
be some slight difficulty in country districts.
I am going to ask the Committee to add
a sub-section to the original Act, which
will, I think, do something at all events
to mitigate certain hardships. I am going
to put it in very general form in order
that I can obtain, if possible, the support
of the Committee to the principle, and
then it might be found desirable to amalgamate the new proposal with what was
Sub-section (7) of the original Act, which
permitted a farmer to sell to his own
labourers. I think that has possibilities
of extension, and I submit that that
would be a fair addition to the Bill before
I do not for a moment object to the
Second Reading.
us.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: What the
hon. and learned Senator has said will
apply more to the Committee Stage, and
the question can be dealt with then. With
regard to the question of any difference
of opinion arising between two departments as to the amount of expenses to be
recoverable, I would say that the principal
Ministry to be affected, other than the
Ministry of Agriculture, is the Ministry
of Finance, which lends accommodation
to the Milk Board and is also responsible
for the Superannuation Fund of Civil
Servants. That is really the only Ministry
affected other than the Ministry of Agriculture.
Unemployment Order
Unemployment Order
THE SENATE
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Motor Vehicles (International Race) Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is a
short and simple Measure couched in the
same terms as the Motor Vehicles (International Race) Act, 1935. The main
purpose of the Bill is to enable the Royal
Automobile Club to exclude from roads
in the area of the race every car not
specially exempted which does not carry
a badge, provided by the club, at a
charge not exceeding 2s 6d. The money
obtained from the sale of these badges is,
of course, used for the financing of the
event. I beg to move, "That this Bill
be now read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Unemployment Insurance (Reduction
in Weekly Rates of Contributions)
Order (Northern Ireland), 1936.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move:
I beg to
That the Order in Council made by
the Governor of Northern Ireland on
the 16th April, 1936, under Section 18
of the Unemployment Act (Northern
Ireland), 1934, as amended by the 4th
Schedule to the Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture) Act (Northern Ireland), 1936, for reducing, with effect
from 6th July, 1936, the weekly rates.
of unemployment insurance contributions payable in respect of employed
persons of 18 years of age and upwards,
be hereby approved.
The object of this Resolution, to the
terms of which I now ask the approval of
the House, is to obtain confirmation of
the provisions of an Order in Council
recently made, whereby the weekly rates.
of unemployment insurance contributions.
payable in respect of persons of the age of
18 years and upwards will be reduced with
effect from 6th July next, which is the
beginning of the insurance year 1936-37.
The reductions in these cases will amount
to ld a week from the contribution payable by the worker, and a similar amount
from the contribution payable by the
employer.
The Order in Council has been made in
pursuance of Section 18 of the Unemployment Act (Northern Ireland), 1934, following on a similar order which has been
made in Great Britain, and which takes
effect from the same date. It is one of
the advantages of our re-insurance
arrangements with Great Britain that we
are enabled to make similar reductions in
the contributions payable in Northern
Ireland, while, at the same time, maintaining the finances of our fund in parity
with those of the fund of Great Britain,
and these reductions will, I am sure, be
welcome to workers and employers alike.
On the basis of the numbers of persons
at present engaged in insurable employment, the total amount of the reduction
will be approximately £42,000 a year in
respect of the workers' contributions and
a similar amount in respect of the employers' contributions-an aggregate of
£84,000.
The actual loss to the Unemployment
Fund in respect of these reductions in the
contributions will be £126,000 a year,
because, of course, the Exchequer itself
contributes to the fund annually an
amount equal to half of the aggregate of
the employers' and workers' contributions,
and, therefore, the Exchequer contribution to the fund under this head will also
be reduced by about £42,000 a year.
The total saving to the Exchequer of
Northern Ireland, including the amount
of £42,000, which I have just mentioned,
will be approximately £50,000 a year,
after account is taken of the financial
adjustments in the "parity" position and
in the contribution payable by the Government of the United Kingdom under
the Re-insurance Agreement.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Electricity (Supply) Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : Before
formally moving the adjournment of the
House I should like to have the views
of hon. Members as to whether they will
be prepared to take all stages of the
Electricity (Supply) Bill on Thursday.
This is a Money Bill, and consists of one
Electricity Bill
Electricity Bill
12 MAY 1936
paragraph intended to extend indefinitely
the period during which the Government
guarantee shall operate in respect of loans
raised by the Electricity Board for
Northern Ireland.
the
The Government desire that
Measure shall receive the Royal Assent
at the earliest possible moment, as they
are advised that the present is a favourable
moment for the issue of stock, and that
delay might lead to a less satisfactory
reception. Hon. Members will be aware
that the low rate of interest at present
ruling in the money market may be
adversely affected by certain international
factors.
Mr. LESLIE: Speaking for a moment
about the Electricity Supply (Amendment) Bill, to which the noble Viscount
has referred, I am afraid I have to say
that, as at present advised, I shall ask the
House not to give that Bill a Second
Reading. I say, "as at present advised,"
deliberately, because I got the actual Bill
only this morning, and I want to be a
little more sure of my ground than I am
at the moment before I come to this
House with the definite proposal I have
indicated. I must
not say anything
about the Bill at the moment, but I am
afraid it is not a Bill which we ought
to pass, and therefore I do not think we
ought to take all stages so quickly, as
the noble Viscount suggested. It is a
Bill of only one Clause, and it is a question of either rejecting it or passing it.
It is not a Bill which can be subject to
any alteration in Committee, and, as far
as that goes, there would be no great
objection. I think Thursday is a little
too soon to take it.
If the noble Viscount would propose
to take all stages on Tuesday, perhaps
that would give hon. Members time not
only to read the one-paragraph Bill
itself, but to read over the statutes which
they might think had some connection
with it, and form an opinion as to where
the passage of the Bill will take them.
I cannot myself consent to taking all
stages of the Bill on Thursday. I would
not take the same objection on Tuesday,
because by that time we should all have
had time to make up our minds.
Mr.
MCALLISTER : I am always
very much against any proposal to take
all the stages of a Bill on any one particular day. I do not think it is possible
for any Bill to be properly scrutinised
and digested in such restricted time. I
respectfully suggest it might meet the
case if we took the Second Reading on
Thursday, and the remaining stages on-
Tuesday.
Mr. LESLIE: No.
I stick to my
Mr. McALLISTER:
suggestion that the Second Reading should
be taken on Thursday and the remaining
stages of the Bill on Tuesday.
Captain PERCEVAL - MAXWELL:
Could the noble Viscount possibly give us
some indication of the date on which this
important event is going to take place?
Viscount
event?
CHARLEMONT:
-
What
I
Captain PERCEVAL MAXWELL:
The date when interest rates are going to
be raised as indicated in your Second
Reading speech. I must confess it is a
great surprise to me to learn that interest
rates are likely to be raised so soon.
should have thought we would have had
cheap money for at least another fortnight. I cannot see why there should be
any difficulty about taking the Committee
Stage of this Bill on any day on which we
take the Second Reading, because it is
admitted there can be no amendment of
the Bill. Therefore I can see no reason
for postponing the Committee Stage. I
think we rather caught ourselves in our
own trap when we recently agreed to
accelerate the passage of the Unemployment Insurance (Agriculture) Bill, because subsequently, during the course of
the debates, some points were raised which
caused us rather to regret our decision to
facilitate the passage of the Measure as
quickly as we had agreed to. I should
have thought that in the case of the
particular Bill with which we are noW
dealing it was a matter of either voting
against the Bill or for it, and surely that
could be done in one day, without bringing the House back to deal with the
remaining stages of the Bill on subsequent
days. I think the course suggested by
the hon. and learned Senator would be
preferable even if it meant the House sitting half-an-hour longer.
335 The late Senator McMahon

THE SENATE
Adjournment
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I asked
for the opinions of hon. Senators, and
I have got them. Would it meet with
the approval of the House if we took all
stages of the Bill on Tuesday? I do not
think the state of the money market will
change very much between Thursday and
Tuesday.
Mr. LESLIE: Yes.
The Late Senator James M'Mahon.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving,
"That this House do now adjourn until
Thursday," I should like to say a few
words regarding the loss we in this House.
have sustained by the untimely death of
Senator McMahon. He was, I understand, only forty-four years of age, and
might legitimately have been expected to
have continued his work in the Senate
of Northern Ireland for a considerable
number of years.
His loss is very much
to be regretted.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I would like to join
in this tribute to the memory of Senator
James McMahon. He was a conscientious
and zealous representative, and I am sure
all hon. Senators will join in this expression of sincere sympathy to his widow
and family in their sudden and irreparable loss.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I should like to
be permitted to say a word in appreciation
of the services of the late Senator
McMahon. He was, undoubtedly, a very
active public representative, and throughout his public career he espoused every
cause in which he was interested with
the greatest sincerity and enthusiasm. A
great deal of his time was devoted to
that very deserving cause, the welfare
of the blind, and he did all that one
man could do to alleviate the suffering
and promote the happiness of the blind
people resident in Belfast. The organisations connected with that class of the
community have already paid tribute to
his services. I will conclude by saying
that the late Senator McMahon was a
very real, loyal and likeable colleague,
and I associate myself with this expression
of sympathy to his widow and the
members of his family.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at twenty
minutes before Four o'clock
until Thursday next.

ORDERS OF THE DAY.
Milk and Milk Products Bill.
Order for the House to be put into

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now resolve

Bill was insufficiently elastic to render it

name appears on the Paper.

Papers Presented
Coleraine Gas Order
19 MAY 1936
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 19th May, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Labour:
Unemployment Insurance (Collection
of Contributions) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1936.
Unemployment Insurance (Subsidiary
Employments) (Agriculture) Regulations
(Northern Ireland), 1936.
Unemployment Insurance (Long Hirings in Agriculture) Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1936.
Ministry of Education:
The Technical School Examinations
Regulations, Amending Regulations No 1,
1936.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Trew's Divorce Bill.
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:
That they had considered Trew's
Divorce Bill, and that the averments in
the Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the Bill, and made Amendment
thereto.
Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the remaining
stages of the Electricity (Supply) Bill.'
-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Coleraine Gas Order.
Viscount Charlemont (Leader of the
House); I beg to move, "That the Draft
Special Order made by the Ministry of
Commerce for Northern Ireland under
Section Ten of the Gas Regulation Act,
1920, with respect to the Borough of
Coleraine be hereby approved.”
I should like to say that one objection
was received to the Council's application
for this Order. It was lodged on behalf
of the Portrush Gas Company, who represented that, in view of the possibility
of the extension of the boundary of the
Urban District of Portrush, within which
they are supplying gas at present, the
Schedule to the Order should be amended
by excluding seven townlands adjoining
the present urban boundary of Portrush.
The Coleraine Borough Council, after
consideration of the objection, decided to
leave the matter entirely in the hands of
the Ministry, and the Ministry decided
to amend the Schedule by excluding five
of these townlands and part of another
townland which was not included in the
objection, but the exclusion of which was
considered advisable in order to provide a
reasonable boundary line for the added
area. The Coleraine Borough Council
have agreed to this.
It will be noted that Clause 5 of the
Order provides for a maximum price of
10s per 1,000 cubic feet. This may
appear high, but the Council had already
obtained powers from Parliament by the
Order of 1934 to charge 10s per 1,000
within the borough. The price in Coleraine at present is 2s 11d per 1,000 cubic
feet.
Clause 6 was inserted by the Ministry
in order to prevent a "dead hand" being
laid by the Borough Council on the added
area.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like the noble
Viscount to tell us if he can whether any
public notice has been given as regards
the proposed terms of the draft Order. I
am obliged to ask because the draft Order
was circulated so that it only reached me
this morning, and, therefore, one had not
much time to make any sort of local
inquiry. But if the locality has not been.
consulted we ought not to be in too great
a hurry to approve of the draft Order
to-day.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
to say the answer is in the affirmative.
On Question, Motion agreed to
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
Questions to Minister.
NEW SCHOOLS IN COUNTY DOWN.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether, in view of
the fact that the building of new schools
in County Down has been unavoidably
delayed for the past two years, he will
give an assurance that the approval of
plans submitted by the Down Regional
Education Committee will be expedited
with all reasonable speed.
The MINISTER OF EDUCATION
(Viscount Charlemont): The answer is in
the affirmative. The Ministry will expedite the approval of such plans as far
as is possible.
DAMAGE BY RATS AND MICE.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. S.
Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he can state the estimated
damage caused by rats and mice in
Northern Ireland, and whether he has
power to compel public bodies to put in
action the powers they possess to abate
the serious loss incurred by these
vermin.
I
Mr. S. CUNNINGHAM: I put down
this question because of the fact that I
received a semi-official intimation that in
Great Britain damage amounting to at
least £50,000,000 a year is caused by
rats and mice. We know that the
Minister of Finance has to adopt every
means possible to raise money, and if we
in Northern Ireland are losing anything
in proportion to the loss in Great Britain.
through the ravages of rats and mice, I
think some action should be taken.
may say that at one time I started to
exterminate the rats and mice at the
place where I live, and I succeeded; but
I do not think I will do it again, because
although I wiped out the rats that infested my grounds I found that my
neighbour's rats came and took their
place. My rats were bad enough, but
my neighbour's rats were worse. Therefore, it shows that unless this extermination is done generally over a large tract
of country it is almost hopeless.
With regard to the damage done by
mice, within the last fortnight I had some
beans of which I was very proud, and in
three nights I lost half of them. I discovered that field mice were responsible,
so I looked up the history of the field
mouse and I read that the amount of
grain they destroy in a cornfield is not at
all in proportion to their size. They are
like squirrels; they store the corn, and
they use a great deal of it. We may be
told that by exterminating rats and mice
we shall destroy the balance of nature.
About the year 1820 there were parts of
Loch Lomond where the brown rat was
unknown. Mr. Colquohoun, in his book,
tells how the brown rat came in, and how,
after two years of fighting, the old Scotch
rat disappeared and the brown rat took
its place. If we could exterminate the
brown rat we would not be upsetting the
balance of nature, because the first brown
rat that came into the British Isles came
in in 1745, and it rooted out the old black
rat which came in with the Crusaders. I
think we
can do without any of those
importations.
I understand that the county councils
and other local bodies have power to
undertake a campaign against rats and
mice, as well as against other vermin,
but they do not appear to take any
action to get rid of the pests. If I allow
my thistles to grow the Ministry of Agriculture can compel me to cut them, and
I think similar compulsion should be used
to exterminate rats and mice. As regards mice, I think I am correct in saying
that of all animals the mouse is the most
liable to carry cancer, and if that is the
case something should be done to keep
it out of the houses of the people. I
sincerely hope the Ministry concerned will
take some steps to compel local authorities to undertake a campaign of extermination of these vermin.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have no
information which would enable me to
give an estimate of the monetary loss
caused by rats and mice in Northern
Ireland.
In reply to the second part of the
question, the Ministry of Home Affairs
has power, under the Rats and Mice
(Destruction) Act, 1919, to take action
if a local authority fails to carry out its
duties under the Act.
I may say, however, that the Ministry
has had no representations recently regarding any need for action.
Some years
Electricity Bill-
Second Reading
19 MAY 1936
ago a complaint was received from the
Belfast Harbour Commissioners, and the
matter was brought to the notice of the
Belfast Port Sanitary Authority, which
is responsible for administering the Act
in Belfast.
According to the last report of the Port
Medical Officer the provisions of the Act
are being enforced in the city, including
the docks and shipping.

Electricity (Supply) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Under the
Electricity (Supply) Act of 1931 the
Ministry of Finance was given authority
to guarantee loans made to the Electricity
Board during the next five years for the
financing of its operations. The existing
guarantee expires on 31st May, 1936, and
the purpose of the Measure now before
the House is to enable the Ministry of
Finance to continue the guarantee. The
maximum amount which the Ministry may
guarantee is contingent on the board's
borrowing powers, which, hon. Members
will recollect, were raised to £2,000,000
a year ago, power being reserved to the
Minister of Commerce in consultation with
the Minister of Finance to raise the maximum limit to £2,500,000.
Hitherto, the work of the board has
been financed by means of short-dated
loans, but a stage has now been reached
when the board desires to fund its indebtedness which, at the end of the last
year of operation, that is to say,
December, 1935, amounted to £1,592,000.
The board's credit is high, and its financial advisers consider that the present
time is favourable for the making of an
issue of stock. While, however, there is
no doubt that the board could successfully
issue stock without the guarantee of the
Ministry of Finance, the Government is
advised that a substantial saving of interest can be effected if the guarantee is
made available.
Hon. Members will appreciate that as
the board is a non-profit earning body,
whose members are appointed by the
Minister of Commerce, this considerable
saving would enure to the direct benefit
of consumers. In view of this fact the
Government believe that it is in the best
interests of the Province that the Ministry
of Finance guarantee should be continued.
Hon. Members may have observed from
the report recently issued by the board
that the undertaking has made remarkable progress. Thus, in the year 1932,
the sales of units amounted to 3,048,000;
in 1933 to 7,195,000; in 1934 to 13,910,000;
and in 1935 to 23,333,000. I am informed
that the sales increase in the first four
months of the current year is not far
short of 50 per cent. on the corresponding
figures for 1935. I beg to move.
Electricity Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill which the
noble Viscount has introduced to our
notice this afternoon is certainly very
short,, but, to my mind, it is not only
an amazing Bill but a preposterous Bill.
Therefore, I must make the Motion for
its rejection which, in the approved
Parliamentary formula, is "That the
word' now be left out and the words
'this day six months' added at the end
of the Motion," made by the noble
Viscount. I think we have, first of all, in
examining the question, to examine once
more what it is we are asked to do. As
a matter of fact, the noble Viscount has
put it to us pretty plainly. He told us
are asked to extend the guarantee,
which may be for some £2,500,000,
from some day in the very immediate
future, for a period which he did not
mention, but which I think is 55 years.
we
Viscount CHARLEMONT: 25 years.
Mr. LESLIE: I thought it was 55.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will tell
you later on.
Mr. LESLIE: At all events, for a
very considerable period. The Act of
1931, the principal Act, Section 27, subsection (3) says "Any money borrowed
by the Board and the interest thereon
may be charged on the undertakings, and
all the revenue of the Board, or on any
specific property forming part of that
undertaking, and shall be repaid within
such period, not exceeding sixty years,
as the Ministry of Commerce may
determine."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is 25
years.
Mr. LESLIE: The Ministry of
Commerce has determined. That, of
course, is the explanation. It is only
25 years, then why are we asked to do
that? I make out from statements in
another place that the amount of saving
will be £7,000 a year. I do not suppose
that that is definite either. It can only be
an estimate, but even as regards that saving
or as regards the proposal generally, we
have to look back at the wording used
in another place in 1931 by the Minister
of Commerce in introducing the original
Bill of 1931, and also the language,
which is practically the same, used by
the Minister in charge of the Bill in this
House in the same year. The language
was, I may say, almost unusually
definite. I quote from the Minister of
Commerce's speech as reported
Hansard of another place.
in
Any such guarantee will not extend
beyond a period of five years from the
date of the establishment of the Board,
by which time it is anticipated that
the Board will have reached a
satisfactory revenue-earning stage."
(Official Report, 12th March, 1931,
Col. 340.)
That was the wording used in the
Commons. The wording used in this
House was much the same, and I need
not detain the House by reading it. But
again last year, as the noble Viscount has
reminded us, power was taken to borrow
an additional £1,000,000. That was
done last year under an Act which received the Royal Assent as recently as
16th July. When the proposal to borrow
the extra £1,000,000 was before Parliament nothing was said in the other House
as to the possibility of continuing borrowing without a guarantee.
Then let us look back at the saving of
£7,000. What is going to be done with
that £7,000? I make out from figures.
given elsewhere that there are some
26,000 consumers at present. The
number given at the end of last year was
about 26,000. Assuming that before the
end of this year the number rises to
28,000 and divide the £7,000 among
them. They would get 5s apiece. Of
course, that is not what would be don
with the money. Again, if you apply
the money in the reduction of the price
per unit, which would be the natural
thing to do, and if we reflect that approximately twenty-three and one-third million
units were being used, the amount of
saving per unit would be microscopic.
The noble Viscount has told us of the
progress of the Board, but I do not think
he has made out any case for suggesting
that there is any abnormal or unexpected
expenditure in contemplation, and he
certainly has not made out a case to the
effect that it is difficult for the Board to
obtain the necessary money. As a matter
of fact, I think I am probably correct in
saying the Board will find they can get
money cheaper to-day in 1936 without the
guarantee than they got it with the guar-
Electricity Bill—
Second Reading
19 MAY 1936
antee in or about 1931.
Some
Then who is to
benefit by this £7,000? If it is the
consumers, directly or indirectly, we must
remember that these consumers are a very
limited class. Everybody in the Six
Counties does not use electricity.
unfortunate people are prohibited from
getting any advantage from electricity.
supplies by reason of living in that somewhat large tract of territory which is not
under the board, but under a private
company. Again, there are some towns
I believe in various places which continue
to use their own supplies. But if the
people who benefit are limited in number
there is no doubt that the liability for
the guarantee will be cast upon the entire
Province. I do not know; it may prove
an onerous burden, but the fact remains
that the guarantee will be by the Province as a whole.
Remember that under the Loans
Guarantee Acts we have a liability of
£13,000,000, which, I believe, we will be
asked at a very early date to increase by
another one and a half million. And when
we reflect on the liabilities under the
Road and Railway guarantees I think we
will say the time has come when Parliament ought to be very careful to see there
is no undue extension of the principle of
guarantees. Therefore in my judgment
the power to give further guarantees
ought to be most carefully watched. There
is not only the responsibility of the public
generally for these guarantees, but there
are other loan responsibilities. There are
in many cases municipal loans affecting a
small number, and there is the general
body of the National Debt with which we
are connected.
a
Now the Board is not, of course,
Government body. I think we were told
that again this afternoon. They are not
supposed to make a profit; they are only
supposed to carry expenses and to supply
electricity as cheaply as possible. I am
afraid I cannot see that they are entitled
to any special terms as against any competitors there may be. Apart from the
whole question of the money involved,
there remains the main principle, and that
is the absolute necessity of keeping faith
with Parliament. Parliament was asked
in 1931 to give a guarantee for five years.
Parliament was told in the plainest possible language that it was not intended to
ask for an extension of that guarantee.
Parliament gave the five years, and I do
not think we can go back on that without
running the risk of imperilling Parliamentary confidence in future in any
similar suggestion that may be made. If
once you start at the top of the
slippery slope we all know the result.
There is not only the abstract question
of keeping faith with Parliament, there
is the more practical question. There
was an expression attributed-I always
trusted incorrectly-to a permanent
Treasury official across the water. He
expressed his disapproval on the ground
that it was opening a serious door. If
the House accepts the proposal it opens
a door, and the very first thing to
pass through that door, I submit,
probably with a grating of gears and the
sounding of a horn, will be a motor. The
Road and Railway Transport authority
will seek to go through, because the
House remembers when the Road and
Railway Transport authority was set up
last year a limited guarantee was given
in exactly the same terms as in the
Electricity Act. Parliament has guaranteed the Road Transport undertaking
for a period of five years, just as they
did in the other case, and, if you are
going to extend the guarantee in the
one case, you have no answer whatever
when we are approached, as I am afraid
we certainly shall be, to give the same
concession once more. I am afraid we
are faced with the plain duty, and that
is to decline to give a Second Reading to
this Bill. Perhaps the only bright spot
in the matter is that we shall not, so
far as I know, and so far as the noble
Viscount indicated in his speech, be doing
any serious harm to anybody, but we shall
be protecting Parliamentary good faith,
and we shall be discouraging future applications to depart from an arrangement
which has been come to. I beg to move.
Captain
PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
This is a Bill which this House must either
pass or reject. We cannot amend it. The
position of those who oppose this Bill
might perhaps be compared to that of
a one-armed pugilist who comes into the
ring with the almost hopeless intention
of knocking his opponent down with the
first blow. Although such a pugilist
would not, I think, be blameworthy if he
lost the fight, I think this House can
hardly accept any great responsibility for
Electricity Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
this Bill, which it must either accept or
reject, but cannot amend. We have been
told from time to time, as we have been
told to-day, that the Electricity Board
is a going concern, and a very prosperous
one.
The consumption of electricity has
obviously increased enormously, and is
going on increasing for the time being.
The Board enjoys a virtual monopoly. It
has power to acquire way-leaves of immense value at very little expense. It is
protected by a tribunal sitting in the
Ministry behind closed doors, whose decisions are not published, and yet it cannot raise money on the same terms as
any other Corporation.
I should like to ask the right hon.
Gentleman, the Lord Mayor of Belfast,
whether the Government has ever suggested guaranteeing the stock of the
Belfast Corporation. The Belfast Corporation pays interest on its stock, and
so does the Electricity Board. For years.
the great railway companies have struggled
on
were a
and raised millions of pounds of
capital. They have had to face very
severe competition, and in the early
days they had to face many claims
and a great deal of opposition. They
also had to pay very high rents
for their way-leaves. They
public service, though perhaps not on quite
the same lines as the Electricity Board
for Northern Ireland.
Were they guaranteed their capital? They were not,
and they had no difficulty in raising their
capital in the open market. Why does
this great Board, which is making so
much money, come to the taxpayers of
this country and desire to be guaranteed?
They say they are going to borrow only
two and a half million pounds. The
money they acquired for the undertaking
amounted last year to
to £13,000,000.
Surely that is sufficient security for
borrowing £2,500,000, without a Government guarantee, if the undertaking is
as sound as it appears to be on the
surface.
The hon. and learned Senator opposite
has made a suggestion that in a few
years' time the Transport Board will be
coming along for a similar guarantee.
Very likely it will. What grounds will
the Government have for refusing such a
guarantee? Is it likely the Transport
Board will be making six times the
amount of money it is likely to borrow?
If the Electricity Board is making nearly
six times the amount it is likely to
borrow, and still has to come to Parliament and ask for a guarantee, the Transport Board, which is not likely to make
anything like that sum would, at least,
have just as much reason and equity for
obtaining a similar guarantee. Perhaps,
however, in spite of all this prosperity
we have heard about, the position of the
Electricity Board is not quite So
financially prosperous at it appears to
be. I am not suggesting there is anything in the least unsound in its position
fundamentally. It is probable that for
many years it will go on earning large
sums of money.
was
But I should like to remind this House
that the grid system originated in
England, and the reason for its origin
an attempt to co-ordinate existing
power units. We in this country
probably copied the system, but without
having the existing power units to coordinate. What we have done is to
large quantities
where there
supply
to
of
power
never
was
places
power before. In the first few years
of this activity naturally a great deal of
power is going to be consumed.
If you
find a starving man and put a great deal
of food in front of him he will eat the
food until he dies. Though there will be
a great consumption of food, when that is
consumed, and when the consuming power
has stopped, you will have to find some
other consumer to take this food.
I suggest that the Electricity Board
have been skimming the cream, and I do
not blame them for that. The cream was
there to be skimmed, and very naturally
they skimmed it, and they did it to very
good purpose. In many cases our street
lighting has not improved by the use of
electricity, and in many places in England
the corporation is going back to gas, because it gives better street lighting. There
are some things for which electricity is
better than gas, and baking is one of
them. But some day sooner or later this
cream will be skimmed and the source of
revenue will dry up unless some other
source of revenue arises.
In England the habit of the farmers is
to live in villages. I think the great bulk
of the farming community in England do
live in villages, but in this country the
farmers are spread in isolation all over
the country. In England you have large
Electricity Bill-
Second Reading
19 MAY 1936
farms of a thousand and two thousand
acres, whereas in this country I understand the average farm is thirty acres.
Therefore you can see that in England
there is a very definite field for the extension of the grid system, because when
the big towns are supplied and linked up
there are still the villages, which will
take, perhaps, an increasing consumption
of electricity. I am not absolutely sure
of my figures because I have only received
them from the Electricity Board, but I
believe it costs something like £350 to
bring a high tension cable one mile.
Thus, hon. Members will realise that it
is very difficult to believe there are many
householders living some distance from
the nearest town from which they
can take their main supply who can
afford this enormous outlay of capital..
I believe that the fundamental error,
which was made by the originators of
the scheme, was that they did not realise
-although they were told that the real
expense in electrical distribution is not
in the generation, but in the transmission. There was a belief at one time
that the standardisation of equipment
would so reduce the cost of equipment
that electricity would become correspondingly cheaper, but I suggest this
has not proved to be the case.
It may
possibly be that in England the cost is
not so great, but in this country, at any
rate, I am told the standardisation of
equipment, and the increase of consumption, has nothing like counterbalanced
the cost of transmission.
There is another point. We have to
realise that commodity prices are low.
Who can say how long they are going
to remain low. Rubber, copper, and
other commodities may rise; in fact,
they have been rising during the past
twelve months, and any appreciable rise
in commodity values is likely to make
the position of the Board very much
more difficult, and to reduce very much
the amount of money the Board will
make. It is, perhaps, as I have said
before, useless to oppose this Bill, but
I think the Bill should have been
amended. We cannot do that, but my
complaint about the Bill is that it is
giving a guarantee up to a certain
amount without any specific control by
the Government of the assets or policy
of the Board. Even if I do not support
the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) by voting against the Bill, I
should like to take this opportunity of
warning the Government that without
such control they may find they have
assumed a serious and permanent
liability. I do not believe for a moment
that at the end of twenty-five years they
will not continue this guarantee if the
Board desires them to do so.
were
In the last five years there have been
large profits and the Government have
still continued the guarantee, although,
as the hon. and learned Senator has
said, it was certainly indicated by the
speeches of Ministers in 1931 that they
So.
not going to do
We may
assume that the same will apply at the
end of this guarantee, and the Government will be saddled with this permanent
liability unless they do something which
will make the liability less serious than
it is at the present moment. If the
Government are stating what is a fact,
that it will make it more expensive
to borrow money without the guarantee,
to reject the Bill at this moment might
cause some serious dislocation in the
Board's plans. We shall doubtless hear
soon from the noble Viscount what
objection he would have if this Bill did
not go through. We shall hear what
inconvenience it would cause. But at
any rate any capital expended on the
undertaking and any efforts made to
bring about the prosperity of the Board's
undertaking have been made largely on
the responsibility of the Government, and
I think so far as the Government can
they ought to see that the Board can
borrow this money.
I
There is another factor which deserves
the attention of this House and of the
Government, and I do not believe the
Government have given it attention.
believe there are geological reasons why
many areas which are very suitable for
population increases can never have an
expanding population because of a
permanent shortage of water supply, a
shortage that can never be made up by
these areas themselves, and there are large
areas in North-East Ulster, particularly,
which contain water catchments, but
these areas being mountainous can never
maintain a population which would make
it worth while for the Electricity Board
to supply them with power.
It is a very
strange thing that the Electricity Board
has been apt in the past to supply areas
Electricity Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
containing practically no water with
enormous electrical power. In one or
two areas I know the Board put in enough
power to supply a large town, and to my
own certain knowledge there is hardly
enough water to supply a small village.
You cannot have factories and houses
without water and drainage.
The Government could help the Electricity Board very considerably if they
were minded to do so by encouraging the
growth of any new centres of population
near existing power lines, and by these
means reduce the Board's overhead
charges. My suggestion is that the whole.
of the water supplies of Ulster should be
placed in charge of a water authority,
which would direct existing surpluses in
the direction most useful for the expansion
of electrical power. This authority could
easily combine the duties of river conservancy as well as this necessary regulation of all the catchment areas. This
would enable the Government to do
something to mitigate the loss which may
eventually accrue to them as the result
of their support of this scheme when the
Electricity Board wishes to extend its
activity in directions which cannot now
be worked economically. Only by this
means can the Government guarantee save
itself from this liability, which I believe
will be an increasing one as time goes on.
Mr. McALLISTER: The case I put
before the House is this, that this Board
has not earned the right to any preferential financial terms. On the contrary,
whether through carelessness in looking
after its own sub-contractors or negligence
on the part of itself, it has carried through
its scheme almost in defiance of the
wishes of the people. You have only to
go into the country to see poles placed
in the middle of small farms, where I
have no doubt if the convenience of the
farmer and the people generally were
taken into consideration these poles could
have been placed in such a situation as
would not have been a source of inconvenience and loss to the farmer concerned.
In addition, I am not in a position to
say whether it is the agents of the Electricity Board themselves or whether it is
the sub-contractors they employ, but it is
a fact that without question and without
the slightest reason they have encroached
upon private property, broken down
fences, and sent men having no experience, good, bad or indifferent, to cut
down trees which have been growing there
for generations.
This Board by its work up to the
present has not deserved preferential
treatment from this or any other House.
I wish, by referring to these matters, one
could make the Board take a reasonable
view of its responsibility, and if it is not
itself responsible for these malpractices.
that have been going on, it should at
least take proper steps by the supervision
of those to whom it lets out the subcontracts. It is on the conduct of the
Board and from general observation and
from the debates and complaints in this
House and complaints outside that I have
no hesitation in saying that this Board
has not earned the right to preferential
treatment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: So many
points have been raised on this Amendment that it would require a cleverer man
than myself and one much more conversant with the deeds and misdeeds of the
Electricity Board to be able to answer
them all. I should like to deal with the
points as well as I can. The hon. and
learned Senator who moved the rejection
raised the point that faith was not being
kept with Parliament. I do not see that
that objection can really be raised. It
is not as if a Bill could be brought in
extending the guarantee to 25 years,
which had originally been asked for five
without the permission and knowledge of
Parliament. Parliament gave one guarantee some years ago and is asked to give a
different one to-day. It is within the
of this
competence of Parliament or
House to reject it, and they cannot complain that they are not being kept faith
with. because they are given every
opportunity of debating the matter.
The other point raised by the hon. and
learned Senator was the possibility, that
if this Bill passed, other concerns may
take advantage of it. I am certain he
would not wish me to consider a hypothetical question. Indeed the Ministry
have enough difficulty in dealing with
concrete questions without considering
hypothetical ones. But I would say any
such application would have to be considered on its own merits and would not
be guaranteed without the authority of
Parliament.
With regard to what the hon. and
gallant Senator Maxwell said, I should
Electricity Bill-
Second Reading
19 MAY 1936
like to say that in England the grid
system he mentioned deals with what may
be termed the wholesale end of distribution industry. Here in Northern Ireland
the Board deals with both wholesale and
retail distribution, and I think, therefore, we are in advance of England in
that matter. As the hon. and gallant
Member may have observed, a committee
is now sitting on the question of electricity distribution in England.
It is not reasonable to expect the
Electricity Board here to be operating at
a very cheap rate at the present moment,
because it is in the initial stages of its
work, and obviously most of the expenses
come at the beginning. He mentioned
the Belfast Corporation undertaking, and
I should like to pay tribute to the
efficiency with which it has been able to
do its business for a number of years,
but it has been distributing electricity in
a densely populated area. The Board
here is operating over a wider and more
sparsely populated area.
the
I would certainly like to bring before
the notice of the Government
interesting remarks of the hon. and
gallant Senator re a possible water controller. I should like to say that the
main object of the Bill as I said is to
effect a saving. It is not for one moment
suggested that the Board will not be able
to raise the money under the ordinary
conditions as an ordinary profit-making
authority, but it can save about £7,500
by this method, and I always understood
people living in this part of the world
look with approval on anything that
saved money if it was only 6d. I would
like to say with reference to the last
speaker's remarks that the policy of the
Board is to place poles as far as possible
in fences and cause the minimum inconvenience to farmers who
the
owners of the land. But if there is any
specific reason for complaint he
bring it forward and I am sure the

right hon. Gentleman the Minister of

Mr. LESLIE: Apparently the time.
has come for me to do what I can in the
way of reply. There have not been many
speeches. The hon. and gallant Senator
opposite really warned the House that
this guarantee might prove a heavier
weight in the future than the Government seem to anticipate. The next speaker
pointed out that in his judgment based
on information furnished to him and his
own observation that the Board is not
entitled to any preferential treatment.
The noble Viscount finally defended the
Government quite successfully on the
charge of breach of faith. I did not intend to use the expression breach of
faith in that way because, of course, I
recognised that the mere coming to
Parliament openly disposed of that. I
meant a possible breach of faith by this
Parliament to the Parliament that gave
the original guarantee. That is not quite
the same thing.
The main point has not, I think, been
touched upon.
The noble Viscount has
not touched the question of the increasing weight of these guarantees on the
Province. He has not touched the other
main question as to the powers of the
Board to get this money on not unreasonable terms without a guarantee. I think
my chief argument remains, and that is
that the Bill has been obtained from one
Parliament with a provision guaranteeing for a limited period, and another
Parliament should not be asked to
extend that guarantee without the
gravest reason, and I cannot find that
the gravest reason has been brought to
our notice this afternoon, consequently
I must persist in the Motion in my name.

Road and Railway Transport Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The necessity for this Bill arises out of the fact
that the Government have been fortunate
enough to persuade Sir Lynden
Macassey, whose invaluable services in
connection with the Road Transport Act
of 1929 will be remembered, to accept
appointment as chairman of the Appeal
Tribunal established under Section 40 of
the Road and Railway Transport Act of
1935. I should like to add that he has
generously intimated that he does not
wish to receive any remuneration for his
services.
Sir Lynden Macassey is not, however,
a practising barrister-at-law in Northern
Ireland, and there is doubt as to
whether, under the existing terms of the
section constituting the Tribunal, he is
qualified for appointment. Sub-section
1 (a) of the Measure before the House is
intended to remove this doubt.
Provision already exists for the
appointment of deputies to act for any
of the members of the Tribunal who may
be unable to act by reason of illness or
other infirmity. Occasions may, however, arise when the chairman might find
himself unable to attend meetings,
possibly of a routine nature, for other
reasons. To meet such a contingency subsection 1 (b) of this Measure makes provision for the appointment of a deputychairman.
I beg to move, "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: This is another short
Bill, but, if I may say so, a Bill which
is far more attractive than that which
the House has just dealt with. The first
thing incumbent upon one-and it is a
duty, in so far as it is a duty at all, it is
a pleasure to discharge-is to say how
very fortunate I think the Government
and Parliament and the whole Province
has been in securing the services of Sir
Lynden Macassey. Apart from his professional eminence at the Bar, he has a
knowledge of transport questions which
is, I imagine, almost if not quite
unequalled in the British Isles. The few
words that are required to ensure his
appointment being free from attack, are
wholly unexceptional.
The Bill introduces, as hon. Members
will have noticed, a difference between
the provisions relating to this Appeal
Tribunal and those relating to the
Arbitration Tribunal where very much
the same form of wording is used. But
although the matters are so completely
and entirely different I do not imagine
that that will give cause for complexity.
However, there is a small point that does
seem to me to arise, and I will mention
it now because it may be the noble
Viscount will be able to answer it at
once. It is contemplated under the
second part of Clause 1 that your Appeal
Tribunal may now be constituted of two
members only. The original provision
was, of course, that there should be three
members, and there were further provisions, one of which was that the
decisions of the Appeal Tribunal shall be
by a majority of members. There was
another which may be more important
for our purpose. It is to the effect that
the Appeal Tribunal may make rules,
with the approval of the Lord Chief
Justice, for, amongst other things,
deciding the number of members of the
Tribunal who are to constitute a quorum.
The point I am coming to is that we
provide for a possible constitution of two
members only. What is to happen if
those two members disagree? Has that
been provided for by rule? Is it intended
to provide for it by rule, or would it be
better for Parliament to insert a few
words in this Bill to put the position
beyond doubt. It could be done by an
Amendment which would not in any way
fall over or distort that part of the Bill
which depends on the Money Resolution
in another place. It would be a mere
matter of machinery, very similar to
Loans Guarantee Bill-
Second Reading
19 MAY 1936
paragraph (a) of Clause 1. It may be
that the noble Viscount has considered
the matter and can inform us, but if not
I will submit an amendment on the
Committee stage. Taking the Bill as a
whole, there is no objection whatever to
anything it contains.
CHARLEMONT:
am
Viscount
I
afraid the hon. and learned Senator may
put down his amendment, as I have not
considered the point.

a

position under which money once guaranteed could not be guaranteed again, and
provided that the amount of the guarantee was not decreased by the amount
repaid, and thus it is possible for the
whole of the fourteen-and-a-half millions
to be outstanding at once. As a rule the
guarantees are of
short
very
currency,
and I believe the loss incurred has been
very trivial; in fact, probably there has
not been any loss at all because of the
savings in unemployment benefit.
However, I hope this will be an end.
of it because, as the House will remember, we have within the last few minutes

Province, which makes seventeen millions
in all. We also have other guarantees
on road and railway transport and other

The increase in the total sum which
may be guaranteed has been made necessary by the revival of our shipbuilding
industry, which in 1931, when the existing limit was imposed, was in a very
depressed condition.
I am informed by my right hon.
Friend, the acting Minister of Finance,
that the amount of fourteen-and-a-half
million pounds will provide a margin
for the future as substantial repayments
may be expected to be made shortly. I
beg to move, "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: Hitherto I have
always been able to support these
Loans Guarantee Bills, and I do
not propose to blot my copybook
this afternoon. But I cannot refrain
from pointing out that I rather hope
we have reached the peak of these
guarantees. Fourteen-and-a-half millions
The
a very large sum of money.
House will remember that a year or two

the
Mr. LONG: Applications under these
Acts are given very careful consideration
and the security offered is closely scrutinised before any liability is entered into
by the Government. May I remind the
hon. and learned Member that it is
through the operation of these Acts that
our shipbuilding industry is busy, and
that thousands of men are employed who
would otherwise be idle. I shall convey

and learned Senator as to the limit of
these guarantees.
