Papers Presented
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 21st May, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Commerce:
The Annual Report under the Companies Acts for the year 1935.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Pigs Marketing (Northern Ireland) (Special Levy) (No. 2) Order,
1936.
Laid before the House, pursuant to
Act, and ordered to lie on the Table.
Questions to Minister.
PROPOSED NEW SCHOOL AT
ISLAND CARRAGH.
Mr. LESLIE: There is very little for
me to say before asking the question
that stands in my name. As the House
will recognise the schools referred to are
in my own neighbourhood, but they possibly do not know that the three schools
named in the question are approximately
two and a half miles apart. They form
a triangle with each point about two and
a half miles from the other. There have
been in that locality murmurings on the
subject of this proposal, and a suggestion
that all is not well, and naturally this
being in Ireland, the greatest protest
seems to come from Ballytober. I understand a petition signed by 98 parents.
from that locality has been sent in,
though I cannot myself believe that
Ballytober school would accommodate 98
pupils. I do know that Ballywatt
school is distinctly ripe for pulling down.
In view of the local murmurings I
thought I might ask the noble Viscount
how the matter stands.
Accordingly, I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether it is proposed to
establish a new school in the townland
of Island Carragh in substitution for
the existing schools known as Ballytober,
Ballywatt and Seneril public elementary
schools; and, if so, whether the North
Antrim Regional Education Committee
is in support of the proposal.
Educa-
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
tion often gives rise to murmurings.
The Ministry, on the application of the
North Antrim Regional Education Committee, provisionally sanctioned in
October last the erection by the Committee of a new school in the townland
of Island Carragh North to replace the
existing Ballywatt, Ballytober and
Seneril public elementary schools. The
site selected has since been approved.
A few days ago, however, the Committee forwarded a memorial from the
residents of Ballytober and district protesting against the erection of this school
and asking for the continuance of a
school in the neighbourhood of Ballytober; the Committee, while apparently
expressing no opinion on the merits of
this objection, request that the whole.
matter of the new school at Island
Carragh be reconsidered. It seems,
therefore, to be a matter of some doubt
as to the measure of support which the
Committee are now prepared to give to
their original proposal. The matter will
be further considered by the Ministry.
THE DEATH RATE IN
NEWTOWNARDS.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
ask this question simply to get the information. There is a tendency, I think, to
start schemes involving expenditure
without really having the facts before
those who arrange them. From what I
understood it was thought that the
health in Newtownards was not as good
as in other towns. I made what I think
were fair inquiries, and I found there
have been no epidemics more than in
other towns nor is the health worse than
in other places. Then as to the outlet,
I can remember that there was an outcry in Belfast about the gas works. It
was stated by those living in the region
of the gas works that they suffered in
health. The facts proved that perhaps
the healthiest part of Belfast was that
round the gas works. Instead of being
disadvantage in regard to health,
though it might be disagreeable, it was
a benefit.
a
Oral Answers

21 MAY
Oral Answers
Later on I think I am correct in saying there was an outcry about Holywood suffering from the
sewage of
Belfast drifting down the Lough, and
that the health was bad there. I am
right in saying that it was proved the
health of Holywood did not suffer.
ask this question for the sake of finding
out before any scheme is entered upon
whether the people had good or bad
health, and whether there was any real
foundation for the suggestion. The
question is entirely about facts which
may be put before the House and the
public as to whether the health of Newtownards and district is worse than that
of other places in the country.
I, therefore, ask the Leader of the
House whether it is a fact that the death
rate is higher in Newtownards than in
other towns in Northern Ireland, and
whether there is more sickness and a
higher death rate in the districts adjoining the outlet of sewerage into Strangford Lough than in other parts of the
Lough shore.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: An examination of the Registrar-General's
returns does not substantiate the view
that the death rate in Newtownards
Urban District is higher than in other
urban districts in Northern Ireland. On
the contrary, the general death rate is
lower than in many other towns; and,
except for one year, the death rate from
infectious diseases during the last five
years had been below the average death
rate from such diseases in all urban districts.
It is impossible to say what the sickness and death rates are in the rural
areas immediately adjoining the outfall
sewer, as they are included in the
statistics for the entire Newtownards
Rural District.
I have some figures here extracted
from the Registrar-General's report if the
right hon. Senator would like to see them
afterwards. In 1935, I may say, the
death rate per thousand in Newtownards
Urban District was .39 as compared with
1.58 in the total urban areas, and 1.66 in
the Belfast County Borough.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Arising out of
the answer given by the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House, I would remind him
that it is not a question so much of the
health of the urban area, but the rural
population object strongly to the odour,
which is very objectionable in the summer
time along the shore.
Mr. LAVERY: I am rather sorry that
the right hon. Member came to this
House to ask about the sanitary condition
of Newtownards. Newtownards, unfortunately, at one time was involved in
enormous expense with regard to the conduct of the town. Since then, undoubtedly, an effort has been made by
several, for some reason I do not know, to
besmirch the town and call Newtownards
all kinds of bad names. I should like to tell
the right hon. Senator who has asked the
question that Newtownards is one of the
healthiest towns in the North of Ireland.
That is proved from statistics. I made
myself wise this morning when I saw the
Order Paper-I got it pretty late by post
and I had not time to make much inquiry
-but I asked the dispensary officer what
condition we were in. We were, he told
me, the very
best. He said the question
was the usual type.
I ask the right hon. Senator to be very
careful what he is doing. I was almost
afraid he was leading himself into
trouble. I also
also inquired from the
sanitary officer and he repeated the very
same thing. We are in a very fair condition. With regard to the bad smell,
undoubtedly we must, as ratepayers, see
that this thing is rectified if there is any
trouble. I understand there was a small
effluvium. We have arranged, whatever
the cost is to be, that things will be made
satisfactory. There is no doubt at all we
intend to carry out any promise we make.
If there is a complaint or anything wrong
we will rectify it. We are doing so and
will continue to do so. I may assure the
right hon. Senator that the health of
Newtownards is the best I have known for
forty years.
It never was better than at
the present time.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
would just like to thank the Leader of the
House for the trouble he has taken and
the answer he has given. It just confirms
what I thought, that Newtownards is,
perhaps, the healthiest town in the North
of Ireland. I thank him for confirming
that.
Transport Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have to
report to the House that His Grace the
Governor of Northern Ireland, in the
name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his Assent
to the following Bills, agreed upon by
both Houses: :-
Electricity (Supply),
Milk and Milk Products,
Motor Vehicles (International Race).
Road and Railway Transport Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Provisions as to chairman
and deputy chairman of Transport Appeal
Tribunal).
Section forty of the Road and Railway Transport Act (Northern Ireland),
1935, shall have effect subject to the
following Amendments:-
(a) In sub-section (1) for the words "a
practising barrister-at-law of not
less than ten years' standing" there
shall be substituted the words
"a practising barrister-at-law of
England or Northern Ireland of
not less than ten years' standing or
a practising member of the Faculty
of Advocates in Scotland of not less
than ten years' standing."
(b) After sub-section (4) there shall be
inserted the following sub-section :
"(4a) The Governor of Northern
Ireland may appoint a person
qualified under sub-section (1) of this
section and being-
(a) one of the members of the Appeal
Tribunal other than the chairman, or
(b) a person who is not a member of
the Appeal Tribunal,
to act in the place of the chairman of
the Appeal Tribunal; and, in the event
of a member of the Appeal Tribunal
being so appointed, that member, when
acting as chairman, and the other member of the Appeal Tribunal shall constitute that tribunal."
Mr. LESLIE: I mentioned this little
matter on the Second Reading, because
it was
a matter which seemed to me
might very possibly give trouble in the
administration of the Act if Parliament
did not deal with it. I have been looking
into precedents as well as I could and first
of all I think I must remind the Committee of the duties this Appeal Tribunal
has to do, particularly in so far as the
road traffic part of its business is concerned. The tribunal is, it will be remembered, to have the duty of fixing
fares in any case referred to it by the
Ministry and in case a local authority or
body of persons feels aggrieved by fares,
and so on, and makes application for reconsideration. The tribunal has to settle
matters of that kind. Under the Railway and Canal Commission Acts it was
specially provided that the Commission
should consist of not less than three members, SO no difficulty arose in that
connection.
When we set up a tribunal under the
Motor Vehicles Act, 1929, to fix fares
and so on, the tribunal which is now to
cease to exist, Parliament expressly provided that all questions brought before
or arising at that tribunal are to be decided by a majority of those present and
voting, provided that in case of an
equality of votes the chairman would
have a second or casting vote. We
directly provided under the Road Transport Act, 1929, in case of an equality
of votes in the tribunal then set up,
to give a casting vote to the chairman of
it. If you do not make some provision
for a casting vote or if the casting vote
is not exercised you may have equal
numbers; and if there is no provision for
a casting vote the ordinary rule must be
that the matter appealed against stands.
The appeal tribunal being divided, the
appeal cannot normally succeed.
That
is the general rule. I do not think it
would be at all desirable to leave things
in that position in regard to the matters
this tribunal will have to decide. Therefore I think the best plan would be to
provide definitely that the member of the
tribunal acting as chairman should have
a casting vote. I do not think the
position ideally satisfactory. It obviously
is not, but it provides means of settling
the question.
There remains the other problem as to
whether it is necessary for Parliament
Transport Bill-
Committee
21 MAY 1936
to take action at all or whether the
matter can be dealt with by rule. The
Transport Act, 1935, provided that the
appeal tribunal might from time to time,
with the approval of the Lord Chief
Justice, make general rules governing
the procedure and practice. Amongst
these rules would be the number of members of tribunal necessary to constitute
a quorum and it also provided, not by
rule but definitely, that the decision of
the tribunal should be by a majority of
members. These are the direct provisions of the 1935 Act. Of course you
will get a majority if a man has a casting vote. It does not matter if he votes
twice. Taking it as a whole, I think it
would be wiser for Parliament definitely
to insert the provision now that we are
providing that the tribunal is to be constituted in a different manner. By this
Amendment I make no suggestion as to
what is to happen if the tribunal is constituted by ordinary members. That is
outside the Title of the Bill. I beg to
move, to insert at the end of the Clause:
"(4b). Where, by virtue of subsection (4a) of this section, the appeal
tribunal is constituted of two members, the member acting as chairman
shall have, and may exercise, a second
or casting vote."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: While the
hon. Senator's Amendment would remove
all possibility of stalemate occurring
through the failure of the tribunal to
agree when constituted of two members
only, it would practically amount to
constituting the tribunal of the deputychairman alone as he could always overrule his colleague.
It is not anticipated that the deputychairman will act under sub-clause 4a
save on occasions when the matters to be
determined are of a routine nature and
involve no point of principle, and if on
such an occasion a difference of opinion
arose between the two members the Government is confident that with the assistance of the chairman a sufficient
measure of agreement would be reached.
Moreover, the avoidance of the difficulty which the hon. Senator anticipates
is one for which the tribunal can provide
when making rules governing its procedure. For that reason I am afraid I
cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: One thing arises out of
that. The noble Viscount said "with
the assistance of the chairman" the
difficulty would be removed. Did he
mean the real chairman or the deputychairman ? Was he relying on the
deputy-chairman exercising ordinary tact.
and commonsense or did he mean the
chairman would deal with it by post or
telephone or something of that sort?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The real
chairman consulted later.
Mr. LESLIE: The matter would
stand adjourned until you get the real
chairman. That being so, there is no
reason to persist in the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short title, construction
and citation) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered That the Bill be reported
without Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without amendment to
the House.
Resolved: "That this report be now
received."-(Viscount Charlemont).
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third time on June 2.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Long).
Resolved: "That this Bill do now
pass."-(Mr. Long).
And a message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.
Sittings of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I see on
the Order Paper that the next sitting
of the House will be on 26th May, that
is to say, next Tuesday. If hon. Sena-
Business of the House
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
tors would be content to have Standing
Orders suspended in order to take the
First and Second Reading of the Egg Bill
on June 2-Tuesday week-I would be
quite content if the House did not meet
next week. The Bill will be in the hands
of Senators on Saturday next or at latest
on Monday morning. Has anybody any
objection to that?
Mr. LESLIE: No, I do not think
there can be any reasonable objection
to that. The great point in the Eggs
Bill is we shall have a full week to read
the Bill. After that we shall want time,
more than two days, between the Second
Reading and the Committee stage. It is
just that sort of Bill that might require
a good deal of consideration and consultation between the different stages.
So far as I am concerned I do not object
to the proposal to take a holiday next
week and have the Firs; and Second
Reading of the Eggs Bill on Tuesday in
the week after.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I quite agree
with the last speaker. I certainly hope
we shall have an interval of a week to
study the Bill.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: If we
have the Second Reading on June 2, I
would give a week before the Committee
stage. The Committee stage would be
on June 9. Then we could have the
Report on the 11th and the Third Reading and Passing on the 16th. We might
possibly be able to do the Report and
Third Reading together. That would
depend on how we get on in Committee.
I beg to move, "That this House do now
adjourn." We will meet the week after
next.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at a
Quarter to Four o'clock
until Tuesday, 2nd June.

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 2nd June, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.

Ministry of Agriculture:
The Grey Squirrels (Northern Ireland)
Order, 1936.
Ministry of Labour:
The Unemployment Insurance (Exempt
Persons) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1936.
The Unemployment Insurance (Persons employed in Agriculture and otherwise) (Benefit) Regulations (Northern
Ireland), 1936.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
New Member Sworn.
Mr. SPEAKER informed the House
that he had received a certificate from
the Clerk of the Crown that Sir Roland
Thomas Nugent, of Portaferry, County
Down, was, on the 29th day of May,
1936, duly elected a Senator to serve in
the Parliament of Northern Ireland in
the room of Mr. James McMahon,
deceased.
Sir ROLAND THOMAS NUGENT
took and subscribed the Oath.
Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the Second
Reading of the Marketing of Eggs Bill."
--(Viscount Charlemont.)
BILL PRESENTED.
Close Season (Amendment) Bill [S],
"to alter the days between which ducks,
partridges, snipes, and woodcocks may
not be killed in Northern Ireland," was
presented by Mr. Leslie.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, "That
this Bill be now read a First time,"
and may I say that it is not proposed to
ask the House for a Second Reading
until the Autumn Session.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be read a
Second time on the first sitting day after
the Summer Recess.
Question to Minister.
NEW LOCK ON RIVER LAGAN.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Dr. Gibson:
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government, in sanctioning expenditure on the construction of the new
lock on the River Lagan at the Belfast
Gas Works specified any time limit for
the completion of the work? What was
the date of approval of the scheme, the
date of signing of the contract, and the
date fixed for the completion of the work;
and whether in view of the serious
menace to the health of the community
caused by the pollution of the air by
emanations from the uncovered mud
banks of the river the Government will
take steps to have this work expedited.
Dr. GIBSON : I put this question
down owing to the number of complaints
I have had from residents in the vicinity
of the River Lagan, and from persons.
who use the embankments in the course
of their ordinary work. Some ten or
twelve years ago the Corporation of the
city of Belfast constructed an embankment along both sides of the River Lagan
above the Ormeau Bridge and on one
side of the Lagan along the Ormeau
Park. Connected with that was the
erection and construction of a lock on
the Lagan to keep the water high enough
to cover the mudbanks along the river.
It must be ten or twelve years ago since
this contract was started. It goes on year
after year, and the length of time that
has been spent upon the construction of
this lock amounts to what I might describe as a scandal. I have put the
question on the Paper in the hope that
I might be able to elicit some information
as to the progress of the work, and the
approximate time in which we might
hope to see this lock completed and the
work carried out as it should be.
Marketing of Eggs Bill 376
Oral Answers.
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The construction of the new
lock on the River Lagan at Belfast within
a period of 15 years was authorised by
the Belfast Corporation Act of 1924. I
may point out, however, that this did
not impose any obligation on the Corporation to carry out the work, but merely
provided that if such work were not
undertaken and completed within the
specified period the powers conferred on
the Corporation for that purpose would
lapse.
The contract for the work was signed
on the 16th August, 1927, and the date
fixed for the completion of the contract
was the 16th February, 1930. There has
been much regrettable delay in regard to
the execution of this work, but I understand that it is expected to be finished
this year.
The construction of the lock was undertaken by the Corporation on its own
initiative, and the Government has no
direct function in regard to it.
With regard to the question of the
suggested danger to public health
occasioned by the delay in the completion
of the work, it may be pointed out that
the scheme was undertaken primarily
with a view to the improvement of the
existing unsightly condition of the river
at this particular point, and, while the
Ministry considers it most desirable from
every point of view that the completion.
of the work should be expedited, it has
no reason to apprehend that any actual
danger to the public health of the city
has resulted from the delay which has
taken place in this respect.
Mr. McALLISTER: Can the noble
Leader inform the House whether there
was any penal sum inserted in the contract to be enforced if the work was not
completed within the stipulated time, and,
if so, whether that penal clause has been
enforced in view of the fact that the work
was not completed within the time specified?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I presume
the hon. Member refers to a penal clause
in the contract between the Corporation
and the contractor?
Mr. McALLISTER: Yes.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
require to have notice of that question.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have agreed to the
Amendments made by the Joint Committee to Trew's Divorce Bill without
amendment. They have passed the
Marketing of Eggs Bill and the Appropriation Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
Marketing of Eggs Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Appropriation Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Ordered, That the Bill be read a
Second time on Tuesday next.
Marketing of Eggs Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time." As hon. Members are
aware, this is by no means the first occasion upon which this House has considered
legislation promoted by the Ministry of
Agriculture with the object of improving
the marketing of eggs. The present
Measure further extends the code of egg
marketing legislation, which was commenced by the Act of 1924, and which
was developed by later legislation in 1926,
1928 and 1931.
Hitherto, the object of legislation dealing with the marketing of eggs has been
primarily to provide for the grading of
eggs for export. At the time when the
Ministry of Agriculture was set up it was
found that eggs from Northern Ireland
were being shipped to Great Britain ungraded, in all kinds of packages, and with
no system of uniformity. Imported eggs
from countries such as Denmark, Holland
and the Baltic States were arriving in
standard packages, graded according to
size and tested for quality. In such
circumstances, it will be readily appreciated that eggs from Northern Ireland
held a low place in the estimation of the
wholesale trade, and commanded a correspondingly low price. If we were to compete successfully with imported graded
supplies it was essential that our eggs
also should be graded. This was parti-
377 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

2 JUNE 1936
Second Reading
cularly the case prior to 1928, since before that date imported eggs did not
require to be marked as such.
The success which has attended our
grading legislation is well known to all
members of this House. The poultry
population increased from 6,800,000 in
1924 to over 10,000,000 in 1935. Moreover, the price obtained for Northern Ireland eggs is now the highest paid for any
imported eggs upon the British market,
and is second only to the price paid for
national mark eggs, which constitute a
comparatively small proportion of the
total home produced supply.
During the past ten years our egg trade
has been placed upon a sound foundation,
and it now has a high reputation upon
the British market. It is essential, however, that that position should be not only
maintained but still further improved.
Competition from imported supplies is increasing and proposals are at present being
canvassed in Great Britain for the introduction of egg marketing schemes there,
which would greatly improve the standard
of British eggs by requiring them to pass
through packing stations where they
would be graded and tested in the same
way as is done by our merchants in Northern Ireland.
Under previous legislation the power
to protect our standards of quality has
rested with the Ministry of Agriculture
which has been able to prosecute for
offences against the Marketing of Eggs
Acts. To maintain our egg trade by
the exercise of penal provisions cannot
commend itself to any Member of this
House if an alternative method can be
found. It is believed that the payment
of the producer on the basis of quality
is such a method. During recent years
we have witnessed the striking success
which has attended the application of
the principle of payment according to
quality in the cases of pigs and milk
marketing. For example, since the introduction of milk grading and the payment of a better price for Grade B than
for Grade C milk there has been a rapid
increase in the production of the
superior grade.
Hitherto the grading of eggs has not
affected the producer. A flat price has
been paid to him irrespective of the
quality of his goods. It is proposed
under this Measure that a higher price
shall be paid for new laid than for
second eggs, that is, eggs which have
been held so that they are no longer
fresh. It will be the function of the
Egg Marketing Committee, constituted
under the Bill, to fix the appropriate
prices to be paid to producers for eggs
of different qualities.
At this point I should like to deal with
any misconception which may exist regarding the powers of this Egg Marketing Committee in regard to price-fixing.
The Government is, of course, fully
aware that the price of Northern Ireland eggs must be governed by the
reputation and supply of Northern Ireland eggs on the British market. It is
clear that no action taken here can affect
the economic level of Northern Ireland
egg prices on the wholesale market of
Great Britain. It will be the task of the
committee, not to attempt to change or
modify these prices, but to determine the
proportions in which the price will be
shared between producers and merchants
in Northern Ireland. Indeed, the Egg
Marketing Committee will have no power
to deal with wholesale prices; its
functions are confined solely to fixing the
price to be paid by the wholesale
merchant to the producer. The price
which the farmer will receive must
depend upon the wholesale price of Northern Ireland eggs in Great Britain, but
that wholesale price depends in its turn
upon the freshness and quality of the
eggs sold by the small farmer in County
Down and County Tyrone. It will be
the task of the committee to devise a
means of price-fixing which will bring
this home to the producer.
The Bill has one other main object,
and that is to ensure that consumers in
Northern Ireland will obtain eggs which
are as good as those exported to Great
Britain. In many instances retailers
obtain their supplies of eggs direct from
the farmer and not from the wholesaler.
The control of the wholesaler, therefore,
has not meant the control of the local
trade in eggs. In order that this may
be obtained retailers of eggs must be
brought within the scope of our legislation, and that is what the present
Measure proposes to do. It will enable
the Ministry of Agriculture to prescribe
the conditions under which eggs may be
prepared or exposed for sale by retail,
while it also provides that all eggs sold
379 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
by retail in Northern Ireland, with a
few minor exceptions, must be described
as either "new-laid" eggs or "second"
eggs. The Bill will thus provide an
important safeguard to the housewife.
In conclusion, may I say that the present Measure in no way modifies the
structure which has been built up to deal
with our export trade in eggs. It does,
however, introduce a new principle into
the method of payment of the producer,
a principle which has already been tried
out with success in the cases of pigs and
milk marketing. It will provide an incentive to our farmers to supply the
quality and grade of egg demanded by
the wholesale market in Great Britain,
and lead, we believe, to a rapid improvement in the quality of eggs marketed by
the producer. It will also provide for
the consumer in Northern Ireland an egg
supply of guaranteed quality. Both
these objects will, I feel sure, commend
themselves to hon. Members.
I am glad to say that the right hon.
Gentleman the Minister of Agriculture
will be here to answer any questions on
the debate that may arise. I beg to
move.
Question proposed: "That the Bill be
now read a Second time."
Mr. McALLISTER: I am in favour
of the principles on which the Bill is
founded, and I am in favour of many
of the Clauses in the Bill, but with regard
to the machinery by which the objects.
are to be carried out I intend to oppose
that at every stage as strenuously as I
can. Notwithstanding the representations that have been made here from time
to time against empowering the officials
of a Department practically to carry out
the major part of the legislation by mere
rules made in a Government office, this
is the most audacious attempt from that
point of view that I have yet come across.
When you read the Bill page by page
and Clause by Clause you are hit in the
face by the fact that practically everything in the whole Bill is delegated to
the officials of the Department. I intend
to suggest to the House that, in order
to save our interests, before this Bill
eventually passes this House, we should
have an undertaking included in the Bill
as a Clause to provide that before any
regulations of this drastic type are put
into operation they must be submitted
expressly, and dealt with in the House
by Resolution or otherwise.
From my
point of view we are abdicating as parliamentarians and are delegating our trust
to the Ministry, and I think the suggestion I have made to the Government
cannot reasonably be opposed.
I myself in a professional capacity from
time to time have come across these regulations under certain Bills, and I said
in this House before, and I have no
hesitation in saying now, they are drastic
regulations made in a Government office
that would never have had the slightest
chance of passing Parliament or passing
this House. I can quote instances where
the regulations are not only drastic but
far more voluminous than the Acts of
Parliament themselves. Both as a public
representative and from the professional
point of view and as an ordinary
citizen I am entirely opposed
this principle, which has surely
reached its limit in this present Bill.
As a matter of fact, this is the most
important branch of the farming industry, and by this Bill we are transferring the whole control of that branch to
a Government department.

We heard from the noble Lord the
Leader of the House that at the present
moment Northern Ireland eggs hold the
highest place in the British market.
Surely then it is the duty of Parliament
if that is the position to keep control and
not take the risk of a deterioration of
that position by handing over control to
a department in an office in this building
here. I notice that on the advisory
committee the farmers and those who produce eggs are only entitled to five representatives, whereas the dealers are entitled
to seven, and when the Minister comes to
reply I would like him to give a logical
reason for the numbers of these appointments. It is not sufficient to get up
say it balances the committee better by
such and such a thing. We want to know
why it balances it. Why should the producers not be entitled to the same number
of representatives as the dealers?
think this House will expect a definite
and concrete answer.
and
I
Another point is that the Minister reserves to himself and his Department the
right to elect the chairman. I would like
to know on what grounds he reserves to

381 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

2 JUNE 1936
Second Reading
himself that right. Possibly it will not
be a man adapted by his knowledge of
the business and his ability who will be
appointed, but a man will be appointed
merely on account of some political service. The practical thing and the fair
thing is to allow the committee to elect
its own chairman. I would like to hear
how the Minister justifies the holding
within the department the election of the
chairman.
With regard to the licence fee of £2
which has to be paid, I think it cannot
be justified. The cost of the administration of the Act would not justify it. It
is far too high. Representations have
been made to me already that some small
shopkeepers will not pay the licenceshopkeepers on the outskirts of towns and
villages who sell only a few dozen eggs a
week. That will be done away with.
Just to show what we are asked to do by
these regulations, I may say that when
one reads this explanatory White Paper
one can see the fluid, unformed state
of mind of the Ministry and officials.
In certain circumstances this fee of £2
is to be payable by the retailer, and he
is not charged on account of the smallness of his business the farthing a dozen.
Just to show the fluid state of the
Ministry's mind it says the trader selling
60 dozens, but goes on to say that is no
guide. Thus, we are delegating to the
Ministry, without any data and without
any information, power to differentiate
between the different licences. That is a
thing Parliament should be very slow to
do.
With these general remarks I hope that
the House in its duty to itself and to
maintain its position in Parliament will
take steps to ensure that these regulations
must have some sanction from Parliament
and that this great trade in Northern
Ireland is not transferred to a Government department. As a matter of fact,
anyone who reads the Bill can see that
the whole matter of the Bill is simply
enabling the Minister's Department to
control the egg trade of Northern Ireland. That is really the whole Bill boiled
down. I affirm that there can be no
objection to the suggestion I have made.
Surely the Ministry do not intend that
their Department should be above Parliament. If they do not, they can have no
fear in bringing their regulations before.
Parliament. If they do not consent I
have no hesitation in saying it is because
they intend possibly or probably-and
from past experience I should say with
certainty to make regulations that
would not for one moment be tolerated
or receive the sanction of this House.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
think this is an excellent Bill, and I
would be very hopeful that as a result
the inhabitants of large cities will get
a decent egg of home production, as they
do in England and Scotland. There is
a question I do not quite agree with in
Clause 3. As the previous speaker said,
five members of the committee are given
to the producers of eggs, five to the "A"
class of dealers and two to the "B" class.
I take it one of the duties of this committee is to arrange prices. The producer
provides all the eggs, but I think the
consumer should have some say, and
should have some representative on the
board. How that representation could
be given I do not know, but surely in
the House of Commons and in this House
there will be Members who are not interested in selling eggs or in producing
eggs, who could be appointed to look
after the interests of the consumer.
He
is really an important person, for if the
farmer does not get a market for his
eggs there is no use in having these
boards. Therefore, I hope the Minister
will be able to explain where the consumer
comes in. In my opinion a Bill of this
nature was greatly needed, and I would
like to see it made as workable as possible.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
approve of the principle of this Bill, but
there are two things about it which make
it very difficult to criticise. One is on
page 5 in Clause 3, where it is provided
how the Committee to regulate questions
that may arise shall be constituted. It
seems to me that the whole importance
of this Clause rests in the three members
appointed by the Minister of Agriculture.
If those are men of broad outlook, men
of commonsense, men of knowledge, then
I cannot see that it makes very much
difference whether the producers have an
equal representation on the Committee
with the retailers or not, because in that
case the three members will see that
fair play is done; but even if these three
members, or perhaps one or two of them,
are unable to turn up at the Committee
meetings it has never been suggested
383 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
why the dealers should be opposed to
the producers, because if the producers
cannot get a reasonable price for their
eggs in the normal course they will cease
to produce and the dealers will have much
less to sell.
I do not see how in a Bill of this sort
there could be a rigid scheme. I think,
in fairness to the Minister of Agriculture, there must be some power to
regulate the scheme according to the
circumstances that arise from time to
time. I have never concealed from the
House that I dislike interference by
Government Departments with trade
and industry, and I dislike the tendency
of modern Government Departments to
act not only as the regulating authority,
but as their own tribunals. In this
case, if you agree with the principle of
the Bill, I think there must be some
latitude allowed to the Ministry of
Agriculture to make regulations. Then
the only thing that arises is how much
latitude ought to be allowed, and that is
a point on which every Member of this
House might have a different opinion.
It seems to me that the way it has
been done is not quite so open to objection as I confess I thought it was when
reading the Bill, because the Ministry
have retained within their choice power
to appoint men whom they select as
capable of carrying out the Bill in the
best interests of both the producer and
the consumer. If there is to be any
authority holding the balance, so to
speak, I cannot think in this case there
could be any other authority than the
Ministry. I should, I admit, have liked
to have seen equal representation, and I
cannot see any reason why there should
not be equal representation, except some
rather weak argument that the retailers
are paying the administration expenses
of the Bill. Although I should from a
personal point of view have liked to have
seen the representation more equal, yet
I cannot attach as much importance to
the inequalities that appear in the Bill as
some Members of this House have done.
on
The other important point is
Page 8, where the Ministry have retained power to prescribe which holders
of retailers' licences granted under this
This
Act shall come within this section.
again I think is open to the same objection, but as to the scheme, as a whole, if
the Ministry's position is justified by
events, then I think the Bill will work
to the advantage of both the retailer
and the producer, and I do not see how
we can raise very much criticism until
we know what orders the Ministry are
going to make, because on those orders
will depend the success or failure of the
Measure. So far the Ministry have
justified a certain amount of confidence
on the part of the producers, and they
will make a wise decision if they will
listen to the representatives. Everyone
who has come in contact with the production of bacon must realise that the
Ministry's schemes regarding pig production have been a definite success.
They certainly seem to have stabilised
the prices in a way which I do not think
existed before the schemes were introduced. For that reason I intend not to
vote against the principle of the Bill,
but to leave it to the Ministry to produce
their regulations, and then, if necessary,
criticise the regulations that are made.
Sir JOSEPH DAVISON: I should like
some information as to the position of the
retailer. At the present time in the cities
and towns in the North of Ireland there
are quite a number of retailers who purchase their eggs in the local markets, and
as far as I can see by this Bill this power
is to be taken from them and placed in the
hands of wholesale dealers. I certainly
would have every objection to a monopoly
of the dealers having control of the marketing of eggs. There are certain penal
Clauses in the Bill which would affect
these men unless they purchased from
shippers, and so on, and I would like to
have some information as to the position
of the local grocers in Lisburn, Ballynahinch and other markets. I have been
told that under the Bill the local grocer
cannot purchase eggs in the local market,
that the eggs must be bought by a wholesale dealer and the grocer has to purchase
them from that dealer. In my opinion
that would be unfair trading.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill before us this
afternoon certainly had, if one may say
so, a somewhat cool reception in another
place, and I cannot say I have observed
any particular enthusiasm for it in the
discussion which has taken place this
afternoon. Not only was the reception
of the Bill something less than lukewarm,
385 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

2 JUNE 1936
Second Reading
but the Bill has not had a good Press,
and I am inclined to think that although
it has been over-discussed as regards some
points it has been under-discussed as regards others. I am quite certain that be
the reception hot or cold there has been
a great deal of misunderstanding of some
of the provisions of the Bill. That can
scarcely be denied by anybody who has
followed the debates.
I most distinctly desire to support the
Bill, and I have that desire for one or
two reasons, one of which was really mentioned by my hon. and gallant Friend
(Captain Perceval - Maxwell) opposite,
namely, the fact that previous proposals
brought forward and enacted through the
agency of the Ministry of Agriculture
have undoubtedly met with something
more than a measure of success. Some of
them have been very productive, and
amongst those that perhaps have been
the most successful are some of those
which evoked the greatest storm of criticism when they were first introduced. I
think the remembrance of that fact might
have been a help to giving the Bill a
better start than it in fact received.
Therefore, I should desire to support the
Bill as a further experiment in the possibility of semi-socialised trading,
trading superintended by the State. The
whole tendency of these Acts, of the
marketing code as a whole, is something
socialistic, but I do not know that in
the twentieth century that is necessarily
to the detriment of the schemes, provided
it is not carried too far.
or
The noble Viscount, in introducing the
Bill, mentioned that previous efforts
towards the improvement of the marketing of eggs had resulted in a very large
increase in the numbers of poultry kept
in Northern Ireland. I think that is
undoubtedly the case. He also spoke of
the increase in the number of eggs exported, but he did not tell us that the
number of eggs exported during the
earlier days perhaps might have been
somewhat greater but for the desire of
the producer to keep some of his eggs
at home to use for hatching purposes in
order that he might get a greater supply
to send across the water. I believe that
is so.
A far larger number of eggs than
formerly are kept for setting purposes at
home. I mention that because it will
be a matter to be referred to perhaps
again at a later stage. As I have said,
I regard this particular Bill as part of
the general agricultural policy, and if
this is a fair criticism-I rather think
the Minister is in agreement with meit may serve to explain to some extent
the fact that this Bill appears to me, and
I hope to others, to have something of
what is called paste and scissors about it.
A good many provisions appear in this
Bill which also appear in other agricultural Bills, but this we can speak of
later on. It may well be that the fact
that this Bill is part of a code may
account to some extent for the criticisms
regarding the machinery put forward by
my right hon. Friend in front of me,
because it is the machinery that is criticised in connection with all these agricultural Bills. But I cannot see that I
myself should have selected the Bill
before us as an example of undue regulation. Like the hon. Senator whom I
have just quoted, and my hon. and
gallant Friend (Captain Perceval-
Maxwell) opposite, I, as the House knows,
hate anything in the shape of bureaucratic interference, but, again like my
hon, and gallant Friend opposite, one has
to admit that something of that nature
is inevitable, and I do not really think
there is very much undue regulation in
the Bill before us.
There are, of course, two problems. One
of them is that of what we might call
the shipping industry side of the business,
and the other is the retail side.
It was
to the retail side that my right hon.
Friend in front referred, I take it, when
he spoke of the consumer as being most
concerned. There are those two questions. As regards the first, the wholesale
question, I think I am right in saying
that practically all the shippers and
wholesale dealers are in favour of the
Bill. I was present at a discussion on
the subject in our local Chamber of Commerce a few weeks
There were
ago.
several people there who are concerned
with dealings in eggs, and practically
all of them were in favour of the Bill.
They all admitted, I think, that it required certain amendments, some of
which have already been made I understand, but they also agreed that, taking
the Bill as a whole from their point of
view, it was one of which they were
something more than moderate supporters.
As regards the retailer we have not, Ț
think, heard quite the whole story yet.
G
387 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
But before passing to the retailer I want
to go back for a moment to the shipping
question, because one of the points on
which I venture to think this Bill has
been misunderstood is the fact that it is
perpetually quoted against it that whatever you can do on this side to raise the
price of eggs you cannot do on the other
side where you have no control whatever
over the import of eggs. We in this
Parliament cannot touch the import of
eggs into Great Britain. That is a point
of great importance. Then it is said
that owing to the fluctuations of imports
there must necessarily be fluctuations of
price, and that those fluctuations will react unfavourably as regards the producer.
I cannot appreciate that argument at all.
It seems to me to be certain that you
cannot avoid fluctuation in price, but
the fluctuation in price will be there
whether you have control on this side or
not. I do not see that the producer will
be any worse off under the new scheme
than under the old, that is if the committee does its work in the way we
expect.
Speaking of the committee, keeping
myself for a few minutes more from
approaching the retail question, and
thinking over what has been said by
earlier speakers, I must say that I do
not feel very much inclined to object to
the constitution of the committee, except
as regards the omission of representatives
of the retailers. That I will come to
again. I do not see, for instance, that
there is any particular reason why the
Ministry should not, in the first instance
at all events, appoint the Chairman. If
I remember rightly in one of our earlier
marketing schemes this same question
was raised, and I think it was decidedit certainly was suggested that the
Ministry's power to appoint the Chairman should be for a limited time. No
doubt the Minister of Agriculture (Sir
Basil Brooke), whom we are all glad to
see in this House, will be able to correct
me if I am wrong, but I think that was
the decision taken. As regards the
absence of consumers on that committee,
I would take upon myself to suggest
that that committee is apparently some
dozen or so strong, and I cannot but
believe that some of that dozen will be
people who are in the habit of consuming eggs at breakfast; and, if so,
some
at least of them will have bought the
eggs either personally or through their
the consumer,
housekeepers. I think the
as such, ought not to be directly represented on the committee.
Passing from that and coming back
to the retailers, I am afraid there is a
Clause-an entire Clause-in the Bill to
which I am unable to agree. That is
Clause 6. There are two Clauses in the
Bill dealing more or less specially with
retailers-Clauses 5 and 6. With
Clause 5 I am pretty much entirely in
sympathy, because I think it will operate
in order to secure greater certainty for
the consumer as to the quality of his
eggs. There are,
as it seems to me,
two minor objections to that Clause, but,
taking it as a whole, I think Clause 5
is a good Clause. But when we come to
Clause 6 I am afraid it will be my duty
at a later stage, if the Bill gets a Second
Reading this afternoon, to move the deletion of that Clause entirely. I believe
that, notwithstanding the Title of the
Bill, you, Mr. Speaker, would admit a
Motion in Committee to omit Clause 6
without the necessity of the House giving
the Committee special instructions as to
their powers.
The omission of Clause 6, as far as I
can see, would only deprive the Bill of a
certain amount of its superstructure. It
would not hit the ship below the water
line. One of the objections to Clause 6
from the retailer's point of view is that
he asks himself, first of all, what he is
going to get out of it. I am afraid I
cannot see that he is going to get anything. Some of the retailers will get
what might be called their candling done
for them, but for that the retailer will
pay in the price he pays the wholesaler,
and for that again he will recoup himself
out of the pocket of the consumer, or
will try to. And he will get something
else which he does not want, if I read
the Bill correctly. He will acquire for
re-sale a certain number of new laid eggs,
and no doubt will dispose of them.
hope he will get as many as he likes. But
we cannot get away from the fact that,
if the Ministry succeeds in flooding Great
Britain with new laid eggs from Northern
Ireland, there will not be many new laid
eggs left for the consumers in Northern
Ireland. We have had that difficulty
before. I do not know if it became acute
in the case of the butter legislation, but
I
389 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

2 JUNE 1936
Second Reading
that is what we would expect to find. If
you require new laid eggs for consumption in Northern Ireland you must not
send all your new laid eggs out of Northern Ireland to Great Britain. But
then the retailer is going to get some of
these other things called seconds. It
seems to me that unless the retailer is able
to dispose of his seconds with reasonable
rapidity those seconds will very quickly
become thirds and fourths, and soon he
will not be able to sell them at all, certainly not to the ordinary breakfast consumer.
When
Then, again, your retailer is to have
the privilege of paying a licence fee. If
he is a small man he is to pay £2, and if
he is a large man he is to pay 10/- plus
a sum not exceeding id per dozen. That
is how it stands in the Bill now.
the Bill first made its appearance it was
a definite figure of 1d, I think, but that
proved difficult to defend elsewhere and
the substitution to which I refer was
made. What that d a dozen will cost
him in the year will depend a good deal
on the size of his business. I do not
know whether the Belfast Co-operative
Society, for instance, sells eggs. I passed
the place this morning and had a look,
but then I reflected that the Belfast Cooperative Society, being a well-managed
concern, would not put their eggs in a
shop window, and, therefore, I do not
know; but if they do they would naturally sell a very large number, and the
effect of this provision on their finances
would not be negligible. I cannot see
what the retailer stands to get out of this
Clause, or that the consumer is going to
be greatly protected either as regards.
quality or price as a result of its inclusion
in the Bill. Wherefore, as I have said,
I shall at a later stage ask the Committee
to remove that Clause from the Bill. I
would venture to say by way of suggestion
to the Ministry that in a Bill of this sort
you might very well try it at first without such a superstructure as I have mentioned, see how you get on without Clause
6, and then if necessary the matter could
be reconsidered, because, after all, nobody supposes that this is going to be the
last Marketing of Eggs Bill. I do not
think I would be in favour of bringing
every regulation before Parliament.
fact, I do not see that in this Bill there
are going to be any regulations made
that will be suitable for bringing before
Parliament.
In
There are other matters of more or less
importance which I shall only briefly
mention. I do not myself approve of constituting the Ministry of Agriculture, or
any other Ministry, interpreter of the
Statute of Frauds, nor do I see any particular object in creating this Committee a
Corporation and providing them with a
common seal when we are not told, if
they have a common seal, what in the
world they are going to do with it. One
cannot imagine that an advisory committee, without funds or without executive function, will have the need to
attach their seal formally to any document, or, again, in looking through this
Bill and the rest of the Marketing of
Eggs code preceding it, I have failed to
discover any nut of sufficient importance
or magnitude to call for the use of the
Nasmyth hammer of indictment.
There remains another point that I
think was mentioned elsewhere, and that
was in my mind when I spoke of the Bill
being under-discussed. That is the
question of whether there will be any
overlapping of the functions which the
Ministry propose to assume under the
Bill, and the work of the local authority
as regards the maintenance of proper
standards under the Food and Public
Health Acts. We shall have to consider
that rather carefully in order to see that
there is no danger of overlapping, and
in order to make such provision as may
be called for if we are not satisfied on
the matter.
There are also two other strong
passages in the Bill which I shall not
mention this afternoon. All these are
matters for Committee, and I do not
think they should present any insuperable difficulty. But we are thrown
back on that question of the retailer's
licence. I am afraid I must say that if
Clause 6 is to remain in the Bill I shall
not be able to support it in its final
stages. I presume it is not the intention
to take the Committee Stage for at least
a week, and I would urge that if amendments are put down the Order Paper
should be circulated as speedily as
possible in order to provide time for a
second or maybe a third edition between
to-day and the Committee Stage.
the whole, though I am obliged to make
particular reservations with regard to the
Bill, I should like to congratulate the
Ministry on their courage in endeavour-
On
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
ing to take the improvements already
effected yet a step further, and I should
also like to express my hope that, whatever the form in which the Bill may
leave this House, it may serve towards
the end which is in view.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Bill, because I
believe it is an honest effort on the part
of the Government and the Ministry of
Agriculture to assist the farming community. As has been mentioned by the
noble Lord, the Leader of the House,
this is another Bill like those that have
already been passed, and which have
proved of great value and benefit to the
agricultural community. Some time
ago we passed a Derating Bill. There
did not seem to be much criticism of
that Bill. Everybody seemed to think
it was a thing that was needed. Then
a Pork Marketing Bill came before the
House, and it was severely criticised
until it got into working order, and now
I do not hear any criticism of it.
producers are satisfied with the work of
that committee, which was appointed by
the Ministry of Agriculture to look after
their interests so far as prices and all the
rest of it are concerned. Then the Milk
Marketing Board came before this House,
and it was also criticised; it, too, has
proved a great success and of great value
to those who produce milk. Now we
have this Egg Marketing Bill, and I find
that we have criticism of the same
character. My theory is this, that we
should prove all things, test all things,
before condemning them. I think that
this Bill when it is put into operation
will prove of great benefit both to the
producer and the consumer.
The
I know something about the marketing
of eggs.
I know that in the past those
who produced eggs were not paid for
their trouble, and I have not the least
doubt that when the various Clauses of
this Bill are put into operation they will
bring about a better class of egg and a
better class of poultry as a whole.
the past the farmer and the egg producer
sold their eggs by the dozen. If the eggs
were large, the producer got the same
price as if they were small. I find that
by this Bill eggs are going to be bought
and sold by weight, and that system will
In
no doubt prove of great benefit to the
producer and the consumer. I think
that when this Bill is working, say, six
months it will have produced a great
change in the tone of those who are
criticising it to-day.
The poultry industry is worth looking
after in Northern Ireland. In the paper
a few weeks ago I saw that the poultry
industry of Northern Ireland was a most
valuable one; the value of the industry last
year amounted to £3,500,000. That is
a great amount of money to be raised
from poultry, and I am sure that when
this Bill becomes operative the amount
will be doubled. There will be encouragement for men who understand poultry to
start poultry farms here, there, and elsewhere throughout the country, when they
see that they are going to be paid for
their trouble. I find that in the first
three months of this year Ulster exported
something like five million dozen eggs.
That is worth considering. If we have
done that under present circumstances,
what will we not do when we get better
prices and a better market? I support
the Bill whole-heartedly, because I believe
that in twelve months' time the producers
of eggs will have more money in their
pockets than they have at the present
time.
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE
(Sir Basil Brooke): I think it is quite
a natural thing that these marketing
Bills should receive a measure of criticism,
because they interfere to some extent
with the existing methods of trading. I
think the natural reaction of those involved in the change would be that they
would like to know first of all whether
that change would put any more money
into their pockets. It is a little difficult
in introducing a Bill of this kind to
convey by words one's confidence that
the working of a Bill of this sort will
in fact mean an increase in the general
circulation of money among those engaged
in the trade. That, of course, is a difficulty that has always got to be faced.
I think, however, hon. Senators know
that the Marketing Bills that have been
introduced by the Ministry of Agriculture
so far have been to some extent successful
in achieving that aim, and I feel confident that this Bill is going to help the
most valuable individual side of agriculture in this country.
393 Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Second Reading

2 JUNE 1936
Shortly before this Bill came before
Parliament I went over to London to
visit the big egg centre there in order
to get a picture in my own mind of what
this would mean, and I am quite certain
of this, that the shippers and wholesale
handlers in Great Britain are going to
buy only eggs which are of the first
quality. They have a choice from every
part of the world. Every country in the
world is sending its products to the
London market. Each year the eggs are
better graded, each year they are better
packed. If we wish to carry on and to
maintain our position we have got to
be prepared to move with the times.
These wholesale dealers are not attracted
by the territorial origin of the eggs;
they do not care where the eggs come
from. The only attractions are quality
and selling power.
There are three things that the Government have to face. They can do nothing; they can leave the trade as it is
at present, and make no further regulations, in which case a situation may
arise such as threatened us last summer,
when the price of eggs was rising on the
London market and there was a very
natural tendency on the part of producers to hold their eggs for that rise.
If that happened, as it did last year,
in a hot season, it does not require great
imagination on the part of hon. Senators,
I am sure, to realise that the eggs would
not arrive on the English breakfast
table in a very savoury condition. It
was only by taking the most stringent
action on that occasion that the egg
position was saved by the Ministry.
In passing, I may say that I was told
by a big wholesale dealer of two incidents. One Empire country was unfortunate enough last year to send over a
consignment of inferior eggs. To this
day that country has not got on to the
English market. That country is a big
country, and perhaps can stand the loss
of the egg trade, but I am quite certain
that a small country such as ours could
not stand it; it would be disastrous to
us. The other case was one where some
Northern Ireland duck eggs-which are
not controlled under the scheme- were
bought by a man from one of these
wholesale dealers. Those eggs were in
a deplorable condition, and the purchaser came back to the wholesaler and
said, "I do not want to see any more
Northern Ireland stuff; I have seen quite
enough of it."
I think when one appreciates the fact
that we hold our position on a quality
basis, one must conclude that we should
not hesitate to take the lead, as we are
doing, in marketing schemes in order to
place our stuff on the first quality market
in England. I have said that we could
do nothing. If, however, we decided to
go one step further we could institute a
Board as we have done in the case of the
Pigs Marketing Board. I think, perhaps,
there is some confusion of thought with
regard to the difference between a board
and a committee or council. If a board
were set up to run this egg scheme, it
would mean that no one would be responsible to Parliament, such as the
Minister of Agriculture, for whatever
action that board took. The board
would have control of its own finances.
It would control all the produce which
it was set up to deal with, as the Pigs
Marketing Board does at the present
time. The present method is different,
because the necessity of maintaining the
sales organisation is very much stronger.
The Pigs Board are in the position of
trustees taking the place of the farmers
who formerly sold their pigs either to the
local curer or to curers in Belfast. If
we set up a board under the egg marketing scheme, that board would have to
take control of all the eggs produced in
Northern Ireland, and would have to set
up a very complicated and very efficient
sales organisation. I am sure it will
be readily understood that any effort to
alter the organisation that exists at present would be disastrous for the industry.
Therefore, under the present method,
the Minister of Agriculture, whoever he
may be, is responsible to the House for
whatever action the committee take. If
the Minister is made responsible for the
actions which are taken in regulating our
supplies it is necessary that he should be
given special power. It would be quite
unfair to say to any Minister, "You are
responsible for all that happens, but we,
as a House, refuse to give you power to
carry out what you think is the best
thing to do."
Admittedly there is a
certain amount of bureaucracy. As I
have said in another place, I object to
bureaucracy probably as much as any
Member of that House. But there is no
alternative unless you go back to the
395 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Sir Basil Brooke]
attitude of doing nothing. If you accept
the position that a certain amount of
bureaucracy is necessary then I think you
must give the Minister or the Ministry
power to carry out the intentions of the
Bill.
Now I would like to deal with one or
two points raised by hon. Senators. I
will not discuss Clause 6, which was
referred to by Senator Leslie, because
that can wait for another day. There is
a difference made in the amount to be
paid for a licence for the reason that if
you were to ask a small shopkeeper to
keep an account in farthings of each dozen
eggs that were sold it would complicate
his accounts and would be quite unnecessary. What we have done is to make the
maximum fee £2, and that represents
only a very small number of eggs. He is
benefiting to this extent; that when he
formerly bought from the wholesalers he
did not always have a guarantee that all
the eggs were of first quality. In passing,
I might say, in reply to the question
asked by Senator Sir Joseph Davison,
that a retailer may purchase his eggs, as
heretofore, where he likes. The responsibility is on him that those eggs, if purchased in Northern Ireland, shall be unmarked, if they are foreign or imported
eggs they should be marked "foreign" or
"imported," and if they are seconds they
should be marked "seconds." The retailer
is a link in the chain and he has a certain
advantage, and I think the fee he is
being charged is not an exorbitant one.
The
With regard to representation on the
committee that, as hon. Members know,
was rather a thorny question in another
place. On paper it might appear that
the producer has not got fair representation on the committee, but in fact the
collectors, the "B" licence holders, are
in this position, and this is the position
that is put forward by the shippers, and
it is, I think, a sound argument.
two representatives of the collectors are
bound, in their own interests alone, to
see to it that the producer gets as good a
price as he can, that the margin between
the producers' and the shippers' price is
as narrow as possible, because if they do
not do so, if they allow a big difference
as between what the shipper gets
and what the producer is paid,
it is quite obvious that the shipper
would do without the collector, who in
most cases in the small towns is a local
grocer, and
he would collect himself,
because he could then afford to send out
his own vans. If there is a small difference in the price I think it is only natural
that the shipper would not be able to
afford to send out his own vans, and the
grocer will do the collecting. Therefore,
where price is concerned, the two retailers
are more likely to vote for the producer
receiving a higher rather than a lower
price.
May I now just touch on three points
which are sometimes looked upon with
some doubt. I have had various questions
addressed to me as to what is going to
happen to the producer. The producer
to be under the impression that
this Bill is going to do something terrible
to him. All that is going to happen to the
producer is this: Heretofore he received
a flat rate from whoever he sold his eggs
to. If he sold his eggs to the shipper
that shipper knew that a certain proportion of those eggs were of second-class
quality. If the shipper paid for those
eggs on the assumption that they were
all of first-class quality, and twenty,
thirty, or forty per cent. proved to be
seconds, he would receive a lower price
for them. Therefore, when the shipper
purchased eggs he did so with that possibility in mind. Under the provisions of
this Bill those producer's eggs will go up
and be tested under the supervision of
the Ministry-and this is a point I want
to emphasise.
The producer is not left at the mercy of
the shipper. Just as under the pork
scheme pork is graded under the Ministry's supervision, so eggs will be tested
under the supervision of the Ministry,
and the Ministry will see to it that the
eggs of the producer are properly graded.
That will mean, roughly, a week's delay
in the producer getting his price for those
eggs. That delay occurs already in the
case of milk sent to the creameries and
pork sent to the curers, and after that
the normal flow will go on. There is no
interference whatever with the present
system of credit given to the grocer who
supplies eggs.
With regard to the shipper, he knows
that he is paying for a quality article,
and, unlike his position in the past,
when he perhaps could not afford to come
back on the producer who supplied bad
397 Marketing of Eggs Bil..

2 JUNE 1936
Transport Bill
eggs, he will know now that he is paying
for a type of egg that is being tested in
his own store, and, to use an easy expression, he is inducing the producer to
produce eggs, not by forcing him to do
so through the courts, but by paying for
the quality which he receives. I have
no hesitation in recommending this Bill
to the House, as I believe it gives a lead
in the marketing of eggs, and is a Bill
which I consider is of the very greatest
importance in an industry which is one
of the staple branches of agriculture in
this country.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Road and Railway Transport Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.

o'clock
399 Destructive Imported Animals

THE SENATE
Marketing of Eggs Bill.
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 9th June, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Trew's Divorce Bill.
The Amendments made by the Joint
Committee were considered and agreed to.
Destructive Imported Animals.
This
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
"That the Order made by the Ministry of
Agriculture, under Section 8 (1) of the
Destructive Imported Animals Act (Northern Ireland), 1933, prohibiting the
importation into and the keeping within
Northern Ireland of grey squirrels be
hereby approved," I may say we have
already had an interesting discussion on
the grey squirrel, and it is not necessary
for me to say very much about it.
species of squirrel has not yet found a
footing in Northern Ireland, but it may,
as people do not know its habits, and it
is an animal of attractive appearance.
One or two attempts have been made to
import specimens. On each occasion the
Ministry of Agriculture have been
successful in dissuading the owners from
carrying out their intention, but persuasion may not always prove successful
and legal powers are therefore sought. I
beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Appropriation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: As this Bill is in its
usual form, and is, as hon. Members are
aware, a purely finance Measure, I do
not think it necessary for me to enter
into any explanation of the Clauses. I
beg to move, "That the Bill be now read
a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now committed."
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered, That the Bill be read the
Third time on Tuesday next.
Marketing of Eggs Bill.
Order for the House to be put intc
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE I (Determination by Ministry
of Agriculture of prices and terms for
purchase of eggs by dealers)—
(1) The Ministry of Agriculture (in
this Act referred to as "the Ministry'')
shall have power to determine from
time to time the prices at, below or
above which, and the terms on which,
eggs or eggs of any particular class or
description may be purchased by
licensed wholesale dealers, and the areas
within which such prices and terms are
to have effect.
(2) The Ministry shall not make any
determination under sub-section (1) of
this section except in accordance with
advice given by the Committee established under this Act.
(3) Without prejudice to the generality of the provisions of sub-section (1)
of this section, the Ministry shall have
power to prescribe the terms on which
and the form in which contracts shall
be made for the purchase of eggs by
licensed wholesale dealers of Class A
from licensed wholesale dealers of
Class B.
(4) Any licensed wholesale dealer who
purchases eggs otherwise than at a price
and on terms which are in accordance
with a determination of the Ministry
under this section shall be guilty of an
offence against the principal Act.
(5) Section seven of the Act of 1926
(which relates to the purchase of hen
eggs by weight when bought by traders)
shall not have effect in the case of any
purchase of eggs to which a determination of the Ministry under this section
applies or is extended in pursuance of
this Act.
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee
9 JUNE 1936
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Subclause (1) to leave out the words "at,
below or above which." These five words
seem to me to convey the very distinct
suggestion that not only fixed prices
should be determined but also maximum
and minimum prices. I cannot find in
the Bill or in the lithographed memorandum which was circulated with the Bill
any indication that there is any intention
or proposal to fix maximum and minimum
prices. The only intention I can detect
is the proposal to arrive at a fixed price,
but that fixed price may vary according
to the description of the egg. If that
be so, and if there is no special reason
for inserting these words, I think they
had better be taken out as likely to cause
confusion.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I regret I am
unable to accept this Amendment, the
effect of which would be to remove from
the Ministry and the Egg Marketing Committee the power to fix minimum or maximum prices, leaving only the power to fix
standard prices. I feel confident that in
considering the question of price fixing
the Egg Marketing Committee will desire
to consider the possibility of minimum.
and maximum prices as well as the fixing
of our standard price. Different methods
may be adopted for different areas and
for different classes of sales. The drafting of this particular provision follows
exactly the precedent of the Milk and
Milk Products Act, 1934, the Agricultural Marketing (Pig Industry) Act,
1934, and the Agricultural Marketing
Act, 1933. I notice that the Joint Milk
Council has, in some instances, fixed
minimum prices and in others has adopted
the principle of a single standard price,
which cannot be departed from either upwards or downwards. The Pig Industry
Council has also used its power to fix
minimum prices in certain instances, and
I feel, therefore, that it is only reasonable that a similar discretion should be
given in the case of egg prices.
Mr. LESLIE: If the Minister wishes
to retain the words I do not think the
Committee will object.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out Sub-Clause (2). This is, perhaps, a
little more troublesome. In considering
this Amendment I must ask the Committee to look at the Amendment which
follows it on the Paper, and also at the
Sub-clause which follows it in the text,
and to consider how Sub-clause (2) stood
when it made its first appearance in
another place.
It may be remembered
that the original Sub-clause (2) was expressed a little differently. The Minister,
I think, was only required to consult the
Committee. He was not required to act
in accordance with their advice. The
Minister said he had always intended to
act according to their advice, and he
accepted an Amendment on the ground
that it made no difference in his intentions. That being so, it seems to me not
only from what the Minister said in
another place, but also from sub-clause
(1) (a) of Clause 4 that it must also be
the intention of the Minister to act in
accordance with the advice of his Committee when it comes to considering the
question dealt with in Sub-clause (3),
that is, the terms and form of contracts.
Therefore it seems to me that we are
driven to the two Amendments on the
Paper as a corollary to what has already
been done elsewhere.
The second Amendment simply means
replacing Sub-clause (2), temporarily removed, with the necessary verbal alteration. I may say at once that there are no
powers in the Section other than those
referred to. In order to keep ourselves
in line with Clause 4, which deals with
the Section, as a whole, we must make
this alteration. As a first step, therefore, I move the Amendment to leave out
Sub-clause (2), but only to reintroduce
it.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am prepared
to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I now formally move
after sub-clause (3) to insert the following new sub-clause :-
"(3) The Ministry shall not exercise
any power conferred by this section
except in accordance with advice given
by the Committee established under
this Act."
This is consequential to the acceptance
of the first Amendment.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am prepared
to accept that.
403 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: My next Amendment
was in sub-clause (5) to leave out "hen."
I am very much afraid the noble Viscount
has stolen the greater part of my
thunder.
If he pleads guilty I will not move this
Amendment, but I will retaliate later on.
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Effect of determinations
of Ministry on contracts).
*
