(2) It shall be the duty of the
Ministry, on the application of any
party to such a contract as aforesaid,
to register the contract within the
period of fourteen days after the
application, unless-
(a) the application is made after
the time at which it is provided by
the proviso to sub-section (1) of this
section that the said sub-section
shall cease to apply to the contract;
or
(b) the Ministry is of opinion-
(i) that the contract is unenforceable by any party thereto by
virtue of the provisions of section
two of the Statute of Frauds or
section four of the Sale of Goods
Act, 1893, or
(ii) in the case of a contract
made during the relevant period,
that the contract was made with a
view to evading the operation of
such determination of the Ministry
as aforesaid.
If any party to a contract is
aggrieved by the omission of the
Ministry to register the contract within
the period aforesaid he may, within
twenty-one days after the expiration of
that period, appeal to the Court and,
pending the determination of any such
appeal, sub-section (1) of this section
shall, notwithstanding anything in the
proviso thereto, continue to apply to
the contract.
(3) On any appeal under this section,
the Ministry and any party to the contract may appear and be heard, and if,
on the hearing of any such appeal, the
Court finds-
*
(a) that the application for registration of the contract was made
before the time at which it is provided by the proviso to sub-section
(1) of this section that the said subsection shall cease to apply to the
contract; and
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Subclause (2) (b), paragraph (i), to leave out
from "unenforceable" to the end of the
paragraph. This is a little more troublesome. I mentioned on the Second
Reading that I am afraid I cannot agree
that it is desirable for the Ministry even
in the first instance to act as a kind of
court of inquiry as to whether the conditions of the Statute of Frauds as regards
the Sale of Eggs Act are or are not complied with in the case of any so-called
contract. Instead of moving the amendment on the Paper I would really very
much rather move to omit the entire
paragraph. I do not do that because I
recognise that the Ministry may want
some protection to prevent bogus or nongenuine contracts being thrown at them.
I do not want to deprive them of any
power which they ought to have. At the
same time I may point out that in what
I may call the mother Act, in this point
the English Act of 1931, nothing like this
paragraph appears. You cannot very
well say a contract which is not a genuine
contract or a contract that is a bogus
contract, for in such case the thing would
not be a contract at all. I do not think
we can have the Ministry entering into a
discussion and really holding a small court
of inquiry, as they would have to do on
a point of this sort. I do not think it
is their job. If the Committee accepts
my amendment it might very well be
followed up at a later stage by cutting
out what is left of the paragraph. I
hesitate to do that in the first instance,
as I have already said, but I do move
the amendment on the Paper. I might
add that we have given the same power
to councils and boards in some of our
other Acts, but that is not quite the
same thing as giving power to the Ministry to deal with a purely legal question.
I do not think it is right.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am unable
to accept this amendment. I understand
the hon. and learned Senator objects to
405 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

9 JUNE 1936
Committee
the Ministry being placed in a position
to express an opinion as to whether a
contract is unenforceable under the provisions of Section 2 of the Statute of
Frauds or of Section 4 of the Sale of
Goods Act, 1893. I would point out,
however, that under Section 9 of the
Agricultural Marketing (Pig Industry)
Act, 1934, the Pig Industry Council has
been given a similar power in regard to
contracts dealing with the sale of pigs,
while the Joint Milk Council is also placed
in a similar position with reference to
milk by virtue of Section 7 of the Milk
and Milk Products Act, 1934. A Marketing Board established under the Agricultural Marketing Act, 1933, is also
empowered by Section 8 of that Act to
exercise the same power. These bodies
act, of course, in accordance with the
legal advice they receive, and I fail to
see why it is in any way objectionable
that the Ministry of Agriculture-which
will, of course, act on the advice of the
Government's legal advisers should have
this power.
Moreover, I would point out the amendment proposed does not, in fact, remove
from the power of the Ministry the
decision as to whether a contract is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds
Leslie, Mr. James G.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.

Amendment negatived.
or the Sale of Goods Act. On the
contrary, it still leaves the decision of
these questions with the Ministry, and
in addition the decision of the question
whether a contract is unenforceable for
any other reason.
In these circumstances and since there
is a right of appeal from the decision of
the Ministry direct to the County Court,
or in certain circumstances to the High
Court, I trust the hon. and learned
Senator will not press his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I always hate to disagree, but I am afraid that this is really
a very important principle. The argument the right hon. Gentleman has used
is that he has done something, which I
believe to be wrong, twice already, and
we ought to do it a third time. As I
have pointed out already, it does not
occur in the English Act of 1931. It is
a question of giving a Minister power to
act on a legal point, and I do not think
it is right. I am sorry I cannot withdraw the Amendment.
Question put, "That these words be
left out."

(1) For the purposes of this Act there
shall be established, as soon as may be
after the passing of this Act, a body to
be called the Egg Marketing Committee for Northern Ireland (in this
Act referred to as "the Committee'),
which shall be a body corporate with
perpetual succession and a common
seal, and with power to acquire and
hold land without licence in mortmain.
*
*
*
*
*
*
(5) The seal of the Committee shall
be authenticated by the signature of
the chairman of the Committee or some
other member of the Committee

Committee
authorised by the Committee to act in
that behalf, and of the secretary or
some other person authorised by the
Committee to act in that behalf.
(6) Every document purporting to be
an order or other instrument issued by
the Committee and to be sealed with
the seal of the Committee authenticated in manner provided by this
section, or to be signed by the secretary or any person authorised to act
in that behalf, shall be received in
evidence and be deemed to be such
order or instrument without further
proof, unless the contrary is shown.

move
in
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to
Clause 3, sub-clause (1), to leave out from
the word "Committee" ("the Committee') to the end of the sub-clause.
This is a question as to whether a purely
advisory committee, without any executive
powers and without any money at its own
disposal, ought to be made a corporation.
In other cases boards and councils have
been made corporations, but they have
something behind them. I do not think
it is desirable that we should scatter corporations wildly round the land, nor do I
see any reason why this particular advisory committee should be elevated into
a corporation. If we do that the position.
will be that they will have to be provided
with a common seal, but with no power
to produce any documents that will require sealing; and their documents will
be received as proof in any court, and we
cannot see any reason to suppose that
they will have any documents that will
have to be produced in court. The only
power they will have is if it was intended
to allow them to have freehold land to
put offices on, but they will not have any
money to pay for a site or offices, because
all the money is, and perhaps properly,
at the disposal of the Ministry itself.
But that pro-
A suggestion was made in another place
that the Committee should be placed in
charge of the Egg Marketing Fund, and
if that suggestion had been accepted a
good deal of what I am saying would have
been rather unnecessary.
posal was very properly rejected, and it
was decided that any funds obtainable
through the agency of the Egg Marketing
Fund should be under the control of the
Minister primarily responsible to Parliament. I cannot see any reason for creating this purely advisory committee into a
corporation. I think it would not be in
accordance with precedent, and it would.
not be appropriate to do so. Accordingly
I beg to move.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I accept that
Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I now beg to move to
leave out Sub-clauses (5) and (6) of
Clause 3. This is consequential on the
other Amendment.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am prepared
to accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Question put, "That Clause 3, as
amended, stand part of the Bill."
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I would like to
ask, in the first place, the estimated cost
of this advisory committee. In the second
place, I should like to know what is the
Ulster Farmers' Union, and how many
members are included in that organisation.
I would also like to put the same question
as applying to the Ulster Agricultural
Organisation Society and the Northern
Utility Poultry Society. I must confess
that as a public man I have never heard
of some of these societies. I have heard a
lot of talk about the Ulster Farmers'
Union, and I would like to know who
they are, because they do not represent
the Ulster farmers. As regards the
Ulster Agricultural Organisation Society
and the Northern Utility Poultry Society,
what claims have they to come in under
this Bill?
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am afraid I
could not enlighten the hon. Member as
to the number of members of these societies, but I am sure the House will appreciate that in forming an electorate of
producers the only method by which the
Ministry could work was by taking the
organised bodies already in existence and
consulting with them as to their representatives to be appointed on the Egg Committee. It would naturally be quite
impossible, as I am sure the hon. Member
will appreciate, to have any sort of an
election of all the producers in this
country, but there is machinery in the
Bill providing that in the event of any
new organisations being set up they will
be consulted.

Mr. McLAUGHLIN : I do suggest
that the Ulster Farmers' Union does not
represent the farmers of Ulster.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: This is an admission by the Minister of Agriculture that
the Ulster Farmers' Union does not really
represent the farmers. I did not receive
an answer to my question as to the estimated cost of this advisory committee.
Therefore I intend to oppose this Clause.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
5, to leave out paragraph (c) of Subclause (5) at the top of page 8. It seems
to me that the dice are, so to speak, being
weighted unduly against a man. It is
bad enough at any time to require a man
to prove his innocence instead of the prosecution proving his guilt, but in this
case we are going a great deal too far.
I do not think it is reasonable to require
as proof that you are not guilty of an
offence against a particular paragraph of
a particular sub-section, that the eggs
were still in the case in which they had
been received from the dealer. A man
may have taken half-a-dozen eggs out of
the case for various reasons, and I do not
think it is a reasonable requirement that
in order to establish his innocence he must
prove that the eggs are still in the egg
case in which they were received from the
dealer. I cannot agree that that is a
provision Parliament ought to allow.
Therefore, I beg to move that it be left
out.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I regret I am
unable to accept this amendment. The
purpose of this provision is to facilitate
the administration of this section, and I
feel that both in the interests of the
Ministry and of the retailer this provision should be retained. It is reasonable that a retailer who purchases eggs
from a Class A wholesale dealer should
for at least a period of three days be
entitled to rely upon the freshness of the
eggs. The onus of the proof that the
eggs have in fact been purchased from a
Class A merchant within three days of
the alleged offence rests with the retailer.
The most natural proof which the retailer
411 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Sir Basil Brooke]
can offer is that the eggs are in the egg
case in which they have been obtained
from the dealer. If they have been removed from this case then quite obviously
the retailer may experience great difficulty
in proving that these eggs were in fact
purchased from licensed wholesale
dealer.
a
I am readily prepared to admit that the
presence of eggs in a particular egg case
may not be conclusive proof that those
eggs had been in fact purchased in that
case from a wholesale dealer within a
period of three days. At the same time
it does afford a certain measure of evidence. Moreover, since it is in fact the
practice for retailers to retail eggs from
the egg case in which supplies have been
delivered from wholesale dealers, I do not
think that any difficulty should be experienced from this provision. It
operates, in fact, as a help to the retailer
by indicating the type of proof which
he should supply to the inspector with
regard to his purchases of eggs from a
wholesale dealer.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I cannot
accept the explanation that this is
merely a matter of simplifying administration. It seems to me to infringe on
an established principle, and you cannot
do that. The offence, or suggested
offence, may very well arise in connection
with half-a-dozen eggs which are on
counter. It may be that the bulk of the
parcel purchased is still in the case, but
the particular eggs would not be. I
think it unfair to put so much on the
shoulder of the defendant. I cannot
withdraw the Amendment.
a
Sir BASIL BROOKE: Perhaps I did
not make myself quite clear. The point
is that if the retailer can satisfy the
inspector that no eggs have been removed
from that case, and that these eggs were
purchased three days ago from a wholesaler, that is one of the best proofs he
can produce that he was not responsible
for the selling of the eggs.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I would like to
know in what way the retailer is to show
his eggs.
If he wants to show them in
a shop window he cannot show the whole
case, and he will be convicted if he takes
some out of the case.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: The hon.
Senator knows very well that one of the
worst methods of advertising the egg
trade is by putting eggs into the window.
Nothing makes fresh eggs go bad more
quickly than that. I think that is one
of the orders that will be made by the
Ministry.
Mr. LESLIE: Still there remains this
question of the possibility of the breaking
of bulk perfectly innocently. All the
eggs may not still be in the box. I quite
agree that if they are in the box, and if
you can prove that is the box you received from the dealer, it may be
suggested by the inspector that it is a
dummy box in which the eggs were put.
All sorts of things may happen.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I agree with
the hon. and learned Senator opposite.
The eggs may have been in the producers'
hands six days before they reached the
retailer, and they may be three days
there after that.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: That would not
happen, because the eggs would have
been passed either as new-laid or second
eggs. They would be forwarded from
the wholesaler to the retailer. If that is
done within three days the chances of
those eggs being of second quality are
remote. The hon. and learned
very
Senator mentioned the fact that the case
might possibly be broken.
I presume he
meant the eggs had been taken out and
some were put on the counter.
Mr. LESLIE: The bulk broken, not
necessarily the box.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: There is a
definite check on that, because the eggs
that come from the wholesaler are weightgraded as well, so that if a case from a
retailer had been filled up with other
eggs by the retailer there would be a
perfectly easy check by the Ministry, bethe weight grading would not
correspond with the weight grading on
cause
the box.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: It
all points to the suggestion I made
originally that this Bill is very difficult
to criticise until the regulations are made,
so much depends on what regulations are
made for carrying the Act into effect.

Mr. LESLIE: I do not think there is
anything more to say. I cannot withdraw the Amendment.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Will this House
have any power to make those regulations
or to control them? You are going to
pass a Bill subject to regulations being
made, and you have no power to make
those regulations. You are giving the
Government a blank cheque.

The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie: Clause 5, page 8,

(i).
Mr. LESLIE: I think there is a Standing Order which provides that a particular
point in Committee may be deferred until
a subsequent Clause has been considered,
and I would ask that this Amendment
stand over until we have considered the
next one, the point being that if the
decision on the next Amendment takes
one course it will simplify the discussion
on this matter, otherwise this particular
paragraph can be more efficiently attacked

The CHAIRMAN: This can stand
over. We will take the next Amendment.
CLAUSE 6 (Licensing of sellers of
eggs by retail).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out Clause 6. This, I am inclined to
think, is probably the most important
question we shall have to consider in
relation to the Bill. The Clause deals
with the licensing of sellers of eggs by
retail and incidentally with, shall I say,
the pains and penalties to be imposed on
a man who presumes to satisfy the
national taste for the breakfast egg. That
is really what the Clause deals with.
McHugh, Mr. J.
McLaughlin, Mr. T.
Nugent, Sir Rowland
Perceval-Maxwell, Capt. John Robt
Porter, Mr. John Porter
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
was
Now, let us see what there is to be said
in support of my suggestion that it would.
be better to leave the Clause out of the
Bill altogether. It may be remembered
that I suggested when speaking on the
Second Reading that this Clause
really only part of the superstructure of
the Bill, and that it could be removed
without hitting the ship below the water
line, that is to say, that the rest of the
ship could go forward, as we hope, to
success and Clause 6 could be left on the
quay. Perhaps if it was found later on
that something of the sort was necessary
the Minister might come back to Parliament and ask for additional powers in
this direction, but my main suggestion is
that we should try the Bill first of all
without this Clause.
Το
Let us now look at the Clause.
begin with it is, of course, an obvious
infringement of the liberty of the subject.
Why should a man be required to take
out a licence to do something he is perfectly entitled to do? This is not a
question of an excise licence such as is
required for the sale of tobacco and other
things, which need not be mentioned, and
if this Clause stands as it comes before
us it will make it illegal, so far as I can
see, to sell a basket of eggs at a bazaar in
aid, shall we say, of a church hall or boy
scouts, or anything else, because that will
certainly be selling eggs by retail. That
will be prohibited under this Clause unless the stallholder or the bazaar com-
415 Marketing of Eggs Bill-.

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
mittee, I do not know which, take out a
licence. That is one of the minor results,
but from that you can argue this, why is
it necessary that these restrictions and
this penalty, as I have called it, should be
imposed?
Before dealing with the main question.
I will go a little further. Why is it
necessary that a person should be 21 years
of age before he or she can sell an egg by
retail? We can understand you must
have a licence. There may be difficulties
in issuing it to a minor, although I am
not aware that that obtains in such
matters as a game licence, or a dog licence,
or some of the other things, but, at all
events, it is a distinct deprivation that
young people may not sell eggs by retail
until they are 21.
Again, if you have to have a licence.
and the Ministry decide to refuse it, and
the Ministry's power of refusal or revocation was somewhat altered in another
place from the first proposal, against such
power as remains I cannot find there is
any appeal whatever. It was put up to
me anonymously through the post the
other day that for the purpose of this
Clause it was not by any means certain
what was meant by eggs. Whether
imported eggs come under this Clause or
not I am not bound to admit, because it
does not say anything specifically on that
point. I do not myself suggest it is
necessary because imported eggs and
pickled eggs are
a somewhat
separate line of rails altogether
but apparently there is doubt in the
public mind, I do not say the Parliamentary mind, as to whether this Clause
6 can apply to imported eggs. If it does
not my suggestion is there is a premium.
put on the sale of imported eggs. That is a
result which I am sure it was never intended should flow from the Clause.
on
I have not yet got a definite answer
as to why there should be a licence and
a licence fee unless it be that the Ministry.
is determined to get the money to run
this Bill from the consumer, and it has
fastened upon the retailer as a convenient
and perhaps not well organised source of
responsibility for supplying the money.
But at the same time the attempted imposition is most extortionate in one respect and unfair in another. We have
to agree, of course, that if a Bill is to be
run for the benefit of a particular trade
or industry the particular trade or industry should pay for it. This Bill, and
indeed the whole egg marketing scheme
as amended by this Bill, is distinctly run
in favour of the wholesale shipper. He is
the man who stands to make the best
profit, because the higher or better the
class of eggs that we send across to
England the better price we get, and the
bigger that man's trade and profit, and
so on. The wholesale shipper is nowadays
required to take out a licence and pay
£3 for it. He has also to pay something
or other in accordance with the quantities
of eggs he sells or ships, just as what I
would call for the moment the ten-shilling
man under this Clause has to pay his 10/-
down and a sum up to 1d a dozen in
respect of his sales. For convenience I
am going to speak of this imposition as
d a dozen, although I recognise, as we
all know, that the Ministry has power
to regulate the sum as long as it does
not exceed d. Exactly the same thing
applies in regard to the shipper. He is
to pay £3 and a sum not exceeding 1d
per thirty dozen eggs. That is to say
that under this Clause the retailer will be
asked to pay 7 times as much as the
wholesale shipper, assuming the maximum
to be still the same in each case. That,
of course, to be perfectly fair, will not
begin to operate until the retailer has
run off his first 2,400 dozen. That should
not take him very long if he is a big
man; but after that this wretched retailer
is to pay 7 times as much as the wholesale shipper. I do not think that is a
good augury for the success of the Bill
or likely to encourage the public to think
that the Bill is going to be fairly run as
between one class of trader and another.
Yet again, as regards the distinction
drawn between the retailer and the wholesaler, let us look at the last line of page 9.
The additional sum is not to be payable
in respect of eggs sold by the holder of a
retailer's licence if he is also the holder
of a Class A licence, that is, if he is a
wholesale shipper, but if he has not got
that licence then apparently he has to
pay.
That seems to me another instance
of what I may call weighting the dice
against the retailer and in favour of the
wholesaler.
Coming back to the fact that the retailer is to be asked to pay, one wants
to know what he is going to get for his
money. I cannot see that he is going
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee
9 JUNE 1936
to get anything. It is quite true, as I
indicated the other day, that he will get
some of his eggs tested before he sells
them. Well, he will pay for that in the
cost of the eggs, because he will not buy
tested eggs for the same price as he would
untested ones. There will be a distinction. Work will have to be done as
between the producer and the retailer;
the retailer will have to pay; whether the
retailer will take it out of the consumer
or not is another story. It may be that
he can, and it may be that he cannot.
A good deal will depend, I suppose,
the exact position at the time. At all
events, if the consumer has to pay, then
the price of eggs must rise.
on
And for what is all this being done?
The retailer and the home consumer are
not greatly interested in the expansion
of the egg trade in Great Britain. The
consumer is interested in the quality and
the price of his eggs, naturally enough,
and it may very well be that the effect
of this Bill will be to improve the general
quality, and so on. We hope it may.
But I do think the Bill would go on
perfectly well without Clause 6 and its
tail of satellites which hon. Members will
have noticed on the Order Paper. If
the Ministry cannot get enough money,
then I think they must either draw in
their horns or put a little more on the
wholesale shipper. And as regards that
wholesale shipper, I think I said I was
gaing to take what he pays at its
maximum of 1d. I think at present the
wholesale shipper's charge per 30 dozen
is down to d. The Minister may not
have the information before him at the
moment to say whether I am right or
wrong, but I think that is so. At all
events, if the wholesale shipper has not
yet got up to his maximum of 1d I cannot
see why he should not go there before
you resort to taxation on another
line of the industry. Whether it
is a proper tax or
whether it is
something in the nature of income tax
is another question which I will not go
into to-day. But I do urge the Committee most strongly to delete this Clause
and let the Ministry get along with the
Bill, or what we leave of it, without this
Clause in it. I think you are more likely
to meet with success in that way than in
any other.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I wish to support
the amendment.
I do so on various
grounds. In the first place, though I
have not had an opportunity of satisfying
myself that I am absolutely right, as far
as I can see there is no appeal from the
Ministry in regard to this matter. If
I am right, then I hold that this House
should not give power to the Ministry to
say whether a retailer is to stay in business or go out. If I am right in my
interpretation of the Bill, that is what
this Clause does. I say that is not a
power that should be left in the hands
of the Ministry.
I also oppose the Clause because the
House has not determined, and has not
the power to determine, what the licence
duty is to be. We allocate that to the
Ministry. I suggest that we, who are
moving about and dealing with the people
day by day, and who are not constantly
sitting in an official office breathing the
official atmosphere, are in a much better
position to say what the ordinary retailer should pay for a licence, if he is to
be licensed at all, than any official in the
Ministry of Agriculture.
I also suggest that it is a cumbersome
thing to ask these small retailers and
grocers to keep such records and such
books as the Ministry may under this
Clause prescribe for them. That is a
burden which should not be placed on
small grocers in country districts, who
really do not keep books. It is an unnecessary burden and one which could not
be complied with.
We have had no guide at all from the
Ministry as to who will get the benefit
of this licence fee of £2 and these
additional fees. The minds of the
Ministry themselves are most fluid and
indefinite about the matter. They have
not the slightest idea as to who is to get
the benefit of it. In the explanatory
paper which they issued they have
suggested that, perhaps, a person selling
60 dozen of eggs in a week would get the
benefit of that maximum of £2. But
it is clearly pointed out that that is not
provided for in the Bill as a guide,
showing that we are now asked to hand
over the administration of this matter to
the Ministry who, after their investigation of the whole question prior to the
introduction of this Bill, have to admit
in their explanatory paper that they have
no idea as to who should get the benefit
of that maximum licence duty.
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I am in favour
of leaving out this Cause, firstly upon the
ground that the Ministry are either
unable or unwilling to say what the
annual cost to the taxpayers of this Committee that is going to be set up will be,
and, secondly, what the cost of running
this Bill will be. I would venture to say
that there is no demand for this Bill, and
there certainly will be objection both from
the producer or the farmer and the consumer of the eggs. I do not see any reason
why I as a consumer of eggs should be
obliged to pay for the retailer's licence
and also to pay 1d per dozen afterwards.
I take it that this Bill is for the improvement of the eggs that are going to be exported, and if that is so I do not see any
necessity for this Clause. Again, I venture to say that if I get any bad eggs
from my shopkeeper I would feel inclined
to take the law into my own hands and go
and fire them at him. I do suggest that
this is a very far-reaching Clause, which
imposes a fresh tax on everybody concerned, and gives nothing in return.
say the Clause has been introduced for
the purpose of creating new offices and of
getting in money for that purpose. I
go further and challenge the Government.
to go and ask the farmers or the consumers for a vote on this Bill and see
what their mandate will be. I certainly
intend to vote for the exclusion of the
Clause.
I
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I regret that I
am unable to accept this Amendment. It
has been found by experience that by far
the most satisfactory method of regulating
the retail trade is by a system for the
licensing of individual retailers. This
system has been adopted in the case of
milk, where all milk distributors and all
shops selling milk require to hold a licence.
from the Ministry of Agriculture.
this way a gradual and progressive
improvement is being effected in the conditions under which milk is being distributed throughout Northern Ireland.
In
The hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) maintains that the necessary
improvement in the conditions under
which eggs are sold by retail can be
carried out effectively by regulations made
by the Ministry without the necessity for
the licensing of retailers. From the experience which the Ministry has gained
in the administration of similar Acts I am
convinced that a licensing provision is
essential to the successful conduct of any
campaign for the improvement of the
conditions under which eggs are sold by
retail. In the first place, it enables the
Ministry to prescribe certain conditions
which have to be complied with by an
applicant for a licence. To start off with
these conditions may be of a very simple
character. The fact, however, that a
licence-holder knows that the conditions
under which he carries on his business
will be brought up for review in connection with his application for a licence, and
the fact that he is called upon to sign a
declaration agreeing to adhere to the conditions upon which the licence is granted,
constitutes a valuable weapon in effecting
the improvements which we all recognise
are desirable. For these reasons I am
convinced that in the interests of the consuming public generally it is desirable
that all retailers should be brought within the scope of a licensing system.
In the second place, the provisions of
this Clause are also of great importance
in connection with the finance of the Bill.
It would be quite impossible for me to
accept the Amendment to leave out the
Clause without considering carefully the
alternative means whereby the necessary
revenue to undertake the administrative
work in connection with the retail trade
could be obtained. As hon. Senators are
aware, the finance of Marketing Acts of
this character must necessarily be largely
self-supporting. Already producers
and wholesale dealers are paying
substantial amounts towards the cost
of the administration of previous
Acts. The principle of the present Bill
in relation to the control of the retail
trade in eggs within Northern Ireland
has been, I think, unanimously accepted
by this House. It is only reasonable,
therefore, that some financial provision
for the administrative work involved
should be made in the Bill, and this is
one of the objects which Clause 6 purports
to achieve. In these circumstances it is
quite impossible for me to accept the
amendment moved by the hon. Senator,
which I trust he will not press.
The hon. Senator who last spoke considered that a tax would be imposed on
the taxpayers of this country. There is
no form of tax under the Bill. The whole
cost of the administration will come out
of the trade, as has been done in all the
421 Marketing of Eggs Bill-
9 JUNE 1936

Committee
other Marketing Acts which have been
brought in by my Ministry.
One other hon. Senator considered that
the wholesaler was not paying sufficient
by way of assistance in the working of
this scheme. I should point out that the
wholesale dealer pays d per dozen for
all eggs sold in this country just as the
retailer pays. It should also be clearly
understood that the retailer in fact does
get a service under this Bill, because his
eggs, as the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie) said himself, are inspected
at the wholesale shop before they are sent
to his shop.
As I mentioned on the Second Reading,
the object of this Bill is not only to
improve the quality of the eggs that are
being shipped, but as far as possible
to guarantee the quality of the eggs that
are supplied to consumers in this country.
That is by no means the least important
part of this Bill. Further, as the retailer is the principal distributor of these
eggs, the quality of which is being guaranteed, I think it is only right that the
retailer, as well as the others, should
bear a fair share of the expense involved.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I hold that this is
a further tax on the taxpayer, although
the right hon. Gentleman persists in
saying that it is not. I respectfully ask
him who are the producers, and who are
the consumers? Are they not the taxpayers?
Sir BASIL BROOKE: It depends
on what you call the taxpayers. If you
buy goods in a shop you are making a
payment, not necessarily paying a tax.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
should like to put to the Minister a case,
which I know is an extreme one. If
someone comes to my door at night and
knocks me up, when the shops are closed,
and I give him some eggs which I myself
have bought, am I a retailer within the
meaning of this Measure? That person
coming to my door would probably not
want to take the eggs without paying
for them. Does the person supplying the
eggs under those circumstances leave himself open to be dealt with as a retailer?
Mr. MCALLISTER: If I am right in
my interpretation of this Bill, an official
of the Ministry of Agriculture can go
anywhere in Northern Ireland and say
that a man shall not carry on business,
and there is no appeal from that decision.
An official might say, "There is no appeal
from me; I will put you out of business."
I suggest that an undertaking should be
given by the Government, before this
Clause is passed, that there will be an
appeal to a court of summary jurisdiction.
where a licence is refused or suspended.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: In reply to the
right hon. Senator, I have only to say
that if he carried out a transaction such
as he described late enough at night the
Ministry of Agriculture would not know.
(Laughter.) With regard to the question.
of appeal, this matter was discussed in
the other House. I considered it very
carefully then, and it was agreed eventually that no person should lose his
licence if it were his first offence. Hon.
Senators will realise that my Ministry
endeavours to lead men along the right
away. If the right of appeal were given
it would mean that every time a licenceholder committed a small offence he would
have to be brought to the court, and
eventually the Ministry would be forced
into the position of a policeman instead
of an instructor.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I have got
to say that the right hon. Gentleman
seems to me to be arguing rather in favour
of Clause 5 of the Bill, part of which we
have passed, than of Clause 6 which is
now before the Committee. Nobody
objects to doing anything in reason to
improve the quality of the eggs sold for
consumption in Northern Ireland, but the
question is whether the retailer ought to
pay so much money for it.
We are
reminded that the wholesaler has to pay
a farthing a dozen if he sells his eggs in
Northern Ireland, but the important
point as regards the eggs shipped across
the water, on which he has only to pay a
penny for 30 dozen, is not dealt with. If
the Minister's idea of leading the producer along the right path is by way of
charging one man seven and a-half times
as much as another, then I am unable to
agree with him. As the Committee are
probably aware, there is a good deal of
feeling about this Clause in the country.
The grocers are not at all pleased with it.
A great many people go into the grocer's
shop in the villages and small towns, and

whether they as consumers share his views
I do not know; but I do think they will
share his resentment at being called upon
to pay such a heavy charge to fulfil the
requirements of this Bill. I do not think
it would be fair to withdraw the Amend-

The CHAIRMAN: We now return to
Clause 5.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 5, sub-clause 5,.to leave out paragraph (i).
I had rather hoped that this could
have been easily disposed of, but unfortunately it is not so. I have to attack
this particular paragraph now on other
In the first place, it seems
grounds.
that we are left very much in the dark
as to what holders of retailers' licences
are to be exempted. That question was
raised in another place, and I think that
the Minister made a suggestion to the
effect that it would depend to some extent
on the size of the business done. That
being so, we are thrown back upon this,
that a certificate as to the quality of the
eggs will depend upon the size of the
business done by the vendor of the eggs.
I am afraid there is no getting away
from that. Is there? Therefore, this
particular paragraph does not seem to be
in the interests of the consumers, and
accordingly I move that it be left out.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am not quite
clear about where we are. Are we back
on Clause 5? This really is a consequential Amendment. If the hon. and
learned Senator was not successful in his
rejection of Clause 6, does this arise now?
Mr. LESLIE: Yes. If the Minister
will allow me, perhaps I had better ex-
McLaughlin, Mr. T.
plain. I asked that my Amendment
should be deferred in order that if Clause
6 were expunged there would be no
necessity to argue about this particular
paragraph, which, of course, could not
have stood if Clause 6 had been taken
out. The Clause was deferred in order
that it might be attacked on its demerits.
That is why I asked that it should be
deferred. Apart from the question of
Clause 6, without which this paragraph
could have no life at all, the certificate
would seem to depend upon the size of
the business transacted by the vendor of
the eggs.
The consumer will be left
without such protection as Clause 5
affords him, and I do not think that is
right.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I do not think
the hon. and learned Senator's conclusions
are correct. Whether a man is a small
retailer or a big wholesaler, I think it
still holds good that he is required to
produce eggs of a certain quality for the
consumer.
Mr. LESLIE: No. Surely what the
paragraph says is:-
This section shall not apply where
eggs are sold, or offered, or exposed for
sale, by retail-
(i) by such holders of retailers'

may be prescribed, or

A person shall not, either by himself,

Sir BASIL BROOKE: I have now
been advised differently. Perhaps the
hon. and learned Senator will forgive
me if I have made a mistake. A man
may suddenly start business in
watering place during the summer, and
an inspector would have to go upon his

eggs will be purchased from that dealer,
so that he will go out of business automatically. I was not quite following the
argument of the hon. and learned Senator

Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that we

Clause 5 ordered to stand part of the

CLAUSE 7 (Extension of powers to
make rules)-
The Ministry may, by rules made
under the principal Act-
(a) prescribe the conditions under
which eggs may be stored, prepared
or exposed for sale by retail; and
(b) provide that all or any of the
prices or terms applying under section
one of this Act to the purchase of
eggs by licensed wholesale leaders
shall extend to the purchase by other
persons or classes of persons of eggs
for resale.
Mr. LESLIE: By way of interlude,
if one may say so, we have reached an
amendment on the Paper which may not
be required to go beyond the discussion
stage. I do not think I told the Committee when speaking on the second
amendment on the Paper that a point
may arise later on raising something of
the same question, that is, the question
of necessarily acting in accordance with
the advice of the committee. Therefore,
I thought we had better put something
on the Paper here in order that the
question might at all events be discussed.
Therefore, I beg to move at the end of
the Clause to insert "Provided that a rule
for the purposes of paragraph (b) of this
section shall not be made except in accordance with the advice given by the Committee."
The whole Clause gives the Minister
power to make rules and to provide that
all or any of the prices or terms applying
under Section 1 of this Act to the purchase of eggs by licensed wholesale dealers
shall extend to the purchase by other
persons or classes of persons of eggs for
resale. That seems to bring in the retailer
and gives the Minister power to make
rules. Before the Minister makes such
rule I think it is the intention to consult
the representatives of the retailers. I do
not know how he will choose his representatives, but I think that is the intention. The Committee will remember
that the retailer is not entitled to representation on the general committee provided for by Clause 3. Suggestions have
been made-I think they were made in
this House as well as elsewhere-that some
retailers' representatives should be added
to that committee. I did not suggest
that myself, and I should not do it now.
If retailers are put on that committee
it would, I think, upset the balance
between the producer and the wholesale
dealer. The fact remains that the retailer is not represented on that committee, and the question is whether we
ought to provide that that committee
should be consulted before the Minister
makes a rule providing for the extension
whether it is necessary to put that in
the Bill or not I confess I am not very
sure. I should be quite willing to accept
an intimation from the Minister very
much on those lines without making this
an occasion for tying him down. As it
is I must move the amendment to keep
myself in order.
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I regret I am
unable to accept this amendment. The
Committee does not contain representatives of retailers, and will not deal with
the retail trade. It would, therefore, be
quite unjustifiable that the Committee
should be consulted with regard to rules
made under this section. I have given
an assurance to representatives of the
retail trade that before making any rules
under this section I shall consult them
direct. The intervention of the Committee in this matter is therefore unnecessary.
Mr. LESLIE: I think everybody is
satisfied and I ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Amendments as to licensed
wholesale dealers).
(1) The Ministry may refuse to grant
a licence under section one of the principal Act to any applicant who has not
previously been licensed under that
section, if the Ministry is satisfied,
after consultation with and on the recommendation of the Committee, that
there is a sufficient number of licensed
wholesale dealers carrying on business
in the area in which the applicant proposes to carry on business as such
dealer.
This provision shall be in addition to
and not in derogation of the provisions
of section one of the Act of 1926.
(2) A licence granted under section
one of the principal Act shall, unless
revoked or suspended, remain in force.
until the thirty-first day of October next
after the day on which it is granted,
and section one of the Act of 1931 shall
have effect accordingly.
Any such licence which is in force on
the day of the commencement of this
section shall, subject as aforesaid,
remain in force until the thirty-first
day of October next thereafter.
(3) A licensed wholesale dealer of
Class A or Class B shall keep such
records and furnish to the Ministry
such returns in respect of his purchases
and sales of eggs as may be prescribed,
and the provisions of section two of the
Act of 1931 shall apply to any such
dealer, records and returns as they
apply to the dealers, records and
returns in the said section mentioned.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out Sub-clause (1). The objection to this
Sub-clause seems to be a fairly clean-cut
one. The effect is really a move in the
direction of creating a monopoly, and that
is a system which ought to be repugnant
to this House as it is repugnant to the
general feeling in the country. If the
Ministry are to refuse to grant a licence,
whether on the recommendation of the
Committee or without it, it means that
some man who wants to embark on a
certain kind of business, not unlawful, is
to be prohibited from doing so. I do not
think we can justify that. If there are,
in fact, too many wholesale dealers
operating in the district to make a living,
then one of those will find that out and
will withdraw from the business. He is
not undertaking the matter on grounds
of health but thinks to make a profit. I
do not think we are just in allowing him
to be shut out. There may be other
reasons besides the number of people
operating in the district, but if we allow
any particular individual to be shut out,
although he may be ready to conform to
the appropriate regulations, I think we
are moving in the direction of monopoly
which is a thing we ought not to do.
Accordingly, I am afraid I must ask the
Committee to leave out the words running
from line 37 to the end of line 3 on the
following page.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: As I mentioned
in my Second Reading speech the great
difficulty that one has to face in these
matters is that this Bill, now going
through the House, is not the same as the
Marketing Act. The Committee under
that Act has power to refuse anything
without any reference to the Ministry and
without any reference to an outside body.
In this case the Ministry will under certain circumstances obtain advice from
their Committee, and it appears to the
Ministry in the highest degree important
that overlapping amongst licensed wholesale dealers engaged in the collection and
in the testing and packing of eggs should
be avoided whenever this is possible. In
order to be run efficiently a packing
station requires an adequate supply of
429 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

9 JUNE 1936
Committee
eggs so as to justify the expense of maintaining suitable grading and candling
machines and the employment of an
adequate staff. In the same way the
multiplication of collectors is also wasteful. Moreover, when there are too many
collectors and too many packers it is the
producer who suffers on account of the
excessive overhead charges which the industry is called upon to bear. The
Ministry does not ask to be empowered
to refuse to grant a licence on the grounds
of redundancy. It asks, however, that
where there appears to be reason to
believe that a new licensee in a particular
area is uncalled for the matter should be
referred by the Ministry to the Egg
Marketing Committee, upon which there
are representatives of both producers and
dealers. The Ministry will then act upon
the advice of the Committee in desiding
whether or not to grant a licence. I
regret, therefore, I cannot accept the
amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think it is an
amendment that ought to be pressed,
although I do not altogether agree with
the principle. We can attack it in the
future if things do not work out satisfactorily. I beg leave to withdraw the
amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

licences).

(Offences and prose-
CLAUSE 12 (Offences
cutions).
(1) Any person guilty of an offence
against the principal Act for which no
special penalty is provided shall for
each offence be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding five
pounds, or on conviction on indictment
to a fine not exceeding two hundred
pounds, and in either case (if a purchase or sale took place) to an additional fine not exceeding half the price
at which the eggs were purchased or
sold:
Provided that the fines imposed on
summary conviction for any offence to
which this sub-section applies shall not
exceed in the aggregate one hundred
pounds.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Subclause (1) to leave out the words "or on
conviction on indictment to a fine not
exceeding two hundred pounds, and in
either case," and to insert "and."
This is a question of proceeding possibly
by indictment under certain circumstances. I am afraid I shall have to say
that although there is some precedent to
be found in the Milk Act of hasty
memory, shall we say, for this particular
provision, and there is, I think, something
of the same sort in one or two other Acts,
you will not find such a provision in the
Meat Act, 1930, nor will you find anything of the sort in the Merchandise
Marks Act, 1887, except in relation to
specified misdemeanours.
Indictment is a somewhat serious
weapon. You, first of all, have got to
get the Attorney-General to let you
breathe, unless you proceed privately,
which is a different story, and you have
then got to satisfy two juries if you go to
assize. I cannot find in the Marketing
of Eggs Act anything which calls for the
use of so serious a weapon as indictment,
had it not been for paragraph (b), or
possibly the tail end of paragraph (a) of
If a
Sub-clause (2).
man forges or
fraudulently alters and uses a document
of any sort it might be a suitable subject.
for indictment, because that is a very
serious offence. The Bill takes that away
and proposes to deal with it summarily
with a penalty of imprisonment for three
months or a fine of £20. But for what
offences under the Marketing of Eggs Act
is it suggested this power should be used?
Is it not rather a case of the unnecessary
waving of the big stick and setting up
Committee
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
THE SENATE

[Mr. Leslie]
vibration in the air from which nothing
but brutum fulmen can result? Would
the Bill not have a better chance of success
if we tried a little less brandishment and
a little more blandishment?
I may say that I should not object to
this provision if the Minister thought fit,
on the next stage of the Bill, to reintroduce the provision in one of the
earlier Acts that the penalty on summary
conviction for a second and third offence
should be at a higher rate. I think that
is perhaps the better way of dealing with
it if the right hon. Gentleman is in any
difficulty, but I do not think there is anything left in these Acts which calls for
proceeding by indictment. Accordingly,
I move the first of the two Amendments
on the Paper.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I regret I cannot accept the Amendment. I should
like to point out that Clause 12 of the
present Bill represents a very considerable
modification of the provisions in regard
to prosecutions which have operated
under previous Marketing of Eggs Acts.
I would remind the hon. and learned
Senator that under the provisions of
Section 8 of the Marketing of Eggs Act
(Northern Ireland), 1924, a person could
on conviction for a third offence be imprisoned for a period not exceeding three
months if the Court were of opinion that
a fine would not meet the circumstances
of the case. By Section 5 of the Act
of 1926 minimum fines for offences against
the Marketing of Eggs Acts were
instituted at the rate of £1 for a first
offence, £3 for a second offence, and £5
for a third or any subsequent offence.
All these provisions have been repealed by
the present Measure, and all offences
against the code of marketing of eggs
legislation will in future be tried by the
Courts under the provisions of Clause 12
of the present Bill.
In these circumstances it appears to the
Ministry reasonable that the right to
proceed by indictment should exist to
take a serious offence against the marketing of eggs code of legislation direct to
the High Court. Hon. Senators will
appreciate that licence-holders under the
Acts may do a very great deal to enhance
the reputation of Northern Ireland eggs.
They may equally do within a short time
much which will ruin the whole trade
reputation of Northern Ireland on the
wholesale egg markets of Great Britain.
In these circumstances the right to proceed
by indictment to take a case to the High
Court where the offence that has been
committed is exceptionally grave, appears
eminently desirable and reasonable.
we are
Mr. LESLIE: Of course I cannot agree
with any of that. The right hon. Gentleman suggests that we are introducing a
less
severe provision because
abolishing the chance of imprisonment for
three months, and we are substituting for
it the possibility of imprisonment up to
two years.
I think that is what it comes
to. I must presume that he contemplates
that if he does proceed by indictment the
court will deal with it as a misdemeanour,
but the whole thing is left entirely in the
air, and I would rather the Minister withdrew this particular proposal.
I cannot
imagine a case in which it would be advis-
Perhaps
able to proceed by indictment.
the Minister would like to reconsider the
possibility of re-introducing that step-bystep business of first, second and third
offences which was in the old Acts, and
which seem to me to be particularly suitable.
As regards the minimum fine, I
can only rejoice that after long years of
strenuous endeavour I have succeeded in
inducing one department to agree with my
views on that subject. I would urge the
right hon. Gentleman to let this matter
stand over for further consideration.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am advised
that it is necessary the Ministry should
have this power. It may not be often
used, but there may be cases that would
not be suitable for the lower courts.
Therefore, it is considered desirable that
we should retain the power.
Mr. LESLIE: As I am quite sure it
will not happen I ask leave to withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee
9 JUNE 1936
At the end of Clause 12 insert as a new
sub-section:
. -
"(7) Where an offence against the
principal Act is also an offence against
any enactment relating to the sale of
food or to the public health and enforceable by any local authority, then
and in such case sub-section (6) of this
section shall be deemed not SO to
operate as to require the consent of the
Ministry to any prosecution the local
authority may see fit to institute.
Provided that no person shall be
liable to be punished more than once
for the same offence.'
Mr. LESLIE: This is perhaps rather
a troublesome little matter. I mentioned
it on the Second Reading. I cannot recall that this particular point has arisen.
before. Such provisions as occur in subclause (6) before us are perfectly common
in various Acts, and I think they occur
in nearly all the Ministry of Agriculture's
Acts except the Marketing of Eggs Act.
As a matter of fact, the provision that a
prosecution is not to be undertaken except
by the consent of the Ministry has been
found very useful, and has conduced
greatly to the successful working of the
Act. The purists might say it was not
constitutionally sound, but more broadminded people have not brought that forward as an objection. The difficulty we
are in is that an offence may be an offence
under this Act, and also under some of
those Acts relating to food and drugs and
so on. The ordinary rule is that where
an offence constitutes an offence under
one or more Acts it can be prosecuted
under whichever Act the appropriate
party likes, provided that no man is
punished twice. That is not only a rule.
of law, but it is absolutely statutory.
The question arises whether we can rely
on that here or whether we are not, by
inserting this Sub-clause (6) without the
qualification I have put forward for consideration, depriving local authorities of
the right and duty of enforcing some of
the Acts of the class I have mentioned.
I left it very wide, because I did not
want specially to mention the dates of
the Food and Drugs Act and the Public
Health Acts, although some of them are
mentioned in earlier Statutes and the
Marketing of Eggs code. There is also
the possibility of the Towns Improvement
Act, but still more there is the possibility
of some enactment of purely local application. But the main point we want to
be clear on is whether, without the saving
sub-clause I am suggesting, this Clause
can operate to prevent a local authority
doing what is at present its statutory
duty. I beg to move.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am prepared
to accept the amendment. I am grateful
to the hon. and learned Gentleman.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
14, in Sub-clause (1) after "(Northern
Ireland), 1931," to insert:
""
'Habitually failed' means failed on
not less than three occasions during any
period of two consecutive years."
I may tell the Committee at once I
do not very much like this amendment.
The difficulty is that the expression
"habitually failed" occurs twice in the
Bill before us. In one case it will not
occur in the Act, but in an earlier Act.
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
The trouble is that there is no appeal
from a decision of the Ministry based on
the accusation that a man has habitually
failed. It seems to me that Parliament
ought perhaps to give the Ministry a line,
and therefore I put in a line, and a pretty
strict line, in order that we might discuss
it and see if it is right. The Committee
will remember that the expression
"habitual" criminal occurs in the criminal
code, and there is also the habitual
drunkard. It defines man who is
habitually drunk, and that does not help
us very much. Unless we can get some
information we ought perhaps to insert
some indication of what we expect the
Ministry to mean, or rather how we
expect the Ministry to interpret the word
"habitually" in the two Clauses in which
it occurs. Accordingly, more for the
sake of discussion than anything else, I
beg to move the insertion in the Interpretation Clause of the words on the Paper.
a
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am afraid I
cannot accept this Amendment, which I
do not think really would achieve the
object the hon. and learned Senator has
in mind. I should explain that the cases
in which the Ministry would revoke a
licence under the provisions of Clause 6
or Clause 9 of the Bill are not those in
which a licence-holder has on two or
three occasions, or even on a dozen or
more occasions, failed to pay for a few
eggs which he has purchased from a
producer on certain days. The circumstances are much
more grave than
this. Cases arise through the unfortunate
habit which prevails so generally in
country districts of giving almost unlimited credit where eggs or milk or some
other agricultural commodity are supplied
to a dealer by a farmer over a contracted
period. No payment is made, and eventually it is discovered that the dealer has
no assets and defaults, leaving farmers in
debt to a very considerable amount. What
the Ministry desires to avoid is being required to continue to allow such a person
to hold a licence and to purchase from
farmers. The hon. and learned Senator
will appreciate that it is far more grave
for a dealer to fail to pay his producers on
one occasion which extends for a period
of six months or even longer than for a
dealer to fail to pay for the eggs which he
collects from a producer on one isolated
day on three occasions during two years. I
am afraid that in these circumstances it is
quite impossible for me to accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think anybody
wanted the Amendment accepted as it
stood, but I may say that what the right
hon. Gentleman has said convinces me
that something ought to be done, though
it might be better to remove the expres
sion "habitually failed" altogether and
substitute something else. The person
whom the Minister wants to get
at, and I am sure we are all
agreement with him, is the bad
payer, or the fellow who does not pay
at all. I am afraid the use of this expression "habitually failed" will not help
him. Perhaps he would like to consider
the matter and bring up a new Amendment on Report. In the meantime, this
Amendment can be withdrawn.
in
Captain PERCEVAL MAXWELL: I
hope the right hon. Gentleman will
give an undertaking to reconsider the
matter, even if nothing further arises.
It is a matter that requires a little redrafting. The word "habitually" is
really not an apt expression to describe
what the right hon. Gentleman explained
to us in his speech.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: We are all
agreed on the general principle of the
matter. I should hesitate long before
discussing or even arguing with the two
learned Gentlemen who have spoken, but
if the Committee would say that this is a
matter which should be reconsidered on
Report, I will see if some more suitable
word can be found.
sure we are
Mr. LESLIE: I am
obliged to the right hon. Gentleman.
For the moment I withdraw my Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee
9 JUNE 1936
I have waited for this moment.
(Laughter.) This is the first instalment
of my revenge upon the noble Viscount
for stealing my thunder, but I will come
to that part of it in a moment. The
Committee may possibly remember that
in speaking on the Second Reading I
drew attention to the question of the
very common practice of selling eggs not
for human consumption but for settings,
and I was very doubtful whether such
sales would be wholesale or retail. They
might very well be both. That might be
done by anybody. I rather wanted to
make sure that the definition of eggs as
it occurs here should not be held to apply
to eggs sold for settings as opposed to
eggs sold for human consumption.
Το
I have to say I was not dealing with the
question of eggs sold in the shell, because
I do not think the Minister can very well
deal with eggs not sold in the shell though
there may be too many of them.
return to the noble Viscount, the Amendment I move is, first of all, to leave out
the words mentioned and to insert instead "laid by domestic hens or domestic
ducks and sold, or intended to be sold,
in the shell, for human consumption." I
think that possibly there may be some way
round it. As it stands the Bill has caught
the eggs sold for settings, and that, I am
sure, is not the intention. Therefore, I
beg to move.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I cannot accept
this Amendment. The restriction of the
Bill to eggs intended for human consumption and the exclusion of eggs intended for
setting would present grave administrative difficulties.
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask the right
hon. Gentleman how he proposes to get
over the point? Does he think the matter
can be met by applying Sub-clause 6 to the
Clause we are dealing with just now, the
fact that a prosecution is not to be instituted except by consent of the Ministry?
Possibly the matter can be met with in
that way.
You will have to apply that
option in the case of the sale of eggs in
bazaars and such places. I am afraid
there is obscurity in the Bill as it stands.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: It would be
quite impossible to differentiate between
one egg and another from the outside of
the shell unless it is candled or dealt with by
a person who is an expert in these matters.
the hon. and learned Senator will appreciate the difficulty there would be if a
wholesaler, a retailer, or any other person
were to say "Those eggs you are inspecting are not for human consumption but
for setting."
Mr. LESLIE: If that happened it
would, of course, become a question of
fact as to what they were for. The cases
of which I am thinking would not happen
like that. The eggs would not be lying
loose in a man's shop for that purpose.
I think perhaps it might almost be left
to the Ministry to deal with it. It is
only a small matter, but this trade in
settings of eggs is an important one. Some
of them are actually sent out of the
country altogether under licence. Perhaps
the Minister can consider between this
and the Report stage whether anything
ought to be done. In the meantime we
need not press the amendment.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: The hon. and
learned Senator can rest quite assured
that a matter of this sort will be treated
with the greatest sympathy by the
Ministry.
Mr. LESLIE: That is probably the
best way out of it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in page 16, line 11, to leave out
"fowls" ("laid by domestic fowls
domestic ducks'), and to insert instead
thereof "hens."
or
This is a drafting amendment. The
expression "domestic fowls" has been used
in the Bill to mean any kind of domestic
poultry. It is felt that a certain ambiguity might attach to the expression
"fowls," and it is considered desirable
to make it clear that the fowls referred
to are hens. My right hon. Friend the
Minister of Agriculture is very anxious
to make the Bill absolutely clear to other
agriculturists, who appear to
Minister of Education to be rather strange
people. It looks to me as if this could
mean nothing but hens, but it is desired
to make it quite clear that domestic fowls
are hens, and not table bullfinches or
sparrows.
me as

House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with amendments.
Ordered, That the Report of the said

Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
the Adjournment of the House I would
like to have the opinion of those hon.
Senators who are here as to whether they
would like better to take the Report
Stage of the Marketing of Eggs
Bill only on Thursday, or whether they
would prefer to suspend Standing Orders
and take the Report stage, Third Reading,
and pass the Measure on Tuesday. It is
just a question of whichever hon. Senators
would like better to do.
Mr. LESLIE: I would venture to suggest
that it would be less inconvenient to take
the Report stage and Third Reading
together on Tuesday. There are one or
two small matters left to be considered,
and Thursday really does not give sufficient time to put anything on the Paper.
If the Bill survives the Report stage we
may just as well divide, if we want to
divide on the Third Reading, on Tuesday
as on any other day. I presume there is
no hurry about the Appropriation Bill,
and I do not think there is anything else
to be done. There are only the Appropriation Bill and a Divorce Bill, and I
do not think that will be ready for
Thursday as a matter of fact. Therefore, everything would seem to point
to Tuesday. I do not suppose it would
be necessary to trouble the Minister of
Agriculture to be here if he has any
other engagement. There can be very
little, I think, on the Report Stage.
On Question, Motion, "That this
House do now adjourn," agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten minutes
after Five o'clock until Tuesday next

Marketing of Eggs Bill.
