Marketing of Eggs Bill 442
CLAUSE 3 (Constitution of Egg
Marketing Committee).
(1) For the purposes of this Act
there shall be established, as soon as
may be after the passing of this Act,
a body to be called the Egg Marketing
Committee for Northern Ireland (in this
Act referred to as "the Committee').
(2) The Committee shall consist of
the following persons:-
Three members appointed by the
Minister of Agriculture, of whom
such one as the said Minister may
designate shall be the chairman of
the Committee;
Five members being representatives
of egg producers and appointed by
the Ulster Farmers' Union, the
Ulster Agricultural Organisation
Society, the Northern Ireland Utility
Poultry Society and other prescribed
societies (if any) in such manner,
and according to such method of
distribution as between the several
societies, as the said Minister may
from time to time determine.
Five members elected, in accordance with the prescribed regulations,
by licensed wholesale dealers of
Class A;
Two members elected, in accordance with the prescribed regulations,
by licensed wholesale dealers of
Class B.
The members of the Committee shall
hold office for such term and subject to
such conditions as the said Minister
may determine.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
3, Sub-clause (2) to leave out "(2) The
Committee" and insert "
which." The
first two amendments on the Paper, of
course, run together. It is really more
by way of direction to the printer than
anything else that I am moving these
amendments. I saw afterwards, when the
Bill was in print, after the Committee
stage, that I had inadvertently left a
sub-clause terminating with a bracket,
which is not usual, and not very desirable.
Therefore, I thought if we left out the
expression "(2) The Committee" and put
in "which" we should make a rather
neater business of it altogether. And
following that I thought we could use
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
up our "(2)" at the proper place at the
beginning of line 35, and so break up a
somewhat long clause into more convenient
parts.
Mr. LONG: I accept that amendment
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not know that this
requires a formal motion, but, if it does,
I beg to move at the beginning of the
last paragraph of sub-clause (2) to insert "(2)".
Mr. LONG: I accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 5 (Sale of eggs by retail
according to description or quality)-
*
*
*
*
*
(b) where eggs are sold, or offered or
exposed for sale, by retail-
(i) by such holders of retailers'
licences granted under this Act as
may be prescribed, or
(ii) under the provisions of section
three of the principal Act or section
three of the Marketing of Eggs Act
(Northern Ireland), 1928.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subparagraph (i) to leave out from "by" to
"or" at the end of the sub-paragraph and
insert "those holders of retailers' licences
to whom the provisions of paragraph (b)
of sub-section (5) of section six of this
Act apply." This is perhaps a rather
more difficult matter than those which
have gone before. The amendment itself
has to be considered in conjunction with
the amendment to lines 16 and 17 on the
next page the amendment which appears
next but two on the Paper. Of course,
that is without prejudice to the fact that
the second amendment can very well
stand on its own legs, as it deals with
a somewhat different subject, but the two,
as the House will see, are somewhat connected.
To deal with the sub-paragraph immediately before us, that is the amendment
which deals with sub-paragraph (i) of
paragraph (b) of sub-clause (6) of Clause
5, I have to say that that particular
proposed provision has been criticised and
objected to on three main grounds. The
first objection to the words which I am
asking the House to omit, as a preliminary
to substituting other words, is that such
protection as Clause 5 gives to the consumer, that is, the protection of inspection and so forth, stands in peril of being
withdrawn by, so to speak, the arbitrary
decision of the Ministry. The Minister
may prescribe what holders of retailers'
licences are to be exempt from the provisions of Clause 5, and if any particular
retailer is exempted from these provisions
it follows that any protection the consumer might derive in the way of being
sure of getting a good egg by virtue of
the provisions of Clause 5 goes also. That
is the first objection.
The second objection is that this exemption is purely arbitrary. There is nothing
said in the Bill as to the grounds on
which the Ministry is to grant exemption.
That is always an objectionable matter,
because it is perfectly possible that exemption may be granted on grounds of
favouritism, or it may be refused on
grounds of prejudice or caprice or
spite, and it is quite certain that
whatever exemptions are granted will
be attacked by those who did not
receive them on one or other of those
grounds. It is the custom in many parts
of the world to use weapons of that sort
whenever a person happens to be disappointed. It may be an objectionable.
custom, but still it is there.
The remaining objection I have already
mentioned, that is, there is nothing in the
Bill itself to indicate the grounds on which
the Ministry would be expected to give
exemption. To the first objection-withdrawal of protection as regards qualitythere is, I am afraid, no answer, but if
the House is prepared to accept the Bill,
as a whole, it seems to me not impossible
that as regards that particular point the
House may be prepared to consider the
Minister responsible for the Bill as one
who is trying to follow out the advice
given by Bacon some centuries ago.
recognises, as he cannot have that which
he desires in the first degree, yet he will
have it in the second or third, that is, he
accepts the position that he cannot impose
these tests on every small retailer in the
country. I think the House, perhaps so
far as that objection goes, would be prepared to let that go as part of the price
they will be willing to pay in order to
secure the somewhat problematical advantages which are expected to flow from
the Bill.
He
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Report Stage
16 JUNE 1936
As to the other two objections, the fact
that there is nothing in the Bill-that
there is no line given as to the grounds
on which exemption shall be granted-I
think we can do something. I think we
can do it without entirely depriving the
Minister of some discretion in the matter.
What I think we ought to do is to set
some standard, here in Parliament, which
would serve as a guide, and if we can do
that we shall, for practical purposes, cut
away the ground from under the feet of
the people who would take the objection
I have just mentioned if exemption was
granted on improper grounds. I propose
to suggest to the House that we should
take means from the lithographed memorandum which accompanies the Bill, both
in the way of direct suggestions in that
memorandum, and of some indirect suggestions-we should take these as a basis
for a kind of welding of the paragraph
before us and the passage in another
paragraph (b) in Sub-clause (5) of the
succeeding Clause.
I think that the House will, perhaps,
pardon me if I read the relative portions of the memorandum to which I
refer. The memorandum says:
"The Ministry has in mind that it
might be desirable to exempt from this
Clause sales by small shopkeepers in
country areas who could not reasonably be expected to test eggs, and who
could not conveniently obtain supplies
already tested. Larger retailers will,
however, be required to test their eggs
themselves—as many do at present-or,
alternatively, buy their eggs already
tested from a shipper."
"Under Clause 6, the second class-that
is the class to which the other Amendment
relates will consist of those licenceholders who only sell, say, 60 dozen eggs
per week. They will pay a composite fee
not exceeding £2 per annum, and will
not be affected by the provisions relating
to the payment of a fee per dozen."
At the end of the memorandum the
Ministry says also:
"A shopkeeper who sells by retail.
only will under Clause 6 have to provide himself with a retailer's licence.
If he sells less than, say, 60 dozen per
week or whatever quantity may be prescribed he will pay a fee not exceeding
£2 for his licence. The Ministry contemplates exempting such shopkeepers
-at any rate in rural areas from the
requirements of Clause 5 whereby they
must sell on a quality basis."
Clause 5 is the Clause a paragraph of
which is before us, and Clause 6 is the
following Clause, with part of which I
propose to weld the new Amendment
standing in my name. I suggest we take
the figure of sixty dozen suggested in
that memorandum. At the same time I
am not in any way wedded to that figure,
and, if the debate discloses a general
desire that some other number should be
substituted for sixty dozen, I should be
prepared to move the succeeding Amendment on those lines. What I want to
ensure is that this matter of exemption,
or grounds for exemption, should be
settled by Parliament, and, although we
cannot do it absolutely definitely-for
which I propose to provide in a future
Amendment-if Parliament cannot settle
the matter let Parliament, at all events,
lay down grounds for settlement, and let
Parliament do that within its own walls
and not leave the whole question to be
settled outside Parliament by a Minister
without the necessity for bringing his
proposals again before Parliament. I
think that is worth while, and I submit
very humbly that the proposal on the
Paper, in conjunction with the other one
to Clause 6, affords means of doing it.
Let us provide definitely that the people
who do not sell more than sixty dozen
eggs a week are to be exempted from the
provisions of Clause 5. It is not ideal, I
admit, but we cannot have everything we
want in this most imperfect world. I
beg to move accordingly.
Mr. LONG: I am sorry that I cannot
accept this Amendment. It is true that
the retailers who will be exempted from
the provisions of Clause 5 will be, in the
main, those retailers who pay licence
fees under the provisions of Sub-section
5 (b) of Section 6. At the same time,
it may not necessarily follow that the
two classes of persons are identical. The
Ministry does not wish to give large scale
exemptions under the provisions of
Section 5; otherwise, of course, the
benefit of that Clause in ensuring that
consumers will buy eggs graded as either
"new-laid" or "seconds" would be lost.
At the same time, it is recognised that,
especially in some districts, there are
persons who are engaged in the egg trade
at certain periods of the year who have
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
not facilities for candling eggs, and who
would have to obtain their supplies of
eggs from a wholesale dealer if the requirement to mark the eggs "new-laid"
or "seconds" applied to them. This is
particularly the case in small seaside resorts during the summer period. Consequently the Ministry may decide to
exempt from the requirements of this
Section holders of retailers' licences in
certain areas, such as the seaside towns
along the County Antrim coast.
As at present drafted, the Clause would
by
permit of exemptions being given
reference to area. If, however, the hon.
and learned Senator's amendment is
accepted the discretion of the Ministry
will be altogether curtailed, and it might
result in large classes of retailers being
excluded from the provisions of Section 5,
although there might be no reason why
this should be the case. In Belfast, for
instance, where most of the small retailers
obtain their supplies from wholesale
merchants, there would appear to be no
reason to exclude smaller retailers from
the provisions of the section. In the
country districts, however, it will be
essential to exempt the smaller retailers
under this paragraph.
Clause 5 of the Bill provides that retailers shall, unless exempted under the
Act, designate all eggs sold by them
as "New Laid" or "Seconds." This, of
course, affords a very valuable protection
to the consumer. The effect of the
Amendment would be to remove
the protection in the case of all purchases from the smaller class of retailers.
The Ministry takes the view, however,
that whereas it would be unreasonable
to expect the smaller retailers who live
in rural areas and who cannot conveniently obtain supplies from a Class A
dealer, to install testing equipment. The
consumer should not be deprived of the
advantage of buying graded eggs in the
case of purchases from retailers, however
small their trade may be, who have ready
access to sources from which they may
obtain supplies already graded.
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask the Parliamentary Secretary one question? I understood him to say that the persons exempted
under the provisions of the paragraph
before us would still be liable to certain
provisions of Clause 5, the requirement
to sell eggs under a particular description
and matters of that sort. The difficulty
is, of course, that at the beginning of
Sub-clause (6) it says distinctly that this
section shall not apply. Therefore,
nothing in the section can apply, and I
want to be sure the Parliamentary Secretary meant the House to understand that
there was not some change.
Mr. LONG: I am informed that they
are not subject to any provisions of the
section.
Mr. LESLIE: I can now reply. If
the House allows the paragraph to stand
as it is the effect will be that those people
who are exempt will be under no restrictions whatever, and the Ministry will be
under no restrictions whatever as to the
people it exempts. I am sorry we have
not heard the views of other hon. Members, but I do not feel that this is an
amendment which ought to be withdrawn.
Amendment negatived.

6, to leave out "fowls" in line 17, on page
449 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

8, and insert instead thereof the words
"hens or ducks." This is a much less

decides to accept the Amendment or not
will make no difference to anybody, but
those who like symmetry in the Acts of
Parliament and who remember that in
another part of the Bill we left out the
word "fowls" and substituted "hens and
ducks' will, perhaps, think we ought to
make the same substitution here. I,
therefore, beg to move, although I shall
not press the Amendment if there is any
objection.
Mr. LONG: I am prepared to accept.
this Amendment, although I do so with
some hesitancy. The important word in
the proviso appears to me to be "eggs"
which are defined by Sub-section 2 of

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave
out from the word 'thereof" in line 35
of paragraph (c) to the end of the paragraph, and to insert instead, "cannot be
relied upon to pay for eggs supplied to
him." The point of this Amendment is
to get rid of the expression "habitually
failed," which failed to commend itself
to any Member of the House in Committee. It has been the subject of consideration and reconsideration, and,
finally, the consensus of opinion appears
to be that the Amendment should run,
"cannot be relied upon to pay for eggs
supplied to him." I am not including in
my Amendment the three words "with
reasonable promptitude," which appear
on the Paper. I submit that the words
I am moving are somewhat better than
the expression "habitually failed," which
nobody seemed able to define, and which
the Bill gave us no assistance in defining.
Mr. LONG: I am prepared to accept
this Amendment.
this
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I think
Amendment will place too much power in
the hands of an individual inspector. I
do not see why a man should be found
guilty before he is tried, and, in my
opinion, the words already in the Bill
are much better than the proposed
Amendment. Who is to be the judge of
whether a man cannot be relied upon to
pay with reasonable promptitude? Is it
suggested that an inspector should go
about the country asking every Tom, Dick
and Harry if so and so is able to pay for
his eggs with reasonable promptitude, and
if he is not that his licence should be
suspended. A dealer may have a bad
week, but he may be in a position to pay
the following week, and is it suggested
that such a man should have his licence
suspended because in one particular week
he was not able to pay with reasonable
promptitude I think that is altogether
wrong, and I shall vote against the
Amendment.
Captain PERCEVAL - MAXWELL:
Is the hon. Senator's objection not really
met by the word "reasonable"?
• Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I may reply to
the hon. Senator. I am afraid that he
-and I am not sure that he was quite
alone-failed to appreciate that the
words "with reasonable promptitude"
were omitted from the Amendment I
actually moved, although they appear in
the Amendment as printed on the Paper.
The answer, of course, is that the Ministry must be satisfied that a man cannot
be relied upon. The Ministry can only
be satisfied by means of reports of their
inspectors. They must get their information from somewhere, and I think that
is a matter which might be left to the
Ministry, because they will be acting not
against the man himself, but in protection of the other people concerned. I
think it is a reasonable suggestion to
make in the original Bill, and I think
the words I have submitted are better
than the words "habitually failed"
which originally appeared.
Mr. LONG: The powers will only be
exercised in the case of a rank defaulter.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 6, to leave out from paragraph
(b) of sub-clause (5) the words, "the
prescribed quantity per week" and
insert, "sixty dozen per week or such
greater quantity as may be prescribed."
Since the marriage with sub-paragraph
H
451 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Report Stage
[Mr. Leslie]
(b) (i) in Sub-clause (6) of the earlier
Clause has been prevented, the only point
that remains is whether Parliament ought
to provide here for a prescribed quantity
downwards below which the £2 is not to
be varied, or whether they ought to leave
the amount of tax entirely to the discretion of the Ministry. Having regard
to those passages from the lithographed
memorandum which I read a few
minutes ago, everybody will expect that
the sixty dozen will be the lowest downward limit, and I think Parliament is
entitled to insert sixty dozen, but at the
same time to leave some discretion to the
Ministry, such as I have endeavoured to
provide for in the form of the Amendment. It cannot be defended that the
Ministry should have absolutely unrestricted power to prescribe the quantity
up or down. I think there should be
some limit set, and the limit I propose is
not a very stern one. The Minister
must not go below sixty dozen, but he
may if he thinks fit go beyond it.
Accordingly, I beg to move the Amendment as it stands on the Order Paper.
Mr. LONG: I sympathise with the
hon. and learned Senator's intention in
this matter, but there are difficulties in
the way of accepting his Amendment. It
is true that the Ministry of Agriculture
had in mind that a retailer who sold less
than 60 dozen eggs per week should be
allowed to pay a composite licence fee
of £2. When further information is
available as to the numbers of licenceholders within this category, it may be
found desirable, however, to increase that
figure of 60 dozen to 80 or 90 or even
100 dozen per week; on the other hand,
it may be desirable to prescribe the
quantity at a lower figure than 60 dozen,
and this is clearly contemplated by the
provision that the prescribed licence fee
may be something less than £2. If the
prescribed quantity is to be fixed at 60
dozen the discretion which is left to the
Department to prescribe the licence fee
at a figure less than £2 becomes largely
inoperative. The Ministry of Agriculture has given a clear indication of
its intentions in this matter, and I think
that, since the question is largely an
administrative one, it is better to leave
the Department a reasonable amount of
discretion. They are, of course, restricted by the sum of £2 as the maximum
composite fee which may be imposed.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to support
this Amendment. It is clear that the
intention of the Government is to raise
this d per dozen by hook or by crook.
Sixty dozen eggs per week at 4d per dozen
represents £3 5s per annum. If the
Government are honest in their intention
both to the producer and the consumer
they will agree to this Amendment, which
allows 60 dozen per week or such greater
quantity as may be prescribed. They
would thus show their good faith with the
I must say that, as far as I can
see, this Bill is a further imposition on
the taxpayer. As far as the consumer
is concerned, the only good this Bill will
do is to add d per dozen to the cost of
his eggs, and the fact that the Government will not accept this Amendment
shows that they are determined to get at
least id per dozen on all the eggs produced in Northern Ireland in future.
retailer.
Question put, "That the words proposed be left out."

453 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

16 JUNE 1936
Report Stage
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 9,
line 36, to leave out from the beginning
of the line to "where" in line 37 ("who
is also the holder of a Class A licence
under section one of the principal Act.")
The question which this Amendment
raises is, in brief, whether there are any
grounds on which you can justify giving
to a retailer who happens to be also the
holder of a Class A licence a concession
which you deny to other retailers who do
not hold a Class A licence. On the face
of things it seems to me inequitable that
one class of retailer should receive a benefit
which the others do not receive. On the
other hand, as the House will recognise,
the wholesale dealer, the Class A man, is
likely to stand to lose very much more
in a matter of this kind than the ordinary
retailer. But then again we have to
remember that the retailer may be a man
holding very varying quantities of eggs.
for sale. He may have what is to him
a comparatively large stock suddenly
threatened I should not like to put it
higher than that-by a thunder storm or
something of that kind, and may decide
to dispose of the eggs he has left for use
in manufacture. If he does that it
seems to me that he has just as good a
claim to escape the d per dozen as has
the wholesale dealer. Accordingly, I move
this Amendment, which will have the
effect of putting all licence-holders on
exactly the same footing whether the gain
derived be great or small. It seems to
me that if that concession is made we
shall only be following out the principle
of the Bill on which the Minister laid so
much stress in another place when he
said that he wanted to induce people to
sell their eggs promptly by means of a
monetary reward; that is, that the man
who sells new laid eggs is to get a better
price than he has got hitherto.
I suggest that the position here is the same.
The man who has-shall we say- -a shopsoiled egg and wants to sell it for manufacture will get the same sort of monetary
reward, because he will be relieved of
the d per dozen. I think the proposal
is an equitable one.
Mr. LONG: I am afraid I cannot
accept this amendment. It is regarded as
important that where this exemption is
given the retailer should also be the holder
of a Class "A" licence. The position is
as follows: In the course of their egg
trade, Class "A" licence-holders necessarily obtain a large quantity of "Second"
eggs. These eggs are generally disposed
of to bakers and other persons who use
eggs in the process of manufacture. These
sales by Class "A" licence-holders are
retail sales and, consequently, a retailer's
licence is required. If, however, the
additional licence fee of d. per dozen
eggs sold by retail were imposed, it would
prejudice the sale of our "Second" eggs,
which compete with imported liquid eggs.
Consequently it is in the highest degree
important that Class "A" licence-holders
should be free to sell their "Seconds" to
bakers and confectioners without having
to pay the additional retailer's licence fee
of id per dozen eggs.
The case of the ordinary retailer is,
however, very different. Any eggs which
he seeks to sell for manufacture are eggs
which have deteriorated in his shop as a
result of holding too long. I think that
in circumstances such as these the full
licence fee of d. per dozen should be
payable since this will operate as an incentive to the retailer to dispose of his
eggs as soon as possible. There is also
the administrative question. Sales by
Class "A" licence-holders are generally
in bulk and cover substantial quantities.
The necessary bookkeeping transactions
in connection with the remission of the
additional sum of 1d. per dozen will,
therefore, present little difficulty. In the
case of the retailer, however, any sales of
eggs for use in manufacture would cover
trivial quantities, and it would be very
difficult to deal with small claims by
retailers for the remission of the additional licence fees in respect of a few
dozen eggs sold by them to a baker.
Mr. LESLIE: Nobody else seems
anxious to defend the unfortunate retailer and, therefore, I do not think the
House ought to be troubled with a
Division.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 9 (Amendment as to revocation and suspension of wholesale
dealers' licences).
Paragraph (b) of sub-section (1) of
section one of the Act of 1931 (which
relates to the revocation and suspension
of the licences of wholesale dealers in
eggs) shall have effect as if the
following proviso were added thereto-
455 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

Report Stage
"Provided that the Ministry shall
not revoke or suspend a licence
granted under the said section unless
the holder thereof has been convicted
of an offence against the principal.
Act, or it is proved to the satisfaction of the Ministry that he has
habitually failed to pay for any eggs
purchased by him, or, if he is the
holder of a Class A licence, that he
has failed to pay--
(i) any prescribed sum payable
by him in accordance with this
section; or
(ii) any additional sum payable
by him under sub-section (5) of
section six of the Marketing of
Eggs Act (Northern Ireland),
1936.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
11, lines 21 and 22, to leave out "has
habitually failed to pay for any eggs
purchased by him," and to insert instead
thereof "cannot be relied
to pay
for eggs supplied to him."
upon
This is, of course, the same Amendment as the earlier one. We are using
the same form of words. The only
difference is that the new form of words
will, strictly speaking, appear in the Act
of 1931, because we are making alterations in that Act by the Bill before us.
Mr. LONG: I agree to accept the
Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 16 (Saving for sale of eggs
for refreshment purposes).
Nothing in this Act shall apply to
the sale or offer or exposure for sale
in an hotel, boarding-house, restaurant
or other place of refreshment, of eggs
intended for consumption as part of a
meal or refreshment provided at such
place.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
16, line 22, to leave out “an” (“an
hotel, boarding-house, restaurant") and
to insert instead thereof "a club."
This is a small amendment, and I have
to apologise to the House for not having
discovered it in time to send it in for the
first issue of the Order Paper. I did not
notice it until rather later. The point is
that the expressions "hotel, boarding
house, restaurant, or other place of refreshment" all refer to places of public
resort while a club is essentially a place
of private resort. The use of a club is
limited to its own members. We might
get over the trouble if there was only
one class of club, but there are two
classes firstly, the class which I would
call the mutual club, in which every
member has a share in the property of
the club, and, secondly, the proprietary
club, where the member has no share. It
is, therefore, necessary to insert the words
"a club" here in order that the members
of a club may have the same protection
for meals served to one of their members
as is given to the ordinary restaurant or
hotel-keeper.
Mr. LONG: I am prepared to accept
the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Resolved, "That the Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Long.)
Resolved, "That the Bill do now pass."
-(Mr. Long.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the Bill with some
amendments, to which they desire their
concurrence.
Adjournment.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until Tuesday next." (Mr.
Long.)
Adjourned accordingly at Four
o'clock.

Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have to
report to the House That His Grace the
Governor of Northern Ireland, in the
name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his Assent
to the following Bills agreed upon by
both Houses:-
Appropriation,
Loans Guarantee,
Marketing of Eggs,
Road and Railway Transport,
Trew's Divorce.
Question to Minister.
PAYMENT OF LAND ANNUITIES.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House why the Ministry of
Finance are taking such drastic steps this
year in pressing farmers for payment of
annuities due 1st May and 1st June, payable up to 14th May and 14th June, by
serving them on 21st May with notice of
sale of holding if not paid within six days,
and in other cases with letters from a
solicitor seven days after the last day for
payment to bank threatening them with
proceedings and costs if annuities due are
not paid within six days; if it has not
been the custom heretofore to allow the
farmer up to August to pay, and, if not
paid then, to issue civil bills for recovery
of the annuity; and in how many cases
annually has this custom failed to bring
in payment of annuities.
In putting this question I wish it to be
understood that I am not speaking for
any particular farmer who might be trying to evade his responsibilities with
regard to the annuities. I am speaking
about the farmer who, possibly owing to
bad times, is not in a position to raise the
ready cash for the payment of the annuities on the stated day. I have certainly
come to the conclusion from long
experience that it is time a bit more consideration was shown to the farmer,
particularly the small class of farmer, in
the matter of the payment of annuities.
I hold here before me a notice dated
21st May, notifying the owner of the
farm that his annuity due on the 1st of
May amounted to £14 19s. He had to
the 14th May to pay it into the bank, and
on 21st May there was a notice served
on him informing him that his farm would
be sold if the amount was not paid within six days, that is, the farm would be
put up for auction. I do say that is
rather drastic. In this particular instance the owner of the farm was not in
the country, and the notice was sent to
the agent and the agent could not get
into touch with the owner within seven
days.
I have another case in point, a poor
unfortunate man who is blind and in very
bad circumstances. His annuity became
due on 1st Mav, and he had to the 14th
to nav. On the 21st May he had a
solicitor's letter informing him that if it
was not paid within six days costs would
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
I do
The
be put on and proceedings taken.
suggest that that is rather drastic.
custom heretofore with regard to the payment of annuities was to allow him up
to August. I do say farmers ought to be
pressed to pay annuities when they fall
due, but if a farmer gets into bad circumstances consideration should be shown,
that is, provided he has paid his annuities
fairly and reasonably in the past. I do
not think it is fair six days after the
account falls due to issue these notices.
Certainly no business firm would be
allowed to give seven days' notice that
a writ would be issued.
The custom that has prevailed up to
now is that notice should be sent to the
farmer informing him that the annuity
is due and should be paid into the bank
within a certain period. Then it has
been the custom to allow a month to
elapse. At the end of the month if the
amount was not paid it would be placed
in the hands of a solicitor to issue civil
bills. Civil bills were issued and the
amount was generally paid before the case
came into court or before the decree was
handed to the sheriff. I suggest that
such a course was reasonable, and there
would be no harm if it were resorted to
in future if the farmer has made
reasonable attempt to pay the annuities.
a
I do not wish to protect the man who
has been trying to evade his responsibilities or who has been giving trouble.
do seriously suggest that the farmer is
the mainstay of this country. Without
I
the farmer we would be nowhere, and without him this House would not be sitting
here. It is time the farmer received a
little more consideration than he is getting at the present time. At the moment
farmers are getting the demand notes for
their rates which, in a great many cases,
are treble what they were in previous
years, and it is not fair that on top of
these demands for rates farmers should
be served with notices from the Ministry
of Finance demanding the payment of
annuities seven days after they fall due,
with a notification that failing payment
within that period their farms would be
put up for auction.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
Senator is seeking to present to the
House a picture of the Ministry of
mediate distraint after unreasonably
Finance threatening farmers with imshort notice. I can assure this House
that there is no truth whatever in any
such suggestion.
Let me explain the normal procedure
as regards arrears of Land Purchase
Annuities. First of all, though halfyearly payments are due on the 1st of
the month the Receivable Orders indicate
that banks will receive payment up to the
14th. At the end of the month in
which the instalment is due, if the
annuity is still unpaid the Ministry issues
what are called six-day notices requiring
payment within that time. If these
latter are ignored, which happens very
often, the Ministry's local solicitors are
instructed to issue civil bills against the
defaulters, but the great majority of
these cases never come before the Court
as the tenant pays the arrears and costs.
Ultimately in those cases where a decree
has to be obtained, and the sheriff makes
a return of "no goods," a sale notice
is issued.
With the exception of one class of case
to which I will refer later, no change
was made from the usual procedure in
the recent 1st May gale, and no change
is contemplated in the case of the 1st
June gale. The normal issue of warning
notices is being followed before cases are
handed over to the local solicitor.
With regard to the custom to which
the hon. Senator refers of allowing
farmers time to pay up to August, I
should be glad if he would let me know,
for the information of the Ministry of
Finance, whether this is a local custom
in his area, as in the Ministry's view the
sooner it disappears the better it will be
for all concerned.
I have said that in connection with the
May gale one change was made. It has
been strongly brought to the Ministry's
notice for some time past that a number
of payers outside the jurisdiction-persons resident in the United States, Great
Britain, and the Irish Free Stateconsistently ignore all warning notices,
knowing that a
civil bill cannot be
served against them
proceedings
instituted against the agents who act for
them-auctioneers, solicitors and
forth. The latter are often to blame for
such
unpunctual payment in
Vexatious and unnecessary delays result,
and in order to bring matters more
quickly to a head the Ministry last month
nor
SO
cases.

School Accommodation
23 JUNE 1936
for the first time made the experiment
of issuing a sale notice forthwith without
the intermediate notices. The result
has justified the experiment-33 out of
47 cases were paid-and I trust that in
future the parties concerned will take
steps to have their annuities paid at the
proper time.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I quite realise
that it is the duty of the Ministry of
Finance to collect the instalments for land
annuities, but I do not think there is any
justification for the introduction of the
change referred to by the noble Leader of
the House. He himself stated in the
course of his reply that the Ministry are,
in many cases, dealing with owners of
farms who are resident in the United
States, Great Britain and the Irish Free
State. Is it reasonable to serve a six
days' notice on a person resident in the
United States to the effect that his farm
will be sold by auction if the instalment
is not paid within that time. I suggest
to the noble Lord that these cases should
be considered on their merits. I should
like to point out that the stock and crop
of a farmer is only available for sale at
certain seasons of the year, and that at
other times the farmer may not have the
money at hand to pay. Therefore, there
should be no hard and fast rule, but each
case should be considered on its merits.
If a man is not able to pay at a stated
time he should be allowed a reasonable
time in which to sell some of his stock in
order to raise the necessary money. In
any case, there is absolutely no justification for the practice that a man residing
in the United States should be given only
seven days in which to pay, and that failing payment within that time his farm
would be sold by auction.
I can
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
assure the hon. Senator that each case
will be considered on its merits.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN : I do contend
that in the case of the poor blind man I
mentioned it was most unfair that, after
the expiration of the fourteen days which
he had to pay the instalment, he should
receive a solicitor's letter informing him
that if the money was not paid into the
bank within six days his farm would be
sold. As regards the noble Viscount's
reference to solicitors and auctioneers, I am
an auctioneer myself and I am the agent
for a good many farmers who pay these
annuities. As far as I am able, when I
have moneys in hands to credit of
clients I pay within the stipulated
time, but surely I should not be
victimised if I have not the money available in hands during the fourteen days
which I am given to pay it. I am entitled
to some consideration, and, provided the
money can be found within a reasonable
time, the farmer should not have his
private affairs exposed to the public by
having his farm put up for auction by the
Ministry of Finance.
Motion for Adjournment.
SCHOOL ACCOMMODATION AT
GREENISLAND.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint."
Mr. BARCLAY: As this House will
not again assemble until the Autumn,
I wish to bring to the notice of the
Minister of Education the question of
the school accommodation at Greenisland.
It is almost seven years since the question
of a new school at Greenisland was first
raised on the floor of this House. The
matter has been dragging on year after
year ever since, and the new school has
not been built up to the present time.
When I make inquiry from the Ministry
of Education as to the cause of the delay
they tell me that as far as they are concerned everything is all right, and that
it is the Regional Committee that is to
blame. When I make inquiry of the
Regional Committee they tell me that as
far as they are concerned everything is
all right, and that it is the Ministry of
Education that is to blame for the delay.
These reports are going before the Ratepayers Association and a committee of
the Presbyterian congregation at Greenisland, both of which bodies are most
anxious to see a new school erected in
Greenisland.
Last winter was very severe, and poor
children who were badly clad and fed had
to walk from two to two-and-a-half miles
to school, where they often had to sit in
wet clothes. It is not any wonder that as
a consequence many of them were laid
up during the winter months. What we
really want is a definite answer from the
Minister of Education as to who is to
blame or where the blame lies.
As I was
Adjournment-
School Accommodation
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
getting into the train to-day a district
councillor touched me on the arm, and
said he was at the Regional Committee
meeting yesterday, and made inquiry. The
Secretary of the Regional Committee told
him that the Ministry of Education was
to blame, and that everything was all
right with the committee. They had sent
for the plans they drew up themselves to
be altered, and the plans are here in
Stormont. Whether they are altered or
not I cannot tell. We want to have a
definite answer to this question, who is
to blame for holding up the building of
this school. We would like to see the
school begun this summer, so that there
might be a probability of these children
having shelter nearer home.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would not
like to say on the floor of this House that
either the Regional Education Committee
or any Regional Educational Committee or
the Ministry of Education was to blame.
At the same time I think there has been
unreasonable delay in the matter of this
school. I think the plans are with the
architect at the present moment. I wish
the hon. Senator had given notice of this
question, because then we could have had
the matter fully inquired into. As it is
I cannot say more than that I will
expedite the matter as much as I can.
Mr. BARCLAY: I gave notice to the
Deputy Leader of the House that I was
going to raise this question. The Deputy
Leader asked me to postpone until this
sitting. All I wish is that the Minister
of Education should send me a reply.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: When the
noble Lord, the Leader of the House,
is looking into the Greenisland case, I
wish he would look into some County
Down cases, because there are half a
dozen equally bad. When we apply to
the Ministry of Education we are told
it is the Regional Committee, and when
we apply at the Regional Committee
they tell us it is in the hands of the
Ministry of Education. We are shoved
about for six or seven years in regard to
building of schools in County Down.
Question put and agreed to.
The House adjourned at half-past
Twelve o'clock until such date as
Mr. Speaker shall appoint.

"to continue certain expiring laws as
respects Northern Ireland," was presented by Mr. Long, read a First time,
ordered to be read a Second time on
Thursday next, and to be printed. (Bill
No. 2 S.)
Vaughan Charity (Administration) Bill,
"to confirm and give effect to a scheme
for the establishment of certain educational and agricultural trusts in connection with the future administration
and control of the charity known as the
Charitable Charter School erected and
endowed by the last will and testament
of George Vaughan, Esquire, and for
purposes connected therewith," was presented by Mr. Long, read a First time,
ordered to be read a Second time on
Thursday next, and to be printed. (Bill
No. 3 S.)
Close Season (Amendment) Bill [S.].
Order for Second Reading read.
The
Mr. LESLIE: Questions dealing with
matters of the kind to which this Bill
relates have been before us more than
once during the last few years. Those
questions relate directly or indirectly to
the preservation of birds which are
either game birds or what, if I may coin
an expression, I would describe as semigame birds such as wild fowl which the
shooter is accustomed to pursue.
questions were before us particularly, as
the House will remember, in 1928, when
we passed our Game Preservation Bill,
and in 1931, when the Wild Birds
Protection Act passed through both
Houses. I suggest that the question of
close seasons for such birds as ancillary
to the main question of the preservation
of the species is a question of very great
public interest. There are many aspects
of the question. There is to a certain
extent the question of the food supply.
because undoubtedly birds which are pre-
Close Season Bill-
Second Reading
13 OCTOBER 1936
served do, owing to the numbers
produced by preservation, form an appreciable item in the food supply of the
country.
There is also the question of trade.
There is some trade in game and other
birds. Then there is the question of
sport, which is a matter on which perhaps one need not deal too extensively.
If there is sport there is the additional
attraction of visitors to Northern Ireland, and we are all very anxious to
attract more and more visitors to Northern Ireland because if they come for
sporting purposes, with sporting instincts,
they are likely to spend money in Northern Ireland, and that is a very popular
performance from the point of view of
Northern Ireland people.
It has been apparent for some time,
if only from letters to the public Press,
that the time has come when certain of
our close seasons ought to be reviewed.
I have thought a good deal over the
matter, and I have discussed it with many
of my friends and acquaintances, and as
a result I produced this little Bill and
circulated it, as the House will remember, on 2nd June. I did that deliberately, because I was anxious that
everybody should have the fullest possible time to consider the matter and to
discuss it and to form their own opinions.
The method in which I thought it best
to approach the subject was, in the first
instance, to limit myself, and secondly
my Bill, to protection at the end of the
close season. That is to say, if one may
borrow terms from common parlance,
and bearing in mind that it is the female
of the species that lays the eggs, I
thought we should, in the first instance
at all events protect, not the immature
schoolgirl who has not yet commenced to
walk out with her boy friend, but her
elder sister who has, and who may expect matrimony in the near future.
More seriously, I do think there
is an appreciable distance in time between.
the first acts of pairing on the part of
the birds and the actual nesting, with
the resultant supply of eggs. Here I
should like to express my thanks to the
numerous correspondents who have
written to me on this subject not only
for their support of the general principle of the Bill, but for the valuable.
suggestions they have sent to me. They
have convinced me that instead of standing up here almost contumaciously to
support my Bill I ought to be here in a
white sheet, and to say to myself, like
Warren Hastings, "I am surprised at my
own moderation." Nearly all of them have
said, "Certainly protect your birds that
have begun to pair at the end of the
close season, but extend the umbrella of
protection out wider and catch the
the
beginning of shooting season.´´
Speaking generally, in the first place,
the shooting season begins too
Secondly, it will follow that if the House
does me the honour to give this Bill a
Second Reading the House may expect
divers amendments in Committee, and
the direction of those amendments will
be further curtailment.
soon.
I may say at once that amendments
for the further curtailment of the shooting season for these birds will, speaking
generally, have my most cordial support.
I expect to be told that I have not gone
far enough, and I am perfectly ready to
respond to the spur which will probably
be applied to me to go further. But I
do not want to go too far. I do not
want the House to forget that in this
matter they will be a Chamber of first
instance, and the result of our deliberations must be submitted to those who sit
in another place. In another place we
may expect criticism, because we must
bear in mind that conditions vary
in
different parts of our small Province.
Now I think I can best help the House
by taking the Clauses of the Bill-there
are very few of them-one by one, and
dealing with each bird as I come to it,
but before doing so I want to reiterate
my request for a Second Reading on the
main principle of the Bill; that is, that
the close seasons referred to ought to be
altered. That is what the Bill says-to
alter the close season.
Now I pass to the Bill itself. Clause
(1) deals with partridges. The present
date for shooting partridges is, as most
of my hearers are aware, from 1st September 1st February, both days
inclusive. That is the result of an Act
of the Imperial Parliament passed in
1899. Before that Act was passed we
had to depend on Acts of the time of
George III. There were two of them.
One made the period from 10th January
to 20th September the close season,
and the other to 1st September the
close season, starting from 10th January.
Close Season Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
The proposal in the Bill as it stands is
that the open season should in future
be 1st September to 15th January, but
from what I have heard I feel convinced
that the shooting public will be prepared
to accept an amendment to postpone the
commencement of partridge shooting
until 1st October. That, I believe,
would command general support.
But many of my friends and advisers
wish to go still further. In their opinion
the shooting of partridges ought to be
prohibited altogether for a period of
three years.
That, I understand, is
following the practice in the Free State
which I am assured has proved successful. When preparing the Bill I debated
as to whether I could do that under the
Title or under a different Title, and I
came to the conclusion that it would be
rather presumptuous on my part, having
regard to the manner in which my suggestion of further curtailment of partridge shooting was received in this House
when the Bill of 1928 was under discussion. But opinion was so general in
this matter that I think the House will
agree that an amendment should be
moved, and the form of the amendment
will be to insert 1st October instead of
1st September as the date of commencement, and then, perhaps, to attach a
proviso, which can be debated separately
from the main question, that for a period
of three years partridge shooting should be
prohibited altogether. That will involve
a trifling alteration in the Title, but
this House, always tolerant, particularly
to a Private Member's Bill, is always
ready to take such action as the circumstances may demand.
Before I pass from partridges I ought
to say that I have no doubt, from my
personal observation-for there are some
few partridges left in my own part of
the country that the birds do pair
earlier than 1st February. Last season
I was out in a bog of mine towards the
end of January and I saw three pairs of
birds. You do not see two birds
together three times in a morning unless
they are paired, and I am assured that
the same experience has overtaken others
who have come across birds.
I now leave partridges and pass to my
second Clause. My second Clause proposes to substitute for the existing Section 7 of the Game Act of 1928 a new
section. I have to explain a little to
the House what Section 7 of the Game
Act of 1928 did. It provided that the
shooting season for snipe should be 12th
August to 15th February, and for woodcock from 12th August to 1st March. The
dates for snipe and woodcock were made
the same. The new date for snipe was
never popular in the northern part of the
Province, and I understand from the
majority of letters I received that it is
not popular in the greater part of County
Down and in some other places. Accordingly, although I know I shall not carry
everybody with me, I suggest we should
extend the season for snipe until 1st
March in order that that concession may
be a kind of make-weight for the reduction in the shooting season for partridges
and for woodcocks and ducks, to which
I am about to refer.
My own experience has convinced me
that snipe in Northern Ireland are mostly
of the migratory type and may as well
be shot before they re-visit Norway. The
home bred snipe is mostly to be found
on the marshes round a lough or the banks
of a river, and it follows that people
who have shootings in those localities
need not shoot snipe after any date they
may select unless they wish to do so. We
have also had a suggestion that the
season for shooting snipe should be postponed until 1st September, but I have
not concurred in the suggestion having
regard to the alterations we made in
1928, when we permitted snipe to be shot.
for a ten shilling licence. I think you
may extend the season for a fortnight
and give pleasure to a great many people.
Now I pass to woodcock. I suggest
that snipe and woodcock have nothing in
common except the length of their
bill. Woodcock are far more a covert
bird than a marsh bird. I am suggesting curtailing the season for woodcock at
both ends, so as to make it correspond
with the date for pheasants. We know
that woodcocks respond to treatment, the
treatment being protection. They are
nesting rather more in Northern Ireland
than they were, and I think there is no
doubt that further protection will have.
favourable results. It follows that the
Bill must be so drawn as to give the
necessary force to any alteration made.
Therefore, I propose, in Committee, to
add a few words to the new Clause 7,
which is to be Section 7 of the Act of
Close Season Bill-
Second Reading
13 OCTOBER 1936
1928, to make it perfectly certain that
the penalties imposed by that Act
shall apply. I may say in passing that
Section 7 of the Game Act, as regards
penalties in the matter of woodcock, are
quite incomprehensible. They refer us
back to an old statute of George III,
which imposes more than one penalty for
an offence against woodcock, and leaves
us rather in the air. However, that is
a trivial matter with which I need not
trouble the House.
Now I come to the last Clause, Clause
3, relating to ducks. I have to admit
at once that this Clause is the most controversial in the Bill. The present law
as regards the shooting of wild ducks
refers to wild ducks of all species. Some
of us will remember discussions we had
in years gone by as to what was meant
by wild ducks, and we were never able
to come to any conclusion. But when
we came to the Wild Birds Protection
Act of 1931 the question was settled,
because we referred to ducks as wild
ducks of all species. In practice you
cannot separate them. If you provide
one season for a mallard you must have
the same season for teal and all other
ducks. Nobody can possibly separate
them. The present season for wild ducks
depends on the Schedule to the Wild
Birds Protection Act, 1931. The close
season is from 1st March to 12th August,
and the open season is from 12th August
to 1st March inclusive. That season can
be varied at any time-and I want the
House to mark that—without reference
to Parliament, by the Minister of Home
Affairs, acting on the advice of his Wild
Birds Committee, and after notice has
been given to the public in, I believe,
the "Belfast Gazette" and other places
where the public may be expected to see
the notice and appreciate what
going on.
is
The Minister can alter that date without reference to Parliament, and it may
very well be that the Parliamentary
Secretary, who is leading the House
during the regrettable but unavoidable
absence of the noble Leader of the
House, will tell us that the Minister is
of opinion that this particular matter of
the preservation of ducks should be left
to administrative action rather than form
an item in a Bill of this description.
That is a perfectly appreciable point of
view, and I should be the last to quarrel
with it provided we get what I am about
to ask for, and that is a very serious curtailment of the open season for shooting.
moment
The Bill, for the reasons I gave before,
has limited itself to the end of the
season. I have had a mass of correspondence pointing out that, although
people agree with that, everybody is
anxious that the season for shooting duck
should not begin before 1st September.
I am referred to the fact that there is an
International Commission sitting at the
to deal with the lamentable
shortages of all kinds of duck, and I
have received a questionnaire on the subject similar, I have no doubt, to that
received by other hon. Senators. Not
only in Northern Ireland, but throughout the civilised world-using "civilised"
in the sense of places in the globe where
they take an interest in game and birds—
there is a lamentable shortage of ducks,
and in America they have limited shooting
to one month in the year. If we do not
do something here. to protect our
diminishing supply of wild ducks in a
very few years to come there will be
practically no wild ducks left in Northern
Ireland. I am informed that in a place
which you, Mr. Speaker, know better
than I do, the supply is at present comparatively adequate, but the gravest
fears are expressed as to what will
happen in the future unless the same restraint is imposed on the public as the
public-spirited proprietor imposes
himself and his guests as regards his
own preserves.
on
We are not asking anybody to submit.
to that which we are not prepared to
accept for ourselves.
If a
man who
preserves game himself stands up and
asks that game may be preserved outside his own borders he is perfectly prepared and willing himself to be denied
such privilege as may flow from a more
promiscuous enjoyment. I think we
shall find that in the first instance in
Committee we shall be asked to extend
the provision for the protection of wild
ducks contained in Clause 3, and perhaps it may well be that after this House
has discussed the matter both in principle on the Bill itself and on the Amendment, it will be decided, on the
recommendation of the Minister, that the
Clause, as amended, should either be
struck out of the Bill in this House or
Close Season Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
left for further discussion in another
place and then be removed there. If
that course were taken I should naturally
be prepared to ask the House, on the
return of the Bill from another place,
to accept the amendment which had been
made, but the object of putting the
Clause in the Bill now and of moving an
amendment to it would be, I think, to
ensure a full discussion of both Houses
on the question, and that is what those
of us who are interested really desire.
We want to know what people think.
We do not want to cram anything down
anybody else's throat, but we want to
get further protection for the particular
classes of birds I have mentioned, and
particularly for ducks. If anything can
be done for ducks we are quite unconcerned whether it is done by Bill or by
Order of the Minister. If it is decided
to proceed by Bill and subsequently by
Act the Bill has been so drafted that
the Minister will still have discretion to
vary the close season for ducks as
whole, or for a particular species of ducks
for Northern Ireland as a whole or for
particular parts. The Bill is not intended to deprive the Minister of discretion, but it is intended to afford a
pointer as to the view which Parliament
now takes of the line which should be
followed. I am afraid I have trespassed
somewhat on the indulgence of the
House, and I now beg to move, "That
the Bill be now read a Second time."
-
a
Captain PERCEVAL MAXWELL:
The hon. and learned Senator who has
just spoken and I have on other
occasions sometimes disagreed on important Measures that were brought before
this House, but on this occasion I wish
to give him my hearty support so far as
he has gone. Personally I am
one of
those people who are a little doubtful
whether he has gone far enough. I was
a little bit surprised when he said at the
beginning of his speech that he did not
propose to do anything to help the
immature schoolgirl. That is just the
proposal which I should have liked to
see him make, and it is my intention, if
I get the opportunity, to move
amendment to protect the flapper, for I
cannot see that either for sportsmanship
or for any other reason flappers should
be shot before they can fly. If you are
an
going to protect the nesting season what
will be the good if you are going to allow
birds to be shot before they are able to
protect themselves.
Most birds are able to protect themselves from the very time they leave the
nest, but it happens that Providence,
for some inscrutable reason, has put the
duck in an exceptional position of
danger. For many weeks after the duck
has left the nest and become almost fully
grown it is absolutely at the mercy of
its enemies. It cannot go fast on its
feet, and even that protection which is
allowed all other birds, by the natural
use of the wing, is denied to the young
duck. Its nesting place is easily one of the
most dangerous that could be chosen for
any bird to nest, namely, the banks of
a river or small stream. For that reason
I hope this House will look tolerantly on
the Amendment I shall put before it to
protect duck until they can fly.
I fully endorse what the hon. and
learned Senator has said about protecting duck when they are mating. There
was a time not so long ago, in the lifetime of some in this House, when quail
could be shot in County Down, and
certainly there was a time when a good
bag of partridges could be had in County
Down. Now you may walk all over
Ireland and you will find no quail. One
reason is that these birds are shot when
crossing France and Spain and are never
allowed to come any further. The same
thing has happened to partridges.
Gradually the country has been stripped
of partridges, and now we have come to
one more stage in the stripping of the
country of wild birds, and that is the
duck. Everyone who has an opportunity
of going out after dark in the winter
realises that the number of duck we
heard ten or fifteen years ago has been
seriously decreased, and whether you
look at it from the point of view of
sport or trade or attraction to visitors,
you are driven to the same conclusion,
namely, that you must do something to
preserve your wild fowl if you are not
going to lose them altogether.
This Bill is not a Bill for the protection of private interests. It is a Bill for
the preservation of a species. It is a
Bill that can be supported conscientiously
by those who enjoy sport, by those who
indulge in and benefit by the trade, and
by those who are pure ornithologists and
Close Season Bill-
Second Reading
13 OCTOBER 1936
dislike to see the disappearance of a
species which for thousands of years have
made their common home in this country.
I am a little bit sorry that it has been
impossible in this Bill to differentiate
between the different classes of wild duck,
because undoubtedly there is less reason
for confining the close season within
narrow limits in the case of some wild
ducks than for others. But I fully
appreciate the difficulty, and it would
have been impossible to have made this
Bill workable without including every
species of duck in the Bill. I believe the
most important part of this Measure is
the Clause relating to wild ducks. The
other parts of the Bill have my approval,
but I hope certain amendments will be
made, particularly in the case of partridges, which I would like to see preserved for some years so that the stock
can be increased.
I hope that when this Bill comes before
another place there will be adequate time
and opportunity for discussion, not only
by those in another place, but by those
who are interested either commercially or
otherwise, in this question, so that it
may be fully ventilated and no one shall
be taken by surprise. If this Bill does
no other service it may begin what will
have to be a long process of education
in the country to bring men's minds to
the point when they will willingly accept
the preservation of wild birds for the
sake of those birds themselves.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
am most anxious to see this Bill passed
into law, and there are one or two points
I would like to mention. I can remember the time when there were a lot of
partridges in County Down, round about
Bangor, and also in Antrim. They were
both good partridge counties, and now
there is scarcely a partridge in either of
them. I think that is a great pity.
Whether protection will help to preserve
them or not is questionable, because I
have heard it stated that the spraying of
corn and potatoes and other crops has
caused more harm than the lack of protection. It would be a great pity if we
lost any of these birds that are SO
beautiful and so useful. In my opinion
the protection of the woodcock is very
desirable. I can remember the time
when a woodcock's nest in this country
was considered a rarity. The former
tenant of this estate at Stormont asked
me to see a
woodcock's
nest in
the woods, and that was considered very
rare. Since then I have known of woodcocks nesting in Belfast. Within the last
ten years I saw a young woodcock and
I discovered where it had been hatched.
If we could encourage the breeding of
woodcock in this country I think it would
be of great benefit.
No sportsman should object to having a
longer close season. A previous speaker
mentioned the wild duck in America. I
notice that in parts of America where
they formerly had flocks of wild duck
they are now becoming very rare, and
I think it would be a pity to allow such
a thing to happen here. I hope this
Bill will meet with the sympathy of
Members of this House and those of
another place. The Bill may require
amendment in some respects, and I am
sure its sponsor will be only too ready and
willing to accept any reasonable Amendment which is designed to improve the
Bill and to further its object. I have
much pleasure in supporting the Second
Reading.
Mr. LONG: I think that the introduction of this Bill has occasioned a
most interesting discussion, and I feel
that it will be the wish of the House that
it should receive a Second Reading. At
the same time I feel that I must say that
I am not satisfied as to the necessity of
the inclusion of Clause 3, for the reason
that the Minister has sufficient power to
carry out the intention of this Clause
under the Wild Birds Protection Act of
1931. I think it would be a pity if anything which might be included in the
Bill would arouse opposition in any form,
the consequence of which might easily
result in annulling the effectiveness of
the Bill. I do not think it necessary,
however, to say anything more on this
matter at present as it is one which we
can consider on a later stage.
The House will, of course, remember
that there is an Advisory Committee
under the 1931 Act which advises the
Minister on such matters as the protection of ducks, and I feel sure that to a
very large extent he will accept their
advice on this question, more especially
as this committee is composed of gentlemen representing every interest whose
welfare must be considered.
Close Season Bill
Adjournment-
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I think I have nothing
to say in reply to the discussion except
to thank hon. Members who have joined
in the debate for the support and encouragement which they have given to
the Bill. Perhaps I may particularly
congratulate the hon. and gallant
Senator (Captain Perceval-Maxwell) on
the rapidity with which he accepted my
leaning towards a little joke about the
flapper. That reminds me of another
point which ought not to be forgotten.
I expect many of us were brought up on
the coloured pictures of Nimrod's "Life
of Sportsmen," and you will remember
the picture of Frank Raby flapper shooting with top hat and tail coat and flintlock gun.
I should remind the House of
the great difference that has come over
guns from the days to which that
picture relates. As regards the Parliamentary Secretary's remarks, I had
already intimated that we were aware of
the difficulty he alluded to, and I think
we shall be able to meet each other's
views at a later date, though not
necessarily in the Committee stage, but
even later on. We do not want to arouse
any unnecessary opposition, and we do
not want to deprive the Minister of the
power given him so recently as 1931.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time") agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.

Rev. Dr. Thompson's Resignation.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have to announce
that this is the last occasion on which
Rev. Dr. Thompson will be present in
this House in his capacity as Chaplain.
I am sure I am giving expression to the
sentiments of every Member of this
House when I say how much we regret
his resignation. I can assure him that we
shall miss him very much, and that he
leaves us with our best wishes.

Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): In moving the Second
Reading of this Bill I should draw the
attention of the House to the fact that
certain enactments which appeared in
the Schedule to last year's Bill are
omitted from the Schedule to this Bill,
These are:-
The Statutory Undertakings (Temporary Increase of Charges), Act,
1918;
Section 2 of the Employment of
Women, Young Persons and Children
Act, 1920; and
The Canals (Continuance of Charging Powers) Act (Northern Ireland),
1925.
The Statutory Undertakings (Temporary Increase of Charges) Act, 1918,
was passed in order to enable various
authorities and companies who carry on
undertakings of public utility, such as
tramways, gas works, etc.. to receive
authority for increasing the charges.
made by them without applying ts
483 Expiring Laws Bill

THE SENATE
Vaughan Charity Bill
[Viscount Charlemont]
Parliament for the necessary power. No
application for an Order under this Act
has been made since 1934 and the
Government Departments concerned are
satisfied that the Act may now be allowed
to expire.
By the Employment of Women and
Young Persons Bill now before Parliament it is proposed to repeal and re-enact
with amendment Section 2 of the
Employment of Women, Young Persons
and Children Act, 1920, which controlled
the employment of women and children
on a system of shifts. It is anticipated
that that Bill will be passed into law
before the end of the present year, and
accordingly the reference to Section 2 of
the 1920 Act has been omitted from
this Bill.
The Canals (Continuance of Charging
Powers) Act (Northern Ireland), 1925,
and certain other enactments relating to
the fixing of tolls and charges by canal
companies were repealed by the Canals
and Inland Navigation Act (Northern
Ireland), 1936, which empowered the
Ministry of Commerce from time to time
by Order to fix maximum tolls and
charges.
It is proposed to continue for another
year
the operation of the Courts
(Emergency Powers) Act, 1914, and
certain enactments amending that Act.
During the Committee Stage of last
year's Bill the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie) raised the question of the
necessity for continuing these Acts, and
a promise was given that the matter
would be looked into before this year's
Bill came before the House. Inquiries
have been made from the officials of the
various county courts concerned, and are
still being made in other quarters. The
House will appreciate that until all
county courts and others interested are
agreed that the necessity for continuing
the Acts in operation has ceased to exist
the only prudent course to adopt is to
provide for their continuance. This
unanimity of opinion has not yet been
attained, and accordingly the Acts are
included again, but judging from the
replies already received, continuance of
the operation of the Acts may not be
necessary next year. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I should, perhaps, take
the opportunity of thanking the noble
Viscount for his redemption of the
promise he made last year as regards
the Courts Emergency Powers Bill.
Certainly I do not offer any opposition
to
the continuance of those Acts for
another year, because it is a case in which
undue haste would be very much to be
deprecated.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Vaughan's Charity (Administration)
Bill [S.].
This
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT :
Measure, together with the attached
Schedule is self-explanatory, and I do
not propose to detain hon. Members with
any further exposition of its purposes.
The main object of the original Charity
established by
will
by the
of George
Vaughan in 1753 was the erection and
carrying on of a school to educate boys
and girls in the several branches of flax
husbandry and linen manufacture, and
in the next place the erection of a church
and churchyard. A school established
under the Charity was closed in 1934,
and the scheme set out in the Schedule
to the Bill has been approved by the
Court as expressing as nearly as may be
the intentions of the founder of the
Charity. I beg to move, "That this Bill
be now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: This is a very interesting Bill. I almost think it is the first
of the class that has come before us, and
one is naturally disposed to be a little
inquisitive. So far as I have been able
to ascertain on reference to the Act of
the Irish Parliament referred to, the
late Mr. Vaughan left a good deal of
property in the County of Donegal, and
one would rather like to know what
has happened to that. There would
seem to be obvious difficulties in
comprising that property in any
scheme sanctioned by the Court of
Chancery in Northern Ireland. Whether
that has any bearing on the main question or not I do not know, but no doubt
at a later stage of the Bill some information can be given to us.
It is idle to go through the scheme as
a whole. It has been approved by the
485 Vaughan Charity Bill
15 OCTOBER 1936

Adjournment
Court and therefore the necessary requirement in re-settling the affairs which are
the object of the bequest seems to have
been made. The requirement I refer to,
of course, is to ensure that any charity
should be administered under the control
of the Court rather than by an individual
Government Department. That would
seem to be perfectly well provided for.
The only other question is as regards
the right of access to the Court. It is
provided that the Minister of Finance,
who comes into the matter as a kind of
vesting authority, and, of course, the
Attorney-General, who is charged with
protecting the public interest, should.
have access to the Court for direction,
but there is no power for the trustees
to approach the Court. It is obvious that
it would be impossible to give direct
power to the trustees in the Bill because,
for one reason, the trustees are not a
body corporate, and, therefore, the
natural channel would be the Attorney-
General. But I am inclined to wonder
whether we ought not to insert a provision that any person interested might
apply to the Court as well as the
Attorney-General, who must, of course,
come in, and the Minister of Finance,
who is perfectly entitled to come in as
acting as a vesting authority.
I shall be inclined to put down a
suggestion that any other person interested might be given leave to apply,
in order that we may be satisfied that
everything has been done to protect the
various other interests that may arise.
One can imagine that the trustees
managing the agricultural college may
have some difference with the Ministry
of Finance, or the Attorney-General
might not agree with them, and so on,
and I think we ought to inquire into
that matter. For that purpose I will put
something on the Paper, not with the
intention of pressing it if we are advised
to the contrary. I think we ought to be
assured a little more about that.
One may presume that the various
episcopal authorities have been consulted,
and also, I presume, the episcopal
authorities are satisfied that the Representative Church Body is the proper
authority, although not domiciled in
Northern Ireland to hold land in
Northern Ireland. That, of course, is
merely following the ordinary practice.
cannot imagine that anybody willoppose the Bill, or that there will be
any controversy about details such as I
have mentioned.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.

formally moving the Adjournment I

Close Seasons (Amendment) Bill.
