In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Further protection of
partridges).
1. The Partridge Shooting (Ireland)
Act, 1899, shall have effect as if the
fifteenth day of January instead of the
first day of February were mentioned
in section one thereof.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie : :-
Clause 1, page 1, line 6, after the word
"if" (as if the) insert "the first day of
October instead of the first day of
September and."
Mr. LESLIE: The Amendment which
I now rise to move was foreshadowed
during the Second Reading debate, and
I think I might say it obtained general
approval. The effect of the proposal will
be that partridge shooting under the
permanent law-and when I say the
permanent law I mean so far as any law
can be permanent; that is, until Parliament alters it-will be from the
1st October, not the 1st September,
until 15th January, both days inclusive. If the Committee accepts this
Amendment, as I hope it will, it will
clear the way for the hon. and gallant
Member (Captain Perceval-Maxwell) to
substitute October for September, as
appears on the Order Paper. All I have
to do is to move that the words on the
Paper be there inserted.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Captain
Perceval-Maxwell:-
:-
At the end of Clause (1) insert the
following new sub-sections:
:-
"(2) notwithstanding anything contained in any enactment it shall not be
lawful for any person between the
fifteenth day of January, nineteen
hundred and thirty-seven, and the first
day of October, nineteen hundred and
forty, wilfully to take, kill or destroy
any partridge, or wilfully to take or
destroy the eggs or nest of any
partridge.
"(3) Any person guilty of a contravention of any provision of sub-section
(2) of this section shall be deemed to
be guilty of an offence under the Game
Preservation Act (Northern Ireland),
1928, and section eight of the said Act
shall apply accordingly."
Captain PERCEVAL- MAXWELL:
The Amendment on the Paper in my
name is consequential on the Amendment
the House has just passed. On the
Second Reading debate I indicated my
intention of moving this Amendment,
and I gave my reasons for doing so.
Partridges in this country have been
nearly extinguished, and it has been
found in the Free State that the restriction of the shooting of partridges has
tended to increase the stock. This
Amendment will cause no hardship to
anyone, because there are so few partridges in the country already. There-
20 OCTOBER 1936
Close Seasons Bill-

Committee
of the First Schedule to that Act of
the following proviso:-
fore, I ask the Committee to pass the
Amendment in the hope that in times
to come we will see a great many more
partridges in Northern Ireland. I beg
to move.
to
Mr. LESLIE : In deference
opinions which have been expressed to me
from several sides and from several
quarters, opinions arrived at after full
consideration of the various questions
that arise, and after consideration of the
known habits of such partridges as there
are, I think it only right to say that I
accept the Amendment, and I hope the
Committee will do the same.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Provisions as regards
snipes and woodcocks).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of Clause 2, to insert the following
new sub-section :-
"(3) Any person guilty of a contravention of any provision of this Section
shall be guilty of an offence under this
Act."
This is a trivial technical Amendment
that I mentioned the other day. I am
advised that it would be more in accordance with the general form of the 1928
Act that we should insert words
definitely attaching the penalties which
are incurred by an infraction of that Act
of 1928. I am afraid the Committee
may think this Amendment resembles
the House that Jack built, having regard
to the wording of the Amendment which
has just been carried. The Amendment
I move will be not exactly as it appears
on the Paper. I am leaving out the
words, "who contravenes," and substituting "guilty of a contravention of." I
beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Further protection of wild
ducks).
The Wild Birds Protection Act
(Northern Ireland), 1931, shall have
effect with the addition to Part III.
"Provided that, from and after
the first day of January, nineteen
hundred and thirty-seven, and with
respect only to ducks, for the expression 'from 1st March' where
that expression occurs in this Part
of this Schedule, the expression
'from 1st February' shall be deemed
to be substituted.'
Captain PERCEVAL - MAXWELL:
I beg to move, in Clause 3, page 2, line
8, to leave out from "March" to "shall"
in line 10 (March 'where that expression
occurs in this part of this Schedule, the
expression from 1st February shall), and
insert "to 12th August where that expression occurs in this part of this
Schedule, the expression from the first
day of February to the first day of
September." The object of this Amendment is to protect ducks for a period
which will enable them to become fully
fledged. I explained to the House on
the Second Reading my reasons for
moving this Amendment, and there is
no necessity to repeat them now.
I beg
to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am in accord with
the proposed Amendment.
Mr. McHUGH: I wish to support this
Amendment, and my reason for doing
so is that I look upon the Minister of
Finance as the greatest offender in the
shooting of immature wild ducks. Some
six or seven years ago there were large
flocks of wild duck on Lake Erne. The
Minister of Finance, being anxious to
get all the money he could, issued
permits at five shilling each to shoot wild
ducks, and there was not a poacher in
the country who did not get one of
these permits. The result was that they
shot all these flocks of duck, and now
there is not one for every twenty that
existed before.
The Minister of Finance is the person
who is primarily responsible for this
offence, but he is not here and I do not
know what penalty can be imposed
upon him. I strongly support this
Amendment because it will put a stop
to this shooting of wild ducks before they
are fully fledged, and I wish it could
be extended to prevent the Minister of
Finance issuing these licences.

THE SENATE

Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

TITLE (A Bill to alter the days between which ducks, partridges, snipes,
and woodcocks may not be killed in
Northern Ireland).
Captain PERCEVAL - MAXWELL:
I beg to move, at the end of the Title
to insert, "and to protect partridges for
a certain period." Some of the Amendments that have been made in connection
with partridges require some Amendment
of the Title of the Bill. It is a formal
matter, and I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I quite agree with the
hon. and gallant Member.
Amendment agreed to.
Title, as amended, agreed to.

now
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendments.
Ordered, That the report of the said.
Amendments be received to-morrow.
Ancient Monuments Bill [S.].
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House: I hope this House will forgive me to-day for speaking in a rather
low voice. There was a certain Lord
Alvanley who was speaking in the House
of Commons about a hundred years ago.
He was continually interrupted, particularly by one Member, and told
told to
"Speak up" and "Speak out." Turning
to the principal interrupter Lord Alvanley said, "I would have thought the ears
of the hon. Member were long enough
to hear anything that I had to say.' It
would be a great pity if the Leader of
this House had to say a thing like that,
but I hope the House will forgive me for

speaking low, and that hon. Members
will do their best to hear what I have
to say.
This Measure is intended mainly to
effect certain amendments in the Ancient
Monuments Act passed in 1926. These
have been found desirable in administering the Act which in all other respects
has worked very smoothly and has produced a marked improvement in the
respect for, and preservation of the
antiquities of the Province, particularly
those which are in private ownership.
The first Clause is one with which it
may be expected the legislature will
heartily agree, namely, the compliment
paid to the Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee in raising its status to that of
an Advisory Council. The mere change
in title does not in any way increase its
powers, but it will enhance its prestige
and suggest a greater permanence than
that of a mere Committee. This compliment has been deserved many times
over by the enthusiastic and selfsacrificing work which the Committee has
done during the last ten years. It has
recently completed the task laid upon it
by Section 5 of the Act of 1926 of drawing up lists of the ancient remains in
the Province in private ownership which
were deserving of being scheduled for
special observation and surveillance. In
order to do this properly it was necessary
that an immense number of monuments
should be visited and their condition
reported upon.
About 350 monuments of antiquity
have thus been scheduled.
As a desirable sequel to this scheduling, and in
order that the mass of information
obtained in the preparatory work should
be made readily available, the Committee
has suggested to the Ministry the printing of a preliminary survey of the monuments of
of the entire Province, and
undertook to provide the material if the
Ministry would agree to undertake the
publication. This offer was accepted,
and the greater part of the material is
now in hand. The whole of this work
and much more has been undertaken as
a voluntary work by the Committee, some
of whose members have even declined to
claim their travelling expenses. Furthermore, members of the Committee have
conducted or supervised several important
excavations in Ulster which have added
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Second Reading
20 OCTOBER 1936
greatly to knowledge. The House will,
no doubt, agree that this public-spirited
work for the advancement of learning is
very modestly rewarded by the compliment in Clause 1 of the Bill.
I may add also that the Bill largely
springs from the initiative of the Ancient
Monuments Advisory Committee and
that all its important Clauses, apart from
mere administrative Amendments, have
been suggested to the Ministry by resolution of the Committee.
Much importance, for instance, is
attached by the Committee to Clause 3,
which requires archæological excavators
to obtain a licence from the Ministry.
This is intended to prevent archæological
excavation by the irresponsible or untrained amateur, who at present, if he
is the owner of the land or has
the concurrence of the owner, can,
by unskilled or reckless excavation,
confuse the evidence of the most
promising archæological sites. The
digging up of Tara in search of the Ark
of the Covenant is typical of the evil that
may arise by uncontrolled excavation.
In almost every civilised country in the
world excavation and the disposal of
archæological finds has been placed under
some degree of control. It need scarcely
be said that such a power as this will
not be administered in any captious or
narrow-minded spirit. Provided an investigator is competent, all those interested in the advancement of learning
will welcome excavation.
Clause 4 requiring production, but not
surrender, of archæological finds is a
slight extension of Section 18 of the
Ancient Monuments Act of 1926, which
required only report in such cases. The
original Clause has been found to work
very well and to confer benefits not only
upon the archæologist, but upon the
finder himself. The purpose of the provision for report in the Act of 1926 was
to secure immediate information of the
place and circumstances of a find before
the details faded from the memory of all
concerned. Its enforcement has
away with the too frequent misfortune
whereby a ploughman or small farmer
dug up an ancient object in his field and
disposed of it for a mere trifle to some
local resident or perhaps a collector, who,
knowing the intrinsic value of the object,
took care to pass as little as possible of
done
it on to the finder. In this manner the
find was never scientifically reported and
frequently disappeared for ever.
I remember when I was in a house in
Kensington which was pulled down in
pre-war days about forty spade guineas
were found in a small urn. They were
nearly all disposed of in public-houses
where they were sold for trifling amounts,
the finder not even being aware that they
were gold. In some cases they were not
sold at all. In any case, they all
disappeared except one.
Now when a find is reported, the
nearest local museum, usually the Belfast
Municipal Museum, is informed, which,
if the object is worth while, gets into
touch with the finder and purchases from
him at a price substantially in excess of
what he would have got by local sale.
The production asked for by Clause 4
of the Bill is merely a corollary of the
provision for report and is intended to
make the report of more value by allowing the object to be actually seen by the
experts of the Advisory Council. The
Clause specifies that this production will
not in any way affect the finder's claim
to ownership.
Clause 6 is intended to repair an
omission in the Act of 1926 which only
allowed the transfer of monuments from
county councils to the Ministry of
Finance and not vice versa. There are
many monuments which, while of considerable local interest, can hardly be
said to be of sufficient national importance to be deserving of maintenance.
by the State, and their custody more
suitably falls to the county councils. It
will, therefore, be advantage if
monuments can be transferred in both
directions. Almost all the Ulster county
councils are showing interest in the preservation of antiquities, and one (Co.
Armagh) has established a county
museum, an example which I hope will
be followed by other county councils.
an
Clause 7 is the fruit of experience in
the administration of the monuments
vested in the Ministry. At first the
preservation of these from damage was
effected by the erection of a notice
couched in somewhat minatory language
warning the public of the severe punishment awaiting any person who damaged
the monument. The suggestion was
made that it would be desirable to place
at each monument in the Ministry's
495 Ancient Monuments Bill---

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
charge a short account stating its nature
and history. These tablets were at first
supplementary to the minatory ones, but
it was found that an explanation of the
nature of the monument was at least as
effective as threats of pains and penalties
to ensure its respect, and therefore the
tablets were developed in that direction
and restricted in the other. Clause 7
gives the Ministry power to erect such
signs at monuments which have been
scheduled for surveillance in the hope
that with them too information will
prove as effective as threats. Vandalism
is usually the child of ignorance.
Clauses 2, 5 and 8 are administrative
provisions which have been embodied in
the most recent Ancient Monuments Act
of Great Britain and for similar reasons
are thought suitable for adoption. I beg
to move.
Mr. S.
CUNNINGHAM: I should
like to say how much I welcome the
introduction of this Bill, but there are
several points I would like to mention in
the hope that the Leader of the House
will be able to enlighten me regarding
them at a later stage. In sub-section (2),
of Clause 3, the Ministry of Finance is
given power to refuse a licence to the
owner of land to dig and excavate on
that land. According to my reading of
the sub-clause, the Ministry have a right
to refuse a licence without the owner
having any right of appeal. I think
provision should be made for an appeal
to the county court judge in cases where
the owner of land considered he had been
harshly treated. I would like to see the
county court judge having the right to
decide the matter where the Ministry
had refused to grant a licence and the
owner felt he had a grievance.
I should also like to know whether
there is any danger of Clause 5 cutting
against the existing law which gives the
Crown the right to take gold and silver
treasure trove. As I read the Clause I
have some doubts as to whether it would
not clash with the power which the
Crown already possess respecting treasure
trove. I am sure it is only necessary to
draw attention to the point to have it
looked into.
There is another matter I should particularly like to refer to. Under subsection (2), of Clause 7,
a person who
injures an ancient monument is liable to
be punished. I would like to see that
extended to certain trees. We have
several trees that are really as much a
monument as is a piece of stone. If such
a tree, of known age and appearance,
could be protected under this Clause
from being carelessly hacked and
destroyed I think it would be a good
thing for the country. There was a case
of a very old building in Chichester
Street which was being demolished, and
the workmen found a box of ancient
tokens. Some one offered them a very
small sum for the box, and the men were
so disappointed that they took the tokens
and threw them into the wet cement.
and of course they are lost. That just
shows that unless the finder is encouraged
to give up such things we are in danger
of losing some valuable archæological
objects. I trust this Bill will have the
support of which it is worthy.
Captain PERCEVAL - MAXWELL:
I think that anyone who has worked on
or seen the work of the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee will be glad
to see the increase in their status. I am
entirely in support of the principle of
this Bill, but there are some points about
it which call for a little further explanation. In Clause 3 the word "archæological" is used, and all through the Bill
the word "archæology" is used. Has
"archæology" ever been defined in any
Act of Parliament ? We all know
roughly what it means, but presumably
the word has some scientific interest, and
it is very difficult for anyone who is not
a scientist to tell whether a certain old
object is necessarily of scientific interest.
Not very long ago a cannon-ball was dug
up not very far from me. No one could
tell whether it was of archæological interest or not. I am not sure whether
celts are of archæological interest now
that every museum has got as many celts
as it can hold. There are all sorts of
questions that can arise which may lead
to litigation and difficulty unless some
clearer definition is given of "archæology."
In this Bill it is of the very greatest
importance to have the matter settled.
because any mistake made about it may
lead to a very heavy fine or imprisonment.
There was a time when prison held a
certain amount of terror, but nowadays
prison has very little terrors for
497 Ancient Monuments Bill-
20 OCTOBER 1936
Census Bill

to take the Committee stage as soon as
I would like to.
hardened criminals. It still carries with
it a certain social stigma, but if amateur
archæologists join the regulars and others.
it may happen that prison will cease
even to have a social stigma. It is very
important that if a man has to go to
prison for digging up celts or other
archæological objects he should know
what he is risking when he digs up these
objects. Personally I think the Bill errs
a little bit on the side of severity. I
think very few archæological objects that
are not gold or silver are worth very
much money, and the possibility of a
fine of £5 or £10 would probably be
a quite enough deterrent to most people
to digging up these objects.
There is one other point I would like
to mention for the consideration of the
Government. It occurs in connection
with Clause 3, sub-clause (4). It does
not seem to me that this sub-clause is
quite wide enough. In some Acts of
Parliament horticulture is within the
definition of agriculture, but I have never
seen any Act of Parliament which includes turf cutting in the definition of
agriculture and certainly turf cutting is
not an industrial occupation. It may be
that most of the objects you wish to
preserve are contained in turf bogs. I
know that a great many of them are,
and I should like to include the specific
operation of turf cutting. I also think
building operations should be included,
but I very much doubt whether building
is an industrial occupation. I notice the
Government have a spasm of leniency in
Clause 7 where they impose a fine but
give no alternative imprisonment in the
event of the person fined becoming recalcitrant and refusing to pay. Perhaps
this was intended after the severity with
which they treat a person in the earlier
Clause. They probably decided they had
gone far enough and would be more
lenient with those who offended against
this Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, "That
the debate be now adjourned."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Why?
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think we have
had time to consider the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: If we
adjourn the debate we shall not be able
Mr. LESLIE: Then in order not to
inconvenience the noble Viscount I ask
leave to withdraw my Motion for the
adjournment of the debate, and I shall
have to take refuge in the other alternative, which is to inconvenience the
House, for I shall have to speak of
matters with which I am not as perfectly acquainted as I hope to be if I
had a little more time.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT:
If the
hon. and learned Member will consent
to the debate being resumed to-morrow
I will agree to his Motion for the
adjournment of the debate.
Mr. LESLIE: Certainly.
On Question. Motion, "That the
debate be now adjourned," agreed to.
Ordered, That the debate be resumed
to-morrow.
Census Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It was
not without mature consideration that
the Government decided that this
Measure was necessary.
For financial
and other reasons it was hoped that it
might be possible to postpone the taking
of a census of population until 1941, the
year in which the decennial census is due
to be taken in Great Britain. It is,
however, more than ten years since the
last census was taken and there is much
evidence that movements of population
within the Province, migration both into
and out of it, and the decrease in the
birth-rate, have rendered the population
estimates available of doubtful value.
Members will not desire to be given
a detailed statement setting out the need
for accurate statistics of the size of the
population and of the density of popu
lation in the various areas of local
administration. Suffice it to say that
the policy of the Government with regard
to such matters as housing and education
must be formed in the light of such
statistics.
The Bill is in the main modelled upon
the Census Act of 1925, but departs from
Census Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
that Measure in two important respects.
The first is with regard to the date of
the census, which is given as Sunday,
28th February, 1937. It has long been
the custom to take the census of population at the end of March or early in
April for the reasons that, while movements of people from their homes are
small at that period of the year, the
weather is not generally severe and there
is more daylight to facilitate the task
of the enumerators than is the case in
the winter months. The date selected
meets the condition that most people are
then living at home, and was decided
upon in view of the time taken to complete the census-this may extend to
about three months beginning with the
preliminary review of enumeration areas
-and if a later date had been chosen
it is possible that the enumerators would
still have been busy on census work when
preparations for the celebration of the
Coronation of His Majesty King Edward
VIII. had begun.
The second matter in which the Bill
before the House differs from the 1925
Act is in the scope of the questions to
be addressed to householders. The
questions relating to orphanhood and
dependency, education, occupation and
industry, National Health Insurance,
afflictions, and nationality, which were
asked in 1926, have been omitted from
the Schedule to the Bill.
Two new questions have been included,
those relating to the usual place of residence and the period of residence in
Northern Ireland of persons born elsewhere. The first question is intended to
obtain a more accurate record of normal
residence than it is possible to secure by
the enumeration of places of occupation
on the census night; it has been rendered
necessary by the growth of the week-end
habit.
The second question is designed to
procure more accurate information with
regard to migratory trends. I beg to
move.
Mr. S. CUNNINGHAM: There is
one question in connection with the
Second Schedule about which I would
like an explanation. Paragraph (6)
says that any person aged fourteen or
upwards shall be asked whether they are
single, married, or widowed. I understand that in the last census paper the
age was sixteen, and I was wondering if
there had been any change in the law
making it legal for a person of fourteen
to marry. I may be wrong, but I always
thought marriage at fourteen years of
age was illegal in this country.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
give the hon. Member the information
at a later stage.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think that
anybody would be likely to complain of
the taking of a census under this Bill;
rather the contrary. There have been
murmurings-I rather think I voiced one
myself not very long ago-at the fear
that a census was not about to be taken.
It is desirable that a census should be
taken at not too frequent intervals. I
rather expect that the result of the
census will be that some of the
good people of Larne will
Larne will be able
to satisfy their natural thirst at a
somewhat later hour in the evening
than at present, the point being
that a change in population may put
some town in a higher category in that
respect, and they attach a great deal of
importance to that. But I do not myself think that the ascertaining of the
actual population is the most important
thing in the census. I think some of the
other matters, including those referred to
by the noble Viscount, are very much
more important. We get not only the
number of people but statistics, and these
statistics may be for building labourers'
cottages and for school children, and
other matters of that kind.
I strongly support the proposal to take
a census, and it seems to me that in some
ways a Sunday in February may have
advantages over a Sunday in April,
which always had to be considered with
regard to the incidence of Easter, and
in this case we shall avoid all that. But
although I am heartily in support of
the Bill as a whole I cannot conceal from
the House my view that the Bill will
require a good deal of attention in Committee. Several matters will arise,
mostly of a small character and none of
them likely to lead to anything in the
nature of controversy. I think it is only
natural that that should happen because,
after all, this is not the first Census Bill
that has been presented, even to this
Parliament, and it is the successor to a
Census Bill-
Second Reading
20 OCTOBER 1936
large number of Census Bills that have
been presented to one Parliament cr
another. Hence it follows that some of
the old phraseology has been imported
and retained in succeeding Measures, and
some of it is, in my opinion, a little out
of date.
In this case two new words have been
used, and, being of a thoroughly conservative nature, I dislke them both and
I shall ask the House in Committee to
restore the ancient words.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: What are
they?
Mr. LESLIE: One of them is to be
found in Clause 1, and the other is in
the First Schedule. In Clause 1 we are
proposing for the first time that a census
with respect to all "living" persons shall
be taken. A person can scarcely be a
person if he is not alive, and I do not
think the intention is to take a census
of the unborn child, who may be a
person. The old phrase was simply "a
census for Northern Ireland," and I
think that is sufficient for our purpose.
The second novelty is in the First
Schedule. Instead of referring to midnight on census day we bring in the
expression, "midnight ending on census.
day," and we are left to wonder whether
the present participle is meant to be
transitive or intransitive, and that
rather makes a difference. Midnight is
like Mr. Euclid's point-that which has
neither parts nor magnitude.
Passing from that to other things,
there are one or two expressions surviving that might be left out. One of
them, I am sorry to say, moved me to
most unseemly mirth when I was reading
the Bill, but there is an omission which,
so far as I can make out, has occurred in
all previous Census Bills. It arises here
from a change we made the last time.
Then we provided that certain returns
were to be filled up by the householder
himself. Before that the enumerator
had to do it, but the effect of what we
did was to bring the householder into
relation with the enumerator in a very
personal sense. It seems to me that we
have made no provision whatever for the
death of the enumerator before he has
finished his business. The Bill plainly
contemplates that the enumerator shall
leave the paper at the man's house on a
Friday or Saturday and collect it on the
following Monday, but what is to happen.
if for some reason or other that same
enumerator cannot come back on Monday. I do not know. The difficulty
can be got over, and I was going to put
a short new Clause on the Order Paper
to meet it. The difficulty does exist,
and in this case we cannot rely on the
general interpretation.
Then, again, there is the question of
the further protection for the secrecy of
the reports. The public has always been
very anxious that full secrecy should be
preserved, and there are two minor
Amendments which I shall suggest and
which will make assurance doubly sure.
Although I believe there was a regrettable
exception after the last census, as a rule
there is nothing whatever to complain of.
Then we
come to a matter which has
already been alluded to, and that is the
details required in the Second Schedule.
I think we have to remember that our
Second Schedule, which is headed "Particulars to be stated in the return," will
have a mandatory effect. That is to say,
we cannot rely wholly on the forms issued
to provide for any little oversight or
anything of that sort.
The two particular Clauses that are
mentioned åre numbers 3 and 8. I shall
suggest a slight alteration in each of
them because the first would result in a
serious over-loading of the census form
by the inclusion of information not
wanted, and would give the householder
a good deal of trouble. The second
difficulty under Clause 8 is the somewhat
troublesome expression "resident," but
those matters can be dealt with at a later
stage. There is no reason to apprehend
that there will be any real trouble with
the Bill in Parliament and I do not think
there will be any trouble outside when
the operation of the census is going on.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.

resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).

Vaughan's Charity (Administration)
Bill [S.].
Order for the House to be put into

Resolved, "That this House do now

In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Confirmation of the
Scheduled scheme and consequential
provisions).
Question put, "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill."
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: On the
Second Reading several questions were
raised on Clause 1 to which I should like
to give a reply.
In reply to the suggestion of the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) that
any interested person should have power
to apply to the court for amendment of
the Scheme, I would refer him to Clause
49 of the Scheme, which provides that
alteration or amendment may be effected
by the court upon the application of His
Majesty's Attorney-General for Northern Ireland, or of the Ministry of
Finance. Two courses are open, therefore, to persons who desire to have the
Scheme amended. They may either put
their views before the Attorney-General,
or before the Ministry of Finance, who,
if satisfied that there is a case for submission to the court, will act accordingly.
It seems to me that this is ample provision for ensuring that any necessary
changes can be easily effected while at the
same time there is a proper safeguard
against frivolous applications to the
court by persons who may think they
have some remote interest in the Scheme.
As regards the nature of control that
will be exercised under the Scheme over
the trustees, I would point out that the
Scheme provides the following safeguards
in respect of matters of finance: Under
Clause 8 the property of the institute
is to be vested in the Ministry of Finance.
Under Clause 25 the consent of
the Ministry of Agriculture must be
obtained before building contracts are
entered into, or building work of a
capital nature undertaken.
It must be borne in mind that the
trustees are only administrative trustees
for the expenditure of income. The
corpus of the endowments remains with
the Ministry of Finance, who can exercise their usual powers under the
Charities Acts. Moreover, in view of
the fact that five out of the nine trustees
are nominated by Government Departments, meticulous control hardly seems
called for, but the institute is not to be
regarded as a Government or even
quasi-Government institution.
With regard to the lands in County
Donegal, I do not think that these at
any time formed part of the Endowments
of the Charter School. The original Will
and subsequent Act of the Irish Parliament made provision not only for the
school, but for substantial family settlements charged to lands other than those
of the Barony of Castlehassett in Fermanagh. The Ministry satisfied itself
that the trusts of the school did not include any property in County Donegal
at the time that the court proceedings
were begun.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the answer of
the noble Lord will be quite sufficient.
The important point was the destination
of that Donegal property, and that has
been effectively disposed of.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

SITTINGS OF THE HOUSE.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now
adjourn." To-morrow it is proposed to
resume the debate on the Second Reading of the Ancient Monuments Bill, and
to take the remaining stages of the
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill and the
Vaughan Charity (Administration) Bill.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I desire to draw
the attention of the noble Leader of the
House to the great inconvenience caused
to Members by reason of the fact that
they do not know sufficiently in advance.
the dates on which the House is to sit.
I did not know until now that the House
was to sit again to-morrow, and when I
came into the Chamber to-day and asked
another hon. Senator when the House
was to sit again, he did not know. I
think it is a great pity hon. Members
do not know beforehand when the House
is to sit. It is due to every Member of
the House that he should know in
advance when the next sitting is to be.
Members have appointments to make and
keep, and they would like to make those
appointments to suit the sittings of the
House. Had I known the House was to
sit to-morrow I should not have made
the appointments I have made for tomorrow. The result is that I shall not
be able to come here.
I do seriously suggest that Members
are entitled to know at least a week in
advance when the House is likely to sit,
or alternatively that there should ho
Sittings of the House 508
Adjournment-
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
fixed days for the House to sit, either
on Tuesday and Thursday or Tuesday and
Wednesday. In any case we ought to
be informed in advance, and the sittings
should not be jumped about from one
day to another.
Mr. BARCLAY: I understood the
noble Leader of the House to announce
when we last met that the Senate would
sit on Tuesday and Wednesday of this
week, instead of on Tuesday and Thursday as usual.
Viscount CHARLEMONT indicated
assent.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: But the Members who were not present did not know
that, and it is not on the Order Paper.
We should really have fixed days in
the week on which the House should sit.
Mr. LESLIE: I, too, am getting a
little anxious about the future arrangements of the House. I am not complaining about our sitting to-morrow, because
I understood last week that we were to
sit to-day and to-morrow. I understand
that we are not going to sit again until
next Tuesday. I sometimes look at the
debates in another place and I have seen
it stated-I presume officially-that in
another place they proposed on Thursday next to take the Second Reading of
the Vaughan Charity Bill and my own
Close Seasons Bill. I was therefore able
to throw out the suggestion that having
regard to the trivial Amendments made
to the Vaughan Charity Bill you might
think fit to suspend Standing Orders tomorrow to put both Bills through their
remaining stages. That seems to be
almost necessary, if I am right. It is
not a case in which any harm can be
done to anyone.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: I am
obliged to the hon. and learned Member
for his suggestion.

(Northern Ireland), 1931, shall have

following proviso:-
""
"
Provided that, from and after
the first day of January, nineteen.
hundred and thirty-seven, and with

Mr. LESLIE: I propose with the con
currence of the House, to move a small
Amendment at this stage, and I must
apologise to the House for the fact that
all the Members have not been able to
have as long notice as I could have
wished. The fact is that during the past
few days, or even during the past few hours,
representations have been made from
the Members who represent the County of
Derry in another place to the effect that
the interests of some people on the shores
of Lough Foyle would be somewhat injuriously affected if we allowed widgeon
to continue to be included, for the
purposes of this Bill, in all species of
wild duck to which this Bill relates. As
the new Bill is now before us I am able
to give the reference in the reprint. I
have to move in Clause 3, page 2, line
21, to leave out the words "only to
ducks" and to insert in place of those
words, "to ducks of all species except
widgeon." That Amendment, if it is
agreed to, will give considerable satisfaction to the people referred to, and will
not, we feel, seriously affect the question
of the preservation of duck, because of
all the anatide widgeon are the most
migratory species of which we know.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
wish to support this Amendment. In my
speech on the Second Reading debate I
mentioned that I should like to see a
distinction made between migratory duck
and duck that were not migratory.
Therefore, I have much pleasure in
supporting the hon. and learned Senator
in giving effect to what I think is a
reasonable alteration to the Bill.
The MAVOR OF LONDONDERRY
(Captain Wilton): I also wish to support
the Amendment and at the same time to
thank Senator Leslie for his consideration
in this matter. Representations have.
been made by quite a number of people
interested in Lough Foyle, and the
Amendment which has now been proposed will meet what would otherwise
have been considered an injustice.
Amendment agreed to.

take the opportunity of expressing the

the Bill and of myself to the Members of

this House for the appreciative reception.
which has been given to the Bill. I am

result.
Let me add one or two final observations. First, I desire to submit that
the proposals in the Bill afford ample

Ancient Monuments Bill [S.].
Order read for resuming adjourned
debate on Question, "That the Bill be
now read a Second time."-(Viscount
Charlemont.)
Question again proposed, "That the
Bill be now read a Second Time."
Mr. LESLIE: First of all let me
express my thanks to the House for
consenting to my Motion yesterday and
allowing the debate to continue this afternoon, thereby affording me a little more
time to prepare the observations I desired
to make. I am afraid I have to say that
the Bill is a Bill which is of primary
interest to only a few of the people of
Northern Ireland. That fact does not
in any way tend to place the Bill outside
the pale of consideration and discussion
in this House; rather, perhaps, the
contrary. Very happily this House is
an assembly not ill constituted for the
consideration of more or less abstract
questions affecting education and the
general spread of knowledge.
ex-
To my mind the Bill has one grave fault,
and that is that the Bill departs from
its Title, and it does so, to my mind,
extensively. The Title of the Bill is "to
amend the law relating to ancient
monuments," and the Bill deals to a very
great extent with what are described as
archæological objects. Ancient monuments, as they are in the bare form of
expression, do not include archæological
objects. The definition of those
pressions are given in the Act of 1926,
and I am afraid that it will be necessary
for me to inflict them on the House.
First of all we must take the definition
of a monument, and we find that in
Section 17 of the Act to which I have referred. Section 17 of our own Act of 1926
runs as follows-and I may remind the
House that the expression "this Act” in
the Act of 1926 will in future include
this Bill also, for the Act of 1926 and
this Bill when it becomes an Act will be
construed together as one:-
In this Act the expression "monument" includes any site, structure, or
erection (whether above or below the
surface) other than an ecclesiastical
building which is for the time being
used for ecclesiastical purposes; and
the expression "ancient monument” includes any monument or part or remains of a monument, the preservation
of which is a matter of public interest
by reason of the historic, architectural,
traditional, artistic, or archæological
interest attaching thereto, and the site
of any such monument or of any remains thereof, and any part of the
adjoining land which may be required
for the purpose of fencing, covering
in, or otherwise preserving the monument from injury, or for the purpose
of preserving the amenities of the
monument, and also includes the means
of access thereto.
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Second Reading
21 OCTOBER 1936
That is the definition of "monument"
and "ancient monument." We pass on
to the following section, and I have only
to read sub-section (3) of Section 18:-
In
this section the expression
"archæological object" means any
object, being a chattel whether in a
manufactured or unmanufactured state,
which, by reason of its archæological
or
historic interest, has a value
substantially greater than its intrinsic
value or the value of the materials of
which it is composed.
I venture to ask the House to consider
specially that definition of archaeological
object, because I think Members will see
that a person finding something or other
may very well be in grave doubt as to
whether what is found is or is not an
archaeological object. I propose now to
follow the noble Viscount through the
various Clauses and so indicate whence
the various difficulties seem to me to
spring. Particularly I keep in mind the
fact that we started by saying we are
going to deal with ancient monuments,
and we proceed to deal to a great extent
with archaeological objects.
As regards Clause 1, I very heartily
concur as to all that has been said about
the work and labour performed by the
Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee,
and I congratulate the members of that
Committee on the Parliamentary honour
about to be conferred upon them, with
the goodwill of us all, in promoting them
to the status of the Ancient Monuments
Advisory Council. Clause 2 of the Bill
is apparently based on Section 9 of the
Measure passed by the Parliament of the
United Kingdom in 1931, but it differs
from it to some extent. In the first place
the Ministry of Finance is introduced.
That is necessary. In England this work
is done by commissioners who are represented in Parliament by, I think, the
First Commissioner of Works. Here we
have not got any separate commissioners,
and the Ministry of Finance acts in that
capacity, one of the numerous occupations of that Ministry. Therefore, I
think no reasonable objection can
be
taken to the introduction of the Ministry
of Finance here. It is not fair to criticise
the introduction of that Ministry here
as another instance of departmental interference. It is, I think, a necessary
consequence.
But I do very much want to know what
exactly is meant by the expression
"investigation". Is it intended that the
person authorised is to be permitted to
dig up land or is it not, and what provision is to be made for reasonable satisfaction for any damage done. I think
the House may not be ill disposed to
accept the Clause with perhaps some
slight amendment. For instance, I think
we shall find it right to protect plantations, along with parks and gardens, and
also any graveyard which is under the
control of an ecclesiastical body.
Churches, as the House will remember,
are not monuments, but the graveyards
of churches ought not to be disturbed
even by Order. Moreover, the question
of consent seems to come in here. Is it
really right that any land whatever
should be entered upon without the consent of the owner as well as the occupier.
There is some protection given by the
proviso, but the House should consider
very carefully whether that protection is
sufficient. On the whole there is not, I
think, any vital objection to the Clause,
although it might be modified.
I
As regards Clause 3, I am very much
afraid that all reference to archæological
objects will have to be taken out.
should like, if I could, to avoid that,
because I think there are proposals in
the Clause which might be of use, but I
think we have first to amend the Title
and then limit the operation of the
Clause, so far as it relates to archaeological objects, to some site which has
been already put on the list issued or
about to be issued by the Ministry. It
is possible that if that were done the
Clause might well stand, subject to some
amendments such as were suggested
yesterday by the hon. and gallant Member (Captain Perceval-Maxwell).
As regards the protection of the site.
from incompetent digging, to which the
noble Viscount referred yesterday, I am
in favour of the Clause. A few years ago
I happened to be at Stonehenge, where
I saw operations actually in progress. An
ordinary workman dug up a spadeful of
soil and brought it to the people who
were watching. They sifted it most carefully for any little traces there were,
but while I was there nothing was discovered. The expert gave a nod of his
head, resumed his cigarette, and the soil
was cast away, but you can do a great
I
515 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
deal of harm by reckless digging when
searching for subterranean objects. On
this Clause I think we shall require a
little more protection for the subject.
I should like to stress to the House
how powerless we are about these archæological objects. Before you can complain
that the provisions of the Act are violated
you must be prepared to show that not
only has the man found something, but
that it is an archælogical object, and
unless you can get hold of it and he
is not bound to produce it or tell you
about it--you are really in an insuperable difficulty. You might as
well
recognise that, and Acts of Parliament
might as well be drawn accordingly.
was
I now pass to Clause 4, dealing with
the finding of archæological objects. It
may be remembered that when this question was raised ten years ago, when the
principal Act was under discussion, I
pointed out that the Clause in that Bill
furnished a nasty suggestion that the
finder of the object was necessarily the
owner; in fact, that the finder
the keeper. There was a subtle suggestion
of that in the original Bill, and I am
very much afraid it is repeated here. I
do not approve of that at all, nor do I
approve of such unjust interference with
the rights of property as this Clause.
would involve. You cannot make out a
case for depriving a man even temporarily
of the possession of his property. There
is no provision in the Bill as to who is to
bear the risk of damage to the property,
or for compensation for the man who
may have lost his property. I think the
Clause is a most improper Clause, and
I cannot see why all it seeks to attain
could not perfectly well be attained by
negotiation and by the exercise of tact
and commonsense rather than by the
brandishing of that big stick to which
I referred ten years ago.
You will not get people to help you
about ancient monuments or archæological objects unless you treat them in a
friendly and conciliatory manner, and
you should never, if you can possibly
avoid it, draw your Act of Parliament
in violent terms. Keep the strength of
your Acts for infractions of the ordinary
criminal law, and do not go inventing
offences right, left, and centre. I shall
submit in due course that that Clause be
completely expunged from the Bill before
us.
Now I come to Clause 5. That Clause,
I submit, is not a Clause that ought to
be submitted to this House. I do not
think that that Clause is competent, and
I do not think we ought to pass it,
because it is directly imposing a charge
upon the public. It will be remembered
that provision was made in the principal
Act, in Section 14, for meeting the expenses incurred. What was said in
Section 14 of the Act of 1926 was-
There shall be paid out of moneys
provided by Parliament such expenses
as may be incurred,
A
and I presume the intention here is that
the expenses consequential on the adoption
of this Clause are also to be paid by
moneys provided by Parliament. That, I
submit, is not a provision that can be
made in this House. What happened in
1926 was that such a Clause did appear
when the Bill was first presented.
warning was given in much the same
sense as the one I am now giving, and
before the Bill left this House the Clause
in question was expunged. Later either
that Clause or something in the nature
of that Clause was inserted in another
place. It may be the intention to follow
the same course on this occasion. As to
that we are not told, but as the Clause
stands I must, as something of a Constitutionalist as well as a Conservative, to
which I referred yesterday in another
connection, protest against the continuation of that Clause in this Bill. I
think it ought to be cut out in Committee,
and I regret it was ever inserted without
some indication that it was not intended
to form part of the Bill.
As
I have nothing to say with regard to
Clause 6, but as regards Clause 7, in
regard to the erection of signs and so on.
and also Clause 8, I think some trivial
verbal alteration may be required,
though nothing of any consequence.
regards Clause 7, one of the most important of our ancient monuments is
Dunluce Castle. I do not know whether
hon. Members have been there lately.
but I passed there yesterday. That
castle and grounds has a frontage to the
Portrush-Bushmills Road of perhaps a
quarter of a mile, and on that 440 yards
there are no fewer than seven notices-
"Dunluce Castle" on posts with cross-
517 Ancient Monuments Bill-

21 OCTOBER 1936
Second Reading
beams painted in the most violent yellow
and black. Really the Ministry of
Finance must not deface the countryside in that manner. In fact, unless
some improvement is effected there and a
similar occurrence avoided elsewhere I
should strenuously object to any person
being prosecuted for defacing such a
sign. In fact, I think he would be performing a public duty.
Taking the Bill as a whole, I do not
suggest that we should reject it, but I
do say we should give it very careful
consideration in the Committee stage,
and in particular should we watch this
question of archæological objects to see
that we are not really legislating against
our purpose by some of the provisions
which we propose to insert. If we want
to deal with archæological objects at all
we must, I think, amend the Title.
The
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I am in
entire sympathy with what I believe are
the objects this Bill seeks to attain, but
at the same time it seems to me that the
Measure as at present constructed is in
the nature of a very complicated and
rather dangerous machine for the purpose
of crushing a very small nut.
powers given by the Bill to the Ministry
of Finance and archæologists are very
drastic. The Ministry of Finance may
issue licences to persons for infringing
the existing rights of landowners, which,
to my mind, are of considerably greater
value to the community than are any of the
archæological objects which are likely to
be discovered by the infringers of those
rights.
""
Under Clause 2 the Ministry of Finance
may issue a licence to a person to enter
upon lands for the purposes of investigation, regardless of the wishes of the
owner or occupier of the land. We do
not know what "investigation" means.
"Investigation may mean prolonged
occupation of that land, it may entail
elaborate measurements, and it may even
mean possible damage to the land. In
my opinion the granting of such wide.
powers to the Ministry of Finance is
altogether incommensurate with the
object sought to be attained.
The occupier of land is referred to in
the Clause. I know that now the unfortunate owner of lands is almost committing a criminal offence in owning them,
but he still has a few rights left, and I
think that where the word "occupier"
is mentioned the word "owner" should
be added. The occupier may not be the
owner of the lands, and he may not care
what is done by the investigator, but
the owner may care very much indeed.
Then when we come to Clause 3 we
are met with an infringement of a
person's rights in another respect. At
the present time, broadly speaking, an
owner or occupier may excavate on his
land for any lawful purpose. Under this
Clause as at present drafted it will be
impossible to construct a hard tennis
court, to remove an unsightly mound, to
sink a well, to make a golf course, or
to do any one of a hundred and one
things on one's own land. To do any
one of those things one will have to go
to the Ministry of Finance for a licence.
It seems to me that there is room for
drastic amendment of the Bill in that
respect.
I should like to support the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) in his insistence that the finder should not be
regarded as the keeper. It is a very
dangerous suggestion to put into people's
heads that because they are the finders
of an archæological object they are the
owners. There is still such a thing as
stealing by finding, and in this Bill we
should not use words which would
encourage people to think it is no longer
a crime to keep something they may find.
While I do not intend to vote against
the Second Reading of the Bill, unless
it is very drastically amended in Committee, I may find myself compelled to
vote against it on the Third Reading.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think that
nearly all the points raised during the
debate yesterday and to-day can be more
suitably dealt with on the Committee
stage, when the facts regarding them can
be more easily ascertained.
There are,
however, one or two points that were
raised yesterday to which I would like
to reply. Senator Samuel Cunningham
asked that power should be given for an
appeal to a county court judge by an
applicant who was refused an excavation
licence by the Ministry of Finance.
Control of excavation being a matter
requiring expert advice, the Minister
would naturally consult his Advisory
Council beforehand, and any action he
may take can always be questioned in
Parliament. Applications to excavate
519 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
will not be many.
Cases of refusal will
very seldom occur, and it seems disproportionate to provide for an appeal to
the court.
The present Bill will not in any way
affect treasure trove, which is a Crown
right. The original section of the Act
of 1926, which introduced the principle
of reporting the find of archæological
objects, expressly disclaimed any intention
to affect treasure trove, unless the rights
of the Crown in the find had already
been waived. The right hon. Senator
also asked that historic or noteworthy
trees should be protected. I am afraid
that is not possible unless the definition
of "Ancient Monument" in Section 17
in the original Act of 1926 is expanded.
The Act expressly defines a monument as
"a site, structure or erection."
a
Captain Perceval-Maxwell raised
point about the definition of "archaeological object" and "archaeology". The
first is already defined in Section 18 of
the Act of 1926. The second term is not
used in the Bill, and, therefore, needs
no definition in the Statute. The
adjective "archaeological" is used in
Clause 3 with other words than "object,'
but the dictionary definition is quite clear
for it is the derived adjective from
archaeology, which, according to a good
definition in Chambers' Twentieth
Century Dictionary is "the science which
deduces a knowledge of past times from
the study of their existing remains".
un-
The hon. and gallant Member also raised
a question about turf-cutting and building
excavations. Taking agriculture in a broad
sense, as relating to the general course of
farm operations, turf-cutting is
doubtedly an agricultural excavation, for
it is conducted on the land and supplies
an essential fuel. Building excavation.
would undoubtedly come under the head
of industrial, as witness the common
phrase "the building industry." Thus
neither would be interfered with if not
prosecuted for discovery of antiquities.
If the hon. and gallant Member would
like to put down an amendment on this
point the Government will consider it.
I think the objection to the Title would
apply to the Act of 1926, which dealt
with finds as well as monuments. Under
Clause 4 of the 1926 Act compensation is
payable for damage done under the Act.
In reply to a point raised by Senator
Leslie this afternoon, I am informed
that only two of the notices on Dunluce
Castle at the present moment were
erected by the Ministry, and we may
hope that the yellow and black notices
were not those erected by the Ministry,
although I have no information on that
point.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time") agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.

of Commons to carry down the said Bill
and desire their concurrence thereto.

and desire their concurrence thereto.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday next. Next week the
House will probably have to sit on Tuesday, Wednesday and Thursday.
I am
not yet quite certain about the necessity
for sitting on Thursday, but it is certain
that the House will have to sit on
Tuesday and Wednesday.
21 OCTOBER 1936
Adjournment-

Business of the House
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The difficulty is that I would like it to go to
another place next week if possible.
Mr. LESLIE: We can see what we
can do.
Mr. LESLIE: I wonder whether the
noble Viscount could give us any information as to the business that will come
before us next week. I am particularly
anxious, of course, about the Bill which
proposes to amend the law relating to
ancient monuments, and incidentally the
amending Act of 1926, which was to
amend and extend the law relating to
ancient monuments, and to make provision for the preservation of objects of
archæological interest and so on. That
Bill will need a good deal of consideration,
and I rather hope the Committee stage
will not be taken as speedily as Tuesday.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
the hon. and learned Member might leave
it at that.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Four o'clock until Tuesday
next.

Census Bill.
Order for the House to be put into

In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Census to be taken in
1937).
census
A census with respect to all living
persons in Northern Ireland shall be
taken in the year nineteen hundred
and thirty-seven in the manner
hereinafter directed and the
day shall be Sunday the twenty-eighth
day of February in that year, or such
other day, being Sunday, as may be
fixed by the Governor of Northern
Ireland in Council.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, page 1, line 5, to leave out
the words "with respect to all living
persons in" and insert instead "for."
The words which appear in the Bill and
which I am asking the Committee to
take out are a departure from the established form. Hitherto it has always
been the custom both in Great Britain
and Northern Ireland to simply refer
to the census about to be taken as a
census "for" Northern Ireland. I think
we should not depart from precedent in
this matter unless any good reason can
be shown for doing so. The actual expression "living persons" seems to be a
little curious, because a mere corpse is
not a person, and I hope it has long
ceased to be necessary to enumerate
corpses even for electioneering purposes.
There is also the suggestion that
can take the Clause to apply to someyou
Census Bill—
Committee
27 OCTOBER 1936
thing to which it does not. I beg to
move, to omit the words "with respect
to all living persons in," and to substitute the old-established expression
"for."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I am not able to accept this
Amendment. The hon. and learned
Senator desires to re-establish the wording of the Northern Ireland 1925 Census
Act. The reference to "persons" has
been incorporated in the present Bill by
reason of the fact that the term "census'
is now freely used in relation to enumeration of live stock, motor traffic and so
forth, not to speak of the census of
production.
It is considered for this
reason desirable to relate the phrase to
a census of human beings. The use of
the adjective "living" before the word
"persons" follows British precedent. It
was used in the British Census Act of
1910 and in the British Census Orders
of 1920 and 1931. In the Irish Census
Act of 1920 which, as hon. Senators will
recollect, was never put into operation,
the phrase used was "persons who abode
therein on the night of the census day."
It was, therefore, considered that the
Northern Ireland 1925 Act lacked precision not only as compared with recent
British Census Acts, but also with the
Irish Census Act of 1920, hence the
adoption of the present wording.
Mr. LESLIE: As there is apparently
a reason for the departure I ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 2 (Central authority for
and expenses of census), and 3 (Enumeration districts and enumerators) ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Preparation and filling
up of forms and making of returns).
(1) Forms for making returns for
the purpose of the census shall be
prepared by the Registrar-General.
(2) The returns for the purpose of
the census shall be made with respect
to the classes of persons specified in
the first column of the First Schedule
to this Act by the person specified
opposite each such class in the second
*
column that schedule; provided
that a separate return may be made in
such cases and subject to such conditions as may be prescribed, and any
person claiming to make such a return
shall be deemed to be the person by
whom the return is to be made with
respect to himself.
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave
out sub-clause (1) of Clause 4. It seems
to me that the words in this sub-clause
are a survival from ancient forms, and
that they are really a mere repetition of
the last two lines on page 1. Unless,
therefore, it is thought that these two
lines serve any useful purpose I think
it would be better to remove them.
When anybody reading the Bill gets as
far as Clause 4 they naturally say to
themselves, "I have seen that before,
and they have to look back to see where
they saw it. So far as I can see we do
not want those lines, and I beg to move
that they be left out.
""
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Any confusion of thought on the part of people
reading the Bill is much to be regretted.
As the hon. and learned Senator points
out, there is a certain amount of
repetition between sub-clause (1) of
Clause 4 and sub-clause (2) of Clause 2.
I am, therefore, prepared to accept his
proposal to omit sub-clause (1) of Clause
4 provided that he is prepared to agree
to the small consequential Amendment
on sub-clause (2) of Clause 4 which is
down in my name. The effect of the
inclusion of these words will be to link
up Clause 4 with Clause 2.
Mr. LESLIE: I concur.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 4, sub-section (2) after
the word "returns" in line 15 to insert
the words, "in the forms issued by the
Registrar-General."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

of returns by enumerators) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.

(3) A certificate purporting to be

or
in
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
Clause 6, sub-clause (3), to leave out in
line 40 the words, "be bound, if possible, to," and insert instead the words,
"if possible." The words I want to
leave out are also survivors of ancient
form, but I do not think they are altogether suitable for modern times. We
cannot imagine, I think, that the
Registrar-General should possibly be
really seriously bound by rule of bail
and that sort of thing, or that he should
try to be bound and find it impossible
to get sureties. I think all the object
we aim at in this sub-clause is to see
that the Registrar-General shall deliver
a general certificate, and so on.
I suggest we should leave out the words, "be
bound, if possible, to," and I have on
the Paper a second barrel, and that is
to re-insert the words, "if possible,'
although I doubt if that is necessary.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept the drafting Amendment
on the Paper, and I prefer that the
words, "if possible" should be inserted.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

by instructions), 8 (Statutory declaration.
by enumerators), and 9 (Information to
be used for census only and returns,
