part of the Bill.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
McAllister: :-
After Clause 9 insert the following
new Clause :.
1. For the purpose of Clause 1 of
Part I of the Second Schedule to the
Intoxicating Liquor Act (Northern
Ireland), 1923, the Registrar-General
may, at the request of the Council of
any County Borough or Urban District or Town, and with the concurrence of the Ministry of Home
Affairs, take a special census of such
area in the year 1937 or 1938 in one of
the months June, July, August or
September, and such census shall be
limited to the particulars specified at
1, 2 and 3 of the Schedule to this Act.
2. The date of such local census
shall be fixed by Order or Orders of
the Governor in Council, and the
Order or Orders shall adapt such provisions of this Act as may be considered
necessary to enable the census to be
taken.
3. The cost of such census to such
amount
as may be certified by the
Ministry of Finance shall be recouped
Exchequer by the local
authority concerned, and such recoupment shall be a charge upon the rate.
to the
The CHAIRMAN: I regret to say
that the Amendment standing in the
name of Senator McAllister is out of
order, for three reasons. The first is
that in substance it is really an Amendment of the Intoxicating Liquor Act, to
which it refers. Secondly, it is outside
the scope of this Bill, which deals with
a census for the whole of Northern Ireland in the year 1937. The third reason
is that the third sub-clause of the
Amendment would impose a charge on
the Exchequer, which is to be recouped
from local rates, and it is outside the
powers of this House to impose such a
charge. For these reasons the Amendment is out of order.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Of course, I bow
to your ruling, but I claim the right
to put one or two points before you
with the object of endeavouring to get
you to change your mind.
The CHAIRMAN: I have ruled that
the Amendment is out of order, and
therefore any argument on that ruling
would not be permissible.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I must say I
never had the same difficulty in another
place.
*
CLAUSE 10 (Penalties for offences).
*
*
*
*
*
(4) If any person employed in taking
the census uses, publishes or communi-
Census Bill-
Committee
27 OCTOBER 1936
cates, without lawful authority, any
information acquired in the course of
his employment, he shall be guilty of
a misdemeanour and shall on conviction be liable to imprisonment for
a term not exceeding two years, or to
a fine, or to both such imprisonment
and fine.
(5) If any person, possessed of any
information which to his knowledge
has been disclosed in contravention of
this Act, uses, publishes, or communicates such information, he shall for
each offence be liable on conviction
under the Summary Jurisdiction Acts
(Northern Ireland) to a fine not exceeding ten pounds.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 10, in the first line of sub-clause
(4) to leave out the words, "in taking
the census," and insert instead, "under
this Act." It seemed to me that the
words in the Bill, "in taking the
census," do not perhaps go quite as far
as we could wish, because it would seem
from other parts of the Bill that the
expression "taking the census" is limited.
to the actual collection of the returns.
If we change those words to those I
suggest we shall be able to take the
matter a little further and perhaps meet
any cases, if they should unfortunately
arise, where the Official Secrets Act may
not be sufficiently applicable. I think
there is something in the way of precedent for the change to be found in
other portions of the Bill. For instance,
in Clause 10 we say, "any other person
employed under this Act." I think
perhaps the Committee will agree that
this change is justifiable.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I accept
the Amendment. It will widen the
scope of the Clause somewhat, but I do
not think there is any objection.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in subclause (5), of Clause 10, after the word
"been" in line 29 ("has been disclosed")
to insert "acquired or." I am inclined
to think that the words I suggest being
inserted here also perhaps to some
tent widen the Clause. It is possible, I
am afraid, with the best intentions, to
acquire information that should not be
disclosed, and it is easy to use informaextion that other people have acquired. I
do not myself think that the words in
the Bill, "has been disclosed," fully cover
acquisition. It seems a doubtful point,
and I beg to move the Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is a
doubtful matter, but I have come to the
conclusion that the form of words in the
Bill is better than that in the hon. and
learned Senator's Amendment. Therefore I cannot accept his Amendment,
though I can give no better reason than
a personal predilection for my own form
of words.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the Committee will accept that.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Senator
Leslie :-
:-
After Clause 10 insert the following
new Clause: :-
11. If an enumerator fails, or becomes unable, to perform in whole
or in part any duty assigned to him
under this Act, then and in such case
such other fit person shall be appointed
to perform such duty in whole or in
part as the circumstances may require;
and for the purposes of this Act, the
expression "enumerator" shall be
construed as to include such other
person.
Mr. LESLIE: This really is the only
Amendment on the Paper which gave
me any particular trouble. Looking at
the English Act of 1920, the general
United Kingdom Act, I came to the
conclusion that in Great Britain there
was no necessity for any words of this
sort. But looking at our own Bill I
find that the personality of the particular enumerator is, so to speak, safeguarded throughout. The same man who
delivers the paper to the householder has
to collect it and look it over and see that
it is filled up properly, and in due time
make his report. It seems to me that
the enumerator and the enumeratee are
brought into strictly personal relationship, and if anything happens to the
enumerator we have no provision to allow
anybody to take his place. I thought
Census Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
that possibly one or two of the passages
in the Interpretation Act might get us
over the difficulty, but I do not think
they do.
The particular point that gives me
most trouble is Clause 10, sub-clause (2)
paragraph (d). We say that the householder, the enumeratee, the person by
whom the return is to be made, is not,
under any circumstances, to publish or
communicate to any
person otherwise
than in accordance with this Act, the
information given him for the purpose
of the return, and there is no protection
if by some accident the personality of
the enumerator is changed. Therefore I
think we ought to put something in the
Bill. I shall be very glad if I am wrong,
but I am afraid the position is as I
have said; that there is no provision in
the Bill for appointing a substitute for
en enumerator who has for some reason
come to grief and failed to perform his
functions.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
very glad to be able to give the hon.
and learned Senator cause for the satisfaction he says he will have if I can
prove him wrong. This proposed new
Clause would have to be read in conjunction with sub-clause (2) of Clause 3.
If an enumerator proves to be incapable
owing to sickness or any other reason,
of carrying his duties to a conclusion,
another enumerator would be appointed
in his place and it is considered that
sub-clause (2) of Clause 3 is sufficient
for this purpose.
In view of the experience of the last census it is considered that the proposed addition is not
necessary either from the legal or the
administrative standpoint.
I cannot accept it
Accordingly,
Mr. LESLIE: I will not press the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSES 11 (Collection of statistics.
until taking of next census), and 12
(Short title) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
FIRST SCHEDULE (Making of the
Returns).
First Column.
Persons with respect to whom
Returns are to be made.
1. Persons present at midnight
ending the census day in a dwelling,
lodgings or rooms, separately occupied
by any private household of which
they are members, guests (including
paying guests or boarders), or employees.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the
First Schedule, to leave out the first
line in paragraph 1 the word, "ending,"
and insert instead the word "on." This
is another case where I prefer the old
form of words. I do not like the expression "present at midnight ending
the census day" because, as I said on
the Second Reading, I do not know
whether the word "ending" is meant to
have a transitive or intransitive meaning. We are left in doubt as to whether
it is midnight that ends or the census
that ends, and that makes rather an
important difference. The old form
which has always been used is simply
"on," and unless we can be shown any
good reason for departing from that I
suggest we should stick to the established form and insert simply the word
"on." I do not think there can be any
doubt that the expression midnight on
Sunday, 28th February, means when the
clock strikes twelve on the night of that
Sunday.
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Amendment proposed, if carried into
effect, should logically run throughout the
whole of the First Schedule. The proposal is a reversion to the wording of
the 1925 Act. The wording of this Act
was very carefully considered not only
by reference to previous legislation in
Ireland, but to more recent legislation
in Great Britain and in the Statutory
Orders made there. In the view of the
Goverment's advisers the words "at midnight ending the census day" are clearer
than the words "at midnight on the
census day," and I see no reason for
accepting the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it is a case
in which the Government must take
responsibility for its choice of words.
do not think there is any necessity to
divide the Committee.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
First Schedule agreed to.
Census Bill-
Committee
27 OCTOBER 1936
SECOND SCHEDULE (Particulars
to be stated in the returns).
1. Full name.
2. Relation to head of household,
3. Usual place of residence, with
address.
4. Age in years and months.
5. Sex.
6. In respect of persons
aged
14 years and upwards, condition as
to marriage (e.g., single, married,
widowed, etc.).
7. Place of birth, stating-
(a) if born in Northern Ireland, the
name of the county and town or
townland; or,
(b) if born elsewhere, the name of the
country, or whether born at sea.
8. If born outside Northern Ireland,
whether a visitor or resident in Northern Ireland, and, if the latter, how
long resident.
9. Religion.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the
Second Schedule, to leave out in paragraph 3 the words, "Usual place of residence, with address," and to insert,
"Normal place of residence if other than
that where the return is made." There
is rather a difficulty, as it seems to me,
in this requirement No. 3. I may remind the Committee that this Schedule
is absolutely mandatory, and therefore if
we leave the words as they are what will
happen will be that a man who has
eight people in his house will have to
recite the address of the house where the
return is made eight times. Unless the
census form is a great deal bigger than
any I have seen before there will not
be sufficient room. I do not think anybody can require a man to write down
the address eight times over.
leave out the paragraph in the first
instance and re-insert it in the form I
have on the Paper we should save a
certain amount of unnecessary trouble,
and we should not lose anything in the
way of accuracy. I beg to move.
If we
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
and learned Senator is attempting to
define what he believes to be with greater
accuracy, the phrase relating to the usual
place of residence. In my view the
objection to his Amendment is that it
fails to ensure that the question of residence is considered in respect of every
person enumerated. The wording in the
Bill was very carefully chosen, and in
the heading of the householder's return
as it will actually be supplied to each
person the following words will appear.
The heading will be "usual residence"
and the following three notes will appear
under it:
1. For persons included in this return.
who usually reside at this dwelling or
establishment write "Here."
2. For those who usually reside elsewhere give full postal address of usual
residence.
3. For those who have no settled place
of residence write "none."
It seems to me that the hon. and
learned Senator is attempting to overrefine in the Schedule. After all, the
Schedule is only a pointer to the subject matter to be included in the return
and, as I have said, the return itself
will make it quite clear as to what is
wanted. I am unable to accept this
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I think one can only
regret that a copy of the form it is
proposed should be used was not circulated to Members with the Bill. If it
had that point would not have arisen,
and I should not have been under the
necessity of asking leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the
Second Schedule to leave out the word
"or" in question No. 8 (visitor or resident), and insert instead, "to, or normally a." I am very much inclined to
think that this Amendment and the
following one may be dealt with as was
the last one, and that the form may
explain it. But I am a little doubtful
as to how far we can allow the wording
on the form to over-ride the words in the
Act. We are in a real difficulty about
residence because, after all, speaking
generally, a person resides where he eats
and sleeps, and a person in a house on
that night, even if it is only for that
Census Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
night, is a resident in the house. I
thought we might get over that by
getting a clearer explanation as to
whether a particular person was normally resident in the house, and if they
were we can go on to find out the date
on which normal residence began.
It seems to me that if you ask for the
actual date you are more likely to get
accurate information than if you ask
"How long resident?" because you know
how a person in the North of Ireland
would deal with a question of that kind.
It may be for years and it may be for
ever. Again I am speaking without
having seen the actual form of return.
The visit may be for a day or for months
or years, and there is the old trouble
about resident. A person may very well
not know which they are. A person who
has come from Scotland and is temporarily employed here may not know how
long his employment will continue, and
he may be in great difficulty to know
whether he is a resident or not, but I
think he could say he is not normally
resident because he has comparatively
recently come from Scotland. The point
is difficult, and the form itself may help
to elucidate matters. However, as the
Bill is mandatory I am inclined to think
the alteration will be an improvement.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
the wording in the Schedule is perfectly.
clear, and what I said before applies
both to this and the next Amendment.
I am sorry the form is not here, but I
think it would make the position perfectly clear.
Mr. LESLIE: There would be no
object in pursuing the matter, or in
moving the next Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie : :-
Second Schedule, line 16, leave out
"how long resident," and insert "the
date when such normal residence
began."
Amendment not moved.
Question put, "That this be the
Second Schedule to the Bill.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT:
On the
Second Reading of this Bill the hon.
Senator (Mr. S. Cunningham) referred
to the alteration made in the question
relating to the married state, namely,
that persons of fourteen or over are now
to state whether they are married, instead of sixteen or over as in the last
Census Act. The reason why the figure
sixteen was mentioned in the last Act
was primarily for administrative convenience as it was desired to utilise the
same column in the householder's return
for obtaining particulars as to orphanhood of children under age sixteen. On
this occasion the orphanhood inquiry is
not being repeated, and hence it is convenient to ask for marital condition from
age fourteen upwards.
The legal position then, as now, is
that in Ireland the minimum legal age
for marriage is fourteen for males, twelve
for females, but as Oriental customs are
not encouraged, it may be taken for
granted that marriages do not occur at
these early ages. The last census disclosed one boy of sixteen married and
twenty-seven girls of that age.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Title and Preamble agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be reported
with Amendments to the House.
Ordered, That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendments.
Ordered, "That the Report of the said
Amendments be received to-morrow, and
that the Bill be printed as amended.

Teachers' Superannuation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: The purpose of the First
Clause of this short Bill is to ensure that
the pensions payable to all teachers whose
service includes part, or the whole, of the
period during which the economy cuts
made in 1931 were imposed, will not be
less than the pensions which they would
have received had the new scales of salary
which took effect as from 1st July last,
been in force during that period.
Clause 2 provides for the funding, for
a period of at least seven years, of
teachers' contributions towards the lump
sum superannuation benefit which was
announced in the Budget speech. The
payment of the benefit is being provided
for by amending superannuation schemes.
which have been laid before the House.
Clause 3 amends Section 3 of the
Superannuation Act of 1925, which provided that in the case of a civil servant
who had previously been a teacher his
teaching service should be reckoned as
pensionable to the extent to which it
would have been so reckoned had he remained a teacher. Hon. Members will
recollect that the Government recently
allowed, in the cases of certain teachers,
part of their service given before any
superannuation scheme existed in respect
of them, to rank for pension, and there
is doubt as to whether the provision of
the 1925 Act is sufficient to allow this
service so to rank in the case of such
teachers who became civil servants.
Clause now before the House puts this
beyond doubt, and the proviso to the
Clause also brings into the scheme a few
former teachers who became civil servants
before any Teachers' Superannuation
Scheme existed in their cases and who
would, therefore, be deprived otherwise
of any benefit in respect of their teaching
service.
I beg to move, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The
Mr. LONG: This is a Money Bill, and
I will limit myself to giving the House a
summary explanation of the various items
with which it is concerned.
The provision of £4,060, sought under
Vote I, Class II, is required in connection
with the census to
be taken next
February; and that sought under Vote
10 of the same Class is required to meet
various expenses arising out of the International Tourist Trophy Race, £1,170;
the redemption of a land purchase
541 Finance (Stamp Duty) Bill

28 OCTOBER 1936
Adjournment
annuity, £426; a scheme for the repair of
the Lough Foyle sea embankments, £84;
and the purchase of part of the foreshore
abutting on the Belfast Lough, £2,300.
The main part of the land purchased will
be utilised in due course by the Belfast
Harbour Commissioners in the development of the projected aerodrome.
The sum of £42,400 required under
Vote 2, Class V, is needed to meet the
cost of the recent reconstitution of the
salaries of teachers.
The sum required under Vote 2, Class
VII, is to provide a grant in aid of
certain repairs at Portballintrae Harbour,
£80; and a contribution from the Government, up to a maximum of £2,250, of a
sum equivalent to 15s for each £1
collected by the linen trade for the purposes of a linen publicity campaign. I
beg to move, "That the Bill be now read
a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to take
this opportunity, on behalf of the fishermen of Portballintrae, just referred to,
of expressing their thanks and the thanks
of that locality to the Government for
asking Parliament to find that comparatively small sum of money, which I am
informed, has enabled repairs to be
executed to the harbour, and which will
be of great advantage to such fishermen
as there are in the district.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now committed."

Third time on Tuesday next.
Finance (Companies Stamp Duty) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: The purpose of this Bill
is to bring the law relating to Stamp
Duty in Northern Ireland into harmony
with that in Great Britain.
Clause 1 exempts from payment of
capital and transfer Stamp Duty companies undergoing reconstruction or in
process of amalgamation.
Clause II grants relief from Stamp
Duty in the case of transfers of property
as between associated companies.
As I have intimated, these concessions
already operate in Great Britain, and
hon. Members will appreciate the desirability of ensuring that companies registered in Northern Ireland are under no
disadvantage in these respects as
pared with companies registered in Great
Britain. I beg to move, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
com-

Bill be now committed."

Third time on Tuesday next.

Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn until Tuesday
next." I desire to intimate that on Tuesday we propose to ask the House to suspend
the Standing Order to take the Second
Reading of the Employment of Women
and Young Persons Bill, which will be
circulated to Members to-morrow. There
are also three Consolidation Bills which
will
be circulated to-morrow. These
Bills, as the House is aware, involve no
new principles, but only embody the law.
as it stands, and they have already been
examined by a Select Committee. The
House can either suspend Standing
Orders to take the Second Reading of
these Bills on Tuesday, or, if they would
prefer to negative the Committee stage,
the Second Reading could be taken on
Wednesday. Personally I think it would
be more convenient to suspend the Standing Orders and take the Second Reading
on Tuesday.
Adjournment-
Business of the House
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: Referring to what the
Parliamentary Secretary has said, I think
there can be no doubt that the best course
to take as regards the Consolidation Bills
would be to suspend the Standing Orders
on Tuesday and leave time for a Committee stage in case any question may
arise. As those Bills have been before
a Joint Committee it is not probable that
any question will arise, but there is
always the possibility that someone may
want to say something, and I think they
ought to have the opportunity. As regards the Employment of Women and
Young Persons Bill, I think most of us
have some idea of what that Bill is about,
and there is no objection to taking the
First and Second Reading of that
Measure on Tuesday. I think that is all
the Parliamentary Secretary proposes to
ask us to do.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Half after Three o'clock
until Tuesday next.

CLAUSE 3 (Further protection of

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Unemployment Assistance (Determination of Need and Assessment of
Needs) Regulations.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That the Regulations made by
the Ministry of Labour on the 15th day
of September, 1936, under sub-section
(2) of Section 46 of the Unemployment
Act (Northern Ireland), 1934, prescribing the manner in which the need of an
applicant for unemployment assistance
shall be determined and his needs
assessed, be hereby approved."
In moving this Resolution I should
like to refer as briefly as I can to the
events which have led up to the framing
of these Regulations.
The Unemployment Act of 1934 had
two main objects; first, it made provision for putting the Unemployment
Insurance Scheme on a sound financial
basis for the future, and secondly, it set
up the framework of a scheme of unemployment assistance for the benefit of
those able-bodied unemployed who
either had exhausted their statutory
title to benefit under the insurance
scheme, or who were not within the
scope of the scheme.
The Act itself laid down that an
allowance could be paid under the Unemployment Assistance Scheme only to
an unemployed person who could prove
that he was in need of such allowance
and provided that the amount of any
allowance to be granted to him should
be determined in reference to his needs.
The need of an applicant was to be determined, and his needs assessed, in
accordance with Regulations made under
the Act, which laid down that in the
framing of the Regulations provision was
to be made for ignoring, either in whole
or in part, certain specified resources
such as health insurance benefit, friendly
society sick pay, and workmen's compensation payments in the calculation of an
applicant's means. Specific provisions
were made in the Act as to the basis
on which capital assets, such as investments, should be dealt with in assessing
an applicant's resources and consequently
his needs. With these specific exceptions
the Act left the detailed matters affecting the scales of allowances and the
method of taking account of earnings
and other resources of an applicant's
household to be dealt with in the Regulations. The Regulations, when made,
were to be laid before Parliament.
The Unemployment Assistance Scheme
came into operation on the 7th January,
1935, from which date the Unemployment Assistance Board set up under the
Act of 1934 became responsible for the
assessment of allowances in the case of
those persons who previously would have
549 Unemployment Assistance
3 NOVEMBER 1936
Unemployment Assistance 550
as
with the former transitional payments
practice of the public assistance committee of their district, whichever might
be more favourable to the applicant.
Effect was given to this arrangement by
the Unemployment Assistance (Temporary Provisions) Act passed in the
spring of last year. It is, I think, unnecessary for me to explain to the House
how this dual system of assessment has
operated in practice; the matter is fully
dealt with in the Report of the Unemployment Assistance Board for the period
ended 31st December, 1935, which was
presented to Parliament during the
Summer recess.
been eligible for transitional payments.
Regulations under the Act of 1934,
similar in all respects to those adopted
for the purpose of the Unemployment
Assistance Scheme in Great Britain,
were drawn up by the Ministry of
Labour. These Regulations, while laying down a standard basis for the assessment of needs by reference to the size
of a household and its resources, did at
the same time allow to the Unemployment Assistance Board and its officers a
certain discretion to adjust allowances to
meet special circumstances, such
relatively high rents, or exceptional
needs in cases of sickness or other distress. In view of the fact that the rates
of allowances had previously been in the
control of a number of public assistance
committees acting on no common or welldefined principles, it was natural that
some allowances would be increased while
others would be reduced by the Board.
Actually the allowances granted under
the Regulations administered by the Unemployment Assistance Board were in
about 70 per cent. of the total number
of cases at least as much as, or more
than, the allowances which would have
been payable under the former transitional payments practice.
After some weeks' experience of the
operation of the Regulations, however,
it was appreciated that in certain
respects they were not sufficiently elastic.
To mention one example, the Regulations, in taking account of the earnings
of members of an applicant's household,
did not differentiate between the married
and the unmarried son or daughter
living as members of their parents'
household beyond allowing to the
married son or daughter the cost of maintenance of his or her own dependants
at the scale rates. It was decided to
review the whole operation of the Regulations with a view to the framing of
fresh Regulations which would ease the
position wherever necessary.
In order to give adequate opportunity
for a review of this kind, and to remove
any possible hardship which might otherwise occur in the meantime, the Governments both here and in Great Britain
decided, as a temporary measure, to give
to
all applicants for unemployment
assistance allowances the right to have
their allowances assessed either on the
basis of the Regulations or in accordance
After very careful consideration of the
many aspects of this difficult problem,
fresh Regulations have now been made
which are intended untimately to supersede the dual basis of assessment now in
operation. It is proposed that the new
Regulations shall be applied in the
assessment of allowances from the 16th
November, and these Regulations
accordingly provide for revoking from
that date the existing Regulations. It
is the intention of my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Labour, to make a statutory order terminating on the same date
the operation of the Unemployment
Assistance (Temporary Provisions) Act,
1935.
It will, however, be observed that the
second proviso to Article 4 of the Regulations makes provision for a gradual
transition, over a period of eighteen.
months, from the conditions existing
under the Temporary Provisions Act to
the conditions contemplated by the new
Regulations. The position is that as
soon as possible after 16th November the
Unemployment Assistance Board
Board will
review all existing assessments. Those
whose allowances would be increased
above the present rates of assessment on
either of the two existing methods of
calculation will be granted allowances
at the new rate immediately from the
Idate of review. Some 7,000 or 8,000
persons, or nearly half of the total number of applicants, will benefit by these
increases. On the other hand, in those
cases-much smaller in number-in
which the new Regulations may produce
an assessment lower than the existing
rate of assessment, the reductions will
be applied gradually over the eighteen
months' period, and in carrying out this
Unemployment Assistance
Unemployment Assistance
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
process the Board will consider any
recommendations made to them by the
local advisory committees which have
been constituted under the Act of 1934.
The Unemployment Assistance Board's
Memorandum, which has been circulated
together with the new Regulations,
illustrates clearly the several respects in
which these Regulations are more
generous to the applicant than the Regulations which they are to supersede. It
is, therefore, unnecessary for me to
refer to the changes in detail. In brief,
the principal changes are these.
First, the scale rates by reference to
which the assessment is to be made have
in several cases been increased, and the
special reduction which was formerly
applicable where there were more than
five members in an applicant's household has been abolished.
Secondly, the rule providing for the
adjustment of allowances having regard
to variations in rents has been made
more flexible, and provision is made
whereby, in making such adjustments,
the Board will have the benefit of the
views of the local advisory committee for
each district, based on their knowledge
of local conditions.
Finally, the new "earnings rule" contained in paragraph 1 of Part II of the
Second Schedule to the Regulations, is
much more generous than the old one,
and reduces substantially the amounts
which earning members of a household
are required to contribute towards the
support of unemployed members of the
household. This is particularly so in the
case of married sons or daughters,
brothers or sisters of an applicant whose
contributions out of earnings towards the
applicant's support will normally in
future not be assumed to be more than
if they were living as lodgers in the
household.
These Regulations, while necessarily
maintaing the principle laid down in the
Act of 1934 of assessing a person's needs
by reference to the resources of all the
members of his household, will, therefore, lessen in several ways the degree of
his dependence on those other members.
These concessions, when added to the
provision previously made for ignoring
in whole or in part certain specified kinds
of resources other than earnings, will
mean that in the majority of cases applicants for allowances will be eligible to
receive the maximum rate provided for
in the scale, without any reduction on
account of the resources of other Members of the household. That is to say
that what is commonly referred to as the
"means test" or the "needs test" will
affect the allowances of only a minority
of the applicants.
The Government do not consider that
the equity of applying such a test can
be seriously challenged. Family life is
built up on a basis of mutual assistance,
and provided that any scheme of State
assistance for the less fortunate members
of the community recognises in a reasonable way the extent to which their
immediate relatives require to make provision for their own personal requirements, it is surely only right to proceed
on the assumption that those relatives
will come to their aid. The Government
is satisfied that the Regulations for
which I now ask the approval of the
House provide a fair and reasonable
solution of a difficult problem. I beg to
move.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not propose to
examine the details of the Regulations
which the noble Viscount has just laid
before the House, for I do not see that
any purpose would be served by doing so.
The amounts allotted to applicants for
benefit seem to be entirely a matter for
another place and not for us, but the
point which, so far as I could hear, the
noble Viscount left out from his very
lucid speech as regards the details was,
why is Parliament asked to-day, on 3rd
November, to give a kind of prospective
approval to certain Regulations which
are not to come into operation until the
15th of this month. That seems to me
to present very considerable difficulty.
The provisions of the Act of 1934, to
which allusion has been made, are:-
Any regulations made by the Ministry under this section shall take effect
on such date as may be specified in
the regulations, but shall cease to have
effect upon the expiration of a period
of twelve months from the said date
unless at some time before the expiration of that period the regulations
have been approved by a Resolution
passed by each House of Parliament.
The Regulations have been made by
the Board and approved by the Minister;
they do contain a date for commence-
3 NOVEMBER 1936 Unemployment Insurance Bill 554
Unemployment Assistance
ment, that date is 15th November, and
they have been laid before Parliament,
and now we are asked to approve of
them. I do think the intention of Parliament must have been that the Regulations were not to be approved until
they had, at all events, come into
operation, and had been in operation.
some little time in order that Parliament
might be in a position to form an opinion.
as to whether or not they should be
approved. That is a point that requires
to be elucidated.
There is another method sometimes
adopted, and that is to say that the
Regulations shall not come into operation
until they have been approved. That is
not the case here, nor is that what has
been done. I could not at the moment
agree to give prospective approval to
Regulations which have not come into
operation and which, therefore, have not
stood the test of time. What would
happen if the House refused to pass
this Resolution this afternoon would not
seem to me to be of any great importance.
The Regulations would still come into
operation on 15th November, and continue, I suppose, until 14th November,
1937. They would then expire, unless it
happened that in the ensuing Session the
Government asked this House to express
approval, which they can perfectly well
do. That might be based on the test
of time, but I do think that before we
are asked to give this approval we ought
to be told why we are asked to do so
before the Regulations are actually in
operation.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
approval of this Motion is asked for
now in case Parliament was not sitting
when the Regulations came into operation. In that case we would have to call
a special Session of Parliament
to
approve of Regulations for which we
might obtain approval beforehand.
Mr. LESLIE: Surely Parliament will
be sitting again before the 14th November next year.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
has been scrutinised, as hon. Members
are aware, by a Joint Committee of both
Houses of Parliament. It does not alter
in any way the existing law, its sole
purpose being to codify in one Act all
existing enactments relating to unemployment insurance.
I beg to move, "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: On looking over
this Bill I find that in sub-clause (4)
of Clause 3 it is provided that:-
Where in consequence of an arrangement made by a poor law authority a
person is engaged in work provided by
a local authority, he shall not, if a
contribution towards his remuneration
is made by the poor law authority, be
deemed to be employed in insurable
employment:
Provided that this sub-section shall
not apply in the case of any person
who has previously been in receipt of
benefit and is employed in full-time.
work provided by the local authority,
but for the purpose of this proviso, a
person shall not be deemed to have
been employed in full-time work unless
he has worked for such number of
hours in each week as would normally
have been worked by him if he had
been employed on the same work otherwise than under such an arrangement
as aforesaid.
My object in referring to this particular matter is to have the meaning
of this sub-clause made quite clear. A
few years ago when we were passing
through very hard times urban councils
and other local bodies started relief
schemes. Instead of giving a full week's
work to the unemployed they were
obliged to give them only four days work
and stamp their card. I want to make
sure that that arrangement will still hold
good in the future, and that this subclause will not prevent local relief committees from employing a man for a
proportion of a week and stamping his
card, thereby qualifying him for benefit.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
supply the hon. Member with the information on the next stage of the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.

Widows', Orphans' and Old Age Contributory Pensions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
codifies the law relating to contributory
pensions. It is also a Consolidation Bill
and in no way alters existing law. I
beg to move, "That the Bill be now read
a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Employment of Women and Young
Persons Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
the Second Reading of this Bill I wish
to emphasise that its object is to improve
the procedure for authorising the employment of women and young persons
on the two-shift system. There has
never been any statutory restriction on
the freedom of men to engage in employment of this kind, and for 20 years
women and young persons over 16 years
of age have also been permitted to accept
employment on a two-shift system.
The present law governing the matter
is contained in Section 2 of the Employment of Women, Young Persons and
Children Act, 1920. The Section embodied the recommendations of a Home
Office Committee which had examined
the extension to women and young
persons of the two-day shift system during the war. That Committee had
unanimously recommended that the
power to authorise employment of this
kind should be continued for an experimental period of five years in order that
its effect could be kept under review.
Since 1925 Section 2 of the Act of 1920
has been continued in operation year by
year by its inclusion in the Expiring
Laws Continuance Act of each Session of
Parliament.
Meantime the system has been watched
carefully by the Home Office in London
and by the Ministry of Labour in
Northern Ireland. Its working in Great
Britain has been examined by a committee composed of members of all shades
of political thought and of experience in
medical, social and industrial matters.
The committee, which included three
women members, one of whom was a
veteran trade union organiser, recommended unanimously that the power to
authorise the system should be continued,
subject to certain modifications of the
procedure for authorising the practice.
The Government at Westminster accepted
the committee's recommendations, and
an Act has been placed on the Statute
Book this year giving legal effect in
Great Britain to the committee's report.
It is clearly desirable that the Factory
Code, which has always been uniform
throughout the United Kingdom, should
continue to be uniform, in view of the
very close industrial connections between
Northern Ireland and the rest of the
United Kingdom. Accordingly, the
Government here decided to introduce a
Bill similar to the Measure which has
become law in Great Britain, and, in
consequence, hon. Senators will have
noticed that Section 2 of the 1920 Act
has been dropped from this year's Expiring Laws Continuance Bill. The
present procedure, therefore, for authorising this system of work will come to an
end at the 31st December, and it is proposed that it should be replaced as from
1st January next by the procedure prescribed in this Bill.
I should like it to be clearly understood at the outset that it is entirely
optional on the part of employers and
workers whether a two-shift system
should be adopted or not. Although the
freedom to work on this system has been
extended to women and young persons,
as I have mentioned, for 20 years, it has
not, of course, been adopted except in
certain types of cases. In Great Britain,
according to the latest figures available.
some 35,000 women and young persons
were working on this system in 810
establishments. In Northern Ireland
557 Women & Young Persons Bill- 3 NOVEMBER 1936

Second Reading
there have been 14 authorisations, the
industries concerned including bleaching,
hosiery, embroidery, linen weaving,
aerated waters, tobacco, handkerchiefs,
household linens, readymade clothing,
twine and thread.
The system has proved of considerable
advantage in meeting production difficulties, when a sudden demand for an
increased output arises from causes such
as a breakdown of plant or a seasonal
pressure. Again, it has proved of value
when a "new line" of goods has been
developed, and it is desirable to test the
market before incurring heavy expense
on new machinery or premises. The output of one department in a factory, too,
may prove temporarily insufficient to
enable full employment to be given in
the remainder.
Some of these difficulties may be
partially overcome by working overtime.
Overtime, however, is open to the objection that it may involve an individual
operative in working unduly long hours.
This is avoided by the two-shift system,
which provides additional employment
through the engagement of
group of workers.
an
extra
Under the existing law an employer
and a majority of the women and young
persons in his employment may make a
joint application for an Order authorising their employment at any time
between the hours of six in the morning
and ten in the evening on any week-day
except Saturday (and between the hours
of six in the morning and two in the
afternoon on Saturdays) in shifts averaging for each shift not more than eight
hours per day, including the breaks
required by the Factory Code. The Ministry satisfies itself that the joint application is, in fact, supported by the majority
of the employees concerned, and the
authorisation, when made, may lay down
conditions for safeguarding the welfare
and interests of the persons to be employed. The authorisation, once made,
continues in force indefinitely, but is revocable in the event of non-compliance
with the conditions of its issue, or if
abuses of any description occur
result of working the two-shift system.
The principal changes which this Bill
makes in the procedure for authorising
this system of working are as follows:-
It was thought in another place that six
o'clock is too early a start for the first
as
a
shift, and, although this is the starting
hour permitted under the present law,
the Bill leaves it to agreement between
the employer and the workers concerned
as to what shall be the hours between
which the two-shift system is to be
worked. This appears in Clause 1, subsection (1) of the Bill. No authorisation may,
of course, be granted, which
might infringe the requirement of the
Factory Code that women and young
persons shall not be employed between
the hours of 10 p.m. and 5 a.m.
The provision under the present law,
that there shall be a joint application by
the employer and the majority of the
workers, is abolished. It has been found
that this procedure is open to criticism.
It has necessitated in practice a canvass
by the employer to obtain the worker's
signature to the joint application. This
may place workers in an embarrassing
position if they are not in favour of the
proposal.
The Bill substitutes for this an application by the employer to be followed by
inquiries by the Ministry, as provided in
sub-section (2) of Clause 1, to ascertain
whether the majority of the workpeople
concerned are willing to work on this
system. Their opinions are to be
obtained by a secret ballot, which will
be carried out by the Ministry itself.
The Bill provides that rules shall be made
for this purpose by a Special Order,
which is to be laid before both Houses of
Parliament and which may be annulled
by either. The only exception to this
procedure will be when the application
relates to a newly established factory or
workshop. In such a case, obviously
there would be no workers available to
consult, and their consent will be signified
by their willingness to accept employment under those conditions.
Sub-section (4) of Clause 1 of the Bill
empowers the Ministry to impose conditions for safeguarding the welfare and
interests of the persons employed on the
shift system. The employer may be required to provide not only suitable
accommodation for clothing and facilities
for meals, but also transport facilities for
workers living at a distance, and, in the
case of young persons between the ages
of 16 and 18, reasonable facilities for
attendance at continuation schools. This
is more specific than the existing law.
559 Women & Young Persons Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
Clause 2 of the Bill empowers the
Ministry to revoke the authorisation
when any conditions attached to its grant
have not been complied with or abuses
have arisen. Sub-section (2) of Clause
2 lays it down that the occupier of the
factory or workshop shall give notice at
once of the discontinuance and of the
resumption of this system of work.
Hitherto, there has been no such obligation to inform the Ministry. Subsection (3) of the Clause amends the
existing law by providing that
authorisation shall remain in force for
no
more than two years. Moreover, when
an authorisation is required to meet some
purely temporary difficulty, it is to be
limited to the period necessary for that
purpose (Sub-section (3) of Clause 1).
The remaining Clauses of the Bill are
merely machinery Clauses.
All existing authorisations will cease
at the end of this year, and the new procedure will apply uniformly in all cases
where employers and women and young
person workers together wish to work the
two-shift system in future.
I think I have now touched upon the
principal changes in procedure which the
Bill makes. The system itself has stood
the test of the last twenty years. It has
been approved by two commitees which
have carefully examined its working. It
provides reasonable facilities (to workers
as well as employers) for dealing with
certain contingencies. It makes for a
shorter working day, for a reduction of
overtime, and for a better distribution
of employment. So much for the system.
As regards the procedure for authorising
the working of this system, the Bill introduces simplifications and amendments
which, the Government is satisfied, will
enable the wishes and the requirements
of both employers and employees to be
met satisfactorily and expeditiously. For
all these reasons, therefore, I commend
the Bill to this House. I beg to move:
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to support the Second Reading of this Bill. I believe this system
will be a great benefit to our industrial
concerns in the North of Ireland. Up
to the present there has been a considerable amount of overtime work amongst
women in making-up trades and other
occupations. There was nothing the
Workers' Association were more opposed
to than overtime, because the women were
asked to work from nine o'clock in the
morning on to seven and eight o'clock.
It was a greater strain on them than was
advisable. This two-shift system will
certainly be a great benefit. I know, as
far as the men are concerned, it has
proved a great success in the big shipbuilding firms. At one time the men
were required to work from 5-30 at night
until six o'clock in the morning. That
was a desperate long shift. I did it
myself and therefore have some experience of it. With the three shifts
there has been considerable relief. It
enables the firms to take the best out of
the machinery, and at the same time
reduces the hours of work for the men.
It has been found to have the same effect
with the two shifts for women.
There has been great controversy
about the starting time in the morning.
I know that the start at six o'clock in
the morning and finishing at two o'clock
is popular with the men, because they
have the rest of the day to themselves
and can attend whatever amusements
they wish. It was argued in another
place that six o'clock was too early, as
it meant that it would be ten o'clock
before the two shifts would finish. This
Bill should be of great benefit to the
industries concerned. There are many
times when a firm finds itself busy. It
may get an extremely large order or a
number of orders, and instead of women
and girls being put on overtime they will
be able to run a double shift. This will
mean the employment of more workers
and taking off a number of unemployed
from the books. At the same time it
will be a much more pleasant time for
the worker. I have no hesitation whatever in supporting the Second Reading
of this Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would also like to
support the Second Reading of this Bill.
I agree with what Senator Cunningham
has said, but I am sorry that the Bill
does not go far enough. I would like
to have seen the Bill embracing shop
assistants. We have a great number of
young women in shops in the city of
Belfast who have no protection whatever.
They are paid a small rate of wage. I
561 Women & Young Persons Bill

3 NOVEMBER 1936
Finance Bill
am informed that the rate runs from 12s
to 16s a week. They have to keep themselves respectable and in decent clothes,
and there is no provision made for meal
hour. When it comes to the time to
take the meal they have got to go out
and purchase it somewhere, and this their
wages cannot afford, the result being that
at the end of the week they have nothing
to bring home to their parents. I would
like to have seen something in this
Bill to give these young people some
protection. Then, it is not fair to other
young persons or to people who pay a fair
wage or give good conditions to their
workers. They have to compete with
those who pay a low wage. I would be
very glad to see some protection given to
these young people in the city, and there
are a great many of them.
Mr. LESLIE: As I understand the
Bill before us, it does not make any
change in the law except that it provides for the better regulation of a system which has been in operation
certainly since 1920. I believe I am
right in thinking that the new provision
as regards the secret ballot in Clause 1
of the Bill will not in any way prevent
a practice which will be more common
perhaps in the small factories outside
Belfast and Derry than in the large
cities, that is, the practice of an employer calling his work people together
and saying to them, "Look here, boys or
girls" in this case girls-"I have got
an order for so-and-so to be done in suchand-such a time. What are we to do
about it?" That is to say, he consults
them unofficially, so to speak. He can
still do that, but if the two-shift system
is adopted it will have to be confirmed
by secret ballot.
I also take it that the whole system
is to be optional. There is no definite
prohibition of overtime if that system is
preferred so long as the overtime is confined within statutory limits. Something
has been said about hours. I confess I tried to do a little sum in head,
my
and I do not see how you can make the
hour of starting any later than six
o'clock if you retain the provision that
a woman is not to be employed after ten
at night. I do not see how you can get
two shifts of eight hours before ten
o'clock at night unless you start at six
or earlier. Passing from that to what
was said by my hon. Friend on the left
as regards shop assistants, I think he
meant the girls behind the counters-
Mr. BARCLAY: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the noble
Viscount will be able to assure us as
regards that that whatever grievances
these young people may suffer from at
all events being employed in a factory.
or workshop is not one of them, and the
Bill is limited to factories and workshops.
I see no reason for objecting to any
Clause in the Bill, and I propose to support it.
I should
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
like to say that the Factories Code, of
which this Bill is really a part, does not
cover shop assistants. My right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs,
has recently appointed a Committee
which is investigating the possibility of
amending and improving the Shops Acts.
The rest of this Bill is really permissive.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Finance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: This Measure is normally concerned in the main with
changes in taxation which arise out
of the Budget statement. As hon.
Members are aware, no changes in
taxation were announced this year and
the Bill deals, therefore, only with
minor questions.
Clause 1 provides that hawkers using
motor vehicles must pay hawkers' licence
duty of £2. Most hawkers using motor
vehicles do, in fact, take out a licence,
but there was doubt as to whether they
were legally bound to do so.
Clause 2 corrects a mistake made in
the Revaluation (Consequential Provisions) Act. The Act made publicans'
licence duties conform to the new
valuations, the operative date being, in
the case of refreshment house licences,
1st April, 1937. This was done in the
belief that licences had already been
issued for the current year on the old
valuations, but it has been found that
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
this was not the case. The Clause ensures that the licensees of refreshment
houses will not have to pay more as a
consequence of the mistake.
Clause 3 reinstates a section in the
Finance Act, 1917, enabling a refund
of part of the liquor licence duties to
be made when licensed premises are
vacated. The section in the 1917 Act
was repealed in error.
Clause 4 is intended to prevent evasion
of the payment of death duties by
deposits being made in the joint names
of two persons.
Clause 5 also appears in the British
Finance Act of the present year.
It
removes, with specified exceptions, the
exemption from estate duty given by the
Finance Act, 1894, to certain classes
of property situate abroad.
It
Clause 6 also appears in the British
Finance Act of the present year.
relieves from double taxation in the two
areas of Northern Ireland and Great
Britain, property in which a surviving
spouse has a life interest.
Clause 7 exempts from death duties
property left to the National Trust.
Such exemption is already in force in
Great Britain, but only now becomes
necessary in Northern Ireland with the
extension of the operations of the Trust
to Northern Ireland.
Clause 8 is included to continue
exemption from death duties in respect
of certain Indian pensions transferred
under the Government of India Act,
1933, from India to London.
Clause 9 is designed to assist the
Valuation Department to obtain information as to the values of property
changing hands or being leased.
Clause 10 provides that summary proceedings may be taken in regard to
transferred taxes. The British Finance
Act of last year made similar provision
in regard to reserved taxes. I beg to
move, "That the Bill be now read a
Second time."
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
There is one point in this Bill which I
think might be changed. On page 5, line
25, permission is given the National
Trust to receive property. They
may receive the property, but power
is given to the Ministry of Finance to
decide whether it may
be received
of the
under the terms
Act. I
think the object of this Act is to
get people who have places of beauty or
interest to leave them to the National
Trust. A person is not encouraged to do
this if he knows that though he may
leave it to the National Trust it is still
left to the Ministry of Finance to say
whether it is exempt from duty or not.
It is Parliament who should say when
property is handed over to the National
Trust if death duty will be charged
upon it. It may be said that if property
is handed over and there is a house on
it and that house be let, why
then should the tenant of that house
escape? The tenant of that house would
not escape because he would be charged
on the fair rent. I understand there is
a Measure before the Imperial Parliament to confirm this. That being
already settled I think in the interest
of the object of this Clause it would be
better if the word "may" was changed
to "shall." Parliament should have the
power of saying what should be done.
It should not be left to the Ministry
of Finance in a question like this. I
do not know if there can be an Amendment of this Bill in this House, but I
hope the Government will look into the
matter.
Mr. LESLIE: The Finance Bill now
before us is to some extent a composite
Measure. Part of it, and the greater
part of it, is outside the scope of this
House altogether. We could not if we
wished interfere with Clause 1, which
deals with hawkers' licences and so on,
but there are Clauses or sub-clauses in
the Bill which are, I think, fully within
our jurisdiction, Clauses which in no
sense of the term impose taxation, or
appropriate revenue, and which are not
based upon a Money Resolution passed
in another place. Of such Clauses and
sub-clauses, Clauses 4 and 9, with which
I propose to trouble the House for a few
minutes, are examples.
As regards Clause 4, I am not going
to say anything about sub-clause (2)
because I recognise that that particular
sub-clause ought not to be interfered
with in this Chamber, but as regards
sub-clause (1) of Clause 4 I think perhaps there is something that should be
said. The Clause aims, and very
properly aims one must admit, at pre-
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
3 NOVEMBER 1936
venting a practice which has grown up
of defrauding the revenue by putting
money on deposit receipt in two names and
then when one man is dead the survivor
coming along and taking out the money
without disclosing the fact to anybody.
That, stated like that, appears to be a
perfectly ordinary fraud which is not to
be encouraged and which Parliament, it
seems to me, is perfectly entitled to take
steps to stop.
There is another side to the question.
It is all very well for us to set to work
to prevent fraud on the revenue, and
we may take such means as we see fit
towards that end, but we must be very
careful we do not inadvertently do an
injustice to anybody or impose any undue
liability or any unnecessary inconvenience on anybody who may be
concerned in a perfectly legitimate transaction. It seems to me that in this
country a deposit account really means
a deposit of money on call, and that the
practice of depositing money for a term,
which is fairly common across the water,
does not obtain to any extent in Northern Ireland. That is my information.
I cannot say whether it is substantially
correct, but I believe it is. There may
be two or three men who may be trustees
appointed by will or settlement or, at all
events, people with documentary
appointment. It may be well that those
trustees who have money in hand should
put it on deposit.
a
I have heard judges in court saying
that trustees coming into possession of
money which they are not at the moment
to invest should there and then, not by
return of post, but before an hour has.
gone over their heads put it in a bank
on deposit in their joint names. If there
The
is one thing not smiled upon by the
courts it is any risk of trust money being
mixed with a man's own money.
whole tendency is to encourage deposit,
if even for a week, of trust money in a
bank. It may very well happen that
trustees may come into possession of a
sum of money, let us say, on the repayment of money lent on mortgage. They
may have decided to invest it in some
highly respectable security on the Stock
Exchange. The matter may have gone
so far that the contract note has been
delivered, and intimation has been received that the money will be required
on such a day. Let us suppose that
before that day comes one of these men
dies, and that the money is due the
next day. What is going to happen?
Do we not want to provide for that? At
the same time it seems to me
we do
require to insert between paragraphs 1
and 2 of sub-clause 1 something that
will protect that class of person. That is
one class-what I have called the trustee
by document. There is, however,
another class of trustee. I expect many
of us, perhaps, have served in the second
class. Money is being got together very
often for some, we will hope, pious
object. The first object that occurs to
me is the provision of an organ for a
church. People collect that money and
it is probably put in the bank in the
names of two or three of them. That
money may be wanted at once. If one
of these men dies there is trouble again.
If money has to be produced suddenly
like that, no doubt people who require
it can obtain it from the bank from day
to day, but that would cost something.
They would be exposed, I think, to
undue expense. I do not think that
ought to be allowed. I think we should
provide against that.
There remains another question, and
that is where one of these depositors may
be executor of the deceased or expects
to be the administrator. Money is
urgently and immediately required for
death duties before he can get probate.
In that case I imagine there would not
be serious difficulty. What the Clause
is aimed at is not so much the prevention
of the money being paid over as it is
the prevention of fraud owing to the
non-disclosure of the assets. I think
that is what is wanted. I think there
would probably be no serious difficulty
as regards death duties, but I should like
to have the door of the clock opened to
enable us to see the wheels go round. I
think that exhausts the difficulties that
occur to me in regard to that part of the
Clause.
I now pass to Clause 9. I do not think
I have anything more to do than to ask
for an assurance. When we look at the
end of the sixth sub-clause, which deals
with the conditions under which particulars obtained are to be available for
the hearing of an appeal against valuation, it seems to me that we want to be
assured that there is going to be what
I call reciprocity of disclosure. That is
to say, that neither the valuator nor the
Teachers' Superannuation Bill- 568
Finance Bill-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
valuatee should be able to spring
suddenly on the other when the appeal is
at hearing, the particulars which have
been collected. If the valuator is going
to rely upon the comparative values of
houses at No. 16 and 17 High Street the
valuatee ought to be so informed before
his case is heard. Similarly, if the
valuatee is going to rely on the valuation
of Nos. 19 and 20 Main Street he should
disclose that to the valuator. I believe
the Clauses are so worded that that can
be perfectly well done, but I think we
ought to be assured that something will
be done by regulation.
Speaking of the regulations reminds
me that the regulations to be made under
this Bill are of the class that ought to be
laid before Parliament. If we decide to
make any other Amendment to the Bill
I would suggest that that should be
inserted, but if we do not make any other
Amendment I do not think it would be
worth while sending the Bill back to
another place just on account of that,
more especially as the Bill has passed
another place, and it is, perhaps, rather
more for them than for us to draw attention to the fact that the regulations ought
to be laid before Parliament.
I should like an assurance as regards
Clause 9. I await with interest what
will be said in regard to Clause 4, subclause 1, because it seems to me in
default of some satisfactory assurance on
the point I have mentioned we ought to
insert an Amendment, and I should submit, of course, that an Amendment would
be completely within the powers of this
House for the reasons which I have stated
previously.
Mr. LONG: With reference to Clause
7, to which Senator Samuel Cunningham referred, this Clause is exactly as it
appears in the United Kingdom legislation. The alteration of the word "may"
for "shall" would tie the hands of the
Estate Duty Office in exercising their
legal functions in a way which would be
undesirable, and for which no precedent
could be found.
With regard to the question of the
release of moneys urgently required, no
more difficulty arises here than arises in
connection with a current bank account
as distinct from a deposit account. The
banker cannot, for example, release
cannot,
moneys held on current account in the
name of a deceased husband even though
the wife may be urgently in need. To
meet, however, the special circumstances
of deposit receipts it will be observed that
the Bill provides, in special cases, where
the Ministry is not yet in a position to
issue a proper certificate to the banker,
that it can issue a consent to the release
of the money to the sole survivor. I can
give the hon. Senator an assurance that
the Estate Duty Office will ensure that
there is no undue delay.
Where the money is held in trust, as
soon as this is established to the satisfaction of the Estate Duty Office, a certificate can at once be issued by it to the
bank that no estate duty is payable, and
the money is thereby released.
With regard to the regulations to be
framed under Clause 9, it is intended to
provide that there shall be reciprocity
between the appellant and the Commissioner of Valuation as regards the disclosure of the cases to be cited in support
of each party's contention. The regulations will provide for their disclosure by
each party in ample time for the court
hearing.
As to the regulations to which the hon.
and learned Senator has referred, I shall
consult the Government on that matter,
and inform him of the result at a later
period.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Teachers' Superannuation Bill.

to stand part of the Bill.

The Government now have the proposed modifications before them and will
not, I anticipate, find much difficulty in
accepting the greater part, if not all of
them. It will, however, be impossible
for the Bill to become law this Session,
and it is proposed to introduce it afresh
in the coming Session in a form which it
is hoped will be satisfactory to all
parties.

Ancient Monuments Bill [S.].
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Subsequent to the Second Reading a discussion took place between a subcommittee of the Ancient Monuments
Advisory Committee and
Committee and those hon.
Members who had voiced objections to
certain provisions of the Bill. I am glad
to say that this exchange of views
indicated that there ought to be little
difficulty in amending the Bill in certain
points in order to meet all possible objections which might be raised as to its being
in any respect too drastic in character.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move. "That this House do now
adjourn." There are various Bills we
can go on with to-morrow. I should like
if possible to take the Committee stage
of the Unemployment Insurance Bill on
Thursday if that would not be too soon.
I do not think it is possible to alter it
very considerably. If we do not take it
on Thursday I am afraid it will mean
a sitting on Monday.
Mr. LESLIE: I have not quite got
the proposal of the noble Viscount.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I propose
to take the further stages of the Finance
Bill to-morrow.
Mr. LESLIE: The further stage of
the Unemployment Insurance Bill?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like the Unemployment Insurance Bill
to be taken on Thursday.
Mr. LESLIE: You are taking everything else to-morrow. Do not take me
as objecting. It is not likely there will
be any Amendment made in the Committee stage of the Finance Bill which
would interfere with taking the Report
stage almost at once. Any amendment
to that Bill would be a very short one.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
we can take the Committee stage of the
Unemployment Insurance Bill on Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
half-past Four o'clock.

Wednesday, 4th November, 1936.
THE SENATE
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Adair's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That the promoters of
Adair's Divorce Bill, which has been
introduced into this House in the present
Session of Parliament, shall have leave
to introduce the same in the next Session
of Parliament, provided that notice of
their intention to do so be lodged in the
Library not later than three o'clock on
Tuesday, March the 2nd, and that all
fees due thereon, up to that period, be
paid.
"That the Bill shall be deposited in the
Library not later than three o'clock on
or before the third day on which the
House shall sit after the next meeting of
Parliament, with a declaration annexed
thereto, signed by the agent, stating that
the Bill is the same in every respect as
the Bill at the last stage of the proceedings thereon in this House in the present
Session.
"That the proceedings on the Bill shall
be pro forma only in regard to the stage
through which the same has passed in
the present Session; and that no new
fees be charged in regard to this stage."
-(Mr. Long.)

Mr. LONG: This is a Money Bill, and
I will be brief in my explanation of its
Hon. Members will recolprovisions.
lect that, under the new Unemployment

excess expenditure in Northern Ireland

on unemployment insurance.
of the introduction of this arrangement
Unemployment Insurance Bill

the Capital Fund, which was being

the fund, has been released.
Clause 1 provides for the payment into
the Exchequer of the interest from the
Capital Fund thus released, and that the
Capital Fund itself will be held available for the purposes set out in subclause (1). Clause 2 extends the period
of repayment of the compensation levy
paid by the licensed vintners under the
Intoxicating Liquor Act. Hon. Members will recollect that the intoxicating
liquor legislation of 1923 abolished spirit
grocers' licences, and that compensation
to the holders of these licences
effected by payments from the Intoxicating Liquor Claims Fund, which
granted loans for the purpose from the
Government Loans Fund. Repayment
of the moneys advanced is being made
by means of the compensation levy
collected from the remaining spirit
licence holders together with the annual
licence duty.

Unemployment Insurance Bill.

CLAUSES 1 (Persons to be insured

age for entry into insurance) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
573 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 4 NOVEMBER 1936

Committee
CLAUSE 3 (Insurable employment).
Mr. LONG: In reply to the hon.
Senator (Mr. McLaughlin) who, on the
Motion for the Second Reading of the
Bill, raised a point as to the effect of the
provisions of sub-section (4) of Section 3,
I should like to point out that as this
Bill is purely a Consolidation Bill these
provisions do not make any change in the
existing law. Sub-section (4), in fact,
merely reproduces the provisions of
Section 15 of the Unemployment Insurance Act, 1928.
If, therefore, in any case coming
within the ambit of these provisions, the
circumstances of the employment are such
that unemployment insurance contributions were properly payable under the
Act of 1928, then contributions will still
be payable in those circumstances under
this Act. The hon. Senator will see that
the question of whether or not a contribution is properly payable depends on two
considerations, first, whether the worker
is a person who previously has been in
receipt of unemployment benefit, and
secondly, whether he is employed to the
same extent on the work scheme provided
by the local authority as if the work
were being carried out otherwise than
under such an arrangement as the subsection contemplates. Obviously, therefore, it is not possible to make a general
pronouncement as to whether employment in any particular case is insurable
until the circumstances have been
investigated.
I should, perhaps, make it clear that
these provisions do not in any way affect
employment on schemes other than work
schemes provided by local authorities in
consequence of arrangements made by
poor law authorities on a basis which
provides for a contribution by the poor
law authority towards the remuneration
of men employed on the scheme.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Thank you.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Bill reported, without amendment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
Report be now received."
Mr. LESLIE: We have now reached
the Report stage of the third of the Consolidation Bills presented to this House,
and I do not think that we ought to pass
from the matter without expressing our
sense of the extremely able manner in
which these three very complicated Bills
have been presented to us. These Bills
represent the culmination of months of
work, and had they not been presented
in the form which they have taken I think
that the duty of this House in considering
them would have been extremely arduous.
As it was, our duties, as you have just
seen, have been very simple.
One would not like it to go out to the
public that so serious an undertaking as
the consolidation of these three important
codes had passed entirely without notice
in this House. It has not been necessary
for us to say anything on our Committee
stage, because the Bills were before a
Joint Committee of both Houses before
they came to us. The fact remains that
we ought to recognise, and express the
hope that people outside who know much
less about it will also recognise that a very
important work has been very adequately
performed.
On question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be read the
Third time to-morrow.

The CHAIRMAN: With reference to
the Amendment standing in the name of
Senator Leslie, I have carefully considered this Amendment. This House
has always hesitated to amend a Bill
which was introduced in another place on
a Financial Resolution, and this particular
Bill, being a Finance Bill, was introduced
on a Ways and Means Resolution, which
proves that, in the opinion of those who
sit in another place, this Bill deals with
matters of taxation. It is true that this
particular Clause does not impose taxation, but it certainly deals with the
machinery of taxation in that it contains.
certain provisions to prevent the evasion.
of the payment of taxes. Taxation and
methods of recovering taxes are so closely
connected that it would be impracticable
for this House to attempt to make a distinction between them. It is, therefore,
my opinion that it would be unwise for
this House to proceed with the Amendment, and I must rule accordingly.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not presume for a
moment to question your Lordship's
decision in any way, but may I ask
whether you will permit us to discuss the
Clause with a view to the possible excision
of the entire Clause?
The CHAIRMAN: The Clause may be
discussed, but not amended.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that, perhaps,
it would be better to make some observations on the general subject, apart, of
course, from what is now before us, on the
Motion for the Adjournment. I am sure
that that is in accordance with your
Lordship's wishes, and, I think, the
wishes of the whole Committee.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
