Motion for Adjournment.
PROCEDURE ON FINANCE BILLS.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn." If hon. Members have no objection it is proposed to
take the remaining stages of the Employment of Women and Young Persons Bill
to-morrow. If this is not done it will
mean that a more or less formal meeting
of the House will be necessary either on
Friday or Monday.
Mr. LESLIE: First of all I should
like to support the Parliamentary Secretary's suggestion that the remaining
stages of the Employment of Women and
Young Persons Bill should be taken
to-morrow. We have studied the Bill
rather carefully since it was introduced
and, speaking entirely for myself, I cannot think that any amendment of any
consequence is likely to be proposed.
Procedure of Finance Bills 584
Adjournment-
THE SENATE
more
[Mr. Leslie]
Passing to the other question, as your
Lordship will remember, the somewhat
difficult question of our relations
on financial matters with the other
House has been before us
than once. It is usually sprung
upon us at very short notice, and
I cannot but think that we have reached
a position where we have to approach the
consideration of such matters without due
consideration for your Lordship, and
perhaps without due regard to the convenience of the Members of the House.
The situation is distinctly involved. We
all know what is said in the Government
of Ireland Act. We have had the advantage of remarks from your Lordship on
one or two different occasions, but I think
that it would be better, if possible, that
something should be done either by
Resolution, perhaps after the matter had
been considered by a Joint Committee of
both Houses, or by the alteration of our
Standing Orders, or something which
would make it a little clearer than it is
at present to the ordinary Member what
Bills are to be regarded as outside the
ambit of this House, and what enactments in those Bills, and what are not.
I need scarcely say-and I need not
remind your Lordship-that you are as
jealous of the duties and privileges of this
House as any other Member, but I may,
perhaps, mention, if only for the consideration of the newer Members of this
Chamber, that we have, as it seems to me,
a distinct duty to see that our complacence and strict intention to conform
very fully to the terms of the Government
of Ireland Act does not render itself
liable to abuse, and that the practice of
what is known, or was known, across the
water as tacking in connection with
Finance Bills is not allowed to show its
head. Of course, we shall have casesand I think, probably, the particular
instance we had this afternoon was as
good as any-where the Clause deals not
so much with the corpus of the tax
but with the trimmings and frills, and
there must always be some slight
difference of opinion at all events as to
whether the particular frilling in question.
ought or ought not to render it necessary
for us to keep silence.
Then again, we have to remember that
if we put a Bill, be it called a Finance
Bill or whatever it may be called, into
Committee, there is a distinct suggestion
that we regard the Bill as one open to
amendment as well as to discussion. I
am inclined to think that, subject to your
Lordship's considered opinion, perhaps
we might do something to make things
a little clearer than they are at present,
and I may venture to remind the House
again, particularly the newer Members,
that some twelve or thirteen years
ago I made a somewhat similar suggestion, having in my mind the idea that
we might produce over here something
analogous to the Speaker's Certificate
brought in across the water as a
consequence of financial changes which
came about subsequent to the passing of
the Parliament Act of 1911. Such a
certificate is given, I believe, after careful consideration, by the Speaker of their
House of Commons, and I believe that
there is something in the nature of a
committee for consultation with, if not
supervision over, the Speaker of the
House of Commons.
Of course, that would not be a matter
that concerns us, but there is an arrangement in force there, as I understand,
which makes the duty of the Chairman
of Committees of the House of Lords
easier, and gives him much more time for
consideration than is allotted to your
Lordship at present. I throw out the
suggestion that something should be done
to try to reduce to black and white an
arrangement to solve these little difficulties which, from time to time, come before
us-difficulties which have been invariably
smoothed over without any difficulty or
resentment on the part of anybody.
Mr. LONG: I shall have pleasure in
conveying the views of the hon. and
learned Senator to the Government.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter before Four
o'clock.
585 Extermination of Rats

5 NOVEMBER 1936
Extermination of Rats
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 5th November, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Suspension of Standing Order.
Standing
Order
Resolved, "That Standing
No. XXV be suspended for this day's
sitting for the purpose of taking the remaining stages of the Employment of
Women and Young Persons Bill."-(Mr.
Long.)
Question to Minister.
EXTERMINATION OF RATS AND
MICE.
Mr. Samuel CUNNINGHAM: I beg
to ask the Leader of the House whether
he will make representations to the
Minister of Agriculture with a view to
his Department co-operating with the
Local Authorities in a combined effort
for the extermination of rats and mice.
I ask this question because I see that
this week the Minister of Agriculture in
England has asked the 750 representatives
of the 750 local authorities in England
and Wales to have a concerted attack
upon rats.
It has been found that an
attack upon them in one district is of
very little use, and they want if possible.
to wipe them out. I am asking the
question in the hope that the Minister
of Agriculture here will undertake to
have during this winter
throughout the Six Counties on these
vermin.
an attack
We here are trying to raise what money
we can and when we consider the amount
of goods lost to the State-I cannot get
the figures for Northern Ireland, but I
think a low estimate for England is that
these vermin destroy £1,000,000 worth
of stuff each week-I think it would be
well worth the Government's while to do
something to try to eradicate or reduce
them. I also noticed in the papers today that in the House of Lords they have
appointed a Commission to deal with the
pest of rabbits. They are beginning to
be very destructive and for the same
reason they are not very much used for
food-I think the sooner they are
smoked out of the country the better.
Just before leaving this subject I
would also draw the Minister's attention
to the amount of damage done in this
country by the wood pigeon. I believe
that these birds come over from the
Continent, principally from Germany,
and come to us in the winter, and we
know what they cost us in our green
crops, and the amount of food they eat.
I notice in one of the papers that someone had introduced the squirrel dormouse,
and it increased so much in the Midlands
of England that they had to have a
crusade against it and it was reduced.
But I see it is increasing again. I think
that our mice are just as bad, especially
the field mouse, as this dormouse can be
in England, and I ask the question in
the hope that something will be arranged
during the winter to have a concerted
attack to reduce their numbers.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to support
the right hon. Gentleman in regard to
the question of the extermination of rats
and mice throughout the country. I agree
with what he says as to the amount of
damage done year after year by rats.
During the summer I was in the country,
and inquired from some of the farmers
if they had many rats. I was told that
at the present time rats are very common
and have almost taken possession of some
of the farm premises. On asking the
reason, I was told that owing to the
extension of the pig industry there is
more food for rats. Poultry also bring
rats. I inquired if anything was being
done to get rid of the rats, and they
said, "Oh, well, when we get one we
kill it," and that is all. I think the
Government, through the Ministry of
Agriculture, should get in touch with
the county councils and district councils
and try to impress on these farmers the
necessity for exterminating the rats.
necessary the Government should give a
small grant to those who take in hand
the extermination of rats, so that we
might get rid of these pests, which have
really taken possession of the country
districts at the present time. I have
pleasure in supporting the right hon.
Gentleman on this question.
If
587 Women & Young Persons Bill
Bills Read the Third Time 588
THE SENATE
Mr. LONG: As the right hon. Senator
is probably aware, the Rats and Mice
(Destruction) Act, 1919, confers powers
upon Local Authorities to secure the destruction of rats and mice. The Act
further confers power on the Ministry of
Home Affairs to take action if a Local
Authority fails to carry out its duties
under the Act.
The Ministry of Agriculture has no
powers whatever under the Act, but I
understand that the Ministry would be
happy to afford such help as might be
practicable to Local Authorities when
exercising their functions under the Act.
I understand that no representations
have been made in Northern Ireland as
to the destruction of these pests. Should
such representations be made the Ministry of Agriculture will, of course, be
glad to advise the local authorities in
the organisation of a rat week or other
method of destruction. I shall bring
the matter to the notice of the Ministry
of Agriculture.

Prorogation of Parliament.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have it in command from his Grace the Governor to
read forthwith in this Chamber to the
Senators and Commons of Northern
Ireland therein assembled his Grace's
Proclamation proroguing this present
Session of Parliament.
Moved: "That the Gentleman Usher
of the Black Rod be commanded to inform the Commons that the Proclamation
proroguing the present Session of Parliament is to be read forthwith, and inviting the Commons to attend in order to
hear the same."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
was
The Commons, being at the Bar, with
their Speaker, the Proclamation
read to both Houses of Parliament by
the Speaker of the Senate (in pursuance of his Grace's Command), as
followeth :-
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR
OF NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
For Proroguing the Present Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance
of certain enactments amending the said
Act, that the Governor of Northern
Ireland shall, in His Majesty's name,
summon, prorogue and dissolve the
Parliament of Northern Ireland:
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that
this present Parliament of Northern
Ireland should be prorogued to the
twenty-fourth day of November, one
thousand nine hundred and thirty-six :
Now I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD
Duke of Abercorn, Knight of the Most
Prorogation
End of Session
12 NOVEMBER 1936
Noble Order of the Garter, Knight of
the Most Illustrious Order of Saint
Patrick, Governor of Northern Ireland,
by this Proclamation do publish and
declare His Majesty's Pleasure that the
said Parliament be, as from the reading
of this Proclamation in the Parliament
of Northern Ireland, prorogued to Tuesday, the twenty-fourth day of November,
one thousand nine hundred and thirty-six.
AND I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to
be that the said Parliament shall on
the said twenty-fourth day of November,
one thousand nine hundred and thirtysix, assemble and be holden at Stormont
for the despatch of divers urgent and
important affairs. and the Senators and
Members of the House of Commons of
Northern Ireland are hereby required to
give their attendance accordingly.
GIVEN this twelfth day of November,
one thousand nine hundred and
thirty-six.
(Signed by his own hand)
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the Third Session (opened

Tuesday, 24th November, 1936.
The Fourth Session of the Fourth
Parliament of Northern Ireland appointed
to meet at Stormont on 24th November,
1936, in the First year of the Reign of
King Edward VIII, was opened by the
Governor (His Grace the Duke of
Abercorn, K.G., K.P.) in person.
The House met at Twelve noon of the
Clock, Mr. SPEAKER (The Right Hon.
Viscount Bangor, O.B.E., D.L.) in the
Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor, being seated.
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is his Grace's
pleasure that they attend him immediately in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, his Grace delivered the
following speech from the Throne: --
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
The coming year will be rendered
memorable by the Celebrations solemnising the Coronation of King Edward
the Eighth. I am confident that in no
part of his Majesty's far flung Empire
will greater loyalty and devotion be
exhibited than here in Northern
Ireland.
I am pleased to learn that the
improvement which has been evident
in our industrial position for some time
past has been well maintained throughout 1936. While last year I observed
that the increase in exports of linen
goods was largley attributable to the
development of Dominion markets, I
am glad this year to note that a further
expansion has been experienced in
foreign markets.
The aggregate tonnage of vessels
launched from our shipyards during
the past twelve months did not reach
the high level attained in 1935, but I
rejoice to learn that, taking that period
as a whole, the volume of employment
has been substantially maintained.
I am gratified that prospects of
further activity in the shipyards are
promising, and that agriculture and the
linen industry are exhibiting signs of
considerable improvement in their
respective spheres. Favourable conditions in our three primary industries
will be reflected in all other undertakings and tend to increase the
purchasing power of the community.
It is also a source of great satisfaction
to me to know that in the near future
A
King's Speech
King's Speech
THE SENATE
the manufacture of aircraft is to be
established in our midst, and I have
every reason to believe that the products
of this new important enterprise will
gain a reputation equally as high as
that enjoyed by other manufactures
of Northern Ireland.
The number of persons registering
at the Employment Exchanges has
fallen by almost five thousand since
the beginning of the year, and it is
encouraging to find that our principal
industries continue to provide employment for increased numbers. On the
average over ten thousand more persons
are being employed in the insured
industries than twelve months ago, and
over twenty-five thousand more than
five years ago. As a result, there are
substantial increases in the contribution income of Unemployment, Health
Insurance, and Pensions Funds respectively.
Two extensions of the Social Services
call for my special mention. The scheme
for including agricultural workers in
Unemployment Insurance, which was
sanctioned last Session, has been inaugurated smoothly with a general measure
of acceptance. The Unemployment
Assistance Scheme contemplated by the
Act of 1934 will be completed during
the currency of this Session by the
enlargement of its scope from the 1st
April next to include all persons
employed in occupations insurable
under the Contributory Pensions
Act.
In order to make further financial
provision to relieve Local Authorities
of the burden left with them until
that date, a Measure will be introduced
continuing for the present financial
year the temporary assistance which
has been afforded to them since 1934
by way of Government grants, and you
will also be invited to consider proposals providing from 1st April next
for the amount of contribution to be
paid by the Local Authorities to the
Unemployment Assistance Fund.
The statutory date fixed for holding
Local Government Elections falls within
the period next year when preparations
will be far advanced in connection with
the Coronation Celebrations.
advised that difficulties would inevitably result from the holding of Elections
I am
at such a time, and it is considered
by my Ministers that it would be
fitting that existing members of the
Local Authorities, upon whom will
fall the responsibility for initiating
various schemes in that connection,
should not have to vacate office on the
eve of bringing them to successful
fruition. A Measure will, therefore, be
laid before you enabling Local Govern
ment Elections to be postponed to a
more convenient date subsequent to the
period of the Coronation Celebrations.
You will be invited to consider proposals empowering Local Authorities to
regulate and control the exhibition of
advertisements.
Legislation will be introduced to
amend and consolidate the law regulating conditions of employment in
factories and workshops; to enable blind
persons to receive pensions at a lower
age than at present; to make medical
treatment available for young persons
below the age of sixteen who have
entered employment; and to establish
a voluntary scheme, on a contributory
basis, to provide pensions for persons
of limited means.
Legislation will be submitted for
your consideration amending the Horse
Breeding Act, 1926, and the Measure
which was introduced last Session for
improving the quality of ice-cream sold
in Northern Ireland and for ensuring
that it is manufactured and sold only
under hygienic conditions will be laid
before you during the forthcoming
Session.
It was found impracticable last
Session to proceed with the Bills
dealing with Miscellaneous Law Reform
and the principles of Arbitration, consequently these matters will be brought
forward for your consideration.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Estimates for the Public Services
will be laid before you in due course.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I rejoice to learn that conditions in
regard to law and order are satisfactory,
and I am confident that it will be the
desire of all sections of the community
that this happy state of affairs should
continue.
Papers Presented
Debate on the Address
24 NOVEMBER 1936
I commend the steps taken by my
Government in tendering every assistance to the Departments concerned
with National Defence.
I am pleased to observe that the
several Measures which have been
passed during the last few years for
the purpose of improving generally the
marketing of agricultural produce and
regulating the prices paid to farmers
are meeting with continued success.
It is to be hoped that Local Authorities will take full opportunity of the
facilities afforded by the Act passed
last year in regard to labourers' cottages.
It is a matter of gratification to me
to know that substantial progress continues to be made in the development
of the tourist traffic industry, which is
year by year increasing in importance,
and that satisfactory results continue to
be achieved by the campaign to stimulate within Northern Ireland the
demand for goods produced at home
and in the Empire overseas. The two
voluntary organisations carrying out
these important services are to be congratulated on the success which has
attended their efforts.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your deliberations.
Then his Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.

Laid before the House (pursuant to

Adair's Divorce Bill.
The Speaker laid upon the Table a certificate that the Regulations embodied in
the Resolution of the Senate of the fourth
day of November had been complied with
in respect of the following Bill:-
Adair's Divorce.
Address in Reply to His Majesty's
Most Gracious Speech.
Mr SPEAKER having read the King's
Speech,
Sir EMERSON HERDMAN (in
morning dress): I beg to move, "That
an humble Address be presented to His
Grace in the following words: -
"Your Grace,
"We, His Majesty's most dutiful
and loyal subjects, the Senate of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to your
Grace for the Speech which your Grace
has addressed to both Houses of Parliament."
I feel greatly honoured indeed by
having been asked to propose a humble
Address of thanks for the Gracious
Speech which we heard read this morning, and especially by having the privilege of proposing it on this occasion of
the opening of what will be our Corona-
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address

THE SENATE
[Sir Emerson Herdman]
tion Parliament. It will be indeed a
memorable year and a year in which we
hope to welcome His Majesty over
to Ulster with the same fervour of loyalty
as we welcomed him when he was Prince
of Wales and when he opened this noble
building which we are in at present.
But that honour on my part is greatly
mixed with awe that I, whose attempts
at oratory are generally made on a day
in July and generally in a wind-swept
field where nobody listens to what one
says, and on occasions when oratory comes
very far down in the list of entertainments, should have to make a speech
before such a galaxy of orators as
we have on both the Government and
Opposition side of the House. I hope
they will bear with patience my feeble
efforts. For one thing, I intend to be
very short.
I do not think the most
somnolent Senator amongst us will have
to settle down to his forty winks before
I have stopped.
The beginning of His Grace the
Governor's Speech strikes a very welcome note, namely, the improvement in
the linen trade and the increased activity
in shipbuilding, the two basic foundations
of prosperity in Northern Ireland. I.
myself, can endorse what His Grace said
about the linen trade. I happen to be
engaged in one end of it, namely, the
spinning of yarns.
Never was that end
as busy or on as stable a foundation as
it is at the present moment. We would
most sincerely like to thank the Ministry
of Commerce for all they have done for
us in the difficult and most delicate
negotiations in regard to quotas and
permits which are now absolutely vital
to all exporters of varns to the various
countries. No trouble was too much for
them. They have greatly helned the export trade. and if other Government
Departments do as well as the Denartment of Commerce-and I am sure thev
do we ought to be very proud indeed
of them and of such a competent Minister and such an excellent staff.
The next good news I notice is we are
going to have an aircraft factory. We
all know that during the war the aeroplanes of ourselves and our allies were
mostly clothed with Ulster linen. Therefore, I think that when we have an aeroplane factory of our own in which we
can build aeroplanes that are not only
winged with Ulster linen but made with
Ulster hands we ought to be second to
none in the air.
Touching upon unemployment, the
figures that have been quoted in the
Gracious Speech, namely, 10,000 more
workers employed than there were a year
ago, and the vast number of 25,000 more
at work than there were five years ago,
speak for themselves, and long may they
continue to do so. I always considered
that it was a hardship on our agricultural
workers that they were not included in
the unemployment insurance scheme.
That has now been remedied, and a great
boon has thereby been conferred on the
agricultural labourer. We all know that
farming is the backbone of our Province,
and the agricultural labourer is the backbone of farming. Therefore, it is a
matter for satisfaction that the injustice
under which he has suffered in the past
has now been removed in this respect.
It will also be a source of gratification
to those in Ulster who are unfortunate
enough to be afflicted with blindness to
know that our Government are keeping
step by step with the Imperial Government in doing all they can to alleviate
further their unfortunate circumstances.
I am also glad to see that the Government have not forgotten that noble
animal the horse, which has always been
my most faithful friend and companion
ever since I could get on one, and will,
I hope, continue to be as long as I can
still ride one. Horse breeding is now a
very important industry in Northern
Ireland, and despite the better land in
the South and the earlier start of our
friends there we are going rapidly ahead,
and the biggest dealers by far in this
commodity are now, I am pleased to
state, sons of Ulster. We can not only
breed horses, but we can also breed riders
to ride them. Before the war we used
to have an annual point-to-point race
against the South Irish Horse, and at
first they were inclined to think we were
rather easy prey, but when we beat them
in three successive years they changed
their opinion of us. I shall always
remember the occasion when an Ulsterman won the National Hunt Cup at
Punchestown, and if you had heard the
hon. Senator on my left (Mr. Porter-
Porter) telling his fellow members of the
Kildare Street Club what he thought of
their riding abilities you would have
enjoyed it.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
24 NOVEMBER 1936
I think we ought to congratulate the
Minister of Home Affairs and those under
him who are responsible for the preservation of law and order in this Province
on the splendid way in which they have.
carried out their difficult and trying
work. The present peaceful state of the
country is a source of great gratification
to all the law-abiding citizens of Northern
Ireland.
Ice-cream is referred to in the Gracious
Speech. I am afraid I am not a judge
of an ice-cream cooler, but I am sure
that anything that is done to keep icecream pure and clean is all for the benefit
of our young folk and those who are
blessed with a sweet tooth. It would also
be a good thing if this trade was not
practically in the hands of foreigners,
as it is at the present time, because I am
sure it would be carried on much better
by Ulstermen.
As regards national defence, not only
is our Government doing everything
possible in this matter, but I am proud
to say that Northern Ireland still maintains its supremacy as the best recruiting
area in the United Kingdom. I sincerely
hope the War Office will not forget this
fact when allotting the extra
battalions that are talked of, and which
I hope will be established before very
long. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
new
Mr. QUIGLEY (in morning dress):
I feel highly honoured in having
been asked to second this Address,
though I know that the task could have
been placed in much better hands. However, I think it is appropriate that a
large employer of labour should move the
Address, and a representative of the
working classes second it. After what
the hon. Senator, Sir Emerson Herdman,
has so ably said, there is little if anything
new which I could add, but I should like
to make a few remarks even at the risk of
repetition.
The opening paragraph of the Gracious
Speech very fittingly refers to
to the
Coronation next year. We in Northern
Ireland are loyal subjects, devoted to the
Crown. We do not forget the visit paid
to us by the present Sovereign when he
was Prince of Wales. He opened our
Parliamentary Buildings at Stormont in
1932, and I am quite sure that the reception which he then got still lives in his
memory. There is no part of the British
Empire where his Majesty is held in
warmer affection than in Northern Ireland, and we shall join in the Coronation
celebrations with characteristic fervour
and enthusiasm. I can hardly picture to
myself any section of the community
ignoring the celebrations. We have a
great democratic country.
governed by dictators. It is a travesty
of truth to say that law-abiding citizens
are subject to oppressive measures, and
surely, with such freedom and liberty as
we now enjoy, we should all gather round
the Throne as the centre and pivot of our
Constitution.
We are not
I should like to offer a word of congratulation to the Ulster Government
upon the way in which peace has been
preserved. Without peace and good order
we cannot be happy and prosperous. Unfortunately, there are people who create
trouble and set class against class, but
thanks to the firmness of the Government
and the vigilance of our police force, their
criminal activities have been suppressed.
We know what has occurred in the past,
and we hope that no revolutionary organisation will ever again be allowed to gain
a footing in our midst. As Mr. Justice
Megaw recently said, "It would be unwise to ignore the danger from such
organisations. However impotent they
may be at present, it would be idle to close
our eyes to the fact that such exist."
National defence is also a matter that
calls for close and immediate attention.
Not only do we want peace at home, but
we desire peace abroad. Unfortunately
we see nations arming to the teeth; ready
to break into warfare. We cannot sit idly
by as if all were well. The League of
Nations, in which so many of us placed
absolute confidence, has been unable to
stop aggression, and so we must take steps
to defend ourselves in case of attack.
Belfast is not invulnerable, and if well
equipped aeroplanes dropped bombs and
other destructive missiles in the city we
might suffer severely in life and property. Therefore, it would be very
desirable if an aerodrome were constructed
on the Belfast Harbour Commissioners'
estate and used as a base for our Royal
Air Force.
The development of the tourist traffic
of the Province, to which reference is
made in the Gracious Speech, is a natural
and satisfactory outcome of the efforts
which have been made for a number of
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
years by organisations to popularise
Ulster as a tourist centre. In Northern
Ireland we can offer the holiday-maker
from across the water attractions which
are not to be excelled in any part of
the British Isles or even in Europe. We
have here practically every form of
nature unspoiled by the artificiality
which characterises many holiday resorts
in other places, and experience has
shown that the beauty and natural charm
of our country only requires to be
brought to the notice of those in Great
Britain and overseas to be appreciated
and availed of. Every succeeding year
more and more tourists are landing on
our shores, and it only remains for those
who are more directly concerned with
tourist traffic, and particularly hotel and
boarding-house keepers, to see that
adequate arrangements are made for the
comfort of visitors, and that the accommodation provided for them leaves no
room for complaint. I think a few more
up-to-date hotels would be a paying
proposition.
the
It is a matter for universal satisfaction
that the number of persons registering at
the employment exchanges has decreased.
by almost five thousand since
beginning of the year, and that in this
respect also we have kept step with Great
Britain. As the Gracious Speech indicates there are unmistakable signs that
the wave of prosperity for which we have
been so long and patiently waiting has
at last reached our shores, and it is fervently to be hoped that it has come to
stay. There are still many unemployed
in our midst, and until industry is in a
position to absorb them all, it is the duty
of the State to make at least some provision for them. In this respect our
Government can claim to have carried
out its obligations to the letter in the
past. Whatever sins of commission or
omission may be laid to the account of
the Northern Government, the neglect of
the unemployed is not one of them.
Although it has entailed a tremendous
drain on our limited financial resources,
we have resolutely treated our unemploved in exactly the same way as the
unemploved in Great Britain have been
dealt with. They have all along enioved
the same benefits, and they have suffered
no disabilities in comparison. What this
means to the finances of the Province
may be gauged by the fact that at the
present time no less a sum than
£5,600,000 is being paid annually on
social services. That is exclusive of cost
of administration.
The steady improvement which has
taken place in the industrial position of
the workers is strikingly illustrated by
the fact that in 1931, when I had the
honour of seconding the Address, the
average number of insured persons in
employment between the ages of 16 and
64 was
some 178,150, whereas for the
corresponding period this year the number was 203,650, not including agricultural workers. The inclusion of this
class of worker in the insurance scheme
was a step in the right direction, and I
hope that before very long the Government will be able to go a step further
and embrace within the scheme that very
deserving army of what are called blackcoated workers-clerks and such likewhose means do not permit them to make
provision for themselves and their wives
and families when they are deprived of
employment.
Those
The new regulations which have just
been approved by Parliament governing
the assessment of needs in connection
with unemployment benefit should prove
a great boon and will abolish many of
the hardships that have inevitably prevailed in the administration of the regulations in the past. In future the needs
of the unemploved will be assessed on a
considerably more generous scale, and
although the means test has not been
abolished it will not be calculated on the
same basis as formerly. For instance, in
assessing the income of a family, more
generous allowances will be made to the
earning members of the family.
who have criticised the means test and
clamoured for its total abolition have not
been acting in the best interests of the
unemploved themselves, although they
speak as if they were. They overlook the
fact that a large proportion of the money
available for payment to the unemploved
is directly contributed by the workers
themselves, and some sort of means test
is obviously necessary for the protection
of the working classes. Otherwise it
might easily happen that families drawing unemployment benefit would have a
larger aggregate income than other
families who were contributing to the
scheme and not participating in its
benefits. Even the leaders of the Labour
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
24 NOVEMBER 1936
Party in Great Britain have admitted the
necessity of a means test-in fact, it was
from a Labour Government that the idea
of a means test was first mooted. Mr.
George Lansbury, speaking in the House
of Commons, said:-
"I am not prepared to give people
money year after year without knowing what is their own personal position.
That is to say, that if a person has
gone out of ordinary benefit and has
some means of his own to maintain
himself, I am not prepared to pay him
State money."
I think that puts the justification for
the means test in a nutshell, and
effectively disposes of the unthinking
criticisms levelled against it.
I notice that legislation is to be
introduced to enable blind persons to
receive pensions at a lower age. It would
be a great blessing if we could grant a
little State aid to the deaf and dumb.
Handicapped as they are their case is
deserving of the most sympathetic consideration.
As I have already said, it is pleasing
to learn of the improvement in the
industrial position in Northern Ireland,
because that naturally means a reduction
in unemployment figures. Last year our
external trade amounted in value to
over £92,000,000 as compared with
£88,500,000 during 1934. Those who
say that Ulster is down and out are very
wide of the mark. The imports and exports which passed through the Belfast
Harbour last year amounted to 3,630,000
tons, the highest figures since 1913. For
the first time in the history of the port
the tonnage of shipping cleared passed
the 4,000,000 mark. Our shipbuilding
also showed signs of improvement. Last
year two of the largest vessels launched
in the world were built on the Lagan.
Though the tonnage this year has not
reached the high level attained last year,
yet there is ground for belief that the
increased activity of the past two years
will be maintained next year.
The linen trade, which has experienced
many perplexing difficulties, is also looking up as the hon. Senator, Sir
Emerson Herdman, has told you. We
know that in former years the linen
industry depended mainly on foreign
markets to absorb its products. But
owing to the high tariff policy of the
United States of America and the continued depression in that country, our
home market has become of major importance, and it is gratifying to know
that British markets overseas have been
steadily increasing their purchases. The
result of this change has been that the
decrease in foreign trade has been compensated to no small extent by the increase in the home and Dominion
markets. I think that is a satisfactory
condition of affairs, and we may be sure
that the Government will continue to keep
a close watch on trade negotiations with
foreign countries in order to safeguard
and to further Northern Ireland's
interests wherever these may be affected.
We are told that agriculture is also
showing signs of improvement and we
are all gratified to hear that, because the
farmers in past years have had very hard
times. The various marketing schemes
introduced by
by the Government are
working smoothly and we are glad to
learn that they are proving very helpful.
All the legislation outlined in the
Gracious Speech is important and should
be for the benefit of the whole community.
In conclusion, I would like to congratulate the Government on the way in
which they have attended to our affairs.
In Lord Craigavon we have a wise and
sagacious leader who has rendered magnificent service to the Province over a
long period of years, and in the other
Members of the Cabinet we have gentlemen whose sole desire is to do everything
possible for the welfare of the people of
every class and creed. Long may they
be spared to govern Northern Ireland of
which we are all so proud. I have great
pleasure in seconding the Address.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): It is my duty first of all
to congratulate the hon. Members who
have so ably moved and seconded the
Motion. This House has had the advantage of their counsel for several years,
and has learned to listen to them with
attention and respect.
The Gracious Speech has very properly
reminded us-if we needed such reminding that the year 1937 will be chiefly
memorable as the year of the Coronation
of His Majesty King Edward VIII. I
am sure I am speaking for all the Members of this House when I express the
most ardent hope that the year may be
in every way fortunate. The reign has
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
opened with good auguries; not the least
of which is the continuous and substantial
increase in employment which has come
about as a consequence, mainly, of improvement in the three great industries
of agriculture, linen manufacture, and
shipbuilding.
I will not detain the House with a
further description of the improvement
in the economic situation of the Province;
this has been described in some detail
by the hon. Members who have moved
and seconded the Motion. We cannot
be unmindful of the fact that in some
quarters distress still lingers, but we may
be thankful that in the case of our major
industries recovery has proceeded
smoothly and so steadily during the last
few years.
SO
His Majesty's anxious interest in the
welfare of his poorest subjects is well
known. It is appropriate that the important extensions in the social services
to which the Gracious Speech makes
reference should take place early in his
reign. The inclusion of agricultural
workers in the unemployment insurance
scheme and of persons employed in
occupations insurable under the Contributory Pensions Act, in the unemployment assistance scheme; the provision of
pensions at an earlier age for blind
persons; the provision for the medical
treatment of young persons below the
age of sixteen who have entered insurable
employment; the establishment of a
voluntary scheme to provide pensions for
persons of limited means, and the amendment of our factories and workshop legislation, are further stages in the progress
of that social legislation, which provides
protection against risks, and removes
fears for the future, which remain, even
to-day, part of the accepted lot of the
great majority of mankind.
I have much sympathy with the suggestions made by the hon. Member who
seconded the Motion with regard to the
extension of Unemployment Insurance
benefit to the black-coated workers and
the grant of State aid to certain of the
deaf and dumb. The hon. Member himself pointed to the heavy cost of existing
social services, and it only remains for
me to say that when the time comes that
the community is able to shoulder additianal burdens, then, I am sure, the
claims of these classes will be considered.
We may be justifiably proud that nowhere in the world has such continuous
progress been made in the provision of
social services as in the United Kingdom,
and that this progress has been effected
without civil upheaval by an elected
Parliament and with the willing cooperation of every section of the community. We owe our good fortune in
no small degree to the faith of the nation
in its Constitution and to the wisdom
and love for its people of the Reigning
House.
It may
be of interest to hon. Members
if I give a few statistics regarding employment. On the 26th October, 1936,
approximately 209,250 persons, aged 16-
64, were employed in industries, other
than agriculture, covered by the Unemployment Insurance Scheme. This figure
is about 10,300 more than the figure for
the corresponding date 12 months previously. The increase in employment
between the

years
and 1936 is
approximately 14.3 per cent.
The scheme for the insurance of agricultural workers against unemployment
came into operation on the 4th May last,
and benefits under this scheme commenced to be paid on the 5th November. Up to the present date unemployment books have been issued in respect
of 24,670 workers. It should be understood that this figure represents only a
proportion of the total number of persons
employed on agricultural work, because
farmers and their immediate relatives
are by the Act excluded from insurance
under the scheme.
On 26th October, 1936, the total
number of persons registered at the
Employment Exchanges in Northern
Ireland as unemployed was 65,317, comprising 59,953 persons wholly unemployed, 3,070 persons temporarily
stopped, and 2,294 persons normally in
casual employment. The total figure is
595 less than the number on the Unemployment Register on the 21st October,
1935. A substantial improvement has
been recorded in particular in the linen
industry, where the number of unemploved persons has fallen from 16,275 to
11.087 during the year in question.
The orderly progress of the nation, the
preservation of our individual liberties,
and the maintenance of our peaceful
relationship with other countries, depend
on our willingness and ability to resist
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
24 NOVEMBER 1936
aggression. I am sure that we welcome,
therefore, the allusion in the Gracious
Speech to the readiness with which the
Government has co-operated with the
Defence Departments, and welcome also
-not only because it will provide employment, but equally because the skill
and loyalty of the workers will be available as another link in our defencesthe announcement that an aircraft
factory is to be built in this Province.
I join with the hon. Members who
have moved and seconded the Motion in
their tribute to my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Home Affairs, on the
maintenance of order in the Province,
and I would add also my tribute to the
public spirit of those citizens of conflicting shades of political thought who have
done their part in this work.
This House, which has always shown
a lively interest in the preservation of
the amenities of the countryside, will
welcome, I am sure, the announcement
that proposals to regulate the exhibition
of advertisements are to
to be brought
before Parliament.
I should like to endorse the remarks
of the hon. Member who seconded the
Motion on the good work done by the
Ulster Tourist Development Association.
I agree with him that more modern wellequipped hotels are needed. For some
years past the hotel accommodation in
Northern Ireland has been steadily improving, but the amount of first and
second-class hotel accommodation remains insufficient to satisfy the rapidly
increasing demand. Catering for holiday makers and tourists is now a major
industry in Ulster and there is room, I
believe, for considerable expansion in the
facilities which are available to
visitors.
our
We are glad to see that mention is also
made in the Gracious Speech of the fine
work done by the Ulster Industries
Development Association, which maintains its efforts to keep the public in
mind of the wide range of goods manufactured in the Province.
By the proposed amendment of the
Horse Breeding Act, 1926, referred to
in the Gracious Speech, it is intended to
prevent the use of immature stallions for
breeding purposes, a practice which has
done considerable harm, and which has
grown owing to the fact that stallions
under existing legislation have not to be
registered until they are three years old,
and that their use for breeding purposes
until they have reached that age is not
subject to regulation.
I should, perhaps, say something about
the Arbitration Bill and the Law Reform
(Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill. It is
intended by these Measures to effect
certain reforms in the law which are long
overdue in Northern Ireland. They are
being framed in consultation with both
branches of the legal profession. Hon.
Members will agree that in view of the
close commercial relations existing
between this country and England that
the law relating to arbitration should be
substantially the same in both countries.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: In supporting
the Motion for the Address, I would like
in the first instance to congratulate the
mover and seconder on their speeches.
Sir Emerson Herdman, who is an expert
in the linen trade, has told us of the
assistance that important industry has
received from the Government of Northern Ireland, and of the improvement that
has taken place in the industry, in which
he is such a conspicuous figure. The
seconder gave us an exhaustive speech
on matters such as unemployment insurance, with which he is SO closely
identified.
The first reference in the Gracious
Speech is to the Coronation of King
Edward VIII, and we are all agreed
that in no
part of His Majesty's
Dominions will that great event be received with more joy and satisfaction
than in Northern Ireland. We are
delighted to know that His Majesty is
so worthily following in the footsteps of
his illustrious father, particularly in
taking such a keen interest in the welfare
of the distressed areas, as was evidenced
by his visit to South Wales last week.
It demonstrates the great personal interest which the crowned head of the
British Empire takes in his people, and
we in this loyal part of the Empire will
honour His Majesty all the more for it.
The industrial position in Northern
Ireland has undergone a great improvement, as was shown by the figures quoted
by the mover and seconder of the Motion
for the Address, and the noble Leader
of the House. Reference was made to
the three great industries of Northern
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Hill Dickson]
Ireland-shipbuilding, agriculture, and
linen, but I think they should have been
put in alphabetical order, because agriculture is the largest industry of the
three. On the prosperity of agriculture
depends the prosperity of the others, and
the more money that is made in the one
the more is distributed amongst the
others.
The establishment of the aircraft
factory that is referred to in the Gracious
Speech will create a new industry in
Belfast. We are delighted to know that
such a factory is under construction, but
we hope that the aircraft it will turn
out will be for commercial purposes, and
not for the purpose of destruction.
I think it is very desirable that some
control should be exercised over the
manufacture of ice-cream from the point
of view of hygiene, and that it should
be made from cream only. The question
of the control of advertisements was referred to by the Leader of the House.
We who are members of county councils
have had this matter before us repeatedly. The amenities of whole districts are destroyed by these advertisements, but the only power we have over
them up to the present has been to see
that they are thirty feet from the centre
of the county road. We hope that the
new legislation which will be introduced
will give us wider powers over the erection of these advertisements in the
future.
The inclusion of agricultural labourers
in the unemployment insurance scheme
is a matter for satisfaction. Agricultural labourers are as much entitled to
unemployment benefit as industrial
workers, but I am afraid it will not tend
to increase employment. Both the employers and employees will have to pay
a contribution, and the employees will
be looking for more leisure, and a larger
wage for doing nothing than when they
are employed. However, they are just
as much entitled to the dole as those
in any other industry in the world.
In connection with the marketing
schemes I think that the agriculturist
and the farming community are too ready
to complain. When anything is done for
them-when they get freedom from
rates they give no thanks. When they
get a standard price for pork they give
no thanks. When they get a standard
price for milk they want more. Of
course we are all up against this difficulty
of a higher price for products at the
present time. I think that the consumer
is paying as much for milk as he is entitled to pay, and if there was more
consumption the price fixed is quite sufficient to leave a reasonable profit. I am
delighted to have the opportunity of supporting the Address. I hope that past
legislation may be satisfactory and that
the coming legislation may continue to
be for the benefit of the people of this
country.
Captain PERCEVAL MAXWELL:
The Address has already been SO exhaustively moved, seconded, and supported that it leaves very little for anyone to say in further support, and I do
not feel disposed at the present moment
to break the general harmony of the
House by saying anything not in support
of the Address. When we look back over
the last five or six years and remember
the state in which finance and trade was
left after the great boom of 1929, ani
when we realise that during the war
millions of pounds were spent in expanding industry in a way in which industry
could never have expanded in peace time
in the same period, and when we saw
that employment fell suddenly and
steeply, and when we look at the state
of affairs to-day, then I think we may
congratulate not only the Government of
this country
but the Government of
Great Britain and our own industralists
and our own workers that the state of
affairs which we see to-day is so very
great an improvement on what it has
been in the past. When we see the state
of Europe, States crashing down
finding it impossible to change their
fortunes except by a bloody revolution,
and we
in this House and those in
another place calmly standing round and
talking about great improvements to
come about by co-operation between all
sections of political thought, then I think
we have full justification for congratulating those who have taken part in this
great change, that they have done so by
means of common sense and allowed their
prosperity to increase so much in exactly
four years.
and
We now are concentrating on keeping
what we have got. We see round us
States arming to the teeth, and we very
properly are arming to resist any possible.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
24 NOVEMBER 1936
aggression. We have had a very statesmanlike speech from the Foreign Secretary, laying down definitely the exact
circumstances in which we should be
justified in taking military or naval steps,
and I think we in this House are partly
responsible for taking initial measures to
uphold those principles which he has laid.
down. We are now expanding war
industries. We have been driven to it,
not by any wish of our own people or
by any action of our people, and it is
quite right that this country should gain
something of the money that is being
spent on these industries.
We are seeing
increased shipbuilding in preparation for
war, or for defence I should say, and we
are now going to see a further enlargement in building an aircraft factory.
There is one little item of expense
which we are not going to incur in this
country, that is, the creation of a territorial army.
The Government have some
reasons for not doing so, but personally
I cannot help feeling sorry myself that
we alone in the British Isles have no
territorials, but only the police. By not
having territorials we, I think, miss some
of the money which will eventually be
paid out to that army. Also if our sons
wish to serve they are compelled to do
their service abroad. I personally should
be very pleased if some way could be
arranged of having at least one territorial
unit in this country affiliated to one of
the great regiments which have given so
much credit to Northern Ireland in the
past.
Other things have been done which are
not mentioned in the Gracious Speech.
Art in industry is a thing which I believe
is very essential to the expansion of trade.
Not only is it necessary to educate industrialists in art, but it is far more necessary to educate the people who are going
to use the industrial products, and unless
you
have some centralised means of doing
so I think it will take too long to educate
them in artistic notions, but I was very
glad to see something in the paper the
other day indicating that some steps have
been taken to put art in industry in a
strong position.
We have now to realise that the Belfast
shipbuilding industry, as it was in the
past, is being focussed for air attack,
particularly as we are having this big
expansion of aircraft construction, and
the Government, not content with en-

larging and helping on the work of preparing for defence in a general way, are
concentrating on protecting us from the
great danger of a gas attack by air. There
are some people who say that we in this
country are not in very serious danger
from air attack. I personally do not
hold that view at all. Aeroplanes can
fly so high and so fast that I believe the
fact that they have to pass over England
is no great safeguard for us. Nobody
could see, and probably nobody would
hear, aeroplanes passing over England on
a cloudy day. They might be over
Belfast long before we had any information. Therefore, I am glad to see
the Government are strengthening
measures of defence in the air. In doing
so they will have the active co-operation
of all classes.
There have been some remarks about
ice-cream, and perhaps the matter of icecream is largely a question for children.
But in point of fact it is a potential and
a very big potential way of using milk,
and anything which increases even in a
small way the expansion of the milk
industry is a thing which should be encouraged and protected in the interests
of our largest industry in this country.
Therefore I congratulate the Government on not missing even small details
of opportunity of expanding the great
farming industry.
I have been pressing quietly for some
time for something to be done about
advertisements. I am glad to see that
the corporations and county councils and
the Government are at last going to take
action in this matter. Not only will
this encourage the tourist traffic, but t
will make traffic on the roads very much
safer. The possibility exists of actual
danger, and I am sure the Government
will act in consultation not only with the
county councils on this side but with
those in England who have already
attempted to do this thing, and not so
successfully as we hope the Government
I will do here. I understand that although it was believed in England that
sufficient power had been taken to regulate advertisements that was found not
to be the case.
I
Various amendments of the law which
have been long overdue I think are going
to be dealt with by the Government.
am glad to see they are confining their
activities to necessities and are not going
Debate on the Address
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Captain Perceval Maxwell]
to spread a very big programme of legislation over next session. I think the
Government have been very active in the
past. It is not at all a bad thing that
some legislation should have time to be
digested and tried out before any further
legislation is produced. I only hope that
in trying out this legislation those who
may have been opposed to it in the first
instance or doubtful of its success will
give it every chance of success and,
having realised that their opposition has
been overcome, they will do everything
they can to work the legislation now that
it has become law.
Dr. GIBSON: I would like to join in
the congratulations to the hon. Gentlemen who moved and seconded the
Address. I congratulate them on their
speeches. I rose simply following the
hon. and gallant Member who has just
spoken, because he touched upon some
matters in which I am deeply interested.
In the matter of defence one of the
things that we must attend to is defence
against air raids. As the hon. and
gallant Member has just said, aeroplanes
now travel at such speed and carry such
an enormous load of bombs that if, unfortunately, we are going to have war
declared we might next morning have
bombs dropped. The British Government has formed a Committee for the
purpose of organising the country in the
matter of air raids, and our Northern
Government has followed their example
and appointed a Committee of which
Lieutenant-Colonel Gordon is chairman.
Part of the preparation of dealing with
air raids, and especially gas attacks, is
bound to be undertaken by people who
understand the matter, namely, the Red
Cross Society and the Order of St. John
of Jerusalem. Owing to the lamented
death of Sir Robert Kennedy I happen
at the moment to be the head of these two
organisations in Northern Ireland, and
as a result of a calculation I have made
I find that if one per cent. of the population of Ulster received training in
precautions against air raids and gas
attacks it would require 300 classes of 50
each to train anything like an adequate
number to deal with these gas attacks.
an
air
I
The two organisations I have mentioned
will undertake this training voluntarily.
They will have to go through the country
wherever there is danger of
attack, hire halls, organise classes, and
do a tremendous amount of work.
hope that the provision to be made by
the Government for that work will not
be on a niggardly scale. There will have
to be provision made for first-aid stations,
casualty hospitals, base hospitals, decontamination of clothing, street wardens
to give warning of air raids, and the
preparation of houses to resist gas. All
these things are of the utmost importance and should be dealt with without
delay.
Another thing I am not quite satis-,
fied about, although I presume it is all
right, is the matter of public health.
Public health is not mentioned at all in
the Gracious Speech, and I think it
should have been. I had hoped that an
assurance would be given as to the
operation of the Spahlinger treatment,
that the experiments being made with it
were still being carried on, and that we
would have been told what progress had
been made in stamping out tuberculosis.
Within the last few months a
very
serious situation arose in Belfast. The
Public Health Committee of the
Corporation wanted to build a tuberculosis hospital on a particular site,
and when the proposal was put before the Government, they rejected
it. I hope some assurance will be given
that within a limited period of time a
tuberculosis institution will be built and
adequate provision made to meet a
crying want, especially for children
suffering from tuberculosis in the bones.
I have great pleasure in supporting the
Motion for an Address.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, "That
the Debate be now adjourned."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Debate to be resumed to-morrow.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn." (Viscount Charlemont).
Adjourned accordingly at Twentyfive minutes before Five o'clock.
Papers Presented
Debate on the Address
25 NOVEMBER 1936
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 25th November, 1936.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Education:
Public Elementary Schools Regulations,
1934, Amending Regulations, No. 3,
1936.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
The Motor Cars (Silence Zone) (Holywood) Regulations, 1936.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
BILL
PRESENTED.
Ancient Monuments Bill [S.],
"to amend the Ancient Monuments Act
(Northern Ireland), 1926, and to make
further provision for the preservation of
objects of archæological or historic
interest," was presented by Mr. Long,
read the First time, and ordered to be
read the Second time on the first sitting
day after the Recess.
Deputy Speakers.
Resolved, "That the Senators following
be named Deputy-Speakers of the Senate
during the present Session of Parliament:
Colonial Sinclair.
Sir J. Davison."-(Mr. Long.)
Joint Committees.
Resolved, "That the Senators following
be named of the Joint Standing Committee on Divorce Bills: :-
Dr. Gibson.
Colonel Pakenham.
Mr. Quigley."-(Mr. Long.)
Resolved, "That the Senators following
be named of the Joint Standing Orders
Committee:-
Mr. Barclay.
Mr. Leslie.
Captain Perceval-Maxwell."-(Mr.
Long.)
Resolved, "That the Senators following
be named of the Joint Unopposed Committee on Local Bills:-
:-
Mr. J. Cunningham.
Mr. McLaughlin."-(Mr. Long.)
Resolved, "That the Senators following
be named of the Joint Kitchen and
Refreshment Room Committee:-
Mr. J. Cunningham.
Sir J. Davison.
Mr. Hill Dickson.
Mr. McAllister.
:-
Colonel Pakenham."-(Mr. Long.)
KING'S SPEECH.
Debate on the Address.
Order read for resuming debate on
Question (24th November),
"That an humble Address be presented
to his Grace in the following words:-
"Your Grace,
"We, His Majesty's most dutiful
and loyal subjects, the Senate of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to
your Grace for the Speech which your
Grace has addressed to both Houses
of Parliament." (Sir Emerson
Herdman.)
Mr. LESLIE: In rising to support the
adoption of an humble Address in reply
to the Gracious Speech I should like, if
I may, first of all, to express my humble
congratulations and my acknowledgements to the mover and seconder of the
Address in respect of the admirable and
very pleasant manner in which they performed the duty which they had undertaken. The mover of the Address, Sir
Emerson Herdman, is not perhaps one of
the original Members of this Chamber,
but he has been with us for many years
now, and, having regard to the brief
and distinctly humorous speech with
which he favoured us yesterday afternoon, I cannot help thinking it is a pity
we do not hear his voice more often.
The seconder of the Address, Senator
Quigley, has not been with us quite so
long as the proposer, but the carefully
prepared speech which he delivered
yesterday has furnished yet another
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
intimation of the exhaustive knowledge
which he possesses on some of those very
important questions which fairly often.
come before us.
As regards the Speech itself, I am
going to suggest that the whole Speech
strikes a note of optimism, and I think
we may examine that optimism under
two headings. First of all there is the
more sentimental side. In what his Grace
was pleased to say he used a very confident form of diction, a form of diction
which was well warranted by past experience, when he referred to the
certainty that the occasion of His
Majesty's Coronation would be availed of
throughout Northern Ireland as an
opportunity for showing their wholehearted devotion to His Majesty's Throne
and person.
Passing to what one must describe as
the material side, that is, the reference
to the general welfare of the Province,
one finds the note of optimism again
exemplified and one finds that that note
is not without warrant. It will be remembered that our principal industries,
agriculture, linen and shipbuilding, were
all referred to as instancing in a very
marked degree all the conditions which
make for material wellbeing, wellbeing
which involves not only the accumulation
of additional wealth but what I think
is even more important, an accumulative
reduction in the number of the unemployed amongst us. Unemployment, as
we know, has been for too many years
the curse of Northern Ireland, as it has
been the bane of almost every other
centre of concentrated population. We
rejoice that the experience of the past
month shows that steady reduction to
which I have referred.
I venture to hope that the prognostication contained in the Gracious Speech
as regards a visit to our Province by
His Majesty himself will be fulfilled in
due course. I need hardly say that a
hearty welcome will be accorded to him.
I hope the expression of approval contained in the Gracious Speech of those
steps which have already been taken
within our
area to assist as far as we
can in everything that makes for the
defence of the country will be taken and
regarded, as I am sure it was intended
to be regarded, as a distinct encouragement to us all to do everything that lies
in our power-and the House will remember that our powers in matters of
defence are subject to constitutional
limitation-but I am sure we shall be
encouraged to do all that we can, not
only to amplify the defence of our own
territory, but also we shall do everything we can to ensure that the United
Kingdom as a whole shall be a powerful,
even I might say overwhelming, force in
securing the peace of the world at large.
And now let me pass more to the
details dealt with in the Speech. First
of all I should like to congratulate the
Government on the consideration which
is to be shown on this occasion to those
unpaid members of the local authorities
who devote so much time and attention
to the management of our internal affairs.
It will be a distinct encouragement to
those of them who are engaged in material
plans for the celebrations in connection
with the Coronation to know that in the
words used yesterday they will be allowed
to continue in office until their labours
attain fruition.
I observe there is a proposal for the
consolidation of Acts of Parliament
relating to factories and workshops.
That, when accomplished, will be of great
value and great assistance and it will, I
am glad to think, put almost the final
coping stone on the edifice of consolidation relating to social services which
was begun last session. I am
sure the
preparation of the necessary Measure
will be a task involving some labour and
considerable research, and I feel sure the
result will be such as to justify the labour
expended upon it.
I notice it is intended to proceed
further along the lines which tend
towards the amelioration of those unfortunate people who suffer from blindness.
The point to which special attention is
directed is that of providing pensions at
an earlier age. I cannot but hope that
advantage will be taken of that opportunity to render less stringent the conditions which are at present imposed as
a condition precedent to the reception
of a pension. It has always struck me
that as the existing Blind Persons Act
is administered-and necessarily administered, for I am afraid the words used by
Parliament are imperative-but as it is
administered it seems to me there is undue severity in the matter. The difficulty
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
25 NOVEMBER 1936
to
my mind is this, that a man becomes
blind or loses his sight to a greater or
less degree as the years go on. As that
man's years proceed he becomes less
capable of adapting himself to new conditions. The present condition is that
he must be so blind-I am speaking from
memory-as to be incapable of work.
I do not think that is quite a fair condition to impose. Some regard should
be paid to the fact that the man's
affliction has rendered him incapable of
pursuing what would be his normal occupation. He should not be required to
start afresh in the labour market in competition with those better equipped and
seek for himself a new job. I do think
that something should, if possible, be
done in that direction. If it can be done
our Parliament will once more have performed a work of mercy which will commend itself to all our citizens.
There is also an allusion to the possibility of miscellaneous reform. I think
I spoke of that at the outset of the preceding Session. My hon. and gallant
Friend (Captain Perceval-Maxwell), who
I do not see in his place to-day certainly
mentioned it yesterday, and I do not
think there is any occasion for me to
delay the House further on the subject.
Like other speakers, I should like to
record my satisfaction at the proposal to
take steps to preserve our countryside
from the disfigurement attendant on
public advertisements. I had occasion
last Session to tell the House some perfectly horrible notices that had been
erected round about the outskirts of
Dunluce Castle. I am happy to tell the
House to-day that those peculiarly
objectionable notices have been removed.
I do not know whom I have to thank for
that, nor do I know who was originally
responsible for the insult to the amenities
of the country, but I do hope that when
the question of the regulation of
advertisements comes before us
steps will be taken to restrain the ardour
of the local billposter. He seems to regard every wall and every door and every
tree in the country as a suitable column
for him to desecrate by affixing his
notices, usually in somewhat blatant
terms and rather appalling capitals.
That is part of the question which ought
not to be neglected.
some
And now, at the conclusion of my
speech, in spite of appearances, I am
perhaps becoming a little anachronistic,
and I want to take the House back to
the glacial period and refer to the proposal regarding ice-cream. I am not
sure that ice-cream would not be better
referred to as iced cream, which I understand is the original form of expression.
Passing from that, there are two points.
of view in dealing with the matter. One
of them was dealt with yesterday by the
hon. and gallant Member (Captain
Perceval-Maxwell), and that is the possibility of increasing the use of cream for
purposes of human sustenance. That is,
of course, a very admirable object in
itself, but there is another point of view,
and I confess it is the second that appeals
more to me. That is, taking every step
that can be taken to ensure that this
substance, whether we call it ice-cream
or iced cream, which is usually sold to
children, shall be of a quality that is not
necessarily deleterious to health.
Disquieting reports that have reached
me some from places not too far removed
from where I live-indicate that on one
occasion the local officer of health had
taken seven samples, and found five unfit
for human consumption. It is obvious
that Parliament ought to interfere definitely and decisively in a matter which,
although it appears to be small in itself,
is yet of great importance. The preparation of the Bill will not, I imagine,
give much trouble, nor will its passage
through the House. It will naturally
take the form of a marriage between our
two codes relating to public health and
food and drugs, and I feel sure the offspring of the marriage will be a very
healthy infant, which will in time conduce
to the health of other infants.
I should like to express my full concurrence in the humble Address, which
has been So admirably moved and
seconded, and I should like to declare
myself as heartily in support of that
Address.
Mr. JOSEPH
CUNNINGHAM: In
the first place I should like to join with
other speakers in congratulating the
mover and seconder of the Motion for
the Address on the admirable way they
performed their duty. So far as the
Gracious Speech itself is concerned, I
think it was the most pleasant I have
listened to since I first entered this
Chamber. In successive years we were
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
asked to wait patiently in the hope that
we would eventually emerge from the
period of depression which prevailed for
so long. I am pleased to say that the
Speech to which we listened yesterday
struck a much more optimistic note. The
linen industry has considerably improved,
and is continuing to prosper, while agriculture is in a much better state than
it was, and is also continuing to make
progress in the right direction. In this
wave of prosperity I might also include
the tobacco trade. It is doing wonderfully well at the present time. The North
of Ireland is fortunate in having two of
the largest tobacco factories in the kingdom in its midst. They are both very
busy, and their profit-sharing scheme is
a great help to the workers.
There is one industry that is not in
such a satisfactory state, and that is shipbuilding. We learned from the Gracious
Speech that in 1935 shipbuilding was in
a better state than it was in 1936. Those
of us who are brought into contact with
the skilled workers in the shipyards know
that that is so, because the fact is brought
to our notice very prominently. It is
rather a serious matter that shipbuilding
may one year be very busy and employ
a large number of skilled workers, and
the following year be quite slack with a
large number of workers laid off. It so
happens at this time skilled workers in
shipbuilding centres across the Channel
are very scarce, with the result that our
skilled workers are being sent over to
those centres to do the work available
there. That may not prove to be a good
thing for us, because once there is a
return of activity in the shipyards here
it may be very difficult to obtain the
necessary skilled labour. It is a great
pity there could not be a
distribution of such work.
more even
It is a great hardship on the workers
in another respect, because I have heard
a
considerable number of complaints
recently from men who have a home and
a wife and family in Belfast, and who are
compelled by the labour exchange to go
to such places as Portsmouth to work.
They have not only to keep themselves
while across the water, but they have also
to maintain their homes in Belfast and
send what money they can spare to their
wives. I consider there is every reason
for them to complain very bitterly,
especially when we know that the greater
part of this work across the water is for
Admiralty purposes.
One would naturally think they would
send the work where there was an abundance of skilled labour, and not have the
skilled labour following after the work.
I know the Government has done a great
deal in securing a share of Admiralty
work for Belfast, and I hope they will
persevere in their efforts and if possible
obtain even more work for our shipyards.
Some centres in England which are not
so well equipped to carry out this work
have an abundance of work, and yet in
Belfast, where we have the necessary
facilities and the skilled labour, we have
not nearly sufficient work. Therefore I
hope the Government will continue to
press for a larger share of Admiralty contracts.
It is very satisfactory to learn from the
Gracious Speech that we are to have a
new industry in the city of Belfast in the
form of an aircraft factory. I am quite
satisfied that we have the skilled labour
capable of dealing with aircraft work.
During the war we did this class of work
at Messrs. Harland and Wolff's, and it
gave every satisfaction, and I am confident our workers will give the same
satisfaction in the new aircraft factory.
There is one thing I do sincerely hope,
and that is that the factory will build
aeroplanes for use in civil aviation as well
as for defence purposes. I trust the
factory will obtain sufficient orders to
keep the workers constantly employed,
and that it will not have to depend
entirely on war work.
There is a reference in the Gracious
Speech to the local government elections.
It is a pity that the change made some
time ago when the date of the elections
was changed from January to May
should now be interfered with. At the
same time I am sure we are all agreed
that nothing should be allowed to occur
to mar the Coronation celebrations,
especially in Belfast. In this connection
I would like to draw the attention of the
Minister of Home Affairs to the fact
that immediately after May or June we
enter upon what we in this part of the
country look upon as especially a holiday
season. I refer to the 12th July, and it
would be unfortunate if the Minister of
Home Affairs did not bear that fact in
mind.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
25 NOVEMBER 1936
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Have the elections on that day.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: If
we did we would sweep the country. We
are so enthusiastic at that particular
time that no Party could stand against
us. Even in fairness to my hon. Friend's
Party, I think the elections should be
deferred sufficiently long to take them
clear of the holiday season, namely, June,
July, and August. I think October
would be a very suitable time to have
these elections. A considerable number
of people are away from home in June,
July and August, and it is to be hoped
the elections will be so arranged as to
enable the largest possible poll to be
recorded.
The Gracious Speech refers to another
noteworthy improvement in our social
legislation, namely, the provision of
medical treatment for young persons
under sixteen. I think that is a very
important step, because boys and girls
of fourteen have reached a dangerous
age, and I am very glad they are to be
brought into the medical scheme. The
free medical treatment they will receive
may be the means of saving lives, because in some of these cases they have
difficulty in securing proper medical
treatment. This is going to confer a
great boon, not only on these young
people themselves but on their parents.
Another interesting matter dealt with
in the Speech is the assistance that is
proposed to be given to horse breeding.
Senator Sir Emerson Herdman said
yesterday that he was more acquainted
with horses than he was with ice-cream.
Hobby horses would be more in my line
than real horses, because I do not think
I could hold myself on a horse. At the
same time it is a pleasure to know that
there has been such a great improvement in horse breeding in Northern Ireland. At one period in Belfast
contractor supplied the Corporation with
horses and carts, and sometimes you
would have seen a horse propped up
while the cart was being loaded,
it was in such a terrible state.
No such thing occurs now. We have
a very good class of horse engaged
in that particular work, and any dray
horses or carriers' horses going through
the city are certainly good to look upon.
I do say it is well to find the Governa
ment taking an interest in a business of
that kind.
I look upon the Bill to deal with the
manufacture of ice-cream as being of
very great importance. Any of us that
have lived in the city for a good number
of years know perfectly well that the
sale of this particular refreshment is increasing, and we know further that
it is made in all kinds of dens and places
and made under all kinds of conditions,
and it is very fine to see that the Government are interesting themselves in small
matters of that kind, which mean a great
deal to the health and welfare of the
people, especially working-class people,
