I am extremely glad to see they are
taking notice of this matter and intend
to see that pure ice-cream is manufactured under proper conditions so that
those people who are so fond of the cream
will have a wholesome delicacy to use.
There is another matter that I am
sure most of us agree with and I should
like to draw particular attention to it.
It is a matter I am very much interested
in.
It says here:
It is hoped that local authorities will
take full opportunity of the facilities.
afforded by the Act passed last year in
regard to labourers' cottages.
I do hope, indeed, that the Press will
take special note of it, and that the local
authorities will do something to remove
the pigsties-one can describe some of
the houses as no better-that are inhabited by human beings in some parts
of the country. I do maintain that all
human beings should be properly and
comfortably housed. I am glad to see
that so far as the Government is concerned they are providing an abundance
of houses. I saw where a critic the other
day wished to attack the Government,
and he stated that there are 1,200 houses
vacant. That is to the credit of the Government. It is splendid to think that in
a city like Belfast, which is growing so
rapidly, there are houses erected for the
working-class people and that there are
houses to spare. It will be an opportunity for the people living in unhealthy
dwellings to remove from those to more
healthy dwellings and much more
pleasant atmosphere. I do think the local
authorities through the country should
make an effort, the same as Belfast, to see
that the people are housed properly. I
always remember the speeches of Lord
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
Roberts, when he was trying to waken
the country to the dangers of war prior
to 1914 and when people looked upon
him as a war-monger. He stated at that
time that if the people were well housed
in comfortable homes they would then
be able and more willing and with
greater pride to fight for their country
and the homes left behind them. I do
think that the farm workers especially--
they are the best and most healthy type
of men and the best class that come into
the city-should be properly housed.
trust the hope expressed here that the
local authorities will take full opportunity and do their best to provide
labourers' cottages will be properly
attended to.
I
There is another matter I should like
to refer to. It is not contained in the
Speech, but I think Dr. Gibson referred
to public health yesterday and gave a
warning as to what was necessary for our
protection in case of an air raid. The
statement was made that a sanatorium
proposed to be erected by the Belfast
Corporation was objected to by the Government. I do not think that is strictly
fair towards the Government. When the
Belfast Corporation made an application
to the Government for a loan for the extension of the sanatorium, a Commission
was set up to inquire into the matter, and
it was as a result of the evidence submitted to the Commission that the Commissioner recommended that the application should not be acceded to. The chairman of the Public Health Committee was
one of the strongest opponents, and he
gave evidence before that Commission that
he was quite satisfied the place was not
suitable for a sanatorium. I am sure, at
the same time, he was like the rest of us,
very anxious that a better sanatorium
should be erected in some part of the
neighbourhood of Belfast-not in the city
-and should be erected in a place entirely
suitable to deal with the scourge from
which so many are dying at the present
time. I hope the Government will give
it out pretty clearly that while the Commission objected to this extension, they
do desire that a proper sanatorium be
erected in some outlying district near the
city of Belfast, because it is absolutely
necessary. These sanatoria are very
useful. They are able to reduce the
number of deaths from this disease and
may be able to cure some of these poor
people. I think it is absolutely necessary
that the best possible sanatorium should
be erected in Belfast, especially where
there are so many factories where women
are employed, and which bring on this
very dangerous disease. I have the
greatest possible pleasure in supporting
the Address to his Grace the Governor for
the Speech delivered in this House.
answer.
Since I
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
have been Leader of the House I do not
remember any Gracious Speech which has
come in for less criticism, but there are
one or two points which hon. Senators
have alluded to and which I would like to
There was the one Dr. Gibson
referred to yesterday in reference to experimental work with the Spahlinger
vaccine. The object of this work is to
determine whether the immunity conferred by the vaccine is destroyed if the
vaccinated animals are subsequently subjected to the tuberculin test. It is
essential to clear this point up before
general use can be made of the vaccine.
The Ministry is at present in negotiation.
with Mr. Spahlinger in order to obtain
large supplies of the vaccine for the purpose of a large scale experiment in which
the vaccinated animals will be subjected
to the most intense form of natural infection that can be devised. Dr. Gibson
also referred, as did Senator Cunningham,
to the new accommodation for sufferers
from tuberculosis proposed to be provided
by the Corporation of the city of Belfast.
The facts are as Senator Cunningham.
stated. It is impossible to give a date
when this work will be undertaken, as
the matter is one which calls for careful
consideration, but I am able to inform
the hon. Member that discussion on the
subject is now proceeding between the
Corporation and the Ministry of Home
Affairs, and that he may be sure that
both of these bodies are anxious that the
work shall proceed as soon as possible.
There is only one other point I can
remember in the speeches that needs a
little elaboration. That is the question
of the proposed new aircraft factory. It
is to be remembered that it is for the
production at the present moment of civil
aircraft, not military. Of course, hon.
Senators are aware that a civil aircraft
factory can be transformed into a military
one as occasion demands, but it is for
civil aircraft.
Debate on the Address
25 NOVEMBER 1936

Debate on the Address
I venture to say that if His Majesty
knew the history of all the trouble the
big drums have brought to this country
and caused in this country he certainly
would not have undertaken to beat one.
I venture to suggest that when he does
find out the history and finds out the
trouble the drum has caused he will not
take up another one. We all wish him
happiness, and sincerely trust that during
his reign prosperity and peace will be
maintained not alone abroad but also at
home.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
think that the Gracious Speech, as all
hon. Members seem to agree, is a good
one, because there is not an outstanding
controversial point in it. For a number
of years past we have had debatable
Measures before us, but now it is different.
I am very glad to see that something is
going to be done for horse-breeding in
the North of Ireland. That is a subject
I think might be brought forward, and
one that would help the farmers in the
North of Ireland. We see the big prices
being paid for horses now, and I would
be very glad to see our people sharing in
them. I am also glad to see that the
Ministry are going to have the law reforms spoken of last year carried out.
Another point which I think should
not be missed is in regard to the two
voluntary organisations. One is the
Ulster Tourist Association which was
started by the late Mr. Robert Bailie,
and was carried on by the late Sir
Frederick Cleaver. That association has
done a great deal to popularise tourist
traffic in the North of Ireland. I know
that a number of my people in America
have written to me to put them in touch,
and they have visited the North of
Ireland as the result of information they
got through that association.
The other voluntary organisation which
should get credit is the Ulster Industrial
It has been
Development Association.
trying to develop new business in the
North of Ireland, and personally I have
had two or three inquiries from the other
side. Mr. James Cleland, who has just
recovered from an illness, has shown an
amount of loyalty to this country in
running this association. Altogether, I
think the Gracious Speech is a very happy
one, and I am glad it has met with the
general approval of this House.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I, too, wish to
congratulate the mover of this Resolution
on his brief, concise, and witty speech,
and also the seconder on his all-round
political speech. I wish particularly to
thank the proposer for the reference he
made to the people I represent, and to
point out to him that as far as we are
concerned we are always anxious to
uphold law and order.
In connection with the return visit of
His Gracious Majesty the King, I would
also refer to the beating of the big drum.
I would suggest that not only the
politicians but the statesmen of England,
the Irish Free State, and Ulster should
take advantage of the Coronation year
and try to settle their differences and
let us live in peace and prosperity. I
would say that if ever a cruel wrong was
perpretrated by one country on another
it was perpetrated on Ireland by England, which partitioned our country.
That act alone will for ever remain a
vile blot on England. Every Irishman
worthy of the name loves his native
country. He wants a united Ireland,
and until that is secured this country will
never, as far as I can see, be either peaceful or prosperous.
I was very glad to see that the agricultural workers are again referred to in
the Speech, and I trust the casual garden
labourer will also be brought in to enjoy
the benefits of the Unemployment
Insurance Act. I understand that the
Imperial Parliament has decided to include the casual gardener in the insurance scheme, and I trust that this
Government will do likewise. We all
agree that everything possible should be
done for the blind, and I agree with the
speaker who urged that the deaf and
dumb should be similarly treated. If it
time
is possible at any
to provide
pensions for the deaf and dumb I certainly am of opinion it should be done.
In any legislation that may be introduced on the subject I trust that county
councils will not be deprived of the
power they have at present of granting
pensions to blind persons at the age of
fifty.
I agree entirely with the remarks of
the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) as to the difficulty a blind person
has in obtaining a pension. I am afraid
blind persons are at times victimised. I
have known of a case of a person who
421786
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
was unfit to work through lack of sight
and who obtained certificates from two
specialists of standing in Belfast, and yet
his application for a blind person's
pension was refused. He applied for a
pension a number of times and each time
his application was turned down by the
Government medical referee, sitting
behind closed doors, despite the certificates given by two well-known specialists
in Belfast. In such a case as that a
blind person should be given facilites to
appeal to a higher tribunal, such as the
county court judge.
I also hope that steps will be taken to
provide that when a blind
reaches
person
the age of seventy he will not be deprived
of his blind pension, and that he will get
the old-age pension in addition. I understand that as the law stands at present a
blind person who attains the age of 65 is
entitled to a contributory pension as well
as his blind person, but when he reaches
the age of 70 he has to take either the
blind pension or the old-age pension, but
cannot have both. That is victimising
the blind person of 70, and I maintain he
is entitled to both pensions.
Many allusions have been made to the
prosperous state of the country, but I
would like to ask this question. If everything is as satisfactory as stated in the
Gracious Speech, why is it that the
farmers are growing less flax. Instead of
importing flax into this country every
year, surely the Government should subsidise flax growing here, and thus encourage the farmers to grow more flax.
The
erection of labourers' cottages is a step
in the right direction, and I suggest the
Government should also assist farmers to
improve their own houses by means of a
subsidy or grant. Anyone who goes about
the country will see that some farmers'
houses are nothing better than hovels, and
the Government should encourage such
farmers to improve their dwellings by
giving them a grant or subsidy for that
purpose.
If the Government really want to
develop the tourist traffic I suggest they
should repeal the licensing restrictions so
far as hotels are concerned, and particularly the ban on Sunday opening. It is
irritating to tourists coming here to be
unable to get liquid refreshment in a
hotel on a Sunday unless they have a
meal at the same time. When you are
motoring around the country there are
times when you feel dry and want a
drink.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Icecream.
say
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Motoring on icecream would not do you much good. I
do seriously suggest that the Government should allow hotels to supply liquid
refreshment on a Sunday. I am sorry I
cannot enter into the spirit of this House
with regard to the Coronation, especially
when I consider the way the people I
represent are victimised under this Government. We are deprived-and I say
it without hesitation-of the positions
which we as citizens under your Government are entitled to. I that our
proportion of population entitles us to at
least 33 per cent. of the appointments
under this Government. I venture to
say, and I defy contradiction, that at the
present time not more than five per cent.
of the appointments under Government
Departments are held by Catholics. We
have an example of this treatment here
in the Senate. My Party are entitled to
about eight seats in the Senate. When
I came into the Senate we had five representatives. Since then there have
been two vacancies created. It is a recognised thing in all county councils,
corporations, and district councils, that
when a minority vacancy occurs it is left
to that minority to co-opt a representative. Not SƠ here. Our two
vacancies were filled by the Government,
who appointed two of their own representatives. So much for fair play and
equality of treatment.
I will say that as far as the British
Constitution is concerned no one can
complain of it if it is administered
properly, but I do suggest that the
British Constitution is not administered
here as it should be. As far as Catholics
are concerned
is
its administration
practically a farce. I say once again
that Catholics under the Six-County
Government are victimised, and do not
get the positions to which they are.
entitled. As long as that state of affairs
exists surely no fair-minded individual
can ask our people to take part in the
Coronation of a King whose Constitution
is administered by the Six-County Government in such a way.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
25 NOVEMBER 1926
Sir JOSEPH DAVISON: I wish also
to offer my congratulations to the hon.
Members who moved and seconded the
Address. I am fully in accord with all
they said with regard to the coming
Coronation ceremonies, because I know
that the great majority of the people of
Northern Ireland are intensely devoted
to the Royal House. When our late
beloved King George V came to Belfast
to open the Parliament of Northern Ireland in 1921 he was deeply touched by
the loyal and affectionate welcome that
was given to him and to her Majesty the
Queen on that occasion, and he so expressed himself to the authorities here.
The same loyal welcome was given to the
present King when he was Prince of
Wales and came to open this building, and
I am sure that if he should once again
favour us with a visit his welcome would
be no less loyal and affectionate and wholehearted than it was to his beloved father
and mother. We are looking forward to
the Coronation ceremony with the
warmest approval on account of our
loyalty and devotion, and I hope the
state of the country will remain
peaceful as it is to-day. I hope that all
parties will give their goodwill
establish and maintain peace.
as
to
The mover of the Address spoke with
first-hand knowledge of the improvement
in the linen trade, and we are all delighted
to learn of that improvement, not only
in the linen industry but in agriculture
also. It is to be regretted that shipbuilding is not in quite such a satisfactory condition, but it is welcome news
to everyone that trade generally is improving. There is one aspect of the
unemployment problem to which I should
like to direct attention, and that is the
single young man who finds it very difficult to obtain employment of any sort,
and particularly those who have no training in any skilled industry. The Government and local authorities in charge of
relief schemes have to give preference to
married men, because they are in greatest
need. On the whole, however, we have
reason to be thankful for the improvement in trade that has been brought
about. I am particularly pleased to see
that there is going to be something done
to provide pensions for persons of limited
means that were referred to yesterday as
the black-coated workers. There have
been a good number of traders and shopkeepers, and I suppose I might also say
farmers, who have never enjoyed the
benefit of any of the schemes, although
they have had to pay their contribution
on their employees' cards for health insursurance and in industrial concerns for
unemployment insurance, and I shall be
very glad if the coming scheme will
embrace that class of people, because
many of these men have been very hard
hit indeed. There is not a Member of
this House but knows of many who have
had in former days quite prosperous little
businesses, and were able to maintain
themselves and their families in a certain
degree of comfort. Changed conditions
have come since the war, and many of
these businesses are almost wiped out, and
if the head of the household dies his
widow or his orphans are not entitled
to any State grant. I hope these people
will be taken into consideration when this
Bill is being brought forward. It is a
great hardship undoubtedly, and one
which ought to be remedied as soon as
possible.
With the additional pension that is
going to be given to blind persons I am
entirely in sympathy. As a member of
Belfast Corporation I have had an opportunity of meeting many of the people who
came forward, and I am wholeheartedly
in sympathy with what Senator Leslie
said about blind persons or almost blind
persons, persons who cannot carry on
their ordinary vocation owing to their
sight giving way. I have many a time
thought we were hidebound by the Act.
Belfast Corporation adopted certain provisions of the Blind Persons Act many
years ago and they have been carrying on
the work there. It only cost a few
thousands the first year or two, but it has
grown very considerably owing to the influx of blind persons from outside the city.
I hope something will be done in this Bill
to have a residential qualification for a
certain period before a person can be
entitled. I do not see why county councils
and boroughs should have unloaded on
them people from outside Northern Ireland altogether. It is well known by
every member of Belfast Corporation that
we have had a tremendous number of these
people coming in in years past. The residential qualification was very small, and
I think it should be increased in order
that the people of Northern Ireland
should be protected.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Sir Joseph Davison]
With regard to ice-cream manufacture
Belfast Corporation also made regulations
and it is well known that ice-cream was
manufactured in many places which were
a perfect danger to the people who were
so unfortunate as to partake of the icecream. I am glad that something is going
to be done in that direction.
I am sorry that the Senator, who has
just sat down, considers that the partitioning of Ireland was a blot on Ireland,
but I can say in all sincerity that partition
was absolutely necessary on account of the
conditions which prevailed, and from
what has happened outside since I do not
see that we are going to improve the position in any way. Many of those who are
now resposible for law, order and
authority in the Free State have been
showing animosity to Northern Ireland,
Great Britain and to his Majesty the
King; and while that remains I hope that
partition will remain. We can never
hope to have a united Ireland as long as
present conditions prevail.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to express my
congratulations to the Mover and Seconder
of the Address for the way in which they
performed that duty, and on the speeches
they delivered. I think both speakers
have done justice to the work they were
given to do. They have dealt with the
different points in such a way that I am
not in a position to improve on. I was
glad to see by the Gracious Speech the
great improvement in employment that
has taken place during the past year, and
I hope and trust that that improvement
will continue. Unemployment is getting
less. More men are getting work, and
that means more money brought into circulation for the benefit of all concerned.
But although we have such a big number
returning to work we still have a big
majority of men unemployed. Now the
question arises in my mind, what are we
going to do with these men. There is not
work to
go round, and each year
is adding to the number of boys and girls
coming from the schools, and nothing for
them to do. I was going to make a suggestion that if the Government set up a
committee of inquiry into the number of
men that have been displaced by machinery, because there is no doubt that a
great number of men we have on the
streets to-day have been displaced by
machinery, and when it was found out
how many men each machine has displaced then tax the machine instead of the
men. I think the machine that has displaced them, and put men on the street
should be asked to contribute something
towards the men's upkeep while they were
on the street.
And then regarding the boys in their
teens up to 20 years of age I think something should be done for them. According
to the Press I see that Canada, Australia
and New Zealand are talking about opening their doors again to immigration. If
they open their doors to immigration
they do not want mechanics, and they do
not want clerks or young men that have
been standing about the street corners or
football people or dog racers. They want
mostly men that understand agricultural
work, because that is the work that will be
offered them in any of these colonies. I
was wondering if it could be possible to
make arrangements with farmers so that
they would each take one or two of these
boys, and compensate the farmers while
the boys were serving an apprenticeship
agricultural work. If the boy
served an apprenticeship to the farmer,
of, perhaps, two or three or four
years that boy would be in a position.
when the time came to go to the colonies
to undertake agricultural work. That is
the only salvation for these boys.
to
Mr. LESLIE: What about the Army?
Mr. BARCLAY: I would leave it to
themselves. I would not have compulsion
as far as the Army is concerned. Then
these boys when they go to the colonies.
would understand how to handle agricultural machinery and horses, and know all
about the work of the farm. I just make
that suggestion. I agree with what has
been said about the legislation foreshadowed, and as far as the blind are concerned I am in sympathy with them. I
say that anything that can be done should
be done. As to ice-cream I think it is a
step in the right direction, but I would
make a suggestion here also.
In every
town and every city in the Six Counties
there should be a centre for the manufacture of ice-cream. The ice-cream should
be manufactured at these centres and then
distributed to those who sell and carry it
out to the country. Take the seaside
towns. There are tons of ice-cream consumed every year, and in the past this
ice-cream was not manufactured under
45 Debate on the Address

25 NOVEMBER 1936
Adjournment
proper conditions and under proper supervision. If we had a centre in Belfast,
Coleraine, Portrush, Porstewart and other
places where it would be under supervision
this ice-cream would be pure. It would
be quite wholesome and there would not
be the same danger of disease. I think
the Gracious Speech, on the whole, is
good. It surveys the past and looks into
the future, and I think that when the end
of the session comes and this legislation is
put through this House it should prove of
great benefit to all concerned. I have
great pleasure in supporting the Motion.
for an Address to his Grace the Governor.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the said Address be
presented to his Grace by such Members
of this House as are Members of His
Majesty's Privy Council in Northern
Ireland.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until such date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Adjourned accordingly at
half-past Four o'clock
until such date as Mr.
Speaker shall appoint.
THE SENATE
A PROCLAMATION
BY THE GOVERNOR OF NORTHERN IRELAND.
WHEREAS it is provided by section eleven of the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920, as modified by and in
pursuance of certain other enactments, that the Governor
of Northern Ireland shall, in His Majesty's name, summon
the Parliament of Northern Ireland:
AND WHEREAS both Houses of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland stand adjourned at this present time:
AND WHEREAS under an Instrument of Abdication
dated the Tenth day of December instant, his former
Majesty King Edward the Eighth did declare His irrevocable determination to renounce the Throne for Himself
and His descendants, and the said Instrument of Abdication
has now taken effect, and His former Majesty's Most
Honourable Privy Council, and others, did meet on Saturday
the Twelfth day of December, 1936, and did direct that His
Royal Highness Prince Albert Frederick Arthur George be
proclaimed King by the Style and Title of George the Sixth:
NOW, THEREFORE, I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
Duke of Abercorn, Knight of the Most Noble Order of the
Garter, Knight of the Most Illustrious Order of Saint
Patrick, Governor of Northern Ireland, in His Majesty's
name, do hereby summon the Parliament of Northern
Ireland to meet on Thursday, the 17th December, 1936, to
the intent that such proclamation of His Majesty's accession
as may be signed in the Privy Council of Northern Ireland
may be read to the Members of both Houses of the said
Parliament:
And the Senators and Members of the House of
Commons are hereby required to give their attendance
accordingly.
GIVEN this Twelfth day of December, Nineteen hundred and thirty-six.
(Signed by his own hand)
ABERCORN.
17 DECEMBER 1936
THE
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATES
(OFFICIAL REPORT)
IN THE
FOURTH SESSION OF THE FOURTH PARLIAMENT OF NORTHERN
IRELAND.
FIRST YEAR OF THE REIGN OF
HIS MAJESTY KING GEORGE VI.
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 17th December, 1936.
Whereas by an Instrument of Abdication dated the Tenth day of December
His former Majesty King Edward the
Eighth did declare
declare His irrevocable
determination to renounce the Throne
for Himself and His descendants, and
the said Instrument of Abdication
having now taken effect, whereby
the Imperial Crown of Great Britain,
Ireland and all other His former
Majesty's Dominions is now solely and
rightfully come to the High and Mighty
Prince Albert Frederick Arthur George;
and the Most Honourable Privy Council
of Northern Ireland, and others, having
met this day at Parliament Buildings,
Stormont, and having signed a Proclamation proclaiming His Majesty King
George the Sixth as King, it was directed
by His Grace the Governor of Northern
Ireland (the Duke of Abercorn, K.G.,
K.P.) that the said Proclamation should
be read to both Houses of Parliament.
The Senate met at Five minutes after
Twelve o'clock noon, pursuant to the
Proclamation by His Grace the
Governor, Colonel SINCLAIR (Deputy-
Speaker) in the Chair.
Mr. DEPUTY-SPEAKER: His Grace
the Governor commands this honourable
House to attend him immediately in the
Central Hall.
Mr. Deputy-Speaker, with the House,
proceeded to the Central Hall, where
HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR read
the following Proclamation:
:-
"WHEREAS by an Instrument of
Abdication dated the Tenth day of
December instant His former Majesty
King Edward the Eighth did declare His
irrevocable Determination to renounce
the Throne for Himself and His
Descendants, and the said Instrument of
Abdication has now taken effect, whereby
the Imperial Crown of Great Britain,
Ireland and all other His former
Majesty's dominions is now solely and
rightfully come to the High and Mighty
Prince Albert Frederick Arthur George:
"WE, therefore, the Governor of
Northern Ireland, being here assisted with
these of His former Majesty's Privy
Council of Northern Ireland, with
Numbers of other Principal Gentlemen of
Quality, with the Lord Mayor and
Citizens of Belfast, do now hereby with
one Voice and Consent of Tongue and
Heart, publish and proclaim, That the
High and Mighty Prince Albert Frederick
Arthur George is now become our only
lawful and rightful Liege Lord George
the Sixth by the Grace of God, of
Great Britain, Ireland and the British
Dominions bevond the Seas King,
Defender of the Faith, Emperor of India:
To whom we do acknowledge all Faith
Members Sworn
Address to His Majesty
THE SENATE
and constant Obedience, with all hearty
and humble Affection: beseeching God,
by whom Kings and Queens do reign, to
bless the Royal Prince George the Sixth
with long and happy Years to reign
over us.
""
Given at the Houses of Parliament,
Belfast, this Seventeenth day of
December, in the year of our Lord
One thousand nine hundred and
thirty-six.
"GOD SAVE THE KING."
Members Sworn.
The House having returned,
Colonel Thomas Sinclair (Deputy-
Speaker) took and subscribed the Oath.
Then the following Members took and
subscribed the Oath:-
--:

John Andrew Long, Esq.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast (Sir Crawford
McCullagh).
The Mayor of Londonderry (Captain W.
M. Wilton).
The Right Hon. Samuel Cunningham.
James Hill Dickson, Esq.
Dr. William Gibson.
The Right Hon. Henry Bruce Armstrong.
James Graham Leslie, Esq.
Joseph Cunningham, Esq.
Andrew Quigley, Esq.
William Barclay, Esq.
Sir Roland Nugent.
Captain John Robert Perceval-Maxwell.
Sir Joseph Davison.
John Porter Porter, Esq.
Sir Emerson Herdman.
John Clements Glendinning, Esq.

House resumed.

Message from the Governor.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The following Message has been received from His
Grace the Governor :-
-
"I have received with great satisfaction
the expression of your thanks for the
Speech with which I opened the present
Session of Parliament.
ACCESSION OF KING GEORGE VI.
Address to His Majesty.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, "That an
Humble Address be presented to His
Majesty as follows:
Most Gracious Sovereign,
of
We, Your Majesty's most dutiful
and loyal subjects, the Senate
Northern Ireland, in Parliament
assembled, respectfully tender assurance
of our steadfast devotion to The Crown
and Person of Your Majesty and to
Her Majesty the Queen. The people
of Northern Ireland look forward with
complete confidence to the future, in
the knowledge that Your Majesty's
constant concern will be the happiness
and prosperity of the United Kingdom
and of the whole Empire, to which
Ulster is proud to belong."
It is only a little time ago that I stood
in this House to pray that a happy and
prosperous reign might be granted to
King Edward VIII, and I think it was
the expectation of all his subjects that
he would fulfil the promise of his earlier
life. With all the events of the past
weeks fresh in the mind, it is, I think,
unnecessary for me to say much, but it
would ill become us to forget in the
circumstances of the moment the sterling
work for the Empire which King Edward
performed during the time in which he
was Prince of Wales or during his brief
reign. The results of all his missions to
the great Dominions will endure when all
else has faded away.
The King's Accession-
17 DECEMBER 1936
Address to His Majesty
the
King George VI has ascended
Throne in circumstances of the greatest
difficulty and in a time of the greatest
personal distress. He can be fortified by
the knowledge that there is not one of
his subjects but feels the utmost sympathy
with him, not only as a King but also
as a man. In His Majesty's first message
to Imperial Parliament he says, "I am
resolved to do my duty." As one who
served in the Royal Navy in the greatest
naval battle of modern times perhaps
some recollection of Nelson's signal at
Trafalgar inspired these words. I think
we may be assured that His Majesty will
not fail to carry out his resolve, and that
with Her Majesty the Queen to help him.
in all those onerous duties that fall to
the lot of a King and Queen of England,
together they will revive those traditions.
of the reign of King George V which
raised the Throne of England to the
illustrious heights it attained in the eyes
of the world, and which commanded the
respect and affection of all its subjects.
Mr. LESLIE: I desire to support the
Address which has been moved by the
noble Viscount, the Leader of the House,
in, if I may say so, such well-chosen
words. This is for Northern Ireland a
most auspicious day. It is the day
which affords us our first opportunity of
tendering to our new Sovereign the assurance of our loyalty and devotion towards
him, and it is also the day which enables us
to offer our humble congratulations to His
Majesty on the occasion of his accession
to the Throne. And we offer these
humble congratulations unfeignedly, and
with one consent of tongue and heart.
But it is, as I think, appropriate and
perhaps even inevitable that some passing
reference should be made in this House
and at this time to events antecedent to
His Majesty's accession. We cannot forget and I do not think we should try to
forget how much we owe to our former
King for those unceasing efforts for the
welfare of his realm and subjects to which
he devoted his time and energy, first as
Prince of Wales and subsequently as
King.
We cannot fail, I think, to recognise
that in the final crisis-the causes of which
it is not for us to probe-King Edward
pursued his duty as he saw it. It seems
to me perfectly plain from what we know
of the events of the last fortnight or so
that his then Majesty examined the
problem in the light of what was best for
the Empire and the Crown-not the
wearer of the Crown. And it was, as I
see it, with a kingly intuition that he
decided,, in the words of the poet, that
"the game is more than the player of the
game, and the ship is more than the crew."
And the then King Edward had that
strength so seldom displayed, at all
events during the years of youth and
middle age, to stand aside and bid Godspeed to his successor. For myself I
honour the Duke of Windsor for his
decision and for the way in which he
gave effect to it.
I have thought it right to make these
few remarks for three reasons-I will take
them in the order of time; I do not say
in order of consequence. There was
the oath of fidelity we took last January.
Then there was the touching broadcast
from Windsor with its plea for understanding, and, finally, there was the very
touching message from her Majesty Queen
Mary to her husband's people. With
that, I think, we may leave the past and
turn our attention to the future.
Now his present Majesty, as indeed the
noble Viscount has already reminded the
House, does not ascend the Throne
without a long record of public service to
his credit. He has shown repeatedly the
deep care and sympathy which he bestows
on all the interests of his subjects. And
we know what a grip and good influence
he has over many of the younger members
of the complex organisations and peoples
of the British Empire. We recall, as
the noble Viscount did, that he served
afloat-on H.M.S. Collingwood, I think
-on the day of Jutland, and took his
part in that great and final, if dearly
bought and rather unsensational, victory.
At all events, His Majesty was afloat at
Jutland and did the duty which fell to
him as an officer of the Navy. And we
recall with thankfulness that His
Majesty begins his reign with the assistance of so true and trusty a counsellor
as Mr. Baldwin, the present Prime
Minister of the United Kingdom, to guide
and help him. And he has his Roval
consort, Her Majesty the Queen, who has
long since won her place in the affections
of her new subjects, not only in Scotland,
the country of her origin, but throughout
the Empire. I believe that all will agree
that one of her Majesty's greatest charms
The King's Accession-
Address to His Majesty
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
is that she is a daughter-in-love as well as
a daughter-in-law to Her Majesty Queen
Mary.
We have had Royal Elizabeths before
-there was Elizabeth, Queen Regnant,
whose sailors swept the seas as did their
successors under King George in that
fight off Jutland. We had also
Princess Elizabeth, the daughter of
James VI of Scotland
a
and first
of England from whom indeed His
Majesty derives his hereditary titles
to the Throne. That Royal Lady, it will
be recalled, was known as the "Queen of
Hearts." May it not well be that her
present Majesty will be considered to
have acquired the transference of the
title for herself?
Both their Majesties now adorn a
Throne which is more deeply and
more securely rooted than the Throne
of any dynasty the world has ever
known. That Throne, as I believe, will
endure, because it is based on the goodwill, in the highest sense, of all the
subjects of the Empire. The peoples of
the Empire are determined to maintain
the Throne, and I think historians will
point to the events of the last fortnight
as showing the strength and the solidarity
of that determination.
We wish their Majesties every blessing,
every happiness, and every success in the
arduous tasks that lie before them. They
have, naturally, a great position which
brings with it great responsibilities. We
have no fear but that they will face those
responsibilities, and that they will attain.
the success for which we pray. If I may
I will, in conclusion, once again quote
a poet, and I do
SO in the full
confidence that the
prayer implied
in those words will not fail in its effect:
"God grant their greatness may not fail
through craven fear of being great." I
need scarcely add that I desire to
support the Humble Address.
On Question Motion,
"That an Humble Address be presented to His Most Gracious Sovereign,
We, Your Majesty's most dutiful
and loyal subjects, the Senate of
Northern Ireland, in Parliament
assembled, respectfully tender assurance
of our steadfast devotion to the Crown
and person of Your Majesty and to Her
Majesty the Queen. The people of
Northern Ireland look forward with
complete confidence to the future, in
the knowledge that Your Majesty's
constant concern will be the happiness
and prosperity of the United Kingdom
and of the whole Empire, to which
Ulster is proud to belong,"
agreed to, nemine dissentiente.
Resolved, "That this House do
approach the Governor of Northern
Ireland with the request that he be
pleased to cause the said Address to be
presented to His Majesty."-(Viscount
Charlemont.)
Adjournment.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until such date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Adjourned accordingly at twenty
minutes after Three o'clock
until such date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint.
I
I

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 24th November, 1936) of the
Fourth Parliament of Northern Ireland
in the first year of the Reign of His
Majesty King George the Sixth.
